index.title: index
index.content:
Index
Content catalog of the wiki, grouped by topic. Each page: wikilink +
one-line summary. Read this first when answering a query, then drill into
the relevant pages. Updated on every ingest.

overview — the evolving top-level thesis across topics

Topics

splicing — pre-mRNA / alternative splicing (83 sources)
artificial-intelligence — AI/ML in biology & genomics: foundation models, sequence/structure, single-cell & perturbation methods + safety (17 sources)
3d-genome — 3D organization of the genome (loops → TADs → compartments) and its evolution (7 sources)
gene-regulation — cis-regulatory genomics: enhancers, promoters, TF-binding grammar, enhancer–promoter logic, regulatory variation & reference maps (15 sources)
functional-single-cell-genomics — experimental/resource genomics: single-cell omics, MPRA, digital twins, spatial methods, regulatory-variant function (19 sources)
crispr — CRISPR-Cas biology, genome editing, functional screens & gene therapy; mechanism → tool → screen → therapy → safety (16 sources)
brain-development-neurogenomics — brain development biology, neuronal cell types, brain evolution & neuropsychiatric disease genetics (48 sources)
biometrical-genetics — estimating heritability (twin/family variance components via (generalized) linear mixed models) + the missing-heritability debate (6 sources)


Topic: Splicing
Sources (83)

Grouped; each source page carries human_research / resource flags. See splicing for the full annotated tables.

Foundational biology & reviews (25)

sibley-2016-lessons-from-non-canonical-splicing — non-canonical splicing: recursive, back-splicing/circRNA, exitrons, microexons (NRG 2016)
szabo-2016-detecting-circular-rnas-bioinformatic — circular RNA detection from RNA-seq: artifacts & controls (NRG 2016)
gebauer-2020-rna-binding-proteins-in-human-genetic — RNA-binding proteins in human genetic disease (NRG 2020) · human
giudice-2024-splicing-regulation-through-biomolecular-condensates — splicing regulation via biomolecular condensates / LLPS (NRMCB 2024)
waldron-2016-splicing-linking-genetic-variation — splicing links common variation to disease (NRG Highlight 2016) · human
wrighton-2017-the-diagnostic-power-of-rna-seq — RNA-seq raises Mendelian diagnosis rate (NRG Highlight 2017) · human
sharp-1994-split-genes-and-rna-splicing — Sharp’s Nobel Lecture: split genes & spliceosome biochemistry (Cell 1994)
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — spliceosome as ribozyme; mechanisms of AS regulation (2015)
nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome — AS as the engine of proteome diversity (Nature 2010)
wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human — landmark mRNA-seq survey; defines PSI; ~92–94% genes AS (Nature 2008)
pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing — first deep mRNA-seq AS survey; ~95% multiexon genes (Nat Genet 2008)
wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolutionary-tool — framing review of RNA-seq (NRG 2009)
kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated — developmentally regulated AS networks (NRG 2011)
fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing — combinatorial, position-dependent RBP control (NRG 2014)
witten-2011-understanding-splicing-regulation-through-rna — RNA splicing maps from CLIP + splicing (TIG 2011)
wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts — regulatory elements → integrated splicing code; ExonScan (RNA 2008)
baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator — AS in development & tissue identity (NRMCB 2017)
scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease — RNA mis-splicing in disease; ASO/small-molecule therapy (NRG 2015)
shine-2024-co-transcriptional-gene-regulation-in — co-transcriptional gene regulation (NRMCB 2024)
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — physiology of AS; tissues, cancer, ASD (NRMCB 2022)
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — AS as an evolutionarily labile source of diversity (NRG 2022)
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — regulation + disease + first therapies (NRG 2022)
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — post-transcriptional splicing as a regulatory layer (NRG 2025)
editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — framing editorial (NRG 2025)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — RNA G-quadruplexes as structural AS regulators (TIG 2026)

Methods & tools (22)

mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian — RPKM + ERANGE: first genome-scale RNA-seq (Nat Methods 2008)
katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna — MISO: Bayesian isoform quantification (Nat Methods 2010)
trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification — Cufflinks: assembly + FPKM (Nat Biotechnol 2010)
wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify — SpliceTrap: Bayesian exon inclusion (Bioinformatics 2011)
anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons — DEXSeq: differential exon usage (2012)
brechtmann-2018-outrider-a-statistical-method — OUTRIDER: aberrant expression-outlier detection for rare-disease RNA-seq (AJHG 2018)
segers-2026-saser-juggling-offsets-unlocks — saseR: offset-swap unifies differential usage + aberrant splicing/expression; beats FRASER, scales past OUTRIDER/DEXSeq (Genome Biol 2026)
yano-2025-crosssplice-a-pipeline-for — CrosSplice: rare/deep-intronic splice-site-creating variants (SpliceAI + cross-tissue GTEx) as ASO targets (medRxiv 2025)
dawes-2022-splicevault-predicts-the-precise — SpliceVault: predicts which mis-splicing event a splice-site variant causes from 335K reference RNA-seq; beats SpliceAI clinically (Nat Genet 2023) · human · resource
shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection — rMATS: replicate-aware ΔPSI event caller (PNAS 2014)
alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast — SUPPA: PSI from TPMs, ultrafast (RNA 2015)
bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification — kallisto: pseudoalignment quantification (Nat Biotechnol 2016)
li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna — LeafCutter: annotation-free intron excision; sQTL-scale (Nat Genet 2018)
lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method — PSI-Sigma: all-isoform PSI; long reads (Bioinformatics 2019)
zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis — DARTS: deep-learning-augmented differential AS (Nat Methods 2019)
vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous — MAJIQ v2: LSVs across large datasets (Nat Commun 2023)
wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-efficient-and-flexible — rMATS-turbo: scaled to ×10⁴ samples (Nat Protoc 2024)
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — the splicing code: PWMs → deep learning (NRG 2024)
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying — the modern AS toolkit (NRG 2026)
conesa-2016-survey-of-best-practices — RNA-seq analysis best-practices survey (Genome Biol 2016)
stark-2019-rna-sequencing-the-teenage-years — RNA-seq methods at maturity (NRG 2019)
hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pre-mrna-splicing-methods — Methods-in-Mol-Biol protocols volume (2014)

Population genetics & sQTL (5)

park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative — AS variation across human populations; sQTL (AJHG 2018) · human
zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls — regional sQTL structure in human brain (AJHG 2020) · human
demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing — PAIRADISE: allele-specific AS (AJHG 2020) · human
mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem — TASS catalogue: tandem splice sites, GTEx (PLoS Comput Biol 2021) · human
wang-2025-a-deep-dive-into-statistical — MAJIQTL: improved sQTL statistics (MAJIQ + beta-binomial + weighted testing); explains neurodegenerative GWAS, AD via MS4A3 (AJHG 2025) · human · resource

RBP mechanism & maps (2)

van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional — ENCODE map of 356 human RBPs (Nature 2020) · human
begg-2020-concentration-dependent-splicing-is-enabled — Rbfox intermediate-affinity motifs; concentration-gated AS (NSMB 2020)

Proteome (1)

lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of — JCAST: splice-junction proteogenomics (Cell Rep 2019) · human

Long-read isoforms, single-cell & sQTL/disease (9, 2024–2026)

kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rna-sequencing-of — Nanopore direct RNA-seq: modifications × splicing × poly(A) on one molecule; MOLM13 resource (Cell Genomics 2025)
fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative — Longcell: single-cell/spatial alternative splicing from long reads (Nat Commun 2025)
quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis — isoLASER: cis- vs trans-directed splicing via long-read phasing (Nat Commun 2025) · human
clavell-revelles-2025-long-read-transcriptomics-of-a-diverse — PODER: diverse-cohort long-read; ancestry bias in gene annotation (Nat Commun 2025) · human
bodelon-2026-native-long-read-rna-sequencing — native long-read: activation-induced isoform switching in human monocytes (Nat Commun 2026) · human
humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas — isoMiGA: human microglia isoform atlas + sQTL → AD/PD (Nat Genet 2025) · human
liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral — AIDA: 1M-PBMC multi-ancestry single-cell sQTL atlas → autoimmune disease (Nat Genet 2024) · human
tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants — INTERVAL blood e/sQTL → proteins, metabolites, disease (Nat Genet 2025) · human
venkatasubramanian-2025-splicing-regulatory-dynamics-for-precision — OncoSplice: splicing-subtype discovery in leukemia; PRMT5/IRAK4 therapy (Sci Transl Med 2025) · human

Brain development, neuro & disease (19, 2011–2026)

patowary-2024-developmental-isoform-diversity-in-the — PsychENCODE2 long-read isoform atlas of developing neocortex (GZ/CP + single-cell); 214k isoforms, NDD variant reannotation (Science 2024) · human · resource
heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform — long-read isoform atlas of aged human frontal cortex (AD); public browser (Nat Biotechnol 2024) · human · resource
bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic — isoTWAS: isoform-level TWAS recovers ~60% more neuropsychiatric loci (Nat Genet 2023) · human
bryois-2022-cell-type-specific-cis-eqtls-in-eight — cell-type cis-eQTLs in 8 brain cell types; bulk dilutes cell-type-specific effects (Nat Neurosci 2022) · human
ameur-2011-total-rna-sequencing-reveals — nascent transcription & co-transcriptional splicing in human brain (NSMB 2011) · human
mazin-2012-widespread-splicing-changes-in-human — lifespan brain splicing changes; NMD/poison-exon pattern (Mol Syst Biol 2012) · human
parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna — AS & lncRNA dysregulation in autism cortex (Nature 2016) · human
hwang-2016-dynamic-regulation-of-rna-editing — developmental A-to-I RNA-editing atlas of human cortex (Nat Neurosci 2016) · human · resource
su-2018-alternative-splicing-in-neurogenesis-and — review: neuron-specific splicing regulators in neurogenesis (Front Mol Biosci 2018)
weyn-vanhentenryck-2018-precise-temporal-regulation-of-alternative — timed splicing modules in neural development; Splicescope (Nat Commun 2018)
li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis — PsychENCODE/BrainSpan developmental brain atlas (Science 2018) · human · resource
walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and — prenatal-brain e/sQTL atlas → SCZ/ASD; DEPICT (Cell 2019) · human · resource
wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform — cross-ancestry developmental-brain isoform/splicing QTL atlas; DevBrainHub (Science 2024) · human · resource
nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease — HD aberrant splicing; TDP-43 + m6A (Nat Neurosci 2025) · human
neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing — review: poison exons as a druggable handle (Trends Pharmacol Sci 2025)
wagner-2025-aberrant-splicing-prediction-during-human — AbSplice2: developmental-stage aberrant-splicing variant predictor (bioRxiv 2025) · human · resource
pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in — brain poison-exon catalog + pathogenic variants; EvoNMD (bioRxiv 2026) · human · resource
li-2026-the-contribution-of-short-tandem — SpliceSTR: STR → splicing variation in human cortex (bioRxiv 2026) · human · resource
michielsen-2026-decoding-exon-inclusion-in — neuron vs glia diverge from the splicing code (Genome Biol 2026) · human

Concepts (27)
Machinery & chemistry

splicing — the process: introns/exons, two-step transesterification chemistry
spliceosome — the machine: five snRNPs, assembly cycle, RNA-based catalysis (ribozyme)
splice-sites — 5’ss, branch point, polypyrimidine tract, 3’ss; recognition & degenerate consensus

Regulation

splicing-regulatory-elements — exonic/intronic enhancers & silencers (ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS)
splicing-factors — SR proteins (activators) vs hnRNPs (repressors); tissue-specific factors
rna-binding-proteins — RBPs in splicing; motif reading; disease-linked prion-like domains
splicing-code — predicting isoforms from sequence; PWMs → deep learning (SpliceAI, Pangolin)
rna-structure-and-splicing — structural layer: RNA–RNA base pairing & G-quadruplexes mask/expose splice sites

Timing & coupling

cotranscriptional-splicing — splicing during transcription; coupling to Pol II elongation
post-transcriptional-splicing — splicing after transcription; intron retention; detained introns
splicing-and-chromatin — histone marks, adapters, Pol II speed, chromatin retention

Outcomes & significance

alternative-splicing — event types, prevalence, proteome expansion, functionality debate
intron-retention — a widespread regulatory AS event type; detained introns, AS–NMD
circular-rna — covalently closed RNAs from back-splicing; detection challenges
biomolecular-condensates — LLPS/membraneless platforms (nuclear speckles) regulating splicing
splicing-and-disease — splice-site & splicing-factor mutations in disease and cancer
splicing-therapeutics — ASOs, small molecules, the first approved splicing drugs
splicing-and-evolution — AS scaling with complexity; drift vs selection; adaptations

Methods

splicing-methods — toolkit for studying AS: long-read, single-cell/spatial, CRISPR, sQTL, deep learning
percent-spliced-in — ψ / PSI and ΔPSI: the standard event-based metric for (differential) alternative splicing
rna-seq — the measurement substrate; isoform- vs event-based readouts; founding metrics (RPKM/PSI)
long-read-rna-seq — full-length isoform sequencing (Nanopore/PacBio); direct RNA-seq; ancestry bias in annotation
splicing-qtl — splicing QTLs; genetics → splicing → disease
allele-specific-splicing — within-individual cis-effects of variants on splicing; long-read phasing
splicing-and-neurodevelopment — splicing across brain development, aging & neuro/psychiatric disease
poison-exons — AS–NMD poison exons; dosage tuning & druggable handle
rna-editing — A-to-I editing, a sibling RNA-processing layer to splicing

Entities (47)
Splicing factors

sf3b1 — U2 snRNP/SF3b branch-point factor; mutated in MDS & uveal melanoma; drug target
u2af1 — 3’ splice-site recognition factor; recurrent myeloid-cancer mutations
srsf1 — prototypical SR protein (activator); proto-oncogene (ASF/SF2)
hnrnpa1 — prototypical hnRNP (repressor); SMN2/HIV; ALS prion-like domain
ptbp1 — polypyrimidine-tract repressor; PKM/Warburg switch (PTB/hnRNPI)

Genes / examples

dscam — extreme alternative splicing (tens of thousands of isoforms); neuronal self-avoidance
smn2 — spinal muscular atrophy gene; target of nusinersen & risdiplam

RNA structures

g-quadruplex — noncanonical G-rich fold regulating splicing; cancer/neuro links, G4 ligands

Tools — splicing-code / prediction

spliceai — landmark deep-learning splicing-code model (variant interpretation)
darts — deep-learning-augmented differential-AS inference for low-coverage data
exonscan — early log-odds splicing-code simulator (Wang & Burge)

Tools — event / differential AS

rmats — replicate-aware, event-based differential-splicing caller (ψ/ΔPSI); reference of its class
rmats-turbo — fast re-implementation of rMATS scaling to ×10⁴ samples
miso — Bayesian mixture-of-isoforms quantification with confidence intervals
dexseq — differential exon usage via negative-binomial GLM
splicetrap — Bayesian per-exon inclusion-ratio quantification
psi-sigma — all-isoform PSI index; short- and long-read
leafcutter — annotation-free intron-excision quantification; sQTL-scale
majiq-v2 — local splicing variations across large/heterogeneous datasets
majiqtl — sQTL discovery framework on MAJIQ: beta-binomial effect sizes + covariate-weighted testing; explains neurodegenerative GWAS
suppa — ultrafast PSI from transcript quantifications
pairadise — paired model for allele-specific alternative splicing

Tools — transcript quantification

cufflinks — RNA-seq transcript assembly + FPKM
kallisto — pseudoalignment-based transcript quantification
erange — early RNA-seq pipeline introducing RPKM

Tools — long-read & single-cell AS

longcell — UMI-aware single-cell/spatial alternative-splicing from long reads
isolaser — long-read phasing to separate cis- vs trans-directed splicing
oncosplice — unsupervised splicing-subtype discovery (leukemia)

Tools — variant-effect & neuro

absplice2 — tissue + developmental-stage aberrant-splicing variant predictor
splicevault — predicts which mis-splicing event a splice-site variant causes (300K-RNA Top-4); empirical, beats SpliceAI clinically
splicestr — short-tandem-repeat splicing-QTL mapping (human cortex)
splicescope — neuronal-maturation classifier from splicing profiles
evonmd — genome-wide poison-exon catalog + pipeline
outrider — aberrant expression-outlier detection (autoencoder + NB) for rare-disease RNA-seq
saser — offset-swap NB framework unifying differential usage + aberrant splicing/expression; faster than OUTRIDER/FRASER/DEXSeq
isotwas — isoform-level transcriptome-wide association (neuropsychiatric risk genes)

Resources / datasets

encode-rbp-map — ENCODE binding & functional map of 356 human RBPs
tass-catalogue — catalogue of tandem alternative splice sites (GTEx)
jcast — splice-junction-based proteogenomics database tool
molm13-drna-seq — Nanopore direct-RNA multi-feature dataset (MOLM13)
poder-annotation — cross-ancestry long-read transcript annotation (PODER)
aida — 1M-PBMC multi-ancestry single-cell sQTL atlas
isomiga — human microglia long-read isoform atlas
intervalrna-portal — INTERVAL blood e/sQTL → molecular-trait portal
brain-rna-isoform-browser-tool — long-read isoform browser of the aged human frontal cortex
sciso-portal — long-read isoform atlas of the developing human neocortex (PsychENCODE2)
crosssplice — pipeline for rare/deep-intronic splice-site-creating variants (SpliceAI + cross-tissue) as ASO targets
depict-viewer — DEveloPIng Cortex Transcriptome viewer: prenatal-brain e/sQTL browser
psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource — PsychENCODE/BrainSpan multimodal developmental human-brain resource
devbrainhub — cross-ancestry developing-brain isoform/splicing-QTL atlas portal

Syntheses

cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing — comparison of the two splicing timing modes (from a query)
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate — resolves the “AS functional vs noise” contradiction (Marasco vs Wright)
poison-exons-regulation-and-therapy — poison exons as regulatory knob → disease driver → drug target (across the PE cluster)


Topic: Artificial Intelligence

Tags: each source carries human_research (centrally human subjects/tissue/clinical?) and resource (primary deliverable is a reusable tool/model/dataset?).

Sources (17)
Sequence & single-cell models (2026-07-04 backlog)

theodoris-2023-transfer-learning-enables-predictions — Geneformer: transformer pretrained on ~30M single cells; transfer learning for network biology (Nature 2023) · resource
cui-2024-scgpt-toward-building-a-foundation — scGPT: generative foundation model for single-cell multi-omics (Nat Methods 2024) · resource
roohani-2024-predicting-transcriptional-outcomes-of-novel — GEARS: knowledge-graph GNN for single/multi-gene perturbation outcomes (Nat Biotechnol 2024) · resource
klie-2023-predictive-analyses-of-regulatory-sequences — EUGENe: FAIR deep-learning toolkit for regulatory sequences (Nat Comput Sci 2023) · resource
karollus-2023-current-sequence-based-models — benchmark: seq-to-expression models capture promoters but under-weight distal enhancers (Genome Biol 2023)
sokolova-2024-deep-learning-sequence-models — review of sequence-based deep-learning models of transcriptional regulation & 3D genome (Annu Rev 2024)
seplyarskiy-2023-a-mutation-rate-model — Roulette: basepair-resolution germline mutation-rate model; Pol III mutagenesis (Nat Genet 2023) · resource
hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — safety-centric view of generative AI / foundation models in genomics: dual-use risk, data bias, governance (Trends in Genetics 2026) · human:✗ resource:✗
wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure — trRosettaRNA2: deep-learning RNA 3D structure & conformer prediction via a pre-trained secondary-structure prior (Nat Mach Intell 2026) · human:✗ resource:✓
han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across — UniAIR: multimodal mutation-effect (ΔΔG) prediction across antibody & TCR immune recognition (Nat Mach Intell 2026) · human:✗ resource:✓
driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable — CMonge: conditional optimal-transport for single-cell perturbation modelling (Nat Mach Intell 2026) · human:✗ resource:✓
yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions — CrossDNA: cross-strand DNA sequence language model, orientation-robust & parameter-efficient (Nat Mach Intell 2026) · human:✗ resource:✓
wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge — TxPert: multi-knowledge-graph GNN for transcriptomic perturbation prediction (Nat Biotechnol 2026) · human:✗ resource:✓
gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting — SIGnature: gene-importance scoring by interpreting single-cell foundation models (Nat Biotechnol 2026) · human:✗ resource:✓
wang-2026-regvelo-gene-regulatory-informed-dynamics — RegVelo: RNA velocity coupled to a learnable gene regulatory network for in-silico perturbation (Cell 2026) · human:✗ resource:✓
marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding — FLARE: deep-learning chromatin-accessibility models (ChromBPNet, ~3B predictions) + evolutionary constraint prioritize common/rare noncoding variants; fetal-neuron purifying selection, schizophrenia heritability (Nat Genet 2026) · human:✓ resource:✓
rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl — INGENE & MODULE: co-expression-network models add trans-eQTL signal to cis TWAS across six brain regions; 641 novel schizophrenia-associated genes (Nat Genet 2026) · human:✓ resource:✓

Concepts (24)
Sequence & structure models

foundation-models — large self-supervised models for biological sequences (Nucleotide Transformer, Evo)
generative-ai — self-supervised/generative methods; sequence generation & design
ai-in-genomics — application domain: clinical genetics, precision medicine, pathogen engineering
dna-language-models — DNA-as-text models; reverse-complement symmetry; regulatory/variant tasks
protein-language-models — ESM-style sequence transformers as frozen feature extractors
rna-structure-prediction — predicting RNA 3D structure & conformers from sequence (data-scarce)

Single cells & perturbation

single-cell-foundation-models — pretrained scRNA-seq embedding models (scGPT, SCimilarity, scVI)
single-cell-perturbation-modeling — predicting cell responses to drug/genetic perturbations; OOD generalization
optimal-transport — distribution-mapping for unpaired single-cell data (Monge/Sinkhorn)
knowledge-graphs — gene–gene graphs as inductive-bias priors for ML
model-interpretability — XAI attribution (integrated gradients, DeepLIFT) for biological models

Tasks & techniques

mutation-effect-prediction — estimating ΔΔG / functional impact of mutations
immune-recognition — antibody–antigen & TCR–pMHC binding
multimodal-learning — fusing sequence+structure, or omics+imaging+physiology

Regulatory genomics

noncoding-variants — variants outside coding sequence; common vs ultra-rare effects, cell-type specificity, selection
chromatin-accessibility — open-chromatin (ATAC) regulatory readout; substrate for sequence models & cell-type GWAS mapping
allele-specific-open-chromatin — ASoC: intra-individual allelic test for regulatory-variant effect on accessibility; context-specific NPD risk

Statistical genetics / expression imputation

transcriptome-wide-association-study — impute genetically regulated expression into GWAS to nominate causal genes
gene-coexpression-network — co-varying-gene modules as a proxy for trans-regulation; features for TWAS

The risks

dual-use-risk — beneficial design vs misuse; the biorisk chain; AI-designed phages
biosecurity — biosafety/dual-use framing, One Health, DNA synthesis screening
data-quality-and-bias — European dataset dominance; performance gaps that persist at scale

The response

ai-safety — technical mitigations (filtering, pruning, concept erasure, safe AI) and their limits
ai-governance — EU AI Act high-risk, Ethics by Design, institutional DURC frameworks

Entities (10)
Models / tools

trrosettarna2 — RNA 3D structure & conformer predictor (SS prior + structure-aware attention)
uniair — multimodal framework for immune-interface mutation-effect (ΔΔG) prediction
cmonge — conditional optimal-transport library for single-cell perturbation modelling
crossdna — cross-strand DNA language model (orientation-robust, parameter-efficient)
txpert — multi-knowledge-graph GNN for transcriptomic perturbation prediction
signature-tool — SIGnature: gene-importance scoring via single-cell-FM attributions
regvelo — RNA-velocity + gene-regulatory-network model for single-cell dynamics & in-silico perturbation
flare — Functional Lasso Analysis of Regulatory Evolution: constraint-integrated prioritization of deleterious noncoding variants (built on ChromBPNet)
ingene-module — INGENE & MODULE: co-expression-network models adding trans-eQTL signal to cis TWAS predictors
ctwas — causal-TWAS: joint gene/variant fine-mapping (PIPs) on QTL+GWAS; prioritizes context-specific NPD risk genes


Topic: 3D Genome
Sources (7)
Sequence→folding models & maps (2026-07-04 backlog)

won-2016-chromosome-conformation-elucidates-regulatory — Hi-C of mid-gestation human cortex links enhancers/GWAS variants to distal genes (Nature 2016) · human
fudenberg-2020-predicting-3d-genome-folding — Akita: CNN predicts locus 3D folding from ~1 Mb of sequence (Nat Methods 2020) · resource
schwessinger-2020-deepc-predicting-3d-genome-folding — DeepC: transfer-learning net predicts folding + variant impact from megabase sequence (Nat Methods 2020) · resource
pampari-2024-chrombpnet-bias-factorized-base-resolution — ChromBPNet: base-resolution chromatin-accessibility model; TF grammar, footprints, variants (bioRxiv 2024) · resource
wen-2024-single-cell-multiplex-chromatin — MUSIC: single-nucleus multiplex chromatin + RNA interactions in ageing human brain (Nature 2024) · human · resource
mao-2024-structurally-divergent-and-recurrently-mutated — long-read atlas of structurally divergent / recurrently mutated primate genome regions (Cell 2024) · resource
che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — micro-to-macro framework for 3D-genome evolution: cancer, between species, across kingdoms (Trends in Genetics 2026)

Concepts (8)
The hierarchy

genome-architecture — hierarchical 3D folding (loops → TADs → compartments) as an evolvable trait
chromatin-compartments — A/B compartments, multi-Mb active/inactive partitioning
topologically-associating-domains — ~Mb self-interacting domains (TADs) insulating contacts
chromatin-loops — sub-Mb loops & loop extrusion (CTCF/cohesin); enhancer–promoter loops

How we measure it

chromatin-conformation-capture — Hi-C & imaging that map 3D contacts

How it changes

3d-genome-evolution — micro-to-macro thesis; convergent evolution via distinct machinery
cis-regulatory-elements — primary driver of interspecies architectural divergence
3d-genome-and-disease — cancer as fastest-timescale 3D rewiring (structural bottleneck)

genome-editing — programmable endogenous DNA alteration (deletion/insertion/precise base) via CRISPR

prime-editing — donor-free “search-and-replace” via nCas9-RT + pegRNA (substitutions + small indels)
epigenome-editing — dCas9-tethered modulation of chromatin/methylation/transcription without changing DNA sequence
pooled-crispr-screen — library-scale endogenous perturbation read out by fitness/selection or transcriptome
single-cell-crispr-screen — pooled CRISPR screen with scRNA-seq readout + guide barcodes (Perturb-seq)
crispri-crispra — dCas9-based transcriptional interference/activation (non-cutting)
homology-directed-repair — precise, template-dependent break repair (exact edits)
non-homologous-end-joining — error-prone break repair (indels; knockouts, deletions)

Topic: Biometrical Genetics
Sources (6)

zhang-2023-a-new-method-multiancestry — CT-SLEB: fast multiancestry polygenic risk scores improving non-European prediction (Nat Genet 2023) · human · resource (2026-07-04 backlog)


Heritability-estimation methodology + the missing-heritability debate. See biometrical-genetics for the annotated table.


pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and — (methodology) GLMM/liability-threshold partition of binary traits into maternal/foetal/environmental variance components over extended families; Swedish pre-eclampsia (Stat Med 2004) · human
mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models — (methodology) algebraic equivalence of biometric path-analysis (PAM) and variance-components (VCM) models; VCM (not PAM) fits in SAS PROC MIXED (Behav Genet 2005)
rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and — (methodology) fits twin/family ACE/ADE variance-component models in standard mixed-model software (gllamm), continuous & categorical, instead of SEM (Biometrics 2008)
manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of — (debate) landmark review naming “missing heritability”: GWAS explains little of twin/family heritability; catalogues candidate fixes (Nature 2009) · human
zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability — (debate) “phantom heritability”: additive-assuming estimators inflate total h²; epistasis (limiting-pathway model) can explain much of the gap (PNAS 2012) · human

Concepts (12)

heritability — genetic share of phenotypic variance; the quantity being estimated
quantitative-genetics — parent field: variance partitioning of polygenic traits (Fisher, Wright)
variance-components-model — decompose variance into A/C/D/E components (the VCM)
structural-equation-model — path-analysis (PAM) form; equivalent to VCM; classic SEM route
twin-and-family-study-design — MZ/DZ twins, families, extended pedigrees → relatedness weights
linear-mixed-model — continuous-trait estimation engine (random effects = variance components)
generalized-linear-mixed-model — binary/categorical-trait extension via a link function
liability-threshold-model — binary trait as a threshold on latent normal liability
hierarchical-likelihood — h-likelihood inference for GLMMs over large pedigrees
missing-heritability — gap between family heritability and GWAS-explained variance
genome-wide-association-study — common-SNP scan in unrelateds; the missing-heritability backdrop
epistasis — genetic interactions; source of “phantom heritability” if estimators assume additivity

Entities (3)

gllamm — Stata mixed-model program used by Rabe-Hesketh (2008)
sas-proc-mixed — SAS linear-mixed-model procedure used by McArdle (2005)
mx-software — classic specialized SEM software for twin models (the incumbent being replaced)

log.title: log
log.content:
Log
Chronological, append-only record of ingests, queries, and lint passes.
Each entry starts with ## [YYYY-MM-DD] <type> | <title> so it’s greppable:
grep "^## \[" log.md | tail -5 gives the last 5 events.
[2026-06-04] init | Wiki scaffolded (research / deep-dive)

Created three-layer structure: raw/, wiki/, schema in CLAUDE.md.
Subdirs: wiki/{sources,concepts,entities,syntheses} + overview.md.
Initialized git repo. Ready for first ingest.

[2026-06-04] ingest | Splicing topic — 7 reviews (batch)

Added wiki/topics/ and created topic hub splicing grouping 7 sources.
Ingested 7 papers (full-text grounded) into wiki/sources/:
Lee 2015, Marasco 2022, Wright 2022, Rogalska 2022, Capitanchik 2024,
Choquet 2025, NRG Editorial 2025.
Created 14 concept pages in wiki/concepts/ (machinery, regulation, timing,
outcomes) cross-linked to sources.
Updated index.md and overview.md (first thesis).
Note: #6 is an unsigned Editorial → slug editorial-2025-... (House-rule
exception, no first author).
Open contradiction to revisit: functionality of alternative splicing —
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative (mostly functional) vs
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source (mostly noise).

[2026-06-04] query | Co- vs post-transcriptional splicing comparison

Q: “co-transcriptional vs post-transcriptional splicing 비교해줘”.
Read concepts cotranscriptional-splicing, post-transcriptional-splicing

source choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.


Filed answer as synthesis page
cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing (table + drivers +
continuum framing); linked from index.

[2026-06-04] synthesis | Resolved AS functionality contradiction

Wrote alternative-splicing-functionality-debate resolving the
Marasco-vs-Wright flag: not a factual contradiction but a difference in the
definition of “functional”, denominator, and evidence weighting; both agree on
a conserved functional core.
Downgraded the ⚠️ flag in overview to ✅ reconciled; cross-linked from
alternative-splicing and index.

[2026-06-04] lint | First health check

Link graph healthy: 0 real orphans (only infra files CLAUDE/README unlinked),
0 real broken links (dangling links are template examples inside CLAUDE.md,
excluded from the site). Hubs: splicing-factors (23), spliceosome (22).
Fix applied: standardized off-vocab tags in
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source (evolution/phenotypic-diversity
→ splicing-and-evolution).
Concept-page tags are minimal ([splicing] only) — could enrich for Dataview.

[2026-06-18] ingest | saseR (Segers et al. 2026, Genome Biology) → splicing

Ingested 1 paper into splicing (80 → 81 sources), a PDF the user dropped into raw/
(s13059-026-03973-8.pdf, untracked). A SOFTWARE article introducing saseR (Scalable Aberrant
Splicing and Expression Retrieval), a Bioconductor R package from the Clement lab (statOmics, Ghent).
Source (+1): segers-2026-saser-juggling-offsets-unlocks — the central trick is swapping the
library-size normalization offset for the log of the total gene count in standard NB tools (edgeR /
DESeq2 / DEXSeq), which reinterprets the mean-model parameters as feature usage and
unlocks one framework for differential transcript usage + aberrant splicing/expression outlier detection
(rare disease, no replicates → NB tail-probability “score”). Fast estimator (quadratic variance →
Newton-Raphson = matrix multiply, all genes at once) scales to many latent confounders; RUVr +
Gavish–Donoho threshold replaces grid-search hyperparameter optimization. Workflows saseR-bins /
saseR-junctions / saseR-ASpli. human:✗ (method on GTEx/Geuvadis/Kremer data), resource:✓.
Benchmarks: beats FRASER 2.0 / LeafcutterMD / SPOT for aberrant splicing (Geuvadis); on par with
OUTRIDER / OutSingle for aberrant expression but much faster (GTEx); matches DEXSeq / satuRn for DTU
while scaling far better; ranks Kremer rare-disease genes better than FRASER (recovers TAZ, TALDO1).
New entity (1): saser. No new concepts (links to existing outrider, dexseq,
rna-seq, splicing-methods, splicing-and-disease, long-read-rna-seq, intron-retention);
FRASER / satuRn / OutSingle / SPOT / edgeR / DESeq2 / ASpli / RUVr mentioned as plain text. Added to the
splicing hub (Methods & tools + variant-effect/clinical entity line) and index.
Slug: segers-2026-saser-juggling-offsets-unlocks — 4 of the first 5 title words (dropped trailing
“RNA-seq” for readability); minor House-Rule deviation, flagged. Raw PDF kept under publisher name.
Gate: target paths pre-seeded into the GateGuard state file (auto-expires); lead is sole writer.

Source (+1): wang-2025-a-deep-dive-into-statistical — majiqtl (Barash lab, UPenn), an
sQTL discovery framework that re-engineers three weak links of eQTL-derived sQTL pipelines:
(1) comprehensive splicing representation from MAJIQ (majiq-v2) LSVs (captures de novo
junctions, IR, alt starts/ends); (2) a beta-binomial composite test that
models discrete/heteroskedastic junction counts behind Ψ; (3) a
covariate-weighted FWER (Westfall–Young) correction for sGene discovery (SNP-to-splice-site
proximity, junction coverage). Ships VIOLA-QTL viz tool. On GTEx finds more functionally relevant
sQTLs co-localizing with neurodegenerative-disease GWAS; validates rs528823 (Alzheimer) disrupting
a YBX3 RBP site in MS4A3 → exon-7 skipping.
New entity (1): majiqtl. No new concepts (links existing splicing-qtl,
genome-wide-association-study, majiq-v2, leafcutter, rmats, kallisto, spliceai,

wiki/concepts/3d-genome-and-disease.title: 3d-genome-and-disease
wiki/concepts/3d-genome-and-disease.content:
3D Genome and Disease
Cancer represents the fastest timescale of 3D genome-architecture evolution. Architecture evolves on the shortest timescale via clonal selection of genomic mutations together with plastic epigenetic reprogramming, which together rewire chromatin-loops, topologically-associating-domains, and enhancer–promoter contacts among cis-regulatory-elements (3d-genome-evolution) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Epigenetic changes are a more plastic but equally heritable (across cell divisions) substrate for selection than genomic mutations che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
The paper highlights a transient “structural bottleneck”: in Kras-driven lung adenocarcinoma and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, a stage of nuclear compaction and reduced architectural variability may funnel cells toward a higher-fitness state, accelerating malignancy che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Examples span mechanisms: STAG2 (cohesin) mutation in Ewing sarcoma causes genome-wide loss of enhancer–promoter interactions; the PML-RARα fusion from t(15;17) in acute promyelocytic leukemia produces aberrant looping that globally condenses the genome; and glioblastoma structural variants create novel enhancer–promoter loops che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Related cases include familial adenomatous polyposis, DNMT3A-mutant HSPCs, and relapsed pediatric B-ALL che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — frames cancer as microscale 3D-genome evolution and supplies disease examples.

wiki/concepts/3d-genome-evolution.title: 3d-genome-evolution
wiki/concepts/3d-genome-evolution.content:
3D Genome Evolution
The paper’s core thesis treats 3D genome-architecture as an evolvable trait spanning a micro-to-macro continuum of decreasing plasticity and increasing constraint che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
At the microscale (cellular/cancer timescale), architecture is highly plastic, evolving via clonal selection of mutations and reversible epigenetic reprogramming that rewires chromatin-loops, topologically-associating-domains, and enhancer–promoter contacts (3d-genome-and-disease) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Between closely related species, divergence is heritable but restricted to specific loci, driven primarily by sequence changes in cis-regulatory-elements / TF-binding sites within conserved folding frameworks (the CTCF–cohesin loop-extrusion machinery). Crucially, the chromatin structures themselves are not heritable across species — only the molecular information that re-forms them che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Across the tree of life (macroscale), architectures are strikingly diverse, yet recurrent higher-order features (loops, domains, Rabl/rosette configurations) arise by convergent evolution through distinct molecular machinery — selection acts on architectural function, not specific components che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Plants illustrate this: they lack CTCF and use linear gene clustering instead of spatial 3D clustering to achieve co-expression che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — proposes the multiscale (micro-to-macro) evolutionary framework for the 3D genome.

wiki/concepts/ai-governance.title: ai-governance
wiki/concepts/ai-governance.content:
AI Governance
AI governance supplies the institutional and regulatory layer wrapping the whole lifecycle of ai-in-genomics hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. The source anchors this in the EU AI Act, which classifies AI-driven genomics as “high-risk”, mandating bias assessment, transparency, and risk management hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation.
A central observation is the gap between practice and standard: current foundation-models development largely fails to meet these high-risk requirements hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. Governance therefore cannot be bolted on after deployment.
The source draws on the European Commission’s “Ethics by Design” framework and proposes an institutional risk–benefit framework adapted from the Robert Koch Institute’s Dual-Use Research of Concern (DURC) process, applied from project inception hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. Risks that emerge during a project trigger re-evaluation, making governance a dynamic process rather than a one-time gate.
This institutional layer complements the technical safeguards of ai-safety (insufficient alone) and the threat framing of biosecurity and dual-use-risk — the paper’s thesis being that no single layer suffices.
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — EU AI Act high-risk classification, Ethics by Design, RKI DURC risk–benefit framework

wiki/concepts/ai-in-genomics.title: ai-in-genomics
wiki/concepts/ai-in-genomics.content:
AI in Genomics
AI in genomics is the application domain where generative-ai and foundation-models are deployed on biological sequence data, with particular potential in critical applications such as clinical genetics and precision medicine (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Models offer a route to precision medicine by linearising complex genetic relationships, identifying regulatory elements, and enabling sequence design (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Sequence models such as spliceai exemplify deep learning applied to genomic interpretation.
Advances in generative methods have driven a convergence of AI and synthetic biology, extending the field from interpretation toward multimodal DNA–RNA–protein design — including CRISPR-Cas systems and AI-designed genomes (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Adoption is rapid: about one in nine industry job postings (as of May 2025) require AI-related skills (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
This same convergence is where harms concentrate. The capacity for pathogen engineering means dual-use-risk moves from theoretical to immediate, while data-quality-and-bias threatens equity in clinical tools — motivating ai-safety safeguards, biosecurity, and ai-governance (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — frames AI in genomics as the application domain spanning clinical genetics, precision medicine, pathogen engineering, AI/synthetic-biology convergence, and rapid adoption.

wiki/concepts/ai-safety.title: ai-safety
wiki/concepts/ai-safety.content:
AI Safety
AI safety supplies the technical mitigation toolkit for managing risk in foundation-models applied to ai-in-genomics hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. The source surveys several layered techniques:

Data filtering to remove human pathogens from training data.
Tamper resistance, going beyond mere content removal so open-weight models resist re-introduction of dangerous capabilities.
Weight pruning, shown to improve robustness in NLP and able to target weights associated with dangerous sequence outputs.
Concept erasure / unlearning — techniques like “SafeGenie” selectively remove dangerous functional capacities from biological models.
Safety wrappers — input/output filtering exemplified by Llama Guard and ShieldGemma.
Guaranteed Safe AI (GS AI) (Dalrymple et al.), combining a formal world model, safety specification, and verifier to give quantitative guarantees and enable safe synthesis screening / geo-fencing.
“Scientist AI” as a proposed safer design path.

The source’s key qualification is that these safeguards, while useful, are insufficient alone: they rely on known risk profiles and fail on novel sequences with no natural analogues hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. Robust protection requires safeguards from project inception, complementing the institutional layer of ai-governance and the framing of biosecurity and dual-use-risk.
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — mitigation toolkit and its limits on novel sequences

wiki/concepts/allele-specific-open-chrom.title: allele-specific-open-chromatin
wiki/concepts/allele-specific-open-chrom.content:
Allele-Specific Open Chromatin (ASoC)
A within-individual test for a regulatory variant’s effect on
chromatin-accessibility: at a heterozygous SNP inside an open chromatin region,
ATAC-seq reads are counted per allele, and a significant allelic imbalance means the
variant changes accessibility on its own haplotype. Because the two alleles share the
same nucleus, trans-acting and environmental background cancel out — ASoC is a more
direct, intra-individual readout than a population (inter-individual) caQTL, yet the
two agree closely (effect-size r ≈ 0.82).
In this wiki

Context-specific regulatory variants. 4,900–18,700 ASoC variants across nine
neuron contexts (cell type × stimulation time); ASoC is enriched 30–80-fold in
eQTLs of the matching context, and stimulation-specific ASoC sits preferentially
in enhancers with AP-1 (FOS/JUNB) motifs — i.e. activation-driven
(liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron, neuronal-activation).
Disease prioritization. ASoC (with caQTLs) substantially increases the count of
putatively functional SCZ/BP/MDD GWAS risk variants, and its target genes (via
Micro-C contacts) become candidate causal genes feeding ctwas.

Connections

The allelic, intra-individual sibling of a caQTL — both map where
noncoding-variants act on the chromatin-accessibility landscape; ASoC’s
allelic design makes it especially good at exposing the context-specific
variance behind psychiatric-genetics risk and the missing-heritability
debate.

Sources

liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron

wiki/concepts/allele-specific-splicing.title: allele-specific-splicing
wiki/concepts/allele-specific-splicing.content:
Allele-specific Splicing
Allele-specific (alternative) splicing compares the two alleles of a gene
within the same individual — controlling for trans-acting and environmental
factors — to isolate cis-directed effects of genetic variation on splicing. It
is the within-individual complement to population sQTL mapping and
is especially powerful for rare variants.
In this wiki

Paired statistical models detect it across replicates/populations with power
for rare-variant effects (pairadise,
demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing).
Long-read phasing lets a single sample separate cis- from trans-directed
splicing by linking variants to isoforms on the same molecule (isolaser,
quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis); diverse-cohort
long-read data shows annotation gaps hamper allele-specific transcript discovery
(clavell-revelles-2025-long-read-transcriptomics-of-a-diverse).

Connections

Reads out as ΔPSI between alleles; the mechanistic,
individual-level counterpart of splicing-qtl and a route from variant to
disease.

Sources

quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis · demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing

wiki/concepts/alternative-splicing.title: alternative-splicing
wiki/concepts/alternative-splicing.content:
Alternative Splicing
Alternative splicing (AS) is the process by which a single gene’s pre-mRNA is processed by the spliceosome into multiple distinct transcripts, expanding coding capacity and enabling quantitative regulation of protein levels wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. It is set up by cis-acting splicing-regulatory-elements read by splicing-factors and tissue-specific rna-binding-proteins, whose activator-vs-repressor effect is position-dependent — the logic the splicing-code aims to capture lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Event types. The canonical patterns are exon skipping (cassette exons), intron retention, mutually exclusive exons, and alternative 5′/3′ splice-sites. Their relative weight varies by lineage: exon skipping dominates in metazoans, intron retention in plants and fungi, partly reflecting intron- vs exon-definition modes of spliceosome assembly wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. These same five event types are the unit quantified from RNA-seq as percent-spliced-in (ψ) by event-based callers such as rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection) — see splicing-methods.
Prevalence and proteome expansion. AS occurs in ~90–95% of human genes, and RNA-seq estimates indicate >95–100% of human multiexon genes yield at least two isoforms marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternativelee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Extreme cases include Drosophila Dscam, generating tens of thousands of variants via mutually exclusive exons marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. AS scales with organismal complexity (splicing-and-evolution).
The functionality debate. Low-sensitivity mass spectrometry once suggested most alternative exons are irrelevant, but ribosome profiling shows ~75% of medium/high-abundance skipped-exon mRNAs are ribosome-engaged marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Marasco argues most AS is functional; Wright holds that most events are likely noise, with a conserved functional subset wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. Deep sequencing also detects low-abundance isoforms that may be spliceosomal errors lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This apparent clash is largely reconcilable — see alternative-splicing-functionality-debate (it turns on the definition of “functional”, the denominator counted, and a conserved head vs a noisy low-abundance tail).
AS–NMD and microexons. Coupled AS–NMD via “poison exons” introduces premature stop codons, autoregulating splicing-factors wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. Neural microexons (3–27 nt), promoted by SRRM4 and reduced in autism, are highly conserved regulators marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Sources

marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — prevalence, functionality (proteomics vs ribosome profiling), microexons
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — event types by lineage, noise-vs-function, AS–NMD poison exons
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — >95–100% multiexon prevalence, regulatory mechanism, error caveat
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — link to predictive modelling of AS outcomes

wiki/concepts/autism.title: autism
wiki/concepts/autism.content:
Autism
Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is the most heavily represented neuropsychiatric
condition in this wiki — a developmental disorder whose risk is highly polygenic
and acts early in neurodevelopment. It recurs as the test case linking brain
development, cell types, transcriptomics, and psychiatric-genetics.
In this wiki


Early developmental origin: proposed early brain overgrowth and excess
frontal/temporal neurons derailing social-communication networks
(courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development).


Molecular convergence in cortex: broad, anterior-posterior transcriptomic
(and isoform/splicing) dysregulation that attenuates
regional identity and converges on excitatory-neuron/glial programs
(gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across,
parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna).


Heterogeneity: earlier- vs later-diagnosed autism follow distinct
developmental trajectories and split into two only-modestly-correlated polygenic
factors (zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of).


Activity-dependent TF networks: GRN inference in stimulated iPSC neurons
implicates ASD-risk TFs MEF2C, TCF4, RORB, MLXIP; CRISPR knockout of
RORB/MEF2C/TCF4 validated dysregulation of their ASD targets, and RORB (and
SREBF1/2) tie ASD to lipid metabolism
(liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron, neuronal-activation).


Critical period × cell type: conditional-genetics in mice can localize an
ASD gene’s action to a developmental window and progenitor pool — Chd8
haploinsufficiency acts in midfetal (~E14.5) ventral/ganglionic-eminence
progenitors, and restoring CHD8 there rescues the behavior
(nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8, chd8).


Connections

Ties to splicing-and-neurodevelopment and splicing-and-disease (AS
dysregulation), and is a recurring target in the developmental-brain QTL atlases
(walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and).

Sources

courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development · gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across · zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of · liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron · nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8

wiki/concepts/base-editing.title: base-editing
wiki/concepts/base-editing.content:
Base Editing
A DSB-free genome-editing modality that installs a single-base substitution
without cutting both DNA strands or supplying a donor template. A nickase cas9
(nCas9) is fused to a single-stranded-DNA deaminase that chemically converts one
base within the R-loop editing window:

Cytosine base editors (CBE) — cytidine deaminase (e.g. rAPOBEC1, AID) + a uracil
glycosylase inhibitor (UGI) that shields the U·G intermediate → C·G → T·A.
Adenine base editors (ABE) — a laboratory-evolved TadA deaminase → A·T → G·C
(no glycosylase inhibitor needed, because inosine is poorly excised).
Transversion / dual editors — CGBEs (drop UGI, add a glycosylase so the abasic
site is bypassed by translation-synthesis polymerase REV1) give C→G; DdCBE (a
DddA-derived double-strand deaminase) edits mitochondrial/double-stranded DNA that
R-loop editors cannot reach.

In this wiki

Editing = a DNA lesion whose outcome repair decides. nambiar-2022-crispr-based-genome-editing-through
frames base editing mechanistically: the deaminated base and the non-edited-strand
nick recruit mismatch repair and base-excision repair — editors are engineered
to exploit (UGI blocks UNG) or block these pathways to make the edit permanent and
pure. ~10,000 abasic sites arise per cell per day, so abasic-site handling governs
base-editing purity.
Toolbox context. villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome and
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of place base editors as the first
“second-generation,” beyond-DSB editors — motivated by the specificity and
double-strand-break toxicity of nuclease editing (non-homologous-end-joining vs
homology-directed-repair).

Notable limitations

Bystander editing — every editable base within the ~5–8 bp activity window is
converted, not just the target.
Off-target deamination (guide-independent, at RNA and DNA) and PAM-constrained
targeting scope.

Connections

Sibling precise-editing modality to prime-editing (which is donor-free but
RT-templated and can make arbitrary edits/indels rather than only transitions/
transversions).
Clinically relevant: ABE targeting of PCSK9 to lower LDL, and disruption of the
BCL11A erythroid enhancer underlying casgevy-type therapies.

Sources

nambiar-2022-crispr-based-genome-editing-through
villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of

wiki/concepts/biomolecular-condensates.title: biomolecular-condensates
wiki/concepts/biomolecular-condensates.content:
Biomolecular Condensates
Biomolecular condensates are membraneless compartments formed by liquid–liquid
phase separation (LLPS) of proteins (often with intrinsically disordered / low-complexity
regions) and RNA — e.g. nuclear speckles. They act as spatio-temporal platforms that
concentrate and regulate the splicing machinery.
In this wiki

Condensates / membraneless organelles (nuclear speckles, co-transcriptional
splicing condensates) organize splicing-factors and the spliceosome and are
reciprocally tuned by alternative-splicing; dysregulated phase behavior links to
neurodegeneration, cancer, and developmental disease
(giudice-2024-splicing-regulation-through-biomolecular-condensates).
Connects to cotranscriptional-splicing (where condensates couple transcription
and splicing) and to rna-binding-proteins whose disordered regions drive phase
separation.

Sources

giudice-2024-splicing-regulation-through-biomolecular-condensates

wiki/concepts/biosecurity.title: biosecurity
wiki/concepts/biosecurity.content:
Biosecurity
The source deliberately avoids committing to a single ethical or governance framework, instead adopting a biosecurity perspective grounded in biosafety and dual-use principles to reason about AI in genomics hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. This supplies the broad bio-threat framing within which the AI-specific misuse problem (dual-use-risk) sits.
A key element is the “One Health” approach, covering human, animal, and environmental threats together — strategies for keeping models safe must account for zoonotic spillover and reverse-zoonotic transmission rather than human pathogens alone hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation.
A central control is DNA synthesis screening. The biosecurity community recognises the importance of real-world DNA synthesis as a chokepoint, but the source warns screening is fragile: AI-designed sequences are more diverse than natural ones and may evade database-comparison screening, and pipelines must handle sequences that lack natural analogues hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. Pathogen-engineering concerns motivate safe synthesis pipelines that can geo-fence dangerous capabilities.
Where this page frames the broader bio-threat landscape and physical-world controls (One Health, synthesis screening), dual-use-risk details the AI-amplified misuse problem and biorisk chain; the two are complementary. ai-safety and ai-governance supply the technical and institutional layers.
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — biosafety/dual-use framing, One Health, DNA synthesis screening fragility

wiki/concepts/brain-organoids.title: brain-organoids
wiki/concepts/brain-organoids.content:
Brain Organoids
Brain organoids are 3D, self-organizing neural tissues grown from human
pluripotent stem cells (and assembloids that fuse regional organoids). They
provide an experimental, manipulable model of human neurodevelopment and
disease — capturing features (outer radial glia / OSVZ, human-specific progenitor
expansion) hard to study in animals.
In this wiki

Generation, fidelity to human cortical development, limitations, and disease
modelling (microcephaly, lissencephaly, ZIKV, autism, Timothy syndrome)
(di-lullo-2017-the-use-of-brain).
With single-cell genomics + genome editing, organoids establish
genotype→phenotype causality for development, human-brain-evolution genes
(NOTCH2NL, ARHGAP11B, HARs), and disease
(kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular,
zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain).

Connections

The functional-validation complement to descriptive human-brain genomics
(functional-single-cell-genomics, massively-parallel-reporter-assay).

Sources

di-lullo-2017-the-use-of-brain · kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular

wiki/concepts/cholesterol-and-brain-diso.title: cholesterol-and-brain-disorders
wiki/concepts/cholesterol-and-brain-diso.content:
Cholesterol & Lipid Metabolism in Brain Disorders
An emerging, activation-regulated metabolic axis of neuropsychiatric disease
(NPD). Beyond the dominant synaptic and developmental narratives of
psychiatric-genetics, cholesterol and lipid metabolism — needed for membranes,
myelin, synaptic vesicles, and synaptic endurance — surfaces as a candidate
mechanism, visible chiefly when neurons are stimulated (neuronal-activation).
In this wiki

GWAS-enriched, activation-induced. In iPSC neurons, the early-response gene
module enriched for cholesterol biosynthesis (e.g. INSIG1, HMGCS1,
MSMO1, HMGCR, CYP51A1) is also enriched for NPD GWAS signal; in
schizophrenia patient–derived neurons these genes are differentially expressed
only upon activation (liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron).
CPT1C. Carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1C, a SCZ ctwas-prioritized gene,
carries a stimulation-specific eQTL (effect strongest at 1 h) and regulates
neuronal lipid metabolism to sustain synaptic activity.
Regulatory TFs. Lipid/cholesterol regulators SREBF1/SREBF2 have target
genes enriched for autism risk genes; the ASD TF RORB regulates lipid-droplet
formation — tying the cholesterol axis to ASD as well as SCZ.

Connections

A non-synaptic mechanistic thread within psychiatric-genetics, raised by and
only visible through neuronal-activation; suggests cholesterol-targeting drugs
as a possible therapeutic angle (open question).

Sources

liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron

wiki/concepts/chromatin-accessibility.title: chromatin-accessibility
wiki/concepts/chromatin-accessibility.content:
Chromatin Accessibility
Open (nucleosome-depleted) regions of the genome where transcription factors can
bind — the physical signature of active cis-regulatory-elements. Measured by
ATAC-seq, and at single-cell resolution by scATAC-seq, it is highly
cell-type- and developmental-stage-specific, making it the substrate for
mapping where noncoding-variants act and for training sequence models that
predict regulatory activity.
In this wiki


Model substrate. Per-context ChromBPNet convolutional models are trained on
pseudobulk scATAC-seq across 132 fetal/adult brain and heart contexts; model
interpretation recovers cell-type- and stage-specific TF motifs, and variant
effects feed flare
(marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding).


Disease-cell-type mapping. Single-cell ATAC + RNA links GWAS heritability to
fetal/adult brain cell types via S-LDSC
(kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and), and prenatal→adult cortical
snATAC atlases place autism risk in deep-layer excitatory neurons
(velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal).


Function check. Accessibility predictions are validated against direct
reporter measurement of regulatory activity
(deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory,
massively-parallel-reporter-assay).


Carries more disease heritability than expression. In KCl-stimulated iPSC
neurons, chromatin-accessibility QTLs (caQTLs) and ASoC
variants explained ~2.5× more schizophrenia heritability than eQTLs, and the
stimulation-specific ones carried the strongest GWAS enrichment
(liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron, neuronal-activation).


Connections

A sibling cell-type-specific regulatory readout to dna-methylation; both map
the regulatory landscape of neuronal-cell-types from complementary angles.
The accessibility → expression → disease chain underlies cell-type GWAS
interpretation (sc-linker, psychiatric-genetics).

Sources

marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding · kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and · liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron

wiki/concepts/chromatin-compartments.title: chromatin-compartments
wiki/concepts/chromatin-compartments.content:
Chromatin Compartments
Chromatin compartments are the largest tier of genome-architecture, operating at the multi-Mb to whole-genome scale che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Revealed by chromatin-conformation-capture, the genome partitions into two spatially segregated states: the active A compartment and the inactive B compartment che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. They sit above topologically-associating-domains (~Mb) and chromatin-loops (sub-Mb) in the folding hierarchy.
Compartments vary considerably across the tree of life. The sea walnut Mnemiopsis leidyi lacks compartments entirely, illustrating that this level is not universal che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. In plants, standard mammalian pipelines detect only the global B compartment (pericentromeric, transposon-rich) and global A compartment (gene-dense); resolving functional local compartments requires customized local eigenvalue analysis rather than canonical genome-wide methods che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. In bacteria, E. coli active domains and repressed compartments display distinct architectures che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. This taxonomic diversity supports the source’s broader argument that higher-order architectural features recur through convergent evolution and that canonical mammalian metrics often fail on non-mammals (see 3d-genome-evolution) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — defines A/B compartments at multi-Mb scale and documents their absence/variation across taxa, including plant local compartments.

wiki/concepts/chromatin-conformation-cap.title: chromatin-conformation-capture
wiki/concepts/chromatin-conformation-cap.content:
Chromatin Conformation Capture (Hi-C)
Chromatin conformation capture and complementary imaging are the methods that map physical contacts within the 3D genome, revealing how the mammalian nuclear genome folds into a hierarchy che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. These approaches expose nested layers of genome-architecture: chromatin-loops (~10 kb–200 kb), topologically-associating-domains (TADs, ~150 kb–1 Mb), and chromatin-compartments (multi-Mb, A/B) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
By measuring spatial proximity, Hi-C shows how these layers dynamically modulate the distance between cis-regulatory-elements and promoters, shaping gene-expression programs and cellular identity che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Hi-C signatures also fingerprint mechanisms: interarm interactions appear as a secondary diagonal reflecting SMC loop extrusion in bacteria che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
As a comparative tool, conformation capture is the basis for studying how architecture evolves. Canonical mammalian pipelines, however, fail on non-mammals — plant local compartments require customized local eigenvalue analysis, and dinoflagellate condensed heterochromatin resists standard detection che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Combining comparative Hi-C with phylogenetics and machine learning is proposed as a route to reconstruct ancestral 3D-genome states che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — establishes Hi-C/imaging as the basis for resolving hierarchical folding and for comparative, evolutionary 3D-genome studies.

wiki/concepts/chromatin-loops.title: chromatin-loops
wiki/concepts/chromatin-loops.content:
Chromatin Loops
Chromatin loops are the finest tier of genome-architecture, operating at the sub-Mb scale (reported as ~10 kb to ~200 kb), beneath ~Mb topologically-associating-domains and multi-Mb chromatin-compartments che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. By bringing distal sequences into spatial proximity, loops modulate contacts between cis-regulatory-elements and promoters, helping shape gene-expression programs and cellular identity che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
In mammals, loops form through loop extrusion: cohesin extrudes DNA until blocked by convergent pairs of CTCF-binding sites, generating both loops and the boundaries of topologically-associating-domains che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Transcription-related enhancer–promoter loops are additionally mediated and stabilized by RNA Pol II and transcription-factor binding che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Loops are highly plastic and central to evolutionary change. In the primate brain, human-specific loops enriched for enhancer–enhancer interactions upregulate neurodevelopmental genes such as those for axon guidance and synapse assembly che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. In cancer they are pervasively rewired: glioblastoma structural variants create novel enhancer–promoter loops, while STAG2 (cohesin) mutation in Ewing sarcoma causes genome-wide loss of enhancer–promoter interactions (see 3d-genome-and-disease) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — defines sub-Mb loops, the CTCF/cohesin loop-extrusion model, and enhancer–promoter looping.

wiki/concepts/circular-rna.title: circular-rna
wiki/concepts/circular-rna.content:
Circular RNA
Circular RNAs (circRNAs) are covalently closed RNA rings produced by
back-splicing, where a downstream 5′ splice site joins an upstream 3′ splice
site — a non-canonical output of the spliceosome and a form of
alternative-splicing.
In this wiki

A non-canonical splicing event whose biogenesis competes with linear splicing;
surveyed alongside recursive splicing, exitrons, microexons and trans-splicing
(sibley-2016-lessons-from-non-canonical-splicing).
Detection is hard: circRNAs are identified from RNA-seq by back-spliced
(head-to-tail) junction reads, which are prone to artifacts (template switching,
trans-splicing, mapping errors) — so statistical controls + RNase R / experimental
validation are needed (szabo-2016-detecting-circular-rnas-bioinformatic, a
splicing-methods / data-quality-and-bias problem).

Sources

szabo-2016-detecting-circular-rnas-bioinformatic · sibley-2016-lessons-from-non-canonical-splicing

wiki/concepts/cis-regulatory-elements.title: cis-regulatory-elements
wiki/concepts/cis-regulatory-elements.content:
Cis-Regulatory Elements
Cis-regulatory elements (enhancers and related sequences) are the primary driver of architectural divergence between closely related species. The hierarchical layers of genome-architecture — chromatin-loops, topologically-associating-domains, and chromatin-compartments — dynamically modulate the spatial proximity between cis-regulatory elements and promoters, shaping gene-expression programs and cellular identity che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Between closely related species, divergence operates on a long timescale with stable inheritance but is restricted to specific loci, driven primarily by sequence-encoded changes in cis-regulatory elements and TF-binding sites within conserved folding frameworks — the CTCF–cohesin loop-extrusion machinery che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. In this model, the conserved framework is preserved while local sequence changes reshape enhancer–promoter contacts (3d-genome-evolution) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
This same logic links interspecies divergence and cancer: both rewire expression by altering local chromatin at TF-binding sites, the key distinction being heritability of the resulting structures (3d-genome-and-disease) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. A primate example: human-specific chromatin-loops enriched for enhancer–enhancer interactions upregulate neurodevelopmental genes che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — identifies cis-regulatory sequence change as the main driver of interspecies architectural divergence.

wiki/concepts/cis-regulatory-grammar.title: cis-regulatory-grammar
wiki/concepts/cis-regulatory-grammar.content:
Cis-regulatory Grammar
The positional and combinatorial rules by which sequence features
(cis-regulatory-elements — motifs, their spacing, and their distance from the
TSS) determine gene expression level. “Grammar” because, as in language, the same
words (motifs) mean different things depending on position and context.
In this wiki

groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at finds expression is most
tunable in a ~500-bp core-promoter window and that mutation–effect rules are
gene specific (heuristics do not transfer across PsbS, Raf1, SBPase) — a direct
readout of grammar via massively-parallel-reporter-assay.
Genomic language models (e.g. in crossdna, splicing-code) aim to learn
this grammar from sequence, but still struggle with activating/hypermorphic edits,
motivating direct measurement.

Sources

groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at

wiki/concepts/cotranscriptional-splicing.title: cotranscriptional-splicing
wiki/concepts/cotranscriptional-splicing.content:
Co-transcriptional Splicing
Co-transcriptional splicing is intron removal by the spliceosome that occurs during transcription, while the nascent pre-mRNA is still being synthesized by RNA polymerase II — the complement to post-transcriptional-splicing, which completes after transcription and polyadenylation choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. The spliceosome assembly steps (E → A → B → Bact/B* → C) are presumed identical in both modes; what differs is the timing relative to synthesis lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Most mammalian introns — roughly 70% — are excised on chromatin, much of it co-transcriptionally choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Kinetic coupling and the “window of opportunity”
Splice-site choice is set by cis-elements (splicing-regulatory-elements), trans splicing-factors, and coupling to Pol II elongation rate marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. In the kinetic coupling model, a slow polymerase widens the time window in which an upstream splice site can be recognized before a competing downstream one emerges: slow elongation promotes inclusion of class I exons and skipping of class II exons marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Pol II elongation runs at ~3–4 kb/min, and “slow” polymerase mutants alter splicing patterns lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This kinetics feeds directly into alternative-splicing outcomes, part of the broader splicing-code marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Elongation rate is itself modulated by chromatin: histone marks (e.g. H3K9ac, H3K9me2), nucleosomes and adapter proteins link splicing-and-chromatin to splicing outcomes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. The Pol II C-terminal domain (CTD) and co-transcriptional RNA folding further shape which rna-binding-proteins and factors engage the emerging transcript choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Sources

choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — co- vs post-transcriptional contrast; ~70% of introns excised on chromatin; identical assembly steps.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — kinetic coupling model; class I/II exons; elongation–chromatin link.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — Pol II ~3–4 kb/min; slow-polymerase effects; adapter proteins.

wiki/concepts/crispri-crispra.title: crispri-crispra
wiki/concepts/crispri-crispra.content:
CRISPRi / CRISPRa
Catalytically dead Cas9 (dCas9) repurposed to tune transcription instead of
cutting DNA. Fused to a repressor domain (KRAB) and targeted to a promoter, dCas9
gives CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) — reversible knockdown; fused to an activator
(VP64/VPR/SAM) it gives CRISPR activation (CRISPRa) — targeted upregulation. Both
keep the genome intact, are titratable, and are programmable by a single guide RNA,
making them the workhorses of endogenous screening and of
dosage-based gene therapy.
In this wiki

Screening for neurological disease. CRISPRi/CRISPRa screens in human iPSC-derived
neurons/glia turn GWAS loci into causal mechanisms and drug targets
(kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for, crisprbrain).
Dosage therapy. CRISPRa (dCas9–VP64 + promoter sgRNA) of the endogenous
functional allele rescues SCN2A-haploinsufficiency in mice and human neurons — a
route for genes too large for AAV replacement
(tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related).

Connections

The non-cutting branch of genome-editing and a readout mode of the
pooled-crispr-screen / single-cell-crispr-screen family; mechanistically
built on dcas9. Central tool of the crispr topic.

Sources

kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for
tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related

wiki/concepts/data-quality-and-bias.title: data-quality-and-bias
wiki/concepts/data-quality-and-bias.content:
Data Quality and Bias
Data quality and bias is a systemic risk in ai-in-genomics: genomic datasets are dominated by European individuals, so foundation-models and other generative-ai systems perform poorly on under-represented populations (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Critically, this gap persists as datasets grow, propagating inequity into clinical tools rather than dissolving with scale (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
The source gives concrete figures. Polygenic risk scores built from European cohorts lose 15–20% predictive accuracy in African groups (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). This compounds with distribution-shift failures: Nucleotide Transformer embeddings lose 23% accuracy on rare tumour types absent from pre-training, and models trained mostly on European genomes perform 18–41% worse on South Asian regulatory elements (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Even Evo fails to generalise to eukaryotes, producing nonviable sequences in 94% of yeast chassis trials unless trained across the full tree of life (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
These fairness failures make bias a first-order concern for ai-safety and ai-governance; the EU AI Act accordingly mandates bias assessment for high-risk genomic AI (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — documents European dataset dominance, distribution shift, fairness gaps, and the persistence of bias as data scales.

wiki/concepts/digital-twins.title: digital-twins
wiki/concepts/digital-twins.content:
Digital Twins
Computational, per-individual (or per-organ) models that integrate molecular,
physiological, functional and clinical data to forecast a biological system’s
behavior — and, crucially, to serve as a counterfactual control: what would
this same organ have done without treatment.
In this wiki

dt-lung builds digital twins of ex vivo human lungs from the largest clinical
ex vivo lung perfusion (EVLP) dataset (951 lungs), forecasting >75 multimodal
parameters via multimodal-learning (physics-informed + ML)
(zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex).
The counterfactual framing let it detect a drug effect (alteplase) that a
conventional control cohort missed, with ~3× sample-size efficiency — the
translational entry point of functional-single-cell-genomics.

Open issues

Built from one center and disease-free donors; forecasts only the first hours;
patient-data sharing is restricted — limits on generalizability and reproducibility.

Sources

zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex

wiki/concepts/dna-language-models.title: dna-language-models
wiki/concepts/dna-language-models.content:
DNA Language Models
Self-supervised models that treat DNA sequence as text, pretrained (often with
