masked-token objectives) to learn transferable representations of genomic function
— regulatory elements, variant effects, chromatin state. A genomics specialization
of foundation-models within ai-in-genomics; examples include DNABERT-2,
Nucleotide Transformer, HyenaDNA, GROVER, Caduceus, Evo2, AlphaGenome.
A recurring design tension
DNA is double-stranded, so models must respect reverse-complement symmetry.
Strategies range from data augmentation to equivariant architectures to — in
crossdna — explicit, dynamic cross-strand interaction at the representation
layer, which yields orientation-robust predictions at a fraction of the parameters
(yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions).
Connections

Continuous with the sequence-to-function lineage of the splicing-code and
spliceai (splice-site prediction is a standard DNA-LM benchmark task).
Individual-level prediction (eQTL effect sizes) remains hard when pretraining on a
single reference genome — a data-quality-and-bias limit.

Sources

yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions

wiki/concepts/dna-methylation.title: dna-methylation
wiki/concepts/dna-methylation.content:
DNA Methylation
Cytosine methylation (5mC) is the canonical epigenetic mark of the genome. In the
brain it occurs in two contexts — CG (mCG) and the neuron-enriched
non-CG / CH (mCA) — and is highly cell-type specific, so the methylome
alone can classify cell types. It is read by methylation-sensitive transcription
factors and marks active vs silent cis-regulatory-elements, making it a
primary layer of gene regulation alongside chromatin-accessibility.
In this wiki

Cell-type resolution. Single-nucleus methylome+transcriptome profiling
(snmct-seq) shows cell type explains 80–99% of global methylome variance;
methylome-only label transfer classifies cortical neurons at >97% accuracy
(eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation).
Decoupled from transcription in disease. In autism prefrontal cortex,
differentially methylated regions are enriched in cell-type-specific regulatory
elements yet are largely uncorrelated with gene expression — methylation and
transcriptional dysregulation behave as independent layers
(eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation).
Aging dominates the signal. Age effects on the cortical methylome dwarf
diagnosis effects (~1.4M age-DMRs), gaining methylation with age (most in
excitatory neurons) while CTCF motifs lose it; epigenetic clocks behave very
differently across cell types
(eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation).

Connections

A sibling regulatory-genomics readout to open chromatin
(chromatin-accessibility) and a sibling RNA-processing layer to
rna-editing; all three are cell-type-specific marks mapped to the same
neuronal-cell-types.
Relevant to psychiatric-genetics and neurodevelopment as a candidate
mechanism linking genotype to the developing/aging cortex.

Sources

eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation

wiki/concepts/dual-use-risk.title: dual-use-risk
wiki/concepts/dual-use-risk.content:
Dual-Use Risk
Dual-use risk is the tension whereby the same foundation-models capabilities that enable beneficial biological design (ai-in-genomics) can also be turned toward misuse such as pathogen engineering hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. The central claim of the source is that generative-ai for biological sequences moves this risk “from theoretical to immediate.”
The paper insists misuse be analysed across the entire biorisk chain — design → build → test → learn — rather than as a single point hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. Models can raise the “ceiling of harm”: ML-designed viral libraries yield ~10x more successful variants than traditional NNK libraries, and FMs may overcome evolutionary trade-offs (transmissibility vs virulence) to enable pathogens worse than natural ones, including speculative existential-scale threats.
Feasibility is no longer hypothetical: King et al. created and lab-tested the first AI-generated bacteriophages, some with greater fitness and faster lysis than wild-type, proving genomic design is practically achievable hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation. Yet current bottlenecks (scarce data, lab expertise, restricted materials) confine credible end-to-end misuse mostly to state-level or highly resourced actors; nonstate actors lack the means to translate AI designs into physical harm.
This page focuses on the AI-amplified misuse problem; the broader threat framing and controls are covered in biosecurity.
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — biorisk-chain analysis, King et al. phage demonstration, state-level actor bottlenecks

wiki/concepts/epigenome-editing.title: epigenome-editing
wiki/concepts/epigenome-editing.content:
Epigenome Editing
Programmable modification of the epigenetic state of a locus — chromatin marks,
DNA methylation, transcriptional activity — without altering the underlying DNA
sequence. A catalytically dead Cas (dcas9) serves as an RNA-guided anchor that
tethers an effector domain to a chosen genomic address.

Transcriptional modulation — crispri-crispra: dCas9–KRAB represses (CRISPRi),
dCas9–VP64/VPR/p300 activates (CRISPRa).
DNA-methylation writers/erasers — dCas9 fused to DNMT3A (deposit) or TET1 (remove)
5-methylcytosine (see dna-methylation).
Heritable silencing/reactivation — CRISPRoff (a dCas9–DNMT3A/3L + KRAB fusion)
installs mitotically stable, reversible methylation-based silencing; CRISPRon
reverses it.

A key attraction is that epigenetic edits are non-genotoxic (no break, no sequence
change) and often reversible, unlike nuclease, base, or
prime editing.
In this wiki

Toolbox context. villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome
organizes epigenome editors as a distinct arm of the CRISPR toolbox — alongside
DNA-sequence editors and RNA-targeting editors (rna-editing via Cas13,
REPAIR/RESCUE) — arguing DSB-free, reversible modalities broaden the therapeutic
window. pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of similarly frames CRISPRoff/CRISPRon
and dCas9 effectors as the epigenome-editing frontier.

Connections

Built on the same dcas9 scaffold as transcriptional crispri-crispra screens,
which appear throughout this wiki’s functional-genomics and neurological-disease work
(kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for,
tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related).
Reads/writes the dna-methylation layer that also serves as a rare-disease
diagnostic readout (episignatures).

Sources

villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of

wiki/concepts/epistasis.title: epistasis
wiki/concepts/epistasis.content:
Epistasis (genetic interactions)
Non-additive interaction between genetic loci, where the effect of one variant
depends on the genotype at another. Epistasis contributes to genetic variance but
is not captured by the additive (breeding-value) term that narrow-sense
heritability and standard variance-components-model estimators are built on.
Why it matters here
Epistasis is the mechanism at the heart of
zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability. Standard estimators of total
heritability implicitly assume no genetic interactions; when interactions exist,
those estimators are biased upward, producing “phantom heritability.” Zuk et
al. formalize this with the limiting pathway (LP) model — a phenotype set by
the rate-limiting input among k additive pathways — which generates substantial
phantom heritability for k > 1 (and reduces to the ordinary additive model at
k = 1). Under such a model a large share of the
missing-heritability could be an artifact rather than undiscovered variants.

manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of lists gene–gene interactions
among the candidate explanations for missing heritability, but treats them as one
of several real-but-undiscovered sources rather than as a bias in the estimate.

Epistasis thus sits at the additive-assumption fault line running through the whole
biometrical-genetics topic and its parent field quantitative-genetics;
it is also a concern when interpreting variants from a
genome-wide-association-study.

wiki/concepts/foundation-models.title: foundation-models
wiki/concepts/foundation-models.content:
Foundation Models
Foundation models are large, self-supervised models trained on vast amounts of biological sequence data — DNA, RNA, and protein — that can then be adapted to many downstream tasks in ai-in-genomics (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). The term “foundation models” was coined in 2021, building on the GPT/BERT lineage of generative-ai, whose underlying transformer architecture was introduced in 2017 (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Named examples in the source include the Nucleotide Transformer, trained in an unsupervised manner, and the Evo family (Evo 1 and Evo 2), which enable multimodal DNA–RNA–protein design (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Architecturally, these models rest on transformers, but more recently state-space models such as Mamba have emerged as competitive and more efficient alternatives for long-context data, including DNA, where they capture long-range dependencies effectively (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Sequence models like spliceai sit within this broader deep-learning lineage.
Foundation models are transformative — identifying regulatory elements and enabling sequence design — but the same capabilities raise dual-use-risk and inherit data-quality-and-bias (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — defines biological foundation models, names Nucleotide Transformer and Evo, dates the term to 2021, and covers transformer/Mamba architectures.

wiki/concepts/gene-coexpression-network.title: gene-coexpression-network
wiki/concepts/gene-coexpression-network.content:
Gene Co-expression Network
A network in which genes are linked when their expression co-varies across
samples. Modules of co-expressed genes capture shared regulation — often the
downstream footprint of trans-acting regulators — and so encode information that
local (cis) variant–gene models miss. They are a workhorse representation for
distilling high-dimensional transcriptomes into interpretable, regulation-aware
features.
In this wiki

rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl uses co-expression structure to
build its INGENE & MODULE predictors: candidate trans-acting
variants are aggregated through co-expression modules to add distal regulatory signal
on top of conventional cis eQTL predictors, improving
TWAS power in human brain.

Connections

The mechanism by which TWAS can move beyond cis: co-expression is a proxy for the
trans-regulatory wiring that networks expose.
Conceptually adjacent to knowledge-graphs used for perturbation prediction
(txpert) — both impose structured priors over genes to improve generalization.

Sources

rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl

wiki/concepts/generalized-linear-mixed-m.title: generalized-linear-mixed-model
wiki/concepts/generalized-linear-mixed-m.content:
Generalized linear mixed model (GLMM)
A linear-mixed-model extended through a link function to non-Gaussian
outcomes — binary, count, ordinal. It carries the same random effects (and hence
the same variance components) but models them on a transformed scale, so the
biometrical decomposition applies to categorical phenotypes.
Why it matters here
Many phenotypes of interest (disease yes/no, depression, pre-eclampsia) are
binary, where a plain linear-mixed-model is inappropriate. The GLMM lets
the variance-components-model — and therefore heritability — be estimated
for such traits, typically alongside a liability-threshold-model interpretation
of the binary outcome.

pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and uses a GLMM (fitted by
hierarchical-likelihood) over extended families to partition a binary trait
into maternal/foetal genetic and environmental components.
rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and shows the dichotomous case
(depression) can be handled within standard GLMM software, avoiding the
custom programs earlier work required — a key practical contribution.

Caveat noted across these sources: for non-continuous outcomes the likelihood often
lacks a closed form, so estimation is harder and approximations matter.

wiki/concepts/generative-ai.title: generative-ai
wiki/concepts/generative-ai.content:
Generative AI
Generative AI is the technical substrate beneath foundation-models and modern ai-in-genomics (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). The source places AI methods in biology on a spectrum: supervised methods, where a target or output is paired with a particular sample feature; entirely self-supervised methods; and stacked models that combine elements of both (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Because of the vast amount of data involved, self-supervised methods — referred to in the source as “generative AI” or simply AI — have proven especially effective (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). Their power lies in sequence generation and design: generative foundation-models such as the Evo family enable multimodal DNA–RNA–protein design, while sequence models like spliceai illustrate the broader deep-learning approach to biological sequences (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
This generative substrate is double-edged. The same self-supervised methods that drive beneficial design also amplify data-quality-and-bias and convert dual-use-risk from theoretical to immediate, motivating layered ai-safety safeguards and ai-governance (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Sources

hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation — distinguishes supervised, self-supervised, and stacked methods and frames generative (self-supervised) AI as the technical substrate for sequence generation and design.

wiki/concepts/genome-architecture.title: genome-architecture
wiki/concepts/genome-architecture.content:
Genome Architecture
Genome architecture is the hierarchical 3D folding of the nuclear genome inside the nucleus, revealed by chromatin-conformation-capture and imaging che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. In mammals it resolves into nested structural levels of decreasing scale: chromatin-compartments (multi-Mb, A/B), topologically-associating-domains (~Mb), and chromatin-loops (sub-Mb) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. These layers reconcile two competing demands — compacting a large genome into the nucleus while keeping its information accessible — by dynamically tuning the spatial proximity between cis-regulatory-elements and promoters, thereby shaping gene-expression programs and cellular identity che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
The central thesis of the source is that architecture is itself an evolvable trait, not merely a static scaffold (see 3d-genome-evolution) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. A general trend reported is that more intricate 3D organization parallels increasing organismal complexity, proxied by the number of cell types and tied to independently evolved multicellularity — though this correlation is mainly demonstrated within animals and remains unclear in plants che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. A recent comparative analysis further suggests 3D organization is evolutionarily more flexible (under lower constraint) than many other epigenetic markers che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — defines the folding hierarchy and frames architecture as an evolvable trait correlated with organismal complexity.

wiki/concepts/genome-editing.title: genome-editing
wiki/concepts/genome-editing.content:
Genome Editing
The deliberate, programmable alteration of an endogenous DNA sequence — deletion,
insertion, or single-base substitution — most commonly via CRISPR/Cas9 directed by a
guide RNA. In functional genomics it converts correlation (a variant associated with
a phenotype) into causation (the edited genome shows the effect), and at scale it
powers the endogenous-genome screens collected under pooled-crispr-screen.
In this wiki

Programmed large deletions. gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements
uses paired gRNAs (scandel) to make thousands of overlapping kilobase deletions
tiling a locus — testing which cis-regulatory-elements are required in place.
Precise single-base edits. sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel
(crispey) couples a retron to the guide RNA so a homology-bearing donor
(msDNA) is produced in-cell, installing thousands of exact natural variants
by homology-directed repair for pooled fitness mapping.

Connections

The endogenous complement to episomal massively-parallel-reporter-assay and the
enabling step for both fitness- and transcriptome-readout
(single-cell-crispr-screen) screens.

Sources

gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements
sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel

wiki/concepts/genome-wide-association-st.title: genome-wide-association-study
wiki/concepts/genome-wide-association-st.content:
Genome-wide association study (GWAS)
A study design that scans hundreds of thousands to millions of common SNPs across
many individuals to find variants statistically associated with a trait or disease.
GWAS replaced candidate-gene and family-linkage studies as the dominant tool for
dissecting complex-trait genetics in the late 2000s.
Role in this wiki
GWAS is the empirical backdrop to the missing-heritability thread of the
biometrical-genetics topic. By 2009 GWAS had found >1,200 trait-associated loci,
yet the variance they explained fell far short of the heritability estimated
from twin-and-family-study-design data — the gap named
missing-heritability.

manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of reviews why common-variant
GWAS explain so little of family heritability and what to do about it.
zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability argues the shortfall is partly
illusory: GWAS estimate the additive (numerator) contribution while
epistasis inflates the family-based total (denominator).

GWAS measures the genetic contribution among (largely) unrelated individuals,
in contrast to the pedigree-based variance-components-model of the topic’s other
sources — a different data type targeting the same quantity.

Cross-topic: the SNP/GWAS heritability captured here is what
sc-linker (in functional-single-cell-genomics) partitions across cell
types via S-LDSC — see heritability for the family-vs-GWAS distinction.

wiki/concepts/heritability.title: heritability
wiki/concepts/heritability.content:
Heritability
The proportion of phenotypic variance in a trait attributable to genetic
variance in a population. Narrow-sense heritability h² counts only additive
genetic variance (the breeding value that parents transmit); broad-sense H²
counts all genetic variance (additive + dominance + epistasis).
Core idea
Heritability is the headline quantity that biometrical-genetic analysis exists to
estimate. It is not estimated by measuring genes directly but by partitioning
observed trait variance into latent components — additive genetic (A), shared/common
environment (C), unique environment (E), and sometimes dominance (D) — via the
variance-components-model, using the differing genetic relatedness of
individuals in a twin-and-family-study-design. h² = A / (A + C + E).
In this wiki

For continuous/Gaussian traits, heritability falls out of a
linear-mixed-model whose random-effect variances are the A/C/E components
(rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and,
mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models).
For binary/dichotomous traits, heritability is defined on a latent liability
scale (the liability-threshold-model) and estimated with a
generalized-linear-mixed-model
(pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and,
rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and).
It is a population- and environment-specific quantity, not a fixed property of a
trait — a recurring caveat across quantitative-genetics.


Note: this family/twin (pedigree) heritability is conceptually related to,
but distinct from, the SNP/GWAS heritability partitioned by cell type in the
functional-single-cell-genomics topic (e.g. sc-linker via S-LDSC).

wiki/concepts/hierarchical-likelihood.title: hierarchical-likelihood
wiki/concepts/hierarchical-likelihood.content:
Hierarchical likelihood (h-likelihood)
An inference framework for models with random effects (Lee & Nelder) that works
directly with the joint likelihood of the data and the unobserved random
effects, avoiding the intractable high-dimensional integration that exact marginal
maximum likelihood would require for a generalized-linear-mixed-model.
Why it matters here
For binary traits over large, irregular pedigrees the marginal likelihood has no
closed form (see generalized-linear-mixed-model), so a practical estimation
method is essential.

pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and uses hierarchical likelihood to fit
its many-component variance-components-model for binary traits across extended
families with arbitrary structure and incomplete data — the machinery that makes
estimating heritability feasible in that setting.

This is one of several estimation routes in the biometrical-genetics topic;
contrast the standard-software maximum-likelihood / mixed-model route emphasized by
rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and.

wiki/concepts/homology-directed-repair.title: homology-directed-repair
wiki/concepts/homology-directed-repair.content:
Homology-Directed Repair (HDR)
The precise DNA double-strand-break repair pathway: a homologous donor template
guides accurate copying, so a supplied donor lets CRISPR install an exact,
user-defined edit (a single base, an insertion). HDR is template-dependent and
largely restricted to dividing cells, making efficient, in-place precise editing a
central challenge of genome-editing — and the reason precise-editing platforms
engineer better donor delivery.
In this wiki

Reviewed as one of the two competing break-repair outcomes that define CRISPR
editing precision (pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of).
crispey concentrates an HDR-preferred single-stranded donor (retron msDNA) at
the cut site to reach single-base precision at scale
(sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel).

Connections

The precise counterpart to error-prone non-homologous-end-joining; both are the
cellular repair outcomes CRISPR nucleases exploit (genome-editing, crispr).

Sources

pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of
sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel

wiki/concepts/human-brain-evolution.title: human-brain-evolution
wiki/concepts/human-brain-evolution.content:
Human Brain Evolution
What makes the human brain distinct: expanded cortex, prolonged (“neotenic”)
development, and human-specific genetic innovations — human-accelerated regions
(HARs), human-specific gene duplications (NOTCH2NL, ARHGAP11B, SRGAP2C), and
gene-regulatory rewiring acting during neurodevelopment.
In this wiki

A framing of human brain evolution as slowed developmental tempo (“bradychrony”
vs “tachychrony”) acting together with human-specific genetics and cellular
innovations (lindhout-2024-a-molecular-and-cellular-perspective).
Outer radial glia, cortical-progenitor expansion, and HAR/duplication biology
studied via brain-organoids
(kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular,
zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain).

Sources

lindhout-2024-a-molecular-and-cellular-perspective · kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular

wiki/concepts/immune-recognition.title: immune-recognition
wiki/concepts/immune-recognition.content:
Immune Recognition
The molecular recognition of antigens by the adaptive immune system — antibody–
antigen binding (B cells) and TCR–peptide–MHC binding (T cells). Predicting
how mutations reshape these interfaces underlies vaccine/antibody design and the
study of viral escape.
In this wiki

The application domain of uniair, which unifies antibody and TCR
mutation-effect-prediction in one multimodal framework
(han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across).
Data scarcity and bias for TCR–pMHC structures are a notable
data-quality-and-bias challenge; the work’s immunogen-design relevance ties to
AI biosecurity themes (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).

Sources

han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across

wiki/concepts/interneuron-migration.title: interneuron-migration
wiki/concepts/interneuron-migration.content:
Interneuron Migration
The long-range tangential migration by which GABAergic inhibitory interneurons, born
in the ventral forebrain, travel to the cortex and integrate into circuits — a
developmental step whose disruption is implicated in
autism and epilepsy. Interneuron generation and migration are experimentally
separable stages, each with its own genetic dependencies.
In this wiki

A human forebrain assembloid (dorsal+ventral organoid fusion) plus a pooled
CRISPR loss-of-function screen mapped 425 NDD genes onto interneuron generation
vs migration, finding ~11% affect interneuron development; LNPK and ER dynamics
emerged as a migration driver (meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact,
forebrain-assembloid, pooled-crispr-screen).

Connections

A developmental process within neurodevelopment and neurogenesis; a disease
axis for autism, here dissected with crispr screening in
organoid models.

Sources

meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact

wiki/concepts/intron-retention.title: intron-retention
wiki/concepts/intron-retention.content:
Intron Retention
Intron retention (IR) is an alternative-splicing event type in which an
intron is left unspliced in the mature transcript. Once thought rare in mammals,
IR is now recognized as widespread and regulatory — it can trigger
detained-intron nuclear retention or
NMD, tuning gene output during development, stress, and
disease.
In this wiki

IR is a distinct event class that several methods explicitly model — e.g.
PSI-Sigma (lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method)
and MAJIQ’s de novo IR detection
(vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous) — because
event-based PSI callers built for cassette exons can miss
it.
Developmentally and tissue-regulated IR remodels proteomes and couples to
AS–NMD
(baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator).

Connections

One of the canonical event types alongside cassette exons, alternative
splice-sites, and mutually exclusive exons; closely tied to
cotranscriptional-splicing kinetics and post-transcriptional-splicing.

wiki/concepts/knowledge-graphs.title: knowledge-graphs
wiki/concepts/knowledge-graphs.content:
Knowledge Graphs
Structured graphs of gene/protein relationships (interactions, pathways,
co-functional annotations) used as biological inductive-bias priors for machine
learning — a way to inject prior knowledge so models generalize to unseen
conditions instead of memorizing training perturbations.
In this wiki

txpert fuses multiple complementary graphs — curated databases (STRING, GO)
plus high-throughput screen-derived maps (phenomics PxMap, transcriptomics TxMap)
— in a GNN to predict perturbation responses; combining sources is what drives its
performance (wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge, see
single-cell-perturbation-modeling).
Robust to substantial graph corruption (holds until >60% of edges removed), but
some of the most useful graphs are proprietary.

Sources

wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge

wiki/concepts/liability-threshold-model.title: liability-threshold-model
wiki/concepts/liability-threshold-model.content:
Liability-threshold model
The standard device for applying quantitative-genetics to binary traits.
A disease/affected status is modeled as the visible tip of an unobserved
continuous liability that is normally distributed in the population; an
individual is affected when their liability exceeds a threshold.
Why it matters here
The liability scale restores a continuous, normally distributed phenotype on which
the usual additive-genetic + environmental variance-components-model — and a
well-defined heritability — can be expressed, even though only a yes/no outcome
is observed. In practice this is realized through a
generalized-linear-mixed-model linking the binary observation to the latent
liability.

pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and adopts this liability/GLMM framing to
estimate genetic and environmental variance components for binary complex traits
from extended-family data (pre-eclampsia), separating maternal and foetal
contributions.

It is the binary-trait counterpart to the continuous-trait treatment in
rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and and
mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models.

wiki/concepts/linear-mixed-model.title: linear-mixed-model
wiki/concepts/linear-mixed-model.content:
Linear mixed model (LMM)
A regression model with both fixed effects (population-average covariates) and
random effects (group-specific deviations with estimated variances). The
random-effect variances are variance components — which is what makes the LMM
the natural vehicle for biometrical genetics on continuous traits.
The bridge to biometrical genetics
The central insight of the biometrical-genetics topic: a
variance-components-model for twin/family data can be re-expressed as an LMM in
which the additive-genetic (A), shared-environment (C), and unique-environment (E)
terms become random effects of family members nested within families. Once in this
form, the model is estimable with standard, widely available mixed-model
software instead of specialized SEM programs.

rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and proposes parameterizations
requiring few random effects so that the genetic covariance restrictions hold
without complicated constraints (illustrated on continuous birth weight in twins).
mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models groups this family of
approaches as MEMA (mixed-effects model algorithms), implemented in tools like
sas-proc-mixed, and contrasts it with the SEM/path-analysis family
(structural-equation-model).

For non-continuous (e.g. binary) phenotypes the LMM generalizes to the
generalized-linear-mixed-model.

wiki/concepts/long-read-rna-seq.title: long-read-rna-seq
wiki/concepts/long-read-rna-seq.content:
Long-read RNA-seq
Long-read RNA sequencing (Oxford Nanopore, PacBio) sequences full-length or
near-full-length transcripts, resolving exon connectivity that short-read
rna-seq can only infer from junction reads. It directly observes isoforms,
making it transformative for alternative-splicing — and, via Nanopore
direct RNA-seq, for reading native RNA and its modifications without cDNA
conversion.
Why it matters here

Isoform-resolution everywhere. Single-cell/spatial AS (longcell,
fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative); cis- vs trans-directed splicing
via phasing (isolaser, quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis);
activation-induced isoform switching in primary cells
(bodelon-2026-native-long-read-rna-sequencing).
Atlases & annotation. Cell-type isoform atlases power
sQTL discovery (isomiga,
humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas); diverse-cohort long-read data
exposes ancestry bias in reference gene annotation
(poder-annotation, clavell-revelles-2025-long-read-transcriptomics-of-a-diverse) —
a concrete data-quality-and-bias instance.
Multi-layer readouts. Direct RNA-seq captures modifications, poly(A) length,
and splicing on the same molecule
(kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rna-sequencing-of).

Connections

Complements short-read event callers (rmats, leafcutter) and the
long-read toolkit surveyed in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying;
enables allele-specific-splicing analysis through read-level haplotype phasing.

Sources

See the long-read papers above; foundational context in rna-seq.

wiki/concepts/massively-parallel-reporte.title: massively-parallel-reporter-assay
wiki/concepts/massively-parallel-reporte.content:
Massively Parallel Reporter Assay (MPRA)
A high-throughput, barcoded reporter screen that quantifies the expression
effect of thousands–to–hundreds-of-thousands of sequence variants in a single
pooled experiment. Each variant drives a barcoded reporter; sequencing the barcodes
at the RNA and DNA level gives a per-variant activity measurement — a direct,
empirical map of cis-regulatory function.
In this wiki

groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at runs a protoplast MPRA over

30,000 CRISPR-mimicking mutations in three sorghum photosynthesis-gene promoters,
resolving gene-specific cis-regulatory-grammar and compact activating edits
that beat transgenic enhancers.


Notably, direct MPRA measurement still outperforms a fine-tuned genomic
language model for activating variants — measurement vs prediction, echoing the
ΔPSI measurement theme in splicing-methods (which also
uses MPRAs to dissect splicing-regulatory-elements).

Sources

groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at

wiki/concepts/microcephaly.title: microcephaly
wiki/concepts/microcephaly.content:
Microcephaly
Reduced brain size, typically from impaired neural-progenitor proliferation or
neuronal differentiation during neurodevelopment — a readout that connects
proliferation/differentiation genes to human brain size and neurodevelopmental
disorders.
In this wiki

A genome-wide CRISPR knockout screen in differentiating mouse ESCs found hundreds of
genes essential (or growth-restricting) for neuronal differentiation, and validated
the framework by implicating the plasmalogen enzyme PEDS1 as a recessive NDD
gene; the study also reports a mosaic (region-specific), not uniform, pattern of
size change (amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes, peds1,
plasmalogen-biosynthesis).

Connections

A phenotype of disrupted neurogenesis / radial-glia biology within
brain-development-neurogenomics; dissected here via crispr knockout screening.

Sources

amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes

wiki/concepts/missing-heritability.title: missing-heritability
wiki/concepts/missing-heritability.content:
Missing heritability
The gap between the heritability of a complex trait estimated from family /
twin-and-family-study-design data and the much smaller fraction of variance
explained by the genetic variants discovered for it — classically, by a
genome-wide-association-study. For human height, family studies imply h² ≈
80% while early GWAS explained only ~5%; even in the best-studied traits, common
variants typically accounted for only ~10–30%.
Two framings of the gap
The missing-heritability debate is fundamentally about a ratio —
π = h²(explained by known variants) / h²(total) — and the two papers in this
wiki attack opposite ends of it:

The numerator is too small — the prevailing view, set out by
manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of: real but as-yet-undiscovered
variants (more common variants of smaller effect, rare variants of larger effect,
structural/copy-number variation, gene–environment interactions) remain to be
found. Its proposed strategies are larger GWAS, sequencing, and family-based
designs.
The denominator is too big — the critique of
zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability: standard estimators of total
heritability assume additivity, so genetic interactions (epistasis) inflate
them, creating “phantom heritability.” Part of the gap may therefore be an
artifact of overestimating the total, not missing variants.


A newer, complementary angle from psychiatric-genetics: some of the gap may be
context-specific regulatory variance. In stimulated iPSC neurons, chromatin
caQTLs/ASoC explained ~2.5× more schizophrenia
heritability than eQTLs — much of it only visible upon
neuronal-activation (liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron).

Connection to the rest of the topic
Both framings bear directly on the variance-components-model heritability
estimates that the other biometrical-genetics sources compute from pedigrees —
Manolio takes those family estimates as the (trustworthy) target the GWAS numerator
must reach; Zuk questions whether those very estimates are biased upward. See
quantitative-genetics for the additive-variance foundation both build on.

wiki/concepts/model-interpretability.title: model-interpretability
wiki/concepts/model-interpretability.content:
Model Interpretability
Explainable-AI (XAI) methods that assign importance/attribution of a model’s
output back to its inputs. Gradient-based attributions — integrated gradients,
input×gradient, DeepLIFT — are contrasted with perturbation-based methods (SHAP,
LIME). In genomics, interpretability turns black-box models into hypothesis
generators.
In this wiki

signature-tool applies these attributions to
single-cell-foundation-models to score gene importance, finding that
attribution magnitude need not track raw expression — it emphasizes regulatory
transcription factors and is robust to technical noise
(gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting).
Sequence models use a related idea: in-silico mutagenesis (ISM) attributes
predictions to nucleotides, as in crossdna and the splicing-code models
(spliceai).
Caveat: attributions inherit the biases of the underlying model and are
interpretive, not ground truth.

Sources

gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting

wiki/concepts/multimodal-learning.title: multimodal-learning
wiki/concepts/multimodal-learning.content:
Multimodal Learning
Jointly modeling complementary data modalities in a shared or fused
representation, so each compensates for the others’ gaps. Recurs across this wiki’s
AI work in two flavors.
In this wiki

Sequence + structure — uniair fuses a [[protein-language-models|protein
language model]] with geometric structure (and mixes frozen structure experts) for
mutation-effect-prediction
(han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across).
Physics + data, omics + imaging + physiology — dt-lung hybridizes
physics-based equations with ML over imaging, transcriptomics, metabolomics, and
high-frequency physiology to build digital-twins
(zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex).

Sources

han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across · zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex

wiki/concepts/mutation-effect-prediction.title: mutation-effect-prediction
wiki/concepts/mutation-effect-prediction.content:
Mutation-effect Prediction
Computational estimation of how sequence mutations change function — here,
binding free-energy changes (ΔΔG) at molecular interfaces. A core task for protein
engineering, antibody maturation, and understanding immune escape.
In this wiki

uniair predicts ΔΔG generalizably across antibody–antigen and TCR–pMHC
immune-recognition interfaces, reaching state of the art on SKEMPIv2 and
guiding zero-shot antibody/peptide optimization validated by free-energy
perturbation (han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across).
Related but distinct: cis-regulatory variant-effect prediction on DNA, where
direct measurement (massively-parallel-reporter-assay) still beats genomic
language models for activating mutations
(groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at).

Caveats

The ΔΔG→phenotype mapping is system- and context-dependent; models cover
protein–protein interfaces, not small molecules or nucleic acids.

Sources

han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across

wiki/concepts/neurodegeneration.title: neurodegeneration
wiki/concepts/neurodegeneration.content:
Neurodegeneration
Progressive loss of neuronal structure and function underlying diseases such as
Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, and ALS — a major target for the functional-genomics and
gene-editing approaches collected in this wiki, distinct from the developmental
neurodevelopment focus of most brain sources.
In this wiki

In vivo editing therapy. A non-viral Cas9-RNP nanocomplex edits Bace1 in adult
mouse neurons, lowering amyloid-β and rescuing cognition in two Alzheimer’s models
(park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing, genome-editing,
cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex).
Target discovery. CRISPRi/CRISPRa screens in human iPSC-derived neurons/glia are
argued to be the route from risk variants to neurodegenerative disease mechanisms
(kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for, crispri-crispra).

Connections

The adult-disease end of psychiatric-genetics / brain-development-neurogenomics;
a proving ground for crispr editing and screening in the brain.

Sources

park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing
kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for

wiki/concepts/neurodevelopment.title: neurodevelopment
wiki/concepts/neurodevelopment.content:
Neurodevelopment
Neurodevelopment is the staged construction of the brain: neural-tube
formation → neurogenesis → neuronal migration → differentiation into
neuronal-cell-types → circuit assembly (synaptogenesis, pruning, myelination),
extending into adolescent maturation. It is the backbone of the
brain-development-neurogenomics topic and the developmental window in which
neuropsychiatric risk acts.
In this wiki

Multi-level overviews — macroanatomy → cells → molecules, framed as
gene–environment interaction (stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain); the “normal”
circuit-development timeline as a baseline for illness
(tau-2010-normal-development-of-brain-circuits).
Human-specific features (cortical progenitor expansion, prolonged maturation) are
read out through organoids + single-cell genomics
(kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular,
zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain), and slowed developmental tempo is a
proposed engine of human-brain-evolution.

Connections

The cellular stage on which molecular programs (incl. developmentally timed
alternative-splicing, splicing-and-neurodevelopment) operate; its
dysregulation underlies autism and broader psychiatric-genetics.

Sources

stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain · tau-2010-normal-development-of-brain-circuits

wiki/concepts/neurogenesis.title: neurogenesis
wiki/concepts/neurogenesis.content:
Neurogenesis
Neurogenesis is the generation of neurons from neural stem/progenitor cells —
both embryonic (building the brain) and adult (ongoing in restricted niches). A
core step of neurodevelopment and the process that produces the diversity of
neuronal-cell-types.
In this wiki

Specification: proneural bHLH transcription factors (achaete-scute, atonal /
Neurogenin, NeuroD families) are necessary and sufficient for neuronal
commitment and also bias subtype identity via Notch lateral inhibition
(bertrand-2002-proneural-genes-and-the).
Adult neurogenesis: multipotent neural stem cells in the SGZ (dentate gyrus)
and SVZ→olfactory-bulb niches, governed by extrinsic niche signals + intrinsic
programs (duan-2008-development-of-neural-stem).
Situated in the broader developmental sequence
(stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain).

Sources

duan-2008-development-of-neural-stem · bertrand-2002-proneural-genes-and-the

wiki/concepts/neuronal-activation.title: neuronal-activation
wiki/concepts/neuronal-activation.content:
Neuronal Activation
Activity-dependent gene regulation — the rapid, ordered transcriptional and
epigenomic program neurons run when they fire. Membrane depolarization (in vivo:
experience, stress; in vitro: KCl) drives Ca²⁺ influx, inducing early response
genes (ERGs, e.g. FOS, NPAS4, peaking ~1 h) followed by late response
genes (LRGs, e.g. BDNF, VGF, peaking ~6 h). This activity-dependent layer
shapes synaptic plasticity and neurodevelopment — and is largely invisible in
resting post-mortem tissue, making it a key hidden biological context for
neuropsychiatric disease.
In this wiki

Population-scale model. snRNA+ATAC multiomics of iPSC-derived Glut/GABA
neurons from 100 donors, KCl-stimulated at 0/1/6 h, maps activation across three
neural subtypes (liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron). ERG clusters
are enriched for cholesterol biosynthesis and
NPD GWAS signal; LRGs for axon-guidance/synaptic processes and are more
cell-type-specific than ERGs.
Context-specific / dynamic genetics. Most disease-relevant genetic effects
are activation-dependent: eQTLs, caQTLs, and ASoC
variants appear or strengthen only on stimulation, and the stimulation-specific
ones carry the strongest GWAS enrichment — so activation “unmasks” risk variants
and genes that static brain QTL atlases miss.
Epigenome–transcriptome discordance. Chromatin accessibility and expression
are often out of phase — “epigenetic memory” (open chromatin persists after
expression falls) and “chromatin priming” (accessibility opens before expression
rises) (chromatin-accessibility).

Connections

The activity-dependent counterpart to the developmental programs in
neurodevelopment / neurogenesis; both are contexts in which
noncoding-variants act that resting tissue cannot reveal.
Supplies the “dynamic QTL” evidence behind psychiatric-genetics and the
missing-heritability debate, and the regulatory TFs feed
GRN inference and ctwas disease mapping.

Sources

liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron

wiki/concepts/neuronal-cell-types.title: neuronal-cell-types
wiki/concepts/neuronal-cell-types.content:
Neuronal Cell Types
The brain’s “parts list” — the catalog of functionally distinct neuron (and
glia) types. Defining and classifying them is foundational to
neurodevelopment, to mapping disease to specific cells
(psychiatric-genetics), and to interpreting bulk vs single-cell genomics.
In this wiki

The case for a cell-type census (~60 retinal, ~1,000 estimated cortical types)
(masland-2004-neuronal-cell-types); a hierarchical, molecular-precedence
classification framework borrowing from species taxonomy
(zeng-2017-neuronal-cell-type-classification-challenges).
A caveat for transcriptomic typing: V1 L2/3 projection neurons form one
transcriptomic cluster yet split into distinct types by projection target and
connectivity — expression alone can’t fully annotate cell types
(kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell).
How types are specified: proneural bHLH factors + Notch lateral inhibition
(bertrand-2002-proneural-genes-and-the, see neurogenesis).

Connections

The resolution that bulk RNA-seq lacks (the recurring cell-type-confounding
caveat across the splicing-qtl / brain-genetics literature) — motivating
single-cell atlases in functional-single-cell-genomics.

Sources

masland-2004-neuronal-cell-types · zeng-2017-neuronal-cell-type-classification-challenges

wiki/concepts/non-homologous-end-joining.title: non-homologous-end-joining
wiki/concepts/non-homologous-end-joining.content:
Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ)
The error-prone, template-independent double-strand-break repair pathway: broken
ends are ligated directly, often leaving small insertions/deletions (indels). NHEJ is
active in all cell-cycle phases and dominates over HDR in
non-dividing cells, so it is what most CRISPR knockout editing relies on — and what
programmed deletions exploit when two cuts are made.
In this wiki

One of the two break-repair outcomes framing CRISPR editing precision
(pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of).
scandel uses NHEJ repair of two simultaneous Cas9 cuts to excise programmed
kilobase deletions tiling a locus
(gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements).

Connections

The imprecise counterpart to homology-directed-repair; the default outcome that
makes knockout screens (pooled-crispr-screen) work (genome-editing, crispr).

Sources

pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of
gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements

wiki/concepts/noncoding-variants.title: noncoding-variants
wiki/concepts/noncoding-variants.content:
Noncoding Variants
Genetic variants outside protein-coding sequence — most of the genome, and the
bulk of common-disease and GWAS signal. They act largely by perturbing
cis-regulatory-elements (enhancers, promoters, splice sites) and the
cis-regulatory-grammar that controls where and how much a gene is
expressed. Interpreting them — which variant in a locus is causal, in which cell
type, with what effect size — is a central open problem that
ai-in-genomics sequence models and functional assays now attack.
In this wiki

Deep-learning prioritization. Per-context chromatin-accessibility sequence
models (ChromBPNet) score ~3 billion variant effects across 132 fetal/adult
contexts, and flare integrates evolutionary constraint to flag the most
deleterious noncoding variants
(marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding).
A common-vs-rare dichotomy. Common noncoding variants tend to be more
cell-type-specific; ultra-rare variants have larger, broader effects, with the
strongest purifying selection in fetal neurons
(marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding) — directly relevant to
heritability and the missing-heritability debate.
Direct measurement. Massively parallel reporter assays
(massively-parallel-reporter-assay) test thousands of regulatory variants
empirically — in developing human cortex
(deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory) and crop
genomes (groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at) — the ground
truth that sequence models (yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions)
are checked against.

Connections

The variant-effect counterpart, on the RNA side, is the splicing-code /
spliceai line of work (splicing-qtl); both predict how sequence changes
reshape regulation.
Cell-type assignment of noncoding risk runs through single-cell maps
(chromatin-accessibility, sc-linker,
jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types).

Sources

marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding · deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory

wiki/concepts/optimal-transport.title: optimal-transport
wiki/concepts/optimal-transport.content:
Optimal Transport
A mathematical framework (Monge/Kantorovich) for finding the cost-minimizing map
between two probability distributions. In single-cell biology it is a natural fit
because perturbation data is unpaired — a cell is destroyed when measured, so we
only observe a control population and a perturbed population, not matched pairs.
In this wiki

cmonge builds on the Monge Gap regularizer (which enforces optimality of a
directly parameterized map) and makes it conditional, learning one global map
across covariates rather than per-condition models — the basis of its
generalization in single-cell-perturbation-modeling
(driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable).
Computed with entropic/Sinkhorn divergences; contrasts with dual ICNN approaches
(CellOT) that can be unstable.

Sources

driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable

wiki/concepts/percent-spliced-in.title: percent-spliced-in
wiki/concepts/percent-spliced-in.content:
Percent Spliced In (PSI, ψ)
Percent spliced in — written ψ (psi) or PSI, range 0–1 (or 0–100%) —
is the standard quantitative metric for alternative-splicing: for a given
event it is the fraction of transcripts that include the alternative element
(e.g. a cassette exon) out of all transcripts that either include or skip it.
ΔPSI = |ψ₁ − ψ₂| is the change in that fraction between two conditions and is
the unit of differential splicing.
How it is estimated

From short-read RNA-seq, ψ is estimated from reads mapping to the
inclusion vs skipping isoforms — the inclusion (junction +
exon-body) reads I and the skipping-junction reads S. Naively
ψ̂ = I / (I + S), but read counts must be length-normalized by the
effective lengths of the two isoforms, because longer isoforms collect more
reads (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection).
The estimate’s reliability scales with sequencing coverage of the event:
low-count events have high estimation uncertainty, which is why replicate-aware
tools model per-event read depth explicitly
(shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection).

Why a magnitude threshold matters
Because nearly all events show some nonzero ψ difference, calling “differential”
splicing on ψ₁ ≠ ψ₂ (equality test) is biologically weak. rmats instead
tests |Δψ| > c for a user-chosen threshold c (commonly 5%), reporting only
changes of a meaningful magnitude
(shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection).
Where it appears in this wiki

The summary statistic behind the event-based callers (rMATS, MAJIQ, LeafCutter)
in splicing-methods and sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
The quantity that experimental assays (RT-PCR, MPRA) validate against — e.g.
RNA-seq vs RT-PCR ΔPSI correlated at r = 0.96 in
shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection.

Sources

shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection — formal ψ definition, length
normalization, ΔPSI thresholded testing
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying — PSI/ΔPSI as the event-based
RNA-seq readout, and tool disagreement on it

wiki/concepts/plasmalogen-biosynthesis.title: plasmalogen-biosynthesis
wiki/concepts/plasmalogen-biosynthesis.content:
Plasmalogen Biosynthesis
The synthesis of plasmalogens — ether phospholipids abundant in neuronal
membranes and myelin. The pathway (including the desaturase PEDS1/TMEM189) is
required for normal brain development, and its disruption causes neurodevelopmental
disease — one of several lipid-metabolism threads linking metabolism to brain
disorders in this wiki.
In this wiki

A CRISPR knockout screen for neuronal-differentiation genes nominated PEDS1 (the
plasmalogen-synthesizing desaturase) as a novel autosomal-recessive NDD gene
(amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes, peds1, microcephaly).

Connections

A lipid-metabolism axis of brain-development-neurogenomics, complementary to the
cholesterol/lipid thread in cholesterol-and-brain-disorders; here surfaced by
crispr screening.

Sources

amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes

wiki/concepts/poison-exons.title: poison-exons
wiki/concepts/poison-exons.content:
Poison Exons
A poison exon (PE) is an alternatively spliced exon
that introduces a premature termination codon, so its inclusion routes the
transcript to nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) — i.e. AS coupled to NMD
(“AS–NMD”). PEs let cells tune gene dosage post-transcriptionally and often
autoregulate splicing-factors; many are deeply conserved and developmentally /
brain-specifically regulated.
In this wiki

A genome-wide catalog finds 9,814 human poison exons, enriched for
brain-specific, developmentally dynamic splicing, with thousands of
PE-disrupting pathogenic variants — a “new paradigm” for neurological disease
(pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in, see splicing-and-neurodevelopment).
PEs are a druggable handle: ASOs and U1-snRNP small-molecule “glues”
(risdiplam/branaplam-style) skip or induce PEs to raise or lower a target’s
expression — a splicing-therapeutics strategy
(neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing).
Mechanistically a form of post-transcriptional
control; reviewed as part of developmental AS networks
(baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator,
kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated).

Synthesis

poison-exons-regulation-and-therapy — cross-cutting read of the whole PE
cluster: one mechanism seen as a regulatory knob, a disease driver, and a drug target.

Sources

neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing · pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in

wiki/concepts/pooled-crispr-screen.title: pooled-crispr-screen
wiki/concepts/pooled-crispr-screen.content:
Pooled CRISPR Screen
A massively parallel loss-/gain-of-function assay in which a library of guide
RNAs (or guide pairs) is delivered across a cell population so that each cell
carries one perturbation, the pool is subjected to a selection or readout, and the
relative abundance of each guide before vs after is used to infer that
perturbation’s phenotypic effect. The readout can be survival/fitness (count
guides under selection), bulk (e.g. drug resistance), or rich single-cell
transcriptomes (single-cell-crispr-screen). It is the screening counterpart to
the reporter-based massively-parallel-reporter-assay.
In this wiki

Foundational method. wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells established
genome-scale pooled CRISPR-Cas9 loss-of-function screening in human cells (73k-sgRNA
barcoded lentiviral library) with the first sgRNA-efficacy rules — the origin of the
screen family below.
scRNA-readout screens. dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits
(perturb-seq) and adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening
couple a pooled CRISPRi screen to droplet scRNA-seq, turning the whole
transcriptome into the phenotype — feeding single-cell-perturbation-modeling.
Tiling-deletion screens. gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements
(scandel) programs thousands of paired-gRNA kilobase deletions to scan a locus
for required cis-regulatory-elements under functional selection.
Precise-variant screens. sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel
(crispey) installs thousands of single-base natural variants
via genome-editing and reads each variant’s relative fitness, mapping
causal variation at single-base resolution.

Connections

Where MPRA tests episomal reporter sequences, pooled CRISPR screens act on the
endogenous genome — complementary evidence for regulatory function.
Single-cell readouts (single-cell-crispr-screen) are the high-content extension
of this idea; spatial variants appear in the functional-single-cell-genomics topic.

Sources

wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells
dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits
adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening
gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements
sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel

wiki/concepts/post-transcriptional-splic.title: post-transcriptional-splicing
wiki/concepts/post-transcriptional-splic.content:
Post-transcriptional Splicing
Post-transcriptional splicing is intron removal that completes after transcription and polyadenylation — typically while the polyadenylated transcript remains chromatin-associated — the complement to cotranscriptional-splicing choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. The spliceosome assembly steps are presumed identical to the co-transcriptional mode; only the timing differs choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Up to ~40% of mammalian introns are spliced post-transcriptionally (transcriptome-wide estimates of 27–45%), and >70% of expressed genes carry at least one post-transcriptionally retained intron choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Polyadenylated transcripts can linger on chromatin long after synthesis (median chromatin half-life ~50 min in K562, some genes >170 min), and splicing completion is a rate-limiting step for transcript release from chromatin, gating nuclear export and translation choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Retention, detained introns, and timing
Most retained introns are eventually excised productively rather than degraded; nuclear decay is the minority fate (~4% of protein-coding genes) choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Detained introns are a distinct regulated class — shorter, GC-rich, with weaker splice-sites — held until specific signals trigger removal, used in neurons, spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation and heat-shock responses choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Coupling to NMD provides another post-transcriptional control on protein output marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Timing is shaped by cis features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary structure) and trans splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins), with CLK/SRPK phosphorylation, PRMT methylation and m6A as modulators choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Introns flanking alternative exons are preferentially spliced post-transcriptionally (67% vs 30% for constitutive), plausibly buying time for correct splice-site choice choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. What triggers the eventual reaction — and whether the delay precedes or follows spliceosome engagement — remains unknown choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. See splicing-and-chromatin for the retention machinery and splicing-and-disease (PRMT5/glioblastoma, FUS/ALS) for dysregulation.
Sources

choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — primary: ~40% post-transcriptional; detained introns; release gating; cis/trans determinants; unknown trigger.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — NMD-coupled post-transcriptional control of protein output.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — identical assembly steps; post-transcriptional splicing of slow/long introns.

wiki/concepts/prime-editing.title: prime-editing
wiki/concepts/prime-editing.content:
Prime Editing
A “search-and-replace” genome-editing modality that writes new sequence directly
into the genome without a double-strand break and without a donor DNA template. A
nickase cas9 (H840A) is fused to a reverse transcriptase (RT) and programmed by
a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) whose 3′ extension encodes both the primer-binding
site and the desired edit. The nicked strand primes reverse transcription off the pegRNA,
producing a 3′ DNA flap that carries the edit; flap resolution (FEN1 excises the
competing 5′ flap) installs it.
Prime editing can make all 12 base-to-base substitutions plus small insertions (≤40 bp)
and deletions (≤80 bp) within ~30 bp of the nick — a broader edit repertoire than
base-editing.
In this wiki

Repair decides the outcome. nambiar-2022-crispr-based-genome-editing-through
shows prime editing is actively suppressed by mismatch repair, which recognizes the
heteroduplex and reverts the edit; hence PE4/PE5 co-express a dominant-negative
MLH1 (MLH1dn) to evade MMR, and PE3/PE3b add a second, product-specific nick to
bias repair toward the edited strand.
Toolbox context. villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome traces
the PE1→PE6 optimization arc (RT engineering for thermostability/processivity, pegRNA
stabilization such as epegRNAs) and its extension to large insertions: prime editing
installs recombinase attachment sites (attB/attP) so a serine integrase (Bxb1) can knock
in gene-sized cargo — the basis of TwinPE and PASTE (payloads up to ~36 kb).
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of groups it with base editors as DSB-free
precision editing.

Notable limitations

Efficiency is generally lower than base or nuclease editing; pegRNA design is complex
and empirical.
Can still generate on-target DSBs/indels; in vivo delivery of the large PE construct is
a barrier.

Connections

Complements base-editing (donor-free precise editing) and contrasts with
template-dependent homology-directed-repair and error-prone
non-homologous-end-joining.
Integrase-directed insertion (TwinPE/PASTE) is an alternative to
homology-directed-repair and CRISPR-associated transposases for programmable
large-payload knock-in.

Sources

nambiar-2022-crispr-based-genome-editing-through
villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of

wiki/concepts/protein-language-models.title: protein-language-models
wiki/concepts/protein-language-models.content:
Protein Language Models
Transformers pretrained on large protein-sequence corpora (e.g. the ESM family)
that learn evolutionary/structural features usable as frozen feature extractors
for downstream tasks. The protein-sequence counterpart of dna-language-models
within the foundation-models paradigm.
In this wiki

uniair fuses a protein language model (ESM2) with geometric structure in its
ESSM backbone to predict mutation-induced ΔΔG, outperforming sequence-only PLM
baselines (han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across, see
mutation-effect-prediction).
Predicted structures (ESMFold/OpenFold) can substitute for experimental ones via
latent adapters, extending PLM-based models to structure-scarce settings.

Sources

han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across

wiki/concepts/psychiatric-genetics.title: psychiatric-genetics
wiki/concepts/psychiatric-genetics.content:
Psychiatric Genetics
The genetics of neuropsychiatric disease (schizophrenia, autism, ADHD, bipolar,
etc.) — largely polygenic, pleiotropic, and acting in early
neurodevelopment. A central thread of the
brain-development-neurogenomics topic, and the disease end of the brain
RNA/QTL work in splicing.
In this wiki

Shared architecture: cross-disorder GWAS of 14 disorders finds five shared
genomic factors, 238 pleiotropic loci, and transdiagnostic enrichment in early
development and specific cell types
(grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across).
Convergence on cells & development: ASD risk converges on deep-layer
excitatory neurons and developmental programs
(gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across,
dear-2024-cortical-gene-expression-architecture-links); risk is mapped to
disease-critical cell types via single-cell + GWAS integration
(jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types,
bryois-2022-cell-type-specific-cis-eqtls-in-eight).
Mechanism via RNA: isoform-level TWAS recovers risk genes hidden at the gene
level (bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic,
see splicing-and-disease, splicing-qtl).
Hidden in context: most NPD-relevant genetic effects are activation-specific
— eQTLs, caQTLs and ASoC that appear only when
neurons fire, with caQTLs explaining ~2.5× more SCZ heritability than eQTLs and
ctwas prioritizing risk genes (incl. cholesterol-metabolism genes) the resting
brain misses (liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron,
neuronal-activation, cholesterol-and-brain-disorders).

Connections

Built on developmental-brain QTL atlases (walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and,
wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform,
li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis); autism is the most
heavily represented disorder here.

Sources

grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across · gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across · liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron

wiki/concepts/quantitative-genetics.title: quantitative-genetics
wiki/concepts/quantitative-genetics.content:
Quantitative (biometrical) genetics
The study of continuously varying, polygenic traits by partitioning their
variance into genetic and environmental sources, rather than tracking individual
Mendelian genes. It is the parent field of the biometrical-genetics topic.
Lineage

R. A. Fisher (1918) — reconciled Mendelian inheritance with continuous
variation and introduced the variance decomposition underlying the
variance-components-model.
Sewall Wright (1918, 1921) — path analysis (the PAM root of the
structural-equation-model approach).
Later syntheses (Cattell’s MAVA, Jinks & Fulker 1970, Loehlin) showed these
representations are interchangeable — formalized as the PAM↔VCM equivalence in
mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models.

Modern thread in this wiki
The three sources share a methodological move: take classic biometrical-genetic
models off specialized software and onto standard (generalized) linear mixed
models (linear-mixed-model, generalized-linear-mixed-model), so that
heritability can be estimated for continuous and binary traits from
twin-and-family-study-design data using mainstream statistical tools
(rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and,
mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models,
pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and).

wiki/concepts/radial-glia.title: radial-glia
wiki/concepts/radial-glia.content:
Radial Glia
The neural stem/progenitor cells of the developing cortex — the apical
progenitors that both self-renew and give rise to neurons and glia, scaffolding
neuronal migration along their processes. Their proliferation-vs-differentiation
balance sets the size and composition of the cortex, making the radial-glial
lineage a focal point of neurogenesis and a recurrent convergence point for
neurodevelopmental disease.
In this wiki

A convergent ASD target. Across 11 genetically diverse monogenic
autism mouse models, mutations converge on a transient delay in the
radial-glial lineage — mutant RG cells linger in the proliferating (RG1,
G1/S) state rather than progressing — a developmental-timing perturbation that
resolves by postnatal stages rather than a lasting misspecification
(schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism).
Chromatin-first signal. In that study the earliest and largest
differentially accessible regions (chromatin-accessibility) are in RG cells
at E14.5 (enriched for WNT/BMP and progenitor cell-cycle TF motifs), exceeding
the transcriptional changes
(schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism).

Connections

The progenitor stage that single-cell developmental atlases
(velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal) and
brain-organoids (kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular)
resolve, and where human-specific expansion of neurodevelopment is thought
to act (human-brain-evolution).
Links developmental cell biology to psychiatric-genetics: disease risk acts
on a specific cell type at a specific stage.

Sources

schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism

wiki/concepts/rna-binding-proteins.title: rna-binding-proteins
wiki/concepts/rna-binding-proteins.content:
RNA-Binding Proteins
RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) are the broad class of proteins that read motifs in pre-mRNA and, among other functions, regulate splicing. In splicing they bind splicing-regulatory-elements to control how the spliceosome selects splice-sites and produces alternative-splicing rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. The splicing-dedicated RBPs — SR proteins, hnRNPs, RBM and CELF families, plus tissue-specific factors like Nova, PTB and Rbfox — are exactly the proteins covered as splicing-factors; this page treats RBPs more broadly, while splicing-factors focuses on the SR-activator vs hnRNP-repressor regulatory logic.
RBPs read short, often degenerate motifs, and because higher-eukaryote splice-sites resemble non-splice-site sequences, recognition depends on RBP/snRNP base pairing plus sequence context rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Their action is governed by the position-dependent splicing-code, with the Nova RNA map as a foundational example of motif-position mapping lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. RBP binding also intersects with RNA modifications: m6A readers (YTHDC1 recruiting SRSF3, hnRNPG/RBMX) modulate exon inclusion rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
RBPs are central to splicing-and-disease: hnRNPA1, TDP-43 and FUS carry prion-like domains implicated in ALS lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, and many tissue-specific RBPs (e.g. RBM20/titin in cardiomyopathy) drive homeostasis and disease marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. The same disease-relevant factors recur as splicing-factor mutations in cancer rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — RBPs as position-dependent regulators (Nova map); hnRNPA1/TDP-43/FUS prion-like domains in ALS
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — RBP families reading context-dependent motifs; m6A readers; disease/cancer mutations
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — tissue-specific RBPs (RBM20/titin) in physiology and disease

wiki/concepts/rna-editing.title: rna-editing
wiki/concepts/rna-editing.content:
RNA Editing
RNA editing — predominantly A-to-I editing by ADAR enzymes acting on
double-stranded RNA — is a co/post-transcriptional RNA-processing layer sibling
to splicing that recodes transcripts, alters miRNA/target sites, and can change
splice signals. The wiki includes it as a neighbouring layer that interacts with
alternative-splicing and the broader RNA-modification (“epitranscriptome”)
landscape.
In this wiki

A developmental atlas of A-to-I editing across the human cortex (fetus → old
age) defines distinct editing-rate trajectories; a brain-selective “increasing”
pattern tracks neuronal maturation and ADAR expression and is disrupted in
disease (hwang-2016-dynamic-regulation-of-rna-editing,
splicing-and-neurodevelopment).
Editing/modification crosstalk with splicing appears in disease: aberrant
splicing in Huntington’s disease co-occurs with altered m6A modification and
TDP-43 dysfunction (nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease).

Connections

Conceptually parallel to splicing and to the modification readouts in
direct RNA-seq (which couples isoform structure to m6A);
part of the same multi-layer RNA-regulation picture as
post-transcriptional-splicing.

Sources

hwang-2016-dynamic-regulation-of-rna-editing

wiki/concepts/rna-seq.title: rna-seq
wiki/concepts/rna-seq.content:
RNA-seq
RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) is high-throughput sequencing of cDNA to measure the
transcriptome — the measurement substrate underneath nearly all of this wiki’s
splicing-methods. Splice-junction reads make it possible to detect and
quantify alternative-splicing genome-wide.
Lineage in this wiki

Founding work introduced the core metrics and showed AS is pervasive:
RPKM + ERANGE (mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian),
PSI across human tissues
(wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human,
pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing), and the framing review
(wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolutionary-tool).
Maturity brought best-practice pipelines
(conesa-2016-survey-of-best-practices) and long-read / single-cell / spatial
extensions (stark-2019-rna-sequencing-the-teenage-years).

Splicing-relevant readouts

Isoform-based quantification (Cufflinks, kallisto,
SUPPA) vs event-based PSI/ΔPSI (rmats, MISO,
LeafCutter, DEXSeq) — see splicing-methods.

Connections

Underpins splicing-code training data and splicing-qtl mapping;
long-read RNA-seq is the route to full-length isoform connectivity.

wiki/concepts/rna-structure-and-splicing.title: rna-structure-and-splicing
wiki/concepts/rna-structure-and-splicing.content:
RNA Structure and Splicing
Beyond linear sequence motifs, the secondary and higher-order structure of
pre-mRNA helps determine splicing outcomes. Where splice-sites and
splicing-regulatory-elements describe splicing control as a sequence code,
this page collects the structural layer — folded elements that mask or expose
splice sites and tune where splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins
bind. Structure is an explicit part of what the splicing-code must capture
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
RNA–RNA base pairing
Local and long-range base pairing can dictate splice-site choice. The classic
example is Drosophila dscam, where conserved “docking” and “selector”
sequences pair to select one of 48 mutually exclusive exons; in vivo chemical
probing shows the transcriptome can be highly structured
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. RNA secondary structure is
also one of the cis determinants that shape splicing timing
(post-transcriptional-splicing) alongside GC content, intron length and
splice-site strength choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
G-quadruplexes (rG4s)
A prominent structural regulator is the g-quadruplex — a noncanonical
four-stranded fold in guanine-rich RNA. rG4s near 5′/3′ splice sites act
position-dependently as enhancers or silencers across all alternative-splicing
event types, by recruiting or blocking factors and by masking/exposing splice
sites; ~60% of transcripts contain at least one putative quadruplex sequence and
splice junctions are ~3-fold enriched for G4s on the coding strand
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes. G4-reader proteins include the
hnRNP family, FMRP and SRSF1; DNA G4s can also act indirectly via Pol II
elongation and R-loops (cotranscriptional-splicing)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Disease and therapeutic angle
Structural mis-regulation is tied to cancer and neurological disease (e.g. TP53,
BCL-x, hTERT; FMR1/Fragile X), and G4-stabilising ligands are an emerging way to
modulate splicing — a structural complement to sequence-targeted ASOs
(splicing-and-disease, splicing-therapeutics)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Sources

peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — primary: rG4s as structural AS regulators, readers, disease, ligands
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — RNA–RNA base pairing (DSCAM); structured transcriptome
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — structure as part of the splicing code
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — RNA structure as a cis determinant of splicing timing

wiki/concepts/rna-structure-prediction.title: rna-structure-prediction
wiki/concepts/rna-structure-prediction.content:
RNA Structure Prediction
Computational prediction of RNA 3D (tertiary) structure — and increasingly
conformational ensembles — from sequence. Harder than protein folding because
RNA is highly flexible and experimental 3D structures are scarce (orders of
magnitude fewer than proteins in the PDB).
In this wiki

trrosettarna2 tackles the data-scarcity problem by pre-training on abundant
secondary-structure annotations (bpRNA) and injecting that base-pairing prior
into 3D attention, reaching AlphaFold-3-competitive accuracy at ~1/12 the
parameters and topping CASP16’s automated servers
(wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure).
Varying the secondary-structure input lets the model sample distinct conformers
without experimental data — a step beyond single-structure prediction.

Connections

A sibling of the sequence-prior idea in genomics models like spliceai within
ai-in-genomics; complements the splicing topic’s rna-structure-and-splicing
(which concerns RNA structure’s regulatory role rather than its prediction).

Sources

wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure

wiki/concepts/single-cell-crispr-screen.title: single-cell-crispr-screen
wiki/concepts/single-cell-crispr-screen.content:
Single-cell CRISPR Screen
A pooled-crispr-screen whose readout is a single-cell transcriptome rather
than a survival count. Each cell’s perturbation is identified by an expressed,
sequenceable guide barcode (GBC), and its phenotype is the full scRNA-seq
profile — so one experiment yields, per perturbation, the affected genes, gene
programs, cell states, and even genetic interactions. The founding instance is
perturb-seq.
In this wiki

dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits introduced Perturb-seq and
the MIMOSCA regularized-regression model that deconvolves overlapping
perturbation effects — making the transcriptome a scalable, high-content phenotype.
adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening developed the GBC
multiplexing strategy in parallel and applied it to dissect the three-arm mammalian
unfolded protein response.

Connections

Directly upstream of single-cell-perturbation-modeling (models that predict
perturbation responses are trained and tested on this data) and of
model-interpretability efforts that read perturbation effects off learned
representations.
Spatial and in-situ extensions of the idea appear in
zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics within
functional-single-cell-genomics.

Sources

dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits
adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening

wiki/concepts/single-cell-foundation-mod.title: single-cell-foundation-models
wiki/concepts/single-cell-foundation-mod.content:
Single-cell Foundation Models
Pretrained models (scGPT, SCimilarity, scVI, scTab, scFoundation) that embed
