U2AF1, SRSF2 and sf3b1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — 3’ss recognition, S34/Q157 mutant effects, m6A
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — U2AF35 recurrent mutations in MDS/CLL
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — convergence of myeloid splicing-factor mutations

wiki/entities/uniair.title: uniair
wiki/entities/uniair.content:
UniAIR
UniAIR is a modular, multimodal sequence–structure framework for
generalizable mutation-effect-prediction (binding ΔΔG) across adaptive
immune-recognition (antibody–antigen and TCR–pMHC), introduced in
han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across.
What it is

ESSM fusion backbone — an S-Former co-attention transformer coupling a
protein language model (ESM2) with geometric
structure.
MoFPE — a mixture of four frozen structure-pretrained experts combined via a
structure-aware gating network (multimodal-learning).
UniAIR-LT — a latent adapter aligning predicted-structure embeddings to
experimental ones, for structure-free use.

Standing

State of the art on SKEMPIv2 (PCC 0.684); generalizes to held-out antibody and
TCR test sets; used zero-shot for antibody/peptide optimization validated by FEP.
Limitation: protein–protein interfaces only (no small molecules/nucleic acids).

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence / ai-in-genomics method; safety-adjacent
(viral escape, immunogen design) — cf.
hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation.

Sources

han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across — the method paper

wiki/entities/washu-comparative-epigenom.title: washu-comparative-epigenome-browser
wiki/entities/washu-comparative-epigenom.content:
WashU Comparative Epigenome Browser (Brain M1 data hub)
Introduced in zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory. A public genome-browser data hub for viewing cross-species primary motor cortex (M1) epigenome tracks, hosted on the WashU Epigenome Browser (epigenome.wustl.edu/BrainComparativeEpigenome/).
What it is

Displays cell-type-resolved multiomic tracks (Hi-C, RNA, ATAC and CG methylation) for human, macaque, marmoset and mouse M1, with cross-species genome alignments (e.g. hg38 vs rheMac10).
Backs the study’s comparative analysis of candidate cis-regulatory elements (cis-regulatory-elements), differentially methylated regions and TAD boundaries across 21 shared brain cell types.

In this wiki

The public browsing resource accompanying zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory; a human-brain-evolution / chromatin-accessibility data hub.

Sources

zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory

wiki/entities/won-developing-brain-hic-d.title: won-developing-brain-hic-dataset
wiki/entities/won-developing-brain-hic-d.content:
Won developing-brain Hi-C dataset
High-resolution Hi-C chromatin-contact maps of mid-gestation human cerebral cortex, profiled separately for the cortical/subcortical plate (CP, post-mitotic neurons) and germinal zone (GZ, neural progenitors). Deposited in GEO/dbGaP. Widely reused as a reference for assigning noncoding regulatory variants and enhancers to distal target genes during human corticogenesis, and for tissue-specific interpretation of psychiatric GWAS loci.

wiki/entities/zscape.title: zscape
wiki/entities/zscape.content:
ZSCAPE (zebrafish single-cell atlas of perturbed embryos)
Introduced in saunders-2023-embryo-scale-reverse-genetics. ZSCAPE is an individual-resolved, whole-organism single-cell transcriptomic atlas of zebrafish development paired with a systematic F0 CRISPR perturbation dataset, designed to phenotype gene function as organism-wide changes in cell-type abundance.
What it is

Two components. (1) An annotated wild-type reference atlas: ~1,167 individuals, ~1.2M cells, 19 timepoints (18–96 hpf), hierarchically labeled into 33 tissues / 99 broad cell types / 156 subtypes. (2) A perturbation dataset: 23 genetic perturbations (crispants), ~645 perturbed individuals / ~2M cells. Combined ~1,812 embryos and ~3.2M cells.
Method stack. sci-RNA-seq3 combinatorial-indexing scRNA-seq + sci-Plex oligonucleotide hashing (recovers embryo-of-origin for ~70% of QC-passing nuclei) + F0 CRISPR–Cas9 RNP injection at the one-cell stage.
Analysis. Mutant cells are projected onto the reference and annotated by approximate nearest neighbor; cell-type-abundance variance is modeled with a gamma-response GLM; differentially abundant cell types (DACTs) are called with beta-binomial regression.
Purpose. Reusable resource for mapping genetic dependencies of zebrafish cell types—including rare populations (<1% of the embryo) such as cranial sensory ganglia—via replicated (≥8 embryos/condition) individual-embryo phenotyping.

In this wiki

A whole-organism instance of single-cell-perturbation-modeling and an arrayed cousin of the pooled single-cell-crispr-screen paradigm (dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening).
A functional-single-cell-genomics atlas with deep coverage of neurodevelopment and diverse neuronal-cell-types.

Sources

saunders-2023-embryo-scale-reverse-genetics

wiki/overview.title: overview
wiki/overview.content:
Overview
The evolving top-level thesis of this wiki. It now spans eight topics:

splicing — pre-mRNA / alternative splicing (83 sources, 1994–2026)
artificial-intelligence — AI/ML in biology & genomics: foundation models,
sequence/structure, single-cell & perturbation methods + safety (17 sources, 2023–2026)
3d-genome — 3D organization of the genome, its sequence-based prediction, and its
evolution (7 sources, 2016–2026)
gene-regulation — cis-regulatory genomics: enhancers, promoters, TF-binding
grammar, enhancer–promoter logic, regulatory variation & reference maps
(15 sources, 2009–2024)
functional-single-cell-genomics — experimental/resource genomics: single-cell
omics, MPRA, digital twins, spatial methods, perturbation & regulatory-variant
function (19 sources, 2016–2026)
crispr — CRISPR-Cas biology, genome editing, functional screens & gene therapy;
mechanism → tool → screen → therapy → safety (16 sources, 2014–2026)
brain-development-neurogenomics — brain development biology, neuronal cell types,
brain evolution & neuropsychiatric disease genetics, incl. a deep autism/NDD
genetic-architecture strand (48 sources, 2002–2026)
biometrical-genetics — heritability estimation (decomposing twin/family trait
variance into genetic & environmental components via (generalized) linear mixed
models) + the missing-heritability debate + multiancestry polygenic prediction
(6 sources, 2004–2023)

Grounded strictly in the locally ingested sources. See index for the full
per-topic catalog.

Cross-topic threads: (1) deep-learning models of biological sequence connect
splicing and AI — the splicing-code / spliceai work is one instance of
the foundation-models discussed under artificial-intelligence;
(2) chromatin links splicing and the 3D genome — splicing-and-chromatin
meets genome-architecture.

Current thesis — topic: splicing
RNA splicing is a central layer of gene regulation, and across these sources
the field’s center of gravity has shifted from how splicing works to how it
is controlled and read:

The machinery is essentially solved. The spliceosome is a large,
dynamic, RNA-catalysed machine (a ribozyme related to group II introns), and
cryo-EM has resolved most of its states
(lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative,
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). Remaining questions are
about regulation, not chemistry.
Outcomes are set by a position-dependent code. Weak metazoan
splice-sites + splicing-regulatory-elements read by splicing-factors
determine isoform choice. Modeling this splicing-code has progressed from
position weight matrices to deep-learning models that now interpret ~10% of
pathogenic rare-disease variants
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).
Timing is itself regulatory. Splicing is largely co-transcriptional, but
a large fraction (~40%) is post-transcriptional-splicing, gating mRNA
export and translation (choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Function vs noise is unresolved. alternative-splicing occurs in
~95–100% of human multiexon genes, but how much is functional is debated
(see Open questions).
Mechanism is becoming medicine. Splicing-factor mutations drive cancer and
genetic disease (splicing-and-disease); the first splicing-targeted drugs
are approved (splicing-therapeutics).
A mature measurement-and-genetics stack underpins all of it. A decade-plus of
rna-seq tooling now quantifies splicing two ways — isoform-based
(cufflinks, kallisto, suppa) and event-based (rmats/rmats-turbo,
miso, leafcutter, majiq-v2, dexseq) — and population genetics maps
sQTLs linking variation to disease
(park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative). Tools disagree, so the
open methodological question is which readout to trust when.

Open questions

Functionality of AS — ✅ Mostly reconciled (see
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate): the apparent
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative (functional) vs
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source (noise) clash is largely a
difference in the definition of “functional”, the denominator counted, and the
evidence weighted — not a factual contradiction. Both agree on a conserved
functional core; residual uncertainty is the size of the low-abundance tail,
resolvable only with long-read/single-cell/CRISPR data.
Co- vs post-transcriptional timing trigger — mechanism unknown
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Interpretable, tissue-specific splicing-code models — still open
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).

Current thesis — topic: artificial-intelligence
Across ten sources, foundation-models have become general-purpose
biological tools spanning every modality: DNA (crossdna), protein
(uniair), RNA structure (trrosettarna2), single cells (the FMs
signature-tool interprets), and single-cell dynamics (regvelo couples
RNA velocity with a learnable gene regulatory network). Two patterns recur: (1) injecting domain priors
beats blind scale — secondary-structure priors, explicit cross-strand symmetry,
gene knowledge-graphs, and optimal-transport let small models beat ones
orders of magnitude larger; (2) generalization to the unseen (drugs, cell
lines, mutations, conformers) is the shared frontier, limited by
data-quality-and-bias, and often checked by direct measurement
(massively-parallel-reporter-assay, ΔPSI) where models
are weak. Safety remains the backdrop: generative capability keeps
dual-use-risk immediate and no single ai-safety safeguard suffices,
motivating layered measures plus ai-governance
(hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). The newest entry
pushes deep learning into population genetics: flare scores billions of
noncoding-variant effects from chromatin-accessibility
models and folds in evolutionary constraint, linking the AI methods to
functional-single-cell-genomics atlases and the
biometrical-genetics missing-heritability debate
(marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding). A second statistical-genetics
entry pushes expression imputation beyond cis: co-expression-network models
(ingene-module) inject distal trans signal (gene-coexpression-network) into
TWAS, expanding the imputable transcriptome and
surfacing hundreds of new schizophrenia genes in human brain
(rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl).
Current thesis — topic: 3d-genome
One source so far (che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome): nuclear
genome-architecture (loops → TADs → compartments) is an evolvable trait.
It evolves fastest in cancer (3d-genome-and-disease), diverges between close
species mainly via cis-regulatory-elements within conserved frameworks, and
recurs across kingdoms through convergent evolution with distinct machinery
(3d-genome-evolution). Thin until more sources are ingested.
Current thesis — topic: functional-single-cell-genomics
These sources frame the measurement / resource counterpart to the AI methods:
a single-cell proteomic atlas of the developing human brain
(wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of, single-cell-proteomics), an
MPRA mapping >30,000 cis-regulatory mutations in sorghum
(groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at,
massively-parallel-reporter-assay), and digital twins of ex vivo human lungs
(zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex, digital-twins). The unifying thesis:
direct, high-throughput measurement still beats prediction where models are
weakest (mRNA→protein inference, activating regulatory variants), and these
resources are exactly the atlases, variant maps, and platforms the
artificial-intelligence topic’s models learn from and are tested against.
The endogenous-perturbation foundations that grew up here — the
pooled-crispr-screen family — have now been promoted to their own crispr topic.
What remains in this topic is the reporter-assay / atlas measurement stack, including a
human-neural massively-parallel-reporter-assay of eight psychiatric disorders
(lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric) that turns regulatory
correlation into causation and validates the variants the
artificial-intelligence models nominate.
Current thesis — topic: crispr
Twelve sources trace CRISPR-Cas across a single arc — mechanism → tool → screen →
therapy → safety. It began as the only known prokaryotic adaptive immune system
(hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance,
mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic), was repurposed into a
democratized genome-editing toolkit
(barrangou-2016-applications-of-crispr-technologies-in,
wang-2023-crispr-technology-a-decade-of, pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of),
and scaled into the pooled-crispr-screen family — genome-wide screens
(wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells), transcriptome-readout
single-cell screens (perturb-seq), tiling deletions
(scandel), and precise-variant editing (crispey). Its non-cutting branch
(crispri-crispra) drives dosage screens and therapy. The frontier is the clinic:
in vivo neuronal editing (park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing) and CRISPRa
dosage rescue (tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related) push toward
treatment, where measuring and bounding off-target risk becomes decisive
(stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of). Deep dive:
crispr-screens-and-genome-editing.
Current thesis — topic: brain-development-neurogenomics
Twenty-five sources frame the biology + disease context for the brain-RNA work: how
the human brain is built (neurodevelopment, neurogenesis,
neuronal-cell-types, brain-organoids), what makes it human
(human-brain-evolution), and how its genetics goes wrong — neuropsychiatric risk
is polygenic, pleiotropic, and acts early in development, converging on specific
cells and heavily on autism (grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across,
gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across, psychiatric-genetics).
A recurring lesson — transcriptome ≠ cell identity
(kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell) — is exactly the resolution bulk
genomics lacks, so this topic explains which cells and stages the developing-brain
splicing-qtl/isoform atlases and single-cell methods are mapping, and why
cell-type + developmental resolution is the open frontier. The newest ASD pair
sharpens this: 11 mouse models converge on a transient radial-glial
delay that fades with maturation
(schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism), while the human ASD
cortical methylome shows only modest cell-type-specific change,
uncorrelated with expression and dwarfed by aging
(eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation). The newest entry adds the
missing context itself: population-scale neuronal-activation (KCl-stimulated
iPSC Glut/GABA neurons, 100 donors) shows most NPD genetic effects are
activation-specific — stimulation-driven eQTLs, caQTLs and
ASoC (caQTLs explaining ~2.5× more SCZ heritability
than eQTLs) feed ctwas to prioritize risk genes the resting brain misses, and
implicate an activation-regulated cholesterol axis
(liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron). CRISPR now enters this
topic as a tool: pooled screens in forebrain assembloids and
differentiating neurons map disease genes onto interneuron-migration and
differentiation (meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact,
amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes), while CRISPRa dosage rescue and
in vivo editing move toward therapy for neurodegeneration and NDDs
(tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related,
park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing) — see the crispr topic. The
experimental toolkit for dissecting NDD complexity is reviewed in
sun-2024-strategies-for-dissecting-the-complexity.
Key concepts
Splicing — spliceosome · alternative-splicing · splicing-code ·
post-transcriptional-splicing · splicing-and-disease · splicing-therapeutics.
AI — foundation-models · dna-language-models · single-cell-perturbation-modeling ·
mutation-effect-prediction · model-interpretability · transcriptome-wide-association-study ·
dual-use-risk · ai-safety.
3D genome — genome-architecture · topologically-associating-domains ·
chromatin-loops · 3d-genome-evolution.
Functional & single-cell genomics — single-cell-proteomics ·
massively-parallel-reporter-assay · cis-regulatory-grammar · digital-twins.
CRISPR — genome-editing · pooled-crispr-screen · single-cell-crispr-screen ·
crispri-crispra · homology-directed-repair · non-homologous-end-joining.
See index for the full catalog.

wiki/sources/adamson-2016-multiplexed-si.title: adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening
wiki/sources/adamson-2016-multiplexed-si.content:
A Multiplexed Single-Cell CRISPR Screening Platform Enables Systematic Dissection of the Unfolded Protein Response
One-line: Introduces Perturb-seq — a platform that couples droplet single-cell RNA-seq with expressed guide-barcode (GBC) tagging of CRISPRi perturbations so pooled perturbed cells can be screened with full transcriptomic readout — and applies it to dissect the three-arm mammalian unfolded protein response (UPR).
Key takeaways

Defines the perturb-seq platform: a lentiviral “Perturb-seq vector” carries a Pol II-driven guide-barcode (GBC) cassette plus a Pol III sgRNA cassette, so each cell’s CRISPR perturbation identity is encoded in an expressed, polyadenylated transcript captured alongside the transcriptome in droplet scRNA-seq — an instance of single-cell-perturbation-modeling.
A three-guide vector with three homology-minimized sgRNA constant regions (cr1/cr2/cr3) and three U6 promoters delivers up to three simultaneous CRISPRi perturbations per cell with near-uniform >90% knockdown, enabling combinatorial epistasis experiments.
Companion analytical pipeline (low-rank ICA, “LRICA”) decomposes noisy high-dimensional single-cell data into interpretable components, computationally decoupling perturbation responses from confounders like the cell cycle — a worked example of model-interpretability for scRNA-seq.
Combinatorial CRISPRi of IRE1a/PERK/ATF6 plus chemical UPR inducers delineates and decouples the three transcriptional arms of the UPR at both bulk and single-cell levels.
Two genome-scale CRISPRi screens against an IRE1a (UPRE) reporter identify hundreds of genes whose repression disrupts ER homeostasis, and Perturb-seq of ~100 hits yields high-precision functional clustering of genes.
Single-cell resolution reveals bifurcated UPR-branch activation within cells under the same perturbation and uncovers a dedicated feedback loop in which the IRE1a/XBP1 branch selectively monitors translocon (SEC61) availability.

Notable claims / data

GBC calling: in a pilot of ~6,000 K562 cells carrying 8 distinct GBCs, a single dominant GBC was uniquely inferred for 92.2% of cells (median 45 independent UMI observations per cell); ~6.2% multiplets, ~1.6% unidentifiable. High-confidence calling lets droplets with >1 cell be discarded.
sgRNA from the Perturb-seq vector gave 95.4%–96.2% reduction of integrated GFP; final three-guide vector gave 96%–97% depletion from any of the three cassettes.
UPR epistasis experiment: sgRNAs targeting the three UPR sensors in all single/double/triple combinations, transcriptomes of ~15,000 cells, >80% depletion of targeted genes across conditions; inducers thapsigargin (ER Ca2+ pump inhibitor) and tunicamycin (N-glycosylation inhibitor), DMSO control.
LRICA decomposes expression matrix X = L + S (low-rank L = population dynamics, sparse S = cell-variable noise/effects); ICA on L yields components whose bimodality defines subpopulations. Identified a PERK-dependent thapsigargin-induced G1 cell-cycle arrest interacting with UPR activation (e.g. MYC).
Regulon structure: PERK/ATF4 had the largest, most-uniquely-controlled regulon; ATF6 and IRE1a overlapped more, with IRE1a uniquely controlling translocon/translocon-auxiliary components.
Reporter screens: CRISPRi-v1 (15,977 genes / 20,899 TSSs, 10 sgRNA/TSS) and CRISPRi-v2 (18,905 genes / 20,526 TSSs, 5 sgRNA/TSS). CRISPRi-v2 found 397 high-mCherry/GFP hits; 103/141 CRISPRi-v1 hits reproduced (Fisher p = 8.97e-138); reporter signal weakly correlated with growth (Spearman R = 0.2). All three translocon genes (SEC61A1/B/G) were among the 7 strongest hits.
Follow-up Perturb-seq sub-library: 91 sgRNAs targeting 82 genes; translocon-subunit depletion selectively induced XBP1 splicing / IRE1a signaling, supporting a homeostatic feedback model where IRE1a monitors translocation state.

Connections

Companion paper to dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, the other founding Perturb-seq study published the same period; both define the perturb-seq entity.
Methodologically upstream of later single-cell functional-genomics work in the wiki such as zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics and chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory.
A canonical case of single-cell-perturbation-modeling: pooled perturbations read out as full transcriptomes rather than a bulk reporter. The platform is a pooled/single-cell CRISPR screen (no dedicated wiki concept page yet).

Open questions

How far does GBC multiplexing scale in perturbation order and library size before recombination or dCas9-KRAB titration limits uniform multi-gene knockdown?
Which “non-canonical” hits (mitochondrial, general transcription/translation factors) act through genuine ER-homeostasis disruption versus reporter-system artifacts?
How general is the bifurcated, cell-to-cell heterogeneous branch activation across other stress pathways and cell types beyond K562?

wiki/sources/alamancos-2015-leveraging-t.title: alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast
wiki/sources/alamancos-2015-leveraging-t.content:
Leveraging transcript quantification for fast computation of alternative splicing profiles
One-line: Introduces suppa, a computational tool that derives PSI (Ψ) inclusion values for alternative splicing events directly from fast transcript abundances (TPMs), achieving accuracy comparable to read-based methods while running more than 1000 times faster.
Key takeaways

SUPPA computes relative inclusion levels (percent-spliced-in) of local alternative splicing events from transcript-level abundances rather than from reads mapped to the genome or junctions, sidestepping the time/storage cost of mapping.
It works in two operations: generateEvents derives events from a GTF annotation (or a de novo reconstruction), and psiPerEvent computes Ψ per sample from transcript TPMs, defining Ψ as the abundance of the inclusion-form transcripts (F1) over the abundance of all transcripts in either form (F1∪F2).
SUPPA is agnostic to the quantification method; it was tested coupled to Sailfish and RSEM, and benchmarked against the read-based methods MATS and MISO.
On simulated data SUPPA was closer to ground-truth than MATS and MISO; on RT-PCR–validated events it was slightly superior or comparable; and it was >1000× faster than MATS/MISO for the Ψ-calculation step.
Annotation completeness (full-length transcripts, e.g. RefSeq) matters more than the number of transcripts per gene for accurate Ψ estimation; incomplete annotations (Ensembl) degrade accuracy.
Software is implemented in Python 2.7, has no Python-library dependencies, and is MIT-licensed (released at bitbucket.org/regulatorygenomicsupf/suppa).

Notable claims / data

Supported event types: exon skipping (SE), alternative 5′/3′ splice sites (A5/A3), mutually exclusive exons (MX), intron retention (RI), and alternative first/last exons (AF/AL).
Simulated benchmark (144 events, RefSeq, FluxSimulator reads): Sailfish+SUPPA Pearson R = 0.971, RSEM+SUPPA R = 0.987, vs MATS R = 0.833 and MISO R = 0.862 against ground truth. SUPPA kept R > 0.9 for all event types, including MX where MATS/MISO accuracy dropped sharply.
ΔΨ cumulative-distribution differences between SUPPA and MATS/MISO were statistically significant on simulated data (rank-sum P-values from 8.89×10⁻¹² down to 2.2×10⁻¹⁶).
Experimentally validated set: 163 SE events validated by RT-PCR in MDA-MB-231 cells under ESRP1 overexpression vs empty vector (Shen et al. 2012); on the 60 RefSeq-common events SUPPA was slightly better or comparable to MATS/MISO (ΔΨ distributions not significantly different, P > 0.1).
Annotation effect: RefSeq > Ensembl accuracy across all methods; restricting Ensembl to CDS recovered some SUPPA accuracy, but Ensembl-based SUPPA still trailed MATS/MISO — implicating transcript-quantification quality driven by annotation completeness.
Replicate reproducibility (MCF7/MCF10 cytosolic RNA, Sailfish+RefSeq): Pearson R ≈ 0.86–0.89 overall, rising to ≈ 0.95–0.97 when restricted to genes with summed TPM > 1.
Speed: generateEvents took 3 min for 16,714 RefSeq events (25,937 genes / 48,566 transcripts) and 20 min for 66,577 Ensembl 75 events (37,494 genes / 135,521 transcripts); psiPerEvent ran in under a minute and was >1000× faster than MATS/MISO. End-to-end (raw FastQ → Ψ) with Sailfish+SUPPA on RefSeq events: ~3.5 min (214 s) for synthetic data and ~1 h (4022 s) for the MDA-MB-231 samples.
Annotation-free mode: SUPPA on Cufflinks de novo transcripts gave accuracy comparable to the Ensembl annotation but lower than RefSeq; re-quantifying Cufflinks transcripts with Sailfish gave no improvement — a limit of de novo exon–intron structure prediction from short reads.
Caveats: SUPPA (this version) does not propagate transcript-quantification uncertainty into Ψ, handles only binary event forms, and depends entirely on the annotation/reconstruction quality.

Connections

A transcript-quantification-based alternative to read/junction-based event tools such as rmats, MATS, and MISO; complements the broader landscape of splicing-methods.
Centers on percent-spliced-in as its core output metric.
Pairs with fast quantifiers (Sailfish, RSEM; conceptually similar to kallisto/Salmon-style alignment-free quantification) — the speed of SUPPA is gated by the quantifier rather than by mapping.
Relevant to large-scale and resource-limited alternative-splicing studies, including cancer-focused work (the validation data come from ESRP1-perturbed breast-cancer cells), connecting to splicing-and-disease.

Open questions

Would incorporating transcript-quantification uncertainty (posterior intervals) into the Ψ estimate measurably improve SUPPA’s accuracy, as the authors speculate?
Can the binary event model be extended to multi-conformation / complex events without losing experimental testability (e.g. the QKI-type changes noted for lung adenocarcinoma)?
How much does pairing SUPPA with newer alignment-free quantifiers and more complete long-read-derived annotations close the RefSeq-vs-Ensembl accuracy gap?
Does the no-uncertainty, annotation-dependent design limit differential-splicing applications across many conditions, where read-based posterior methods may be more robust?

wiki/sources/amelan-2026-crispr-knockout.title: amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes
wiki/sources/amelan-2026-crispr-knockout.content:
CRISPR knockout screens reveal genes and pathways essential for neuronal differentiation and implicate PEDS1 in neurodevelopment
One-line: A genome-wide CRISPR knockout screen in differentiating mouse embryonic stem cells identifies hundreds of genes essential (or growth-restricting) for neuronal differentiation, most not yet linked to disease, and validates the framework by implicating the plasmalogen-biosynthesis enzyme PEDS1 as a novel autosomal-recessive neurodevelopment disorder gene.
Key takeaways

Genome-wide pooled-crispr-screen (Brie library, 19,674 genes, 4 sgRNAs/gene) in Cas9-expressing E14 / Sox1-GFP mouse ESCs differentiated with N2B27 medium, sampled at days 0, 4 and 10, maps genes essential for neurogenesis/neuronal differentiation.
Two gene classes emerge: 331 “neural differentiation essential genes” (NEGs) whose sgRNAs are depleted specifically during differentiation, and 110 “neural differentiation growth-restricting genes” (NGGs) whose knockout confers a proliferative advantage.
NEGs are enriched for known NDD genes yet mostly novel candidates; dominant-inheritance NDD genes skew toward transcriptional regulators, whereas recessive NDD genes are enriched for metabolic processes — inheritance mode predictable from pathway.
Eight NEGs (Eml1, Dusp26, Dynlrb2, Mta3, Peds1, Sgms1, Slitrk4, Vamp3) produce neuroanatomical abnormalities in mice; four (Dusp26, Slitrk4, Peds1, Vamp3) cause microcephaly.
Focused validation on PEDS1 (plasmanylethanolamine desaturase, plasmalogen-biosynthesis): a bi-allelic frameshift variant (chr20:g.50153533AG>A; c.104delC; p.Ala35Valfs*16) segregates recessively in two consanguineous families with microcephaly, global developmental delay and congenital cataracts.
Peds1 deficiency in mice accelerates cell-cycle exit, reduces progenitor proliferation, and impairs neuronal differentiation and cortical migration — establishing PEDS1 as a cause of AR NDD (peds1).

Notable claims / data

Screen scale: 3,913 genes essential in ESCs (embryonic stem cell essential genes, EEGs) at FDR < 0.05; overlap strongly with common human cancer-line essential genes (validation). Euler: 1,502 EEG∩common-essential, 371 NEGs total with only 39 overlapping common essential.
1,752 genes depleted during differentiation (days 4/10, FDR < 0.05); 1,421 also essential in ESCs, leaving 331 differentiation-specific NEGs; analyses focus on 297 NEGs with human orthologs.
NEGs enriched for transcription/chromatin regulation and metabolic processes; 61 NEGs are transcription regulators (most significant category), most highly expressed in radial-glia in developing mouse brain single-cell data.
110 NGGs enriched for autophagy, RNA methylation, vacuole transport, mTOR; 24/106 human homologs are known tumor suppressors (P = 4.01×10⁻¹¹, e.g. Pten, P53, Tsc1, Tsc2).
Human phenotype enrichment: NEGs associated with abnormal cerebral white matter morphology (FDR = 0.010) and cerebral subcortex abnormality (FDR = 0.017); microcephaly genes enriched among EEGs/NEGs/NGGs, macrocephaly genes only among NGGs.
ASD genes enriched only with NEGs (mirror image of developmental-delay genes which enrich with EEGs/NGGs); ASD risk strongest for genes essential at day 4 (FDR = 3.44×10⁻⁴).
Mouse microcephaly effect sizes (rostral/caudal): Dusp26 11%/7%, Slitrk4 9%/7%, Peds1 14%/11%, Vamp3 11%/10%; confirmed by high-resolution episcopic microscopy.
PEDS1-104delC variant: protein undetectable by western blot and immunostaining (loss of ER colocalization with calnexin) in COS7 cells — a loss-of-function allele in vitro and in vivo.
Peds1 knockout mESC clones (exon 4 deletion) show increased Sox1-GFP⁻ fraction after 4-day differentiation (66–72% vs 57–59% WT); RNA-seq: 1,075 genes up / 1,266 down, Peds1 itself strongly downregulated (FDR = 1.66×10⁻¹⁰); upregulated pathways include pluripotency and lipid/cholesterol metabolism, downregulated include nervous system development and focal adhesion.
Deliverable resource: interactive web tool neuro-diff-screen (aa-shifman.shinyapps.io/Neuro_Diff_Screen/) for exploring gene essentiality and expression.

Connections

Extends CRISPR functional-genomics toolkit to neural lineage; complements single-cell-crispr-screen / pooled-crispr-screen approaches and genome-editing methods.
Reinforces convergence of NDD genes on neurogenesis and progenitor (radial-glia) biology; connects to autism and psychiatric-genetics via ASD/DD enrichment patterns.
Dominant-vs-recessive pathway dichotomy (transcription/chromatin vs metabolism) offers a rule for interpreting NDD variant inheritance.
PEDS1 finding expands the plasmalogen-deficiency disorder family (alongside PEX7, GNPAT, AGPS, PEX5, FAR1) but without the skeletal abnormalities of classic rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata (RCDP).

Open questions

Which of the ~200 novel NEGs not currently in NDD datasets are genuinely disease-relevant in humans?
How do mouse-specific effects and absence of a specific neuronal cell type in the in vitro system limit generalization (stated limitations)?
Mechanistically, how does plasmalogen loss couple to accelerated cell-cycle exit and migration defects?
Do NGGs (tumor-suppressor-enriched) contribute growth advantage specifically in the nervous system, as HPO “neoplasm of the nervous system” enrichment hints?

wiki/sources/ameur-2011-total-rna-sequen.title: ameur-2011-total-rna-sequencing-reveals
wiki/sources/ameur-2011-total-rna-sequen.content:
Total RNA sequencing reveals nascent transcription and widespread co-transcriptional splicing in the human brain
One-line: Total (rRNA-depleted, non-poly(A)-selected) RNA-seq of human fetal and adult brain and liver shows that a large fraction of reads are intronic, that these mark nascent pre-mRNA, and that a 5′-to-3′ intronic “saw-tooth” coverage pattern is a genome-wide signature of cotranscriptional-splicing — especially prominent in fetal brain and in genes tied to neurodevelopment.
Key takeaways

Total RNA-seq captures immature/nascent transcripts that poly(A)+ selection misses: in matched chimpanzee frontal cortex, 38% of mapped reads were intronic for total RNA versus only 8% for poly(A)+ RNA; human samples showed the same distribution.
Intronic RNA is far higher in brain than liver, and higher in fetal than adult brain. 1,139 genes passed the high-intronic-RNA criterion in brain versus 390 in liver; many more high-intronic-coverage genes in fetal than adult brain.
A 5′→3′ declining coverage slope within long introns (a “saw-tooth” pattern) is explained by nascent transcription plus rapid cotranscriptional-splicing: each intron is spliced shortly after the polymerase finishes transcribing it.
Genes with high intronic (nascent) RNA in brain are enriched for axon guidance, cell adhesion, synaptic transmission, and neuron projection — i.e., neurodevelopmental/synaptic functions.
Introns flanking alternatively spliced (cassette) exons show higher intronic RNA than constitutive exons in the same gene, linking slow co-transcriptional splicing to alternative-splicing regulation (a form of intron retention; relates to percent-spliced-in metrics).

Notable claims / data

Intronic read fraction: total RNA 38% vs poly(A)+ 8% (chimpanzee cortex); reads outside known exons 74% (total) vs 20% (poly(A)+). Brain >> liver intronic fraction, Z-test P < 10⁻³⁰⁰ for both fetal and adult tissues.
~95% of de novo splice junctions bridged two known exon boundaries of the same RefSeq gene; only ~1% connected an exon to a non-exonic region — argues intronic reads are unprocessed transcripts, not independent intronic transcripts.
Co-transcriptional splicing evidence (3′-upstream vs 5′-downstream intron coverage difference) is strongest for the longest introns (>100 kb) and decreases with intron length; ~84% of exons flanked by a large intron showed clear evidence. High-coverage fetal-brain resequencing added ~492–500 million reads for this analysis.
Cassette-exon flanking introns have higher RNA than constitutive exons (P = 8.3 × 10⁻²⁵); NOVA-regulated cassette exons even higher (P = 1.4 × 10⁻²⁵). High-intronic-RNA fetal-brain genes overlap TDP-43 targets (P = 6.0 × 10⁻²⁵) and NOVA-2 targets (P = 8.6 × 10⁻¹⁵).
qrtPCR (NRXN1, GRID2) validated the 5′-high/3′-low intronic gradient and confirmed fetal brain has higher immature transcript levels than adult while mature exon levels are comparable. PCR + Sanger validated co-transcriptional splicing of consecutive exons in ERBB4 and NRXN1 (with a short transcription-to-splicing lag), plus TUBB2B, ZBTB20 and PAH.
Disease link: of the 10 top intronic-RNA fetal-brain genes, NRXN1, PCDH9, MSRA and AUTS2 are implicated in autism (CNVs/translocations); MSRA/AUTS2 in epilepsy; NRXN1/AUTS2 in schizophrenia and ADHD; top noncoding hit C21orf34 associated with ADHD; GAP43 with autism.
Caveats: resolution limited to long introns of relatively highly expressed genes; cannot resolve co-transcriptional splicing of short introns from RNA-seq alone (shown only by PCR). SOLiD 50-bp directional sequencing; human RNA purchased (BioChain); cassette-exon annotation from UCSC Alt Events + mouse NOVA exons lifted to hg19.

Connections

Foundational human-tissue evidence for the cotranscriptional-splicing concept and its splicing-and-chromatin/kinetic coupling theme; complements post-transcriptional-splicing by showing the timing of intron removal in vivo.
Slow co-transcriptional removal of cassette-exon introns connects to alternative-splicing, intron-retention, and percent-spliced-in (kinetics shape inclusion).
Brain/neurodevelopment sibling to kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmental splicing switches) and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator; NOVA/TDP-43 regulatory overlap ties it to neuronal splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins.
Neurodevelopmental/neuropsychiatric disease angle (autism, epilepsy, schizophrenia, ADHD genes) links to splicing-and-disease and scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.
Methodologically a total-RNA, short-read counterpart to modern brain isoform atlases such as humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas.

Open questions

Whether higher fetal intronic RNA reflects higher transcription rate, faster mature-RNA turnover, or more stable/less-processed pre-mRNA — the data cannot discriminate.
Do RNA-binding regulators (NOVA, TDP-43) actively slow co-transcriptional splicing of cassette-exon introns, and is this a developmental control point in the human brain?
Generality to short introns and lowly expressed genes, which are below RNA-seq resolution here.
Whether the high-nascent-transcription neurodevelopmental genes flagged here are causal in the associated disorders.

wiki/sources/anders-2012-detecting-diffe.title: anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons
wiki/sources/anders-2012-detecting-diffe.content:
Detecting differential usage of exons from RNA-Seq data
One-line: Introduces dexseq, a GLM/negative-binomial statistical method (and R/Bioconductor package) for detecting differential exon usage between conditions in RNA-Seq data, with explicit modelling of biological variability for reliable false-discovery control.
Key takeaways

DEXSeq tests for differential exon usage: whether the fraction of a gene’s reads falling into a given exon (or “counting bin”) changes between conditions, rather than assembling full transcripts or estimating isoform abundance.
It belongs to the third of three analysis categories the authors lay out: (1) gene-level counting (edgeR, DESeq, BaySeq); (2) full-length transcript assembly/quantification (cufflinks, MMSeq, Jiang & Wong); (3) directly observable exon-level quantities — DEXSeq’s approach.
Core statistical machinery: per-gene generalized linear models with a negative binomial distribution; the linear predictor decomposes into gene baseline, per-bin exon effect, condition effect on expression, and a counting-bin x condition interaction. A nonzero interaction coefficient flags differential exon usage; the test is an analysis-of-deviance (ANODEV) chi-squared likelihood-ratio test of full vs reduced model.
Key innovation over prior methods (Wang et al. 2008, miso / Katz et al. 2010, AlexaSeq, early cuffdiff): it accounts for biological variability via a per-bin dispersion parameter, sharing information across genes through a fitted mean-dispersion relation.
“Flattening” of gene models: overlapping/variable-boundary exons across transcripts are cut into non-overlapping counting bins; reads overlapping multiple bins are counted in each.
Dispersion is estimated with the Cox-Reid adjusted profile likelihood (McCarthy et al. 2012, as in edgeR), then a per-bin estimate is combined with a fitted mean-dispersion trend alpha(mu)=a1/mu + a0 by taking the maximum (conservative, costs some power but limits false positives).
Implemented as an open-source Bioconductor R package plus two HTSeq-based Python scripts (flatten annotation -> count reads); produces HTML reports and visualizations with a variance-stabilizing axis “warp”.

Notable claims / data

Brooks et al. Drosophila pasilla knock-down data (3 control + 4 knock-down samples): at 10% FDR, DEXSeq found differential exon usage for 259 counting bins across 159 genes. Example gene Ten-m / CG5723, bin E004: p = 2.1e-11, adjusted p = 1.2e-8.
Fitted dispersion-mean relation alpha(mu) = 1.3/mu + 0.012; a0 = 0.012 implies ~11% coefficient of variation in exon usage between biological replicates for strongly expressed exons. Using the expression-absorbing model (3) instead of model (2) lowered dispersion (from 1.6/mu + 0.018), preserving power.
Overdispersion matters: setting dispersions to zero (Poisson assumption) on the Brooks data yielded 36x as many hits (9,432 bins in 3,610 genes), mostly unsupported false positives.
Chimpanzee data (Brawand et al.; prefrontal cortex vs cerebellum): 866 bins in 650 genes at 10% FDR. Biological example hits include PRKCZ (PB1 heterodimerization domain exons, PKMzeta vs PKCzeta), PLCH2 (EF-hand exon), and precortistatin.
ENCODE human cell-line comparison (h1-hESC vs HUVEC, biological duplicates): 7,795 genes affected by differential exon usage.
Benchmark vs cuffdiff: on the Brooks data cuffdiff 1.3.0 called only 37 genes (less power than DEXSeq’s 159); in a mock replicate-vs-replicate comparison (no true effects expected) DEXSeq called only 8 genes while cuffdiff called 455 — indicating poor type-I error control by cuffdiff. Similar pattern on chimpanzee data (DEXSeq 700 genes vs cuffdiff 108 in the real comparison).
Caveat the authors themselves flag: when a large fraction of a gene’s exons are affected, attribution to specific bins becomes unreliable, and differential exon usage cannot always be distinguished from overall up/down-regulation of part of the gene; the per-bin call is trustworthy only when a small fraction of bins are significant.

Connections

A foundational entry under splicing-methods for RNA-Seq-based detection of alternative splicing; explicitly contrasts itself with event/isoform-centric tools such as miso and cufflinks, and conceptually relates to the percent-spliced-in family of metrics (percent-spliced-in) by quantifying relative exon usage.
Methodologically a descendant of DESeq (Anders & Huber 2010) and edgeR, extending negative-binomial GLM count modelling from gene-level differential expression to the exon/counting-bin level.
Sits alongside other read-counting/event detection tools in the wiki’s splicing-tools cluster (e.g. rmats) as an early, replicate-aware, biological-variability-modelling approach.

Open questions

Could leveraging junction (exon-skipping) reads — the ratio of reads skipping vs overlapping a bin — increase power for bins short relative to read length, as the authors anticipate?
How best to interpolate between per-bin and fitted dispersion estimates (beyond the conservative maximum rule), possibly adding covariates beyond the mean?
Can exon-centric testing be combined with isoform-deconvolution methods so that an exon-level analysis becomes a component of transcript-level inference?
Systematic, automated integration of protein-domain / miRNA-target annotation (Pfam, SMART, Prosite) to interpret which differential exons are functionally consequential.

wiki/sources/antaki-2022-phenotypic-spec.title: antaki-2022-phenotypic-spectrum-of-autism
wiki/sources/antaki-2022-phenotypic-spec.content:
A phenotypic spectrum of autism is attributable to the combined effects of rare variants, polygenic risk and sex
One-line: Whole-genome/exome analysis of ~11,000 ASD families shows rare and polygenic risk are inversely correlated and greater in females, defining ASD as a continuum of genetic architectures with distinct phenotypic correlates.
Key takeaways

The genetic architecture of autism spans a spectrum between extremes of monogenic (large-effect rare variant) and polygenic disease, with rare-variant and common-variant loads inversely correlated across cases — direct evidence for a liability-threshold-model.
A “female protective effect” generalizes to all components of risk: female cases carry significantly greater loads of both rare variant (RVRS) and polygenic (CVRS) risk than male cases, consistent with a sex-differentiated liability threshold.
Different genetic factors map onto different symptom dimensions, so psychiatric-genetics risk in ASD is not phenotypically uniform.
Genes implicated by rare variants are ~2x enriched in excitatory and inhibitory neuronal-cell-types of the fetal cortex, whereas genome-wide-association-study genes show no cell-type enrichment, linking rare variants of large effect to early fetal brain development.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 37,375 individuals from 11,313 ASD families (12,270 cases, 5,190 typically developing siblings, 19,917 parents); WGS from UCSD REACH + Simons Simplex Collection, plus exome+SNP genotyping from SPARK. Combined multivariable analysis restricted to 7,181 European-ancestry families (n = 25,391).
Six confirmed factors: de novo LoF (dnLoF), de novo missense (dnMIS), rare inherited LoF (inhLoF), and polygenic scores PS_ASD, PS_SZ, PS_EA. Rare variant analysis restricted to constrained genes (LOEUF < 0.37; MPC > 2 for missense).
Variance in case status: polygenic risk explains ~2%, combined rare variants ~2%, all six factors combined r2 ~4%. Composite genomic risk score (GRS) detects effect sizes ~40% stronger on average than rare-variant (RVRS) or common-variant (CVRS) scores alone.
Sex differences: female vs male cases RVRS P = 4.32e-7, CVRS P = 5.96e-4; GRS distribution skewed upward in females and bimodal (DNM carriers at top).
Cases carrying damaging DNMs have reduced polygenic load; RVRS–CVRS negative correlation P = 0.0037. Prior PRSice-based male-biased anti-correlation was NOT robust across scoring methods (SbayesR here), and gene-by-sex transmission biases were weak.
Parental age effect is multifactorial: de novo SNV rate rises with paternal age (r2 = 0.42 paternal, 0.27 maternal); PS_EA is the strongest inherited correlate of parental age (r2 = 0.017), with genetic effects on parental age tracking effects on educational attainment (PCC +0.76) not social behavior (PCC -0.66).
TADA test defined 125 ASD susceptibility genes (FDR < 0.05); these plus 114 Grove et al. GWAS genes compared against 1,000 random genes in BrainSpan bulk cortex and the CoDEx cell-type resource.
PS_EA is protective for core ASD symptom severity in offspring yet positively associated with case status — an unexplained dissociation, possibly a high-functioning (“Asperger”) subtype.

Connections

Provides an empirical demonstration of the liability-threshold-model and speaks to missing-heritability and heritability partitioning between rare and common variants (manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of, zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability).
Complements landscape-mapping in psychiatric-genetics such as grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across.
The rare-variant enrichment in fetal cortical neuronal-cell-types connects to neurodevelopment and cell-type-critical-disease frameworks (e.g. jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types).

Open questions

Would larger WGS sample sizes allow direct heritability estimation for rare vs common variants and incorporation of SVs/CNVs (excluded here for cross-platform comparability) into the composite GRS?
What mechanism explains the PS_EA paradox (protective for symptom severity yet risk-increasing for diagnosis)?
Are the weak gene-by-sex interactions on behavioral traits genuinely absent or masked by the male-predominant, clinically ascertained case sample?

wiki/sources/baralle-2017-alternative-sp.title: baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator
wiki/sources/baralle-2017-alternative-sp.content:
Alternative splicing as a regulator of development and tissue identity
One-line: A foundational review arguing that coordinated alternative splicing networks — driven mainly by developmentally regulated RNA-binding proteins and coupled to transcription/epigenetics — govern cell differentiation, tissue-identity acquisition and organ development across brain, heart, skeletal muscle, liver and other tissues.
Key takeaways

The field has shifted from cataloguing single splicing events to describing global, molecularly coordinated alternative-splicing networks; the central open challenge is demonstrating their functional/physiological consequences, not just their regulation.
splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins (RBPs) are the principal coordinators of developmental splicing transitions: their expression levels, localization, activity and own alternative splicing are finely tuned per tissue and developmental stage. A tissue-specific “splicing-code” is proposed but hard to decipher because the same motif is read differently by different RBPs depending on context and binding position.
A recurring theme: genes regulated by developmental alternative splicing are often not changed in total expression level — splicing is the dominant regulatory layer for them (e.g. 31% of differentially spliced cortical genes show no expression change).
Splicing is frequently co-transcriptional, so cotranscriptional-splicing, Pol II elongation kinetics, chromatin modifications, nucleosome occupancy and DNA methylation modulate outcomes (splicing-and-chromatin); slow Pol II favors weak-splice-site recognition and exon inclusion. Coupling can run both ways (splicing of chromatin modifiers like EHMT2/LSD1 feeds back on the epigenome).
A major mechanistic motif is productive coupling of alternative splicing to NMD (“AS-NMD”): poison exons / intron retention introduce premature termination codons to downregulate proteins, prominently for RNA-processing regulators during cell differentiation (smooth muscle, erythropoiesis).
Misregulation of these same networks underlies disease — reversion of adult tissues to fetal/neonatal splicing patterns is a hallmark of heart failure and myotonic dystrophy (splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

90-95% of human genes undergo some alternative splicing; of ~20,000 protein-coding genes, high-resolution mass spectrometry finds ~37% generate multiple protein isoforms (caveat: not all AS events yield functional proteins; amplification methods can overestimate splicing events).
Brain: adult cortex vs embryos show ~400 differential AS events; 31% of developmentally AS-regulated genes do not change total expression. ptbp1/PTBP2/SRRM4 drive the neural progenitor→neuron transition (PTBP1 represses PTBP2 exon 10 → PTC+NMD; SRRM4 promotes its inclusion). RBFOX-depleted neurons misregulate >500 cassette exons. NOVA2 controls DAB1 exon 7b/7c skipping governing neuronal migration (rescued by short isoform in Nova2−/− mice).
Microexons (3-15 nt) regulated by SRRM4 tune protein-interaction domains in neurogenesis and are misregulated in autism (SRRM4 upregulation). Tau exon 10 sets 3R/4R isoform ratio; imbalance impairs APP axonal transport.
Heart: cardiomyocyte AS transitions concentrate in the first 4 weeks postnatally; sf3b1 is hypoxia-induced in cardiac hypertrophy, mis-splicing ketohexokinase exons toward a liver isoform that drives anabolic metabolism and contractile failure. CELF1/MBNL1 antagonistically regulate cardiac splicing.
Titin (TTN, highest exon count of any known gene): I-band exons 50-219 are developmentally regulated; neonatal long N2BA (3.7 MDa) → adult short N2B (2.97 MDa); ratio sets sarcomere passive tension and myocardium wall stiffness. RBM20 regulates this via a two-step splicing-repressor mechanism; RBM20 mutations cause human dilated cardiomyopathy. A CRISPR-Cas9 mouse blocking the Mex5/is7-containing TTN isoform produced a dystrophic phenotype in soleus and heart.
Myotonic dystrophy (most common adult muscular dystrophy): CTG/CCTG repeat expansions in DMPK 3′UTR (type 1) or CNBP intron 1 (type 2) sequester MBNL1 and activate CELF1, reverting adult tissues to fetal isoforms — e.g. INSR exon 11 (insulin resistance), CLCN1 exon 7a (myotonia), SCN5A (cardiac conduction defects); ~80% of patients develop cardiac dysfunction.
Other tissues: liver maturation requires ESRP2 (one of few postnatally induced RBPs) and SRSF3/SRSF10/SLU7; pancreas networks via NOVA1 and RBM4 (ISL1, PAX4 → insulin); spermatogenesis shows splicing-epigenetics coupling (MRG15 reads H3K36me3, recruits PTBP1/2 to splice TNP2; knockout → intron retention, round-spermatid arrest, sterility); T-cell activation feedback loop (CELF2↔JNK via MKK7 exon 2 skipping; HNRNPL/PSF control CD45). Erythropoiesis uses stage-specific exon 16 switching in protein 4.1R and MBNL1-regulated Ndel1 exon inclusion.
Caveat the authors stress repeatedly: dramatic splicing changes can have only slight physiological impact (e.g. fibronectin EDA transitions do not majorly affect mouse development), so genome-wide data must be paired with molecular validation.

Connections

Companion physiology-of-splicing review to marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative; complements the mechanism/regulation reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of, and the topic editorial editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Heavily features RBP entities in the wiki: ptbp1 (neuron/smooth-muscle/germ-cell repressor), sf3b1 (cardiac hypertrophy fructolysis), hnrnpa1-family logic (HNRNPA/B in erythropoiesis), and U2AF65 (u2af1 paralog context) in spermatogenesis.
Provides the developmental/tissue grounding for splicing-and-disease and motivates splicing-therapeutics (antisense oligonucleotides reverted adult→fetal splicing in mouse muscle).
The co-transcriptional/epigenetic coupling sections connect to cotranscriptional-splicing, post-transcriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin; the predict-from-sequence difficulty motivates work cataloged under splicing-methods and splice-sites/splicing-regulatory-elements.

Open questions

How to functionally characterize the thousands of developmentally regulated protein isoforms whose roles remain unknown (the authors call this the field’s biggest bottleneck)?
When does a large splicing change actually matter physiologically vs being tolerated (the FN1-EDA paradox)? What predicts functional impact?
Can a tissue-specific splicing code be learned given that cellular composition, RBP combinations and binding-position effects confound prediction from pre-mRNA sequence alone?
How general is splicing↔epigenetics bidirectional coupling in development beyond the few demonstrated loci (EHMT2, LSD1, MRG15/TNP2, CTCF/CD45)?

wiki/sources/barrangou-2016-applications.title: barrangou-2016-applications-of-crispr-technologies-in
wiki/sources/barrangou-2016-applications.content:
Applications of CRISPR technologies in research and beyond
One-line: A 2016 Nature Biotechnology review by two central CRISPR pioneers surveying how RNA-programmed genome-editing with cas9 moved from a bacterial immune system into a democratized toolkit spanning research, therapeutics, agriculture, industrial biotech and biological control.
Key takeaways

CRISPR–Cas9 is a two-component, RNA-guided system: a chimeric single guide RNA (sgRNA, ~80 nt, with a 20-nt target-binding segment) programs the cas9 endonuclease to make a blunt double-strand break (DSB) at a PAM-flanked target, repaired by NHEJ (indels) or HDR (templated replacement).
Its decisive advantage over ZFNs and TALENs is programmability: retargeting requires only synthesizing a new sgRNA rather than engineering a new protein for each site, which unlocked high-throughput and multiplexed applications.
Catalytically dead dcas9 converts the system into a sequence-specific platform for transcriptional repression (CRISPRi, e.g. KRAB fusion), activation (CRISPRa, e.g. VP64 fusion), epigenome editing (acetyltransferase/demethylase fusions), and live-cell chromatin imaging (fluorophore fusions, e.g. CRISPRainbow visualizing up to six loci).
Scaled-up sgRNA:Cas9 libraries enable genome-wide loss-of-function and CRISPRi/CRISPRa screens (see pooled-crispr-screen) to find essential genes, drug targets, and — crucially — to interrogate non-coding regulatory sequences and enhancers (see cis-regulatory-elements, noncoding-variants).
Beyond the bench: in vivo/ex vivo therapeutic correction (eye, blood, liver, muscle), xenotransplantation (PERV-free pigs), crop and livestock breeding, sequence-specific antimicrobials/antivirals, industrial fermentation strains, and gene drives for disease-vector control.

Notable claims / data

Cas9 target recognition: PAM (2–4 bp conserved) recognition first, then seed-sequence (~first 12 bp) complementarity, R-loop formation, and RuvC/HNH cleavage ~3 bp upstream of the PAM-proximal protospacer edge; specificity is also set by conformational control and differential residence time at on- vs off-target sites.
Tool diversification: SpyCas9 nickase/dual-nickase/FokI-fusion variants; smaller orthologs (e.g. SaCas9, NmeCas9) for viral-vector packaging; new class II nucleases Cpf1 and C2c1; chemically modified sgRNAs (2’-O-Me, 2’-F, 2’-cEt) for stability; split-Cas9 and optogenetic/doxycycline-inducible control.
Screening scale: a CRISPR lentiviral screen across five human cell lines identified ~2,000 genes (~1,500 conserved across lines) as core fitness genes; CRISPR knockout screening reported to outperform shRNA and CRISPRi at identifying essential genes.
Adoption metrics (Fig. 3, data from addgene): CRISPR publications, plasmid deposits/requests, depositing labs and recipient countries all rose steeply 2012–2015, far outpacing ZFN/TALEN.
Clinical/regulatory snapshot (as of 2016): two CRISPR–Cas9 cancer-therapy trials approved (China and the US); four countries (Sweden, UK, Japan, China) had approved human-embryo research; a human tripronuclear-zygote germline-editing report had appeared; the International Summit on Gene Editing argued pre-implantation genetic diagnosis obviates most germline-editing needs.
Delivery is the recurring bottleneck: plasmid/viral vectors vs. transient ribonucleoprotein (RNP) or mRNA delivery (avoiding DNA toxicity), lipid transfection, and DNA “nanoclews”.

Connections

Foundational context for the functional-single-cell-genomics thread: the pooled-library screening logic here is the ancestor of single-cell-crispr-screen and perturb-seq, which add single-cell transcriptomic readouts to CRISPR perturbations.
Extends genome-editing and the dcas9-based regulatory/imaging toolkit; the CRISPRi/CRISPRa and enhancer-screening applications connect to cis-regulatory-elements and noncoding-variants work.
Written by Barrangou (CRISPR bacterial immunity/food) and Doudna (Cas9 mechanism/structure), so it doubles as an insider map of the mechanistic and translational literature circa 2016.

Open questions

Which delivery modality (RNP vs mRNA vs viral vs nanoparticle) becomes standard for each target tissue, and how does that gate clinical translation?
How reproducible are CRISPR screens across cell lines, reagents and platforms, and can cheaper/shorter sgRNAs democratize them further?
How will germline-editing ethics and country-by-country regulation evolve past the 2016 consensus, given the pace of the science?
Can rationally engineered PAM-recognition domains eventually yield Cas variants targeting essentially any sequence regardless of G+C content?

wiki/sources/begg-2020-concentration-dep.title: begg-2020-concentration-dependent-splicing-is-enabled
wiki/sources/begg-2020-concentration-dep.content:
Concentration-dependent splicing is enabled by Rbfox motifs of intermediate affinity
One-line: A biochemistry/mechanism study showing that the Rbfox family of splicing factors binds, beyond its canonical high-affinity GCAUG motif, a set of “secondary” intermediate-affinity motifs that only become functionally active when Rbfox concentration is high — enabling a second, concentration-gated wave of alternative-splicing regulation during neuronal differentiation.
Key takeaways

Rbfox proteins are splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins long known to bind the pentamer GCAUG with high affinity, yet about half of in vivo CLIP peaks lack a GCAYG motif — motivating a search for additional binding determinants.
Using RNA Bind-n-Seq with natural transcriptomic sequences (nsRBNS) across a 2.5-order-of-magnitude RBFOX2 concentration range, the authors identify six non-GCAYG “secondary motifs” of the form GHNUG (G_NUG) of intermediate affinity; four (GCUUG, GAAUG, GUUUG, GUAUG) are validated in vivo.
These secondary motifs are bona fide splicing-regulatory-elements: clusters of them placed downstream of an alternative exon drive exon inclusion comparable to a single GCAUG in a fluorescent splicing reporter, but only when Rbfox is abundant.
The functional consequence is concentration-dependent / stage-specific regulation: secondary motifs act later in neuronal differentiation and in neuronal subtypes with the highest Rbfox expression, adding a “second wave” of splicing changes as Rbfox levels rise.
An equilibrium biophysical model explains the behavior: high-affinity primary motifs saturate at low Rbfox levels; abundant secondary motifs only accumulate occupancy once Rbfox is high.

Notable claims / data

nsRBNS performed with recombinant RBFOX2 at 4, 14, 43, 121 (duplicate), 365 nM and 1.1 µM (plus 0 nM control), constant 250 nM RNA; library of ~2,200 human/mouse 3’ UTRs cut into overlapping 110-base segments (longer than typical 20–40 nt random RBNS, allowing motif-cluster analysis).
Of 55,931 detectable sequences at 365 nM, 8,412 (15%) bound at R ≥ 1.1; about half (4,032) contained a canonical GCAYG. Of the 4,358 bound sequences lacking a primary motif, 1,885 (43%) contained ≥1 GHNUG secondary motif.
Iterative 5-mer enrichment (SKA-style, run at 1.1 µM to favor low-affinity sites) recovered GCAUG/GCACG first, then six GHNUG secondary motifs (e.g., GCUUG, GAAUG, GUUUG, GUAUG, GUGUG, GCCUG).
Position-specificity: G1, U4, G5 are the critical recognition sites (consistent with the RBFOX1–UGCAUG structure, PDB 2ERR), and phyloP conservation of GCAUG instances peaks at G1 and G5.
Primary motifs saturate ~365 nM and decline at 1.1 µM; secondary motifs show steadily increasing enrichment, saturating at/above 1.1 µM. Prior SPR Kd values for secondary motifs were ~100–600 nM vs low-nM for GCAUG.
In RBFOX1 HiTS–CLIP (mouse whole brain, ventral spinal neurons), ≥2 secondary motifs were enriched ~5-fold (intronic) and ~2.5-fold (3’ UTR) above background; together with primary motifs they explain a substantial fraction of peaks that lack a primary motif.
Reporter (RG6, Cerulean–RBFOX1, HEK293T): one GCAUG or six copies of a secondary motif drove PSI toward ~100% with exogenous RBFOX1; effect scaled with single-cell Rbfox level by flow cytometry.
Neuronal differentiation time course (mESC → glutamatergic neurons): total Rbfox rises ~5-fold RG→DS3; secondary motifs correlate with ΔPSI only in the higher-Rbfox DS1–DS3 interval, whereas primary motifs correlate at both intervals. Primary-motif exons (n=388) enriched for membrane/cytoskeleton GO terms; secondary-motif exons (n=561) enriched for dendrite development / signal transduction.
Mouse Cd47 exon 10 (36% inclusion increase DS1→DS3) used as an endogenous target; secondary motifs in its 617-nt downstream intron act additively (restoring a 5-motif cluster gave +19% PSI; adding GUUUG and GAAUG gave +10% and +11%).
Across 13 neuronal cell types spanning ~3 orders of magnitude of Rbfox expression: each downstream GCAUG increases exon PSI by ~15%, each secondary motif by ~3.6% (so ~4 secondary motifs ≈ one primary).
Quantitative equilibrium model: estimated nuclear GCAUG concentration ~16–63 nM, GCACG ~3–12 nM, four secondary motifs together ~67–270 nM; secondary motifs reach ~50% occupancy at ~10–50 µM nuclear Rbfox. Filter-binding experiments confirmed secondary motifs are preferentially bound after primary-motif saturation.
Caveats: nsRBNS is in vitro and lacks the native protein environment (partner proteins, modifications, e.g. SUP-12 / LASR-complex hnRNP M that have been credited with GUGUG binding); identified motifs reflect the strongest plus most transcriptome-abundant secondary sites, not necessarily all high-affinity variants.

Connections

Extends the concept of splicing-regulatory-elements and the broader splicing code by arguing for replacing the binary “binding site vs non-binding site” view with one that incorporates site affinity AND transcriptomic abundance, with low-affinity sites mattering when trans-factor (rna-binding-proteins) activity is high.
Mechanistic complement to existing splicing-factors sources (e.g. lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of) by detailing how one RBP family achieves concentration-gated, cell-type-specific regulation.
Uses percent spliced in (PSI / ΔPSI) as the splicing readout (relates to percent-spliced-in).
Draws an explicit analogy to transcription-factor suboptimal/low-affinity motifs (PHA-4, PREP1, GATA/FGF) and to an miRNA equilibrium-occupancy model; speculates secondary motifs may contribute to RBP phase-separated compartments.

Open questions

Are GUGUG/GCCUG bound directly by Rbfox in vivo, or are their CLIP signals attributable to partner proteins (SUP-12, LASR/hnRNP M)? The study leaves direct binding “not excluded.”
How general is concentration-gated secondary-motif regulation across other RBP families, and does it shape phase-separated condensate behavior?
How much of disease-relevant Rbfox dysregulation (autism, epilepsy) is mediated specifically through secondary-motif targets rather than canonical GCAUG sites?
Can secondary-motif affinity tiers be incorporated into predictive splicing-code models to improve in vivo binding-site inference beyond GCAUG filtering?

wiki/sources/bergman-2022-compatibility-.title: bergman-2022-compatibility-rules-of-human-enhancer
wiki/sources/bergman-2022-compatibility-.content:
Compatibility rules of human enhancer and promoter sequences
One-line: A high-throughput reporter assay (ExP STARR-seq) testing 1,000 × 1,000 human enhancer–promoter pairs shows enhancers and promoters are broadly compatible and combine multiplicatively, tuned only subtly by sequence class.
Key takeaways

Introduces exp-starr-seq, an assay measuring RNA output for all pairwise combinations of 1,000 enhancer and 1,000 promoter sequences in K562 cells (604,268 well-covered pairs).
Contradicts the strong “biochemical compatibility” model: most enhancers activate all promoters by similar amounts, unlike the >10-fold promoter-class preferences seen in Drosophila.
Intrinsic enhancer and promoter activities combine multiplicatively to set RNA output (R2 = 0.82), i.e. no strong pair-specific specificity among cis-regulatory-elements.
Two subtle sequence classes emerge: housekeeping-gene promoters carry built-in activating motifs (GABPA, YY1) that make them less responsive to distal enhancers; variably expressed promoters lack these and respond more strongly.
ExP STARR-seq activity correlates with genomic features including chromatin-accessibility, H3K27ac, and nascent enhancer RNA transcription, and this assay is a form of massively-parallel-reporter-assay.

Notable claims / data

STARR-seq expression reproducible across biological replicates (R2 = 0.92 per barcode / per pair); spanned ~6 orders of magnitude.
Promoters showed >1,500-fold dynamic range (strongest: RPL3, HSP90AA1, ACTB); enhancers spanned 682-fold, on average 2-fold above random genomic controls.
Known globin locus-control enhancers HS1–HS3 activated the HBE1 promoter 5.21–15.9-fold vs controls; a GATA1/HDAC6 enhancer gave 6.76/6.87-fold.
Average Spearman correlation of enhancer activation across promoters = 0.81; of promoter activation across enhancers = 0.72.
Multiplicative model: promoter alone explains 48%, enhancer alone 27%, product 82% of variance (rising to 94% for pairs with >20 barcodes). Replicated in an independent MYC-locus luciferase assay (84% variance).
Enhancer/promoter class specificity adds only ~2% of total variance (~4% of enhancer-activation variance).
E1 (strong) enhancers enriched for FOS/JUN (AP-1) motifs and stronger CRISPRi effects; P1 promoters had 3.2-fold weaker intrinsic activity but similar native PRO-seq transcription and more distal CRISPRi enhancers (mean 3.6 vs 0.36 for P2).

Connections

Complements CRISPRi-based enhancer mapping (activity-by-contact) referenced throughout; extends the reporter-assay lineage of sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel and lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric.
Relevant to deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory and groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at on dissecting cis-regulatory-grammar.

Open questions

How well does the multiplicative, broadly-compatible model in episomal STARR-seq hold in native chromatin at endogenous enhancer–promoter distances?
What sequence/TF logic (beyond GABPA/YY1) sets the graded ~2–3-fold responsiveness differences between promoter and enhancer classes?
Do developmental or non-K562 cell contexts reveal stronger enhancer–promoter specificity than seen here?

wiki/sources/bertrand-2002-proneural-gen.title: bertrand-2002-proneural-genes-and-the
wiki/sources/bertrand-2002-proneural-gen.content:
Proneural genes and the specification of neural cell types
One-line: A foundational review establishing that “proneural genes” — basic helix–loop–helix (bHLH) transcription factors of the achaete-scute and atonal families — are necessary and sufficient to initiate neuronal lineages, and that, beyond this generic proneural role, they integrate positional information to specify neuronal-subtype identity, with strikingly conserved logic between Drosophila and vertebrates.
Key takeaways

Proneural genes encode bHLH transcription factors that are both necessary and sufficient (in the ectoderm) to commit cells to a neural fate and generate progenitors poised to differentiate — a core mechanism in brain-development-neurogenomics.
They have a dual role: (1) a generic proneural function (select progenitors, drive pan-neuronal differentiation) and (2) subtype specification, contributing positional/identity information appropriate to the time and place of neuron birth.
The logic is conserved fly↔vertebrate, but the cellular context differs: in Drosophila proneural genes act on quiescent, multipotent ectoderm (loss → epidermal fate; gain → ectopic sense organs); in vertebrates they act on already-specified, self-renewing neuroepithelial stem cells, promoting neuronal commitment at the expense of glia.
Proneural proteins act as heterodimers with ubiquitous E proteins (fly Daughterless; mammalian E2A/E12/E47, HEB, E2-2), binding E-box (CANNTG) DNA motifs; they are negatively regulated by HLH proteins lacking a basic domain (Emc/Id) and by Hes/Her/Esr (Espl) repressors.
A central operating mechanism is lateral inhibition via Notch signalling: a progenitor upregulates the Notch ligand Delta, activating Notch in neighbours, which induces Espl/Hes repressors that downregulate proneural genes — restricting high proneural activity to single cells. Positive-feedback loops (Senseless, Xcoe2, Hes6, Myt1; autoregulation) then lock in commitment.
Proneural genes act atop cascades of downstream bHLH differentiation genes (asense/cato in fly; NeuroD family in vertebrates), promote cell-cycle exit (via Cdk inhibitors p16/p21/p27), and actively inhibit glial fates, positioning them as part of the neurogenesis→gliogenesis switch.
Subtype identity arises through combinatorial action with regionally expressed cofactors (homeodomain and other determinants), e.g. Mash1+Phox2b → noradrenergic; Mash1 → GABAergic (via Dlx); Ngn2+Olig2 → motor neuron; Achaete/Scute + GATA factor Pannier (+ Chip bridge) for region-specific achaete activation.

Notable claims / data

The proneural concept dates to early-20th-century Drosophila bristle mutants; the achaete-scute complex (ac, sc, lsc, ase) was molecularly isolated in the 1980s and found homologous to myc, daughterless and MyoD — defining the bHLH domain. atonal (and relatives amos, cato) form a second proneural family, sharing ~45% bHLH identity with asc vs ~70% identity within asc.
Sequence/structure: 9 of 10 DNA-contacting bHLH residues are conserved across neural bHLH families (explaining common core E-box binding), so DNA-binding divergence and biological specificity likely come from family-specific residues + cofactor interactions, not the conserved contacts. A Scute→Atonal basic-domain swap chimera induces ectopic chordotonal organs and rescues ato-null flies; the divergent residues sit opposite the DNA-contact surface (a cofactor interface). Caveat: Amos and Atonal differ by a single basic-domain residue yet specify differently — so specificity is not in the basic domain alone.
Genetic specificity in fly: external sense organs need ac/sc (redundant); internal chordotonal organs/proprioceptors and founder photoreceptors need ato; remaining olfactory sensilla and multidendritic neurons need amos; ase and cato act post-selection as differentiation genes. asc/ato account for PNS proneural activity but NOT all CNS neuroblasts (some need no known proneural gene).
Vertebrate genetics: Mash1-null mice have severe neurogenesis defects in ventral telencephalon and olfactory epithelium; Ngn1/Ngn2 single mutants lack complementary cranial sensory ganglia, double mutants additionally lose spinal sensory ganglia and many ventral spinal neurons. Math1-null mice lack cerebellar granule cells, inner-ear hair cells and dorsal D1 spinal interneurons; Math5/ath5 loss eliminates most retinal ganglion cells. Clear proneural activity is established for only Mash1, Ngn1, Ngn2 (possibly Math1/Math5).
Glial inhibition: Mash1;Math3 or Mash1;Ngn2 double mutants block neuronal differentiation and cause premature astrocyte generation. Ngn1 inhibits astrocytic differentiation by an unexpected mechanism — sequestering p300/CBP–Smad1 and blocking Stat1/3 phosphorylation, independent of its transcriptional-activation role — paralleling lineage commitment by alternative-fate suppression in haematopoiesis.
Mash1 vs Ngn divergence: ectopic Ngns drive rapid cell-cycle exit and efficient differentiation; asc-family genes are less efficient (interpreted as greater sensitivity to Notch-mediated inhibition). Crucially, ectopic Mash1 can override endogenous programmes and respecify identity, whereas Ngn2 cannot alone — Ngn2 needs Olig2 to induce motor neurons, and Mash1 cannot substitute for Ngn2 in that pairing.

Connections

Topic hub: anchors the developmental-transcription-factor / neuronal cell-type axis of brain-development-neurogenomics; the bHLH→neuronal-subtype logic is upstream of the mature cell-type and isoform diversity catalogued in developing-brain sibling sources.
Mechanistically complementary to sibling brain-development sources that profile the outputs of neurogenesis (gene/isoform expression across development): walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna — this review supplies the upstream regulatory program (proneural bHLH → Notch → differentiation cascade) that those expression atlases sample downstream of.
The recurring “splicing layer in neurodevelopment” theme elsewhere in the wiki (splicing-and-neurodevelopment) operates downstream of and in parallel to the transcriptional proneural cascade described here; this source is strictly about transcription-factor logic, not splicing.

Open questions

Whether Math1 and Math5 act as true proneural genes (selecting progenitors from neuroepithelial stem cells) or only as subtype-specification factors — direct loss-of-progenitor evidence was still lacking.
Identity of additional proneural genes: sympathetic ganglia (Mash1-independent) and ventral telencephalon (residual Mash1-independent progenitors) imply unidentified factors.
The structural/biochemical basis of E-box and cofactor specificity beyond the basic domain; which cofactors confer regional/subtype specificity to Mash1 and Ngns.
Whether proneural genes have distinct roles within restricted glial progenitors (Mash1 in oligodendrocytic, Ngn2 in astrocytic precursors) and any role in adult neural stem cells.

wiki/sources/bhattacharya-2023-isoform-l.title: bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic
wiki/sources/bhattacharya-2023-isoform-l.content:
Isoform-level transcriptome-wide association uncovers genetic risk mechanisms for neuropsychiatric disorders in the human brain
One-line: Introduces isoTWAS (isotwas), a multivariate isoform-level transcriptome-wide association framework that jointly models the correlated isoforms of a gene to predict isoform expression from cis-SNPs, recovering ~60% more neuropsychiatric GWAS loci than gene-level TWAS and prioritizing isoform-resolved disease mechanisms (splicing-and-disease) invisible at the gene level.
Key takeaways

Gene-level integrative methods (TWAS) explain only a fraction of GWAS loci because they use total gene expression as the molecular outcome and ignore the distinct transcript isoforms produced by alternative splicing — a limitation especially acute in brain, where genes are longer, have more exons, and show the most complex splicing.
isoTWAS is a three-step framework: (1) train multivariate predictive models of isoform-level expression from all cis-SNPs within 1 Mb, exploiting the correlation structure across isoforms of the same gene; (2) impute isoform expression into a GWAS cohort (or run a weighted burden test on summary stats); (3) stepwise hypothesis testing — isoform P values aggregated to gene-level via ACAT with gene-level FDR control, then isoform-level FWER control, plus locus permutation and optional Bayesian fine-mapping.
Because isoforms are correlated, multivariate modeling improves prediction of >80% of individual isoforms (~1.8–2.4-fold median gain) and of total gene expression by 25–70%, roughly doubling the number of testable features in trait mapping.
Across 15 neuropsychiatric GWAS, isoTWAS uncovered associations at ~60% more GWAS loci than gene-level TWAS, while remaining well-calibrated to the null — gains reflect true signal, not test-statistic inflation.
A key advantage: isoTWAS finds trait associations for an isoform when the gene itself shows no association — illustrated by isoforms of AKT3, CUL3 and HSPD1 in schizophrenia and PCLO across disorders, each driven by a strong isoQTL but weak/absent eQTL.
