A truly readable “splicing code” is not yet attainable from genome sequence: the same short degenerate motif can be bound by multiple proteins/isoforms, the same protein reads the same sequence differently in different cell types, and machine-learning predictions are limited because bulk tissues superimpose splicing responses of multiple cell types.
splicing-regulatory-elements (SREs) are distinguished from the core splicing signals (5’SS, branchpoint, 3’SS) and are subdivided into ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS depending on exonic vs intronic location and enhancer vs silencer function.
Classic trans-acting splicing-factors are the SR proteins (generally exon-binding, purine-rich, positive) and the hnRNPs (e.g. hnrnpa1, ptbp1/PTB, generally repressive), but loss-of-function profiling shows roughly equal numbers of induced inclusion and skipping events for both classes.
Regulators ultimately act through the core spliceosome — by modulating snRNP assembly/maturation, competing for limiting machinery, blocking the exon-to-intron-definition transition, or modulating U1 dynamics.

Notable claims / data

“Exon definition”: a weak 3’SS is more efficiently recognized when a strong 5’SS lies within ~100-150 nucleotides downstream (U1 snRNP at 5’SS and U2 snRNP at 3’SS mutually enhance recognition).
Most regulatory studies focus on 200-300 nt adjacent to splice sites, but remote/distal regulatory sequences can be just as important (Rbfox RNA “bridges”), implying exome sequencing misses many disease-relevant deep-intronic SRE mutations.
Proteomic crosslinking studies indicate up to ~1,000 proteins can be directly crosslinked to mRNA in HeLa cells or mouse ES cells, many lacking canonical RNA-binding domains.
CLIP requires RNA and protein to be within ~1 Å for UV crosslinking; refinements include PAR-CLIP (higher crosslinking efficiency) and iCLIP (individual-nucleotide resolution). Table 1 catalogs CLIP-derived binding motifs for ~25 regulators (e.g. NOVA YCAY; RBFOX1/2 GCAUG; MBNL1/2 YGCY; PTB CU-rich; srsf1 GGAGGA; hnRNP A1 GNNAGN; U2AF65 pyrimidine-rich).
U1 snRNP is ~10-fold more abundant than other splicing snRNPs in mammalian cells; extended U1-5’SS base-pairing (induced by hnRNP L and hnRNP A1) can act as a repressive complex.
Position-polarity rule (first shown for NOVA): intronic binding upstream of an exon represses inclusion while binding downstream activates inclusion; mechanism still unresolved (tethered RBFOX1 work shows distinct domain requirements for activation vs repression).
hnRNP C directly competes with U2AF65 at Alu-associated 3’SSs to protect the transcriptome from Alu exonization.
Signaling-to-splicing: the Akt-SRPK-SR axis transduces EGF signaling to global splicing changes (RNAi of SRPK1/2 abolishes most EGF-induced events); SR protein phosphorylation is controlled by cytoplasmic SRPKs and nuclear CLKs; Ca2+/CaMK IV regulates splicing via the CaRRE motif and hnRNP L.
Caveat threaded throughout: minigene/reporter and in vitro selection results often fail to generalize because one reporter samples only one regulatory context; affinity purification shows SRE-to-RBP relationships are frequently not one-to-one (direct vs indirect interactions are hard to separate).

Connections

This is an upstream/foundational review for the wiki’s regulatory-biology cluster: it gives mechanistic grounding for rna-binding-proteins, splicing-factors, and splicing-regulatory-elements, and complements the other regulation reviews rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
The “splicing code” framing and its limits connect to computational/ML directions discussed in capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the and to the broader cataloguing of RBP motifs (CLIP/Bind-n-Seq) that underpins tools like spliceai.
Specific entities it discusses already have wiki pages: ptbp1, hnrnpa1, srsf1; the proteome-expansion theme links to nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome.
RNA-structure dependence of RBP access connects to RNA secondary structure / dscam-style competing-structure regulation and to G-quadruplex work in peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.

Open questions

What exactly is the molecular mechanism for the intronic position-polarity effect (why upstream = repress, downstream = activate), and which auxiliary proteins mediate it?
How can multi-RBP, cell-type-specific combinatorial codes be deconvolved from bulk tissue data so that a predictive splicing code becomes feasible?
How much disease-relevant regulatory information lies in distal/deep-intronic SREs that exome sequencing cannot capture, and can CRISPR genome engineering in native context map it?
How are RBPs with multiple jobs (splicing plus transcription/stability/localization, e.g. SRSF2 in Pol II pause release) functionally disentangled?
Do kinetic (vs thermodynamic) binding preferences of RBPs account for regulatory specificity currently missed by standard binding assays?

wiki/sources/fu-2022-rare-coding-variati.title: fu-2022-rare-coding-variation-provides
wiki/sources/fu-2022-rare-coding-variati.content:
Rare coding variation provides insight into the genetic architecture and phenotypic context of autism
One-line: Joint TADA analysis of rare PTVs, missense variants and CNVs across 63,237 individuals discovers 72 ASD genes (373 with developmental-delay meta-analysis) and maps them onto distinct fetal cortical cell types.
Key takeaways

Landmark autism rare-variant study (Autism Sequencing Consortium + Broad-CCDG + iPSYCH-BROAD) integrating protein-truncating, missense and copy-number variants at single-gene/exon resolution to sharpen psychiatric-genetics gene discovery.
Extends the Bayesian TADA model to include CNVs, unaffected-sibling de novo variants and gnomAD constraint (LOEUF), yielding 72 ASD genes at FDR ≤ 0.001 (185 at FDR ≤ 0.05).
Meta-analysis with developmental-delay (DD) cohorts partitions neurodevelopment risk genes along a temporal axis: DD-predominant genes act in less differentiated progenitor/immature neuronal-cell-types (neurogenesis-stage), ASD-predominant genes in maturing excitatory neurons.
ASD-predominant genes overlap schizophrenia-associated genes, supporting shared pathways across neuropsychiatric disorders.
Reinforces that most ASD heritability is common/polygenic, but rare de novo loss-of-function variation drives current gene discovery.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 63,237 ASD-cohort individuals (20,627 affected) + 91,605 DD-cohort samples (31,058 affected); 58.7% of family data previously unpublished.
Association-evidence composition: de novo PTVs 57.5%, damaging missense 21.1%, CNVs 8.44% — but CNVs conferred the greatest relative risk.
Exome CNV discovery (GATK-gCNV) benchmarked vs WGS: 86% sensitivity, 90% PPV for rare CNVs spanning >2 exons; 83% sens/97% PPV for de novo CNVs.
3.95% of ASD cases vs 1.39% of unaffected siblings carried a de novo coding CNV (OR 2.91, P = 2.2×10⁻²¹). De novo deletions of a constrained gene (LOEUF <0.4): 9.33-fold enrichment, comparable to a genomic-disorder segment deletion.
Female cases carried more de novo CNVs than males (6.0% vs 3.5%, OR 1.8) — analyses favor a female protective effect over ascertainment bias.
Maternal parent-of-origin bias for de novo CNVs restricted to NAHR-mediated genomic-disorder loci (16p11.2 ~95% maternal); non-NAHR de novo SVs showed paternal bias.
TADA-NDD joint analysis: 373 genes at FDR ≤ 0.001 (664 at FDR ≤ 0.05); 54 genes unique to the joint analysis (14% yield increase). De novo CNVs hitting a TADA-NDD gene: 134 in ASD cases vs 1 in siblings (OR 48.9).
Estimated cross-phenotype sharing: 70.1% of DD genes affect ASD; 86.6% of ASD genes affect DD. 36 ASD-predominant vs 82 DD-predominant genes (posterior >0.99). Example contrast: ARID1B de novo mutations ~7-fold more frequent in DD than ASD; DSCAM evidence is ASD-specific.
Top genes: SCN2A, SHANK3, CHD8, SYNGAP1, ADNP, PTEN, FOXP1, CHD2, POGZ, GRIN2B; PLXNA1 showed missense overtransmission localized to the Plexin domain.

Connections

Successor to and extends the ASC exome studies (e.g. su-2018-alternative-splicing-in-neurogenesis-and context of neurodevelopmental gene function) and complements the CHD8-focused developmental work in nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8.
Cell-type enrichment framework parallels prenatal cortical single-cell atlases used in velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal and disease-critical-cell-type methods in jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types.
Autism gene/expression context overlaps transcriptomic dysregulation work in gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna.
Constraint/rare-variant risk framing connects to heritability and missing-heritability discussions (manolio-2009, zuk-2012).

Open questions

How much additional ASD signal lies in inherited missense variants and intragenic/exon duplications that larger cohorts could resolve?
Can gene-level CNV analysis be operationalized for clinical diagnostic testing given deletion of a constrained gene rivals genomic-disorder segments?
Which of the pleiotropic ASD/DD genes are truly ASD-specific neurobiological drivers (e.g. DSCAM) versus broadly developmental (e.g. ARID1B)?
What mechanism underlies the maternal bias specific to NAHR-mediated genomic-disorder formation?

wiki/sources/fu-2025-single-cell-and-spa.title: fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative
wiki/sources/fu-2025-single-cell-and-spa.content:
Single cell and spatial alternative splicing analysis with Nanopore long read sequencing
One-line: Introduces longcell, a statistical/computational pipeline for isoform quantification from single-cell and spatially barcoded Nanopore long reads that recovers cell barcodes/UMIs, corrects sequencing/truncation/mapping errors, and models splicing heterogeneity within and between cells/spots.
Key takeaways

Long-read single-cell/spatial splicing analysis is hampered by Nanopore’s low per-base accuracy (85–99%), which corrupts UMI recovery and causes read truncation and misalignment; existing tools that ignore the UMI inflate isoform quantification. Longcell tackles these noise sources head-on.
Core preprocessing identifies and corrects UMI scattering — sequencing errors split observed UMIs from one true molecule into many small/singleton clusters, inflating UMI counts (worse at higher PCR amplification). Longcell clusters putative UMIs within meta-isoform groups, then adaptively prunes small clusters.
Uses the UMI cluster (PCR duplicates of one molecule) to build a consensus alignment, correcting wrongly mapped short internal exons (microexons) and 5’/3’ read truncations before isoform assignment.
Downstream, models per-cell percent-spliced-in (ψ) with a Beta-Binomial framework, decomposing exon-usage variation into intra-cell vs inter-cell heterogeneity via a mean-invariant dispersion metric φ; includes a generalized likelihood-ratio test for differential splicing between cell populations.
To avoid biases from incomplete annotations, analyses are run at the meta-splice-site level (splice sites grouped by co-variation across reads) rather than the isoform level.
Applications: spatial isoform switching (mouse olfactory bulb, CRCLM), widespread high intra-cell heterogeneity in highly expressed genes, and recovery of splicing-factors regulatory targets in a single-cell CRISPR perturbation screen validated by targeted sequencing.

Notable claims / data

Misalignment of short internal constitutive exons depressed bulk ψ to an average of 0.67 across 1,146 exons from 325 genes (read count > 30) in a colorectal metastasis sample; Longcell’s mapping correction raised it to 0.92 (expected ≈1). VPS28-201 example: a 29 bp exon misaligned in 20% of reads.
Simulation benchmark (220 cells, 1000 isoforms, 187 genes): Longcell achieved the highest per-cell Spearman correlation with gold standard (mean 0.76 / 0.80 / 0.82 for low/mid/high-quality data); pairing UMI preprocessing with IsoQuant (Longcell+IsoQuant) gave a 13.73% Spearman improvement over IsoQuant alone.
Full-transcriptome simulation (23,560 isoforms, 918 cells, R10 quality): Longcell+IsoQuant best (mean Spearman 0.87); Longcell and wf-single-cell tied second (0.85).
Library-matched PacBio–Nanopore benchmark (18 genes, 110 isoforms, 5,881 Jurkat cells): Longcell gave most accurate per-cell quantification vs Isoseq cross-validation (mean Spearman 0.43, +8.72% over Sicelore2).
MOB Illumina-guided benchmark: Longcell+IsoQuant best (mean Spearman 0.70, +20% over IsoQuant’s 0.58).
For highly expressed genes, most alternatively spliced exons show low φ (low inter-cell, high intra-cell heterogeneity) — isoforms co-expressed at similar ratio within each cell (e.g. RBIS, ZFAS1 at ~1:1). High-φ exceptions show cell-type-specific switching (MYL6 exon 6; Pkm exon 9 continuous switch along neuroblast→glutamatergic differentiation, Pkm-201→Pkm-202).
Spatial: MOB Plp1 isoform switch between olfactory bulb layers reproduced (312 meta-sites in 224 genes differentially spliced across layers, FDR 0.05); Mapre3 shows per-cell exclusive isoform choice uncorrelated with tissue layer.
CRISPR screen: gRNA library targeting 9 splicing factors in naïve/stimulated Jurkat (Cas9+). Detected 7 meta-sites/5 genes (naïve) and 11 sites/6 genes (stimulated) affected (FDR 0.05). HNRNPLL knockout promoted PTPRC exon 4/6 inclusion (PTPRC-214→PTPRC-209, corroborating known biology); novel: PCBP2→DGUOK (validated by targeted sequencing), CELF2→PTS and CELF2→ARHGEF1. Effect sizes correlated between stimulated/unstimulated (p=0.001).
Practical guidance: a mean PCR amplification fold of ~5 already enables consensus correction of many truncation/mapping errors — shallow amplification recommended.
Caveat: all metrics are reference-based; unannotated isoforms (e.g. inferred VIM-new) can confuse all algorithms — motivation for the meta-splice-site strategy.

Connections

longcell sits in the splicing-methods family alongside event/junction callers like rmats, leafcutter, and psi-sigma, but is specialized for single-cell/spatial long-read data and UMI-aware denoising — a niche the bulk tools and the short-read-oriented li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna (LeafCutter) do not cover.
Like LeafCutter, it adopts an annotation-light, sufficient-statistics philosophy (meta-splice-sites vs intron clusters) and a Beta/Dirichlet-style overdispersion model for differential splicing.
Benchmarked against / complementary to IsoQuant, Sicelore2, FLAMES, wf-single-cell, and PacBio Isoseq (all plain text — not yet wiki entities).
Connects alternative-splicing to splicing-factors regulation via single-cell CRISPR, and to spatial transcriptomics (10x Visium).

Open questions

How well does the Beta-Binomial φ metric scale to genes with very low per-cell coverage, where bootstrap CIs are wide?
Can de novo isoform discovery be integrated without the annotation biases illustrated by the VIM-new example?
What drives intra-layer isoform choice (e.g. Mapre3) that is uncorrelated with spatial domain — a regulatory program orthogonal to tissue identity?
Generalization beyond 10x/Visium and Nanopore R10 to other platforms claimed but not benchmarked here.

wiki/sources/fudenberg-2020-predicting-3.title: fudenberg-2020-predicting-3d-genome-folding
wiki/sources/fudenberg-2020-predicting-3.content:
Predicting 3D genome folding from DNA sequence with Akita
One-line: A convolutional neural network (akita) that predicts locus-specific 3D genome-architecture from ~1 Mb of DNA sequence alone, enabling in-silico mutagenesis and variant interpretation.
Key takeaways

akita transforms ~1 Mb (2^20 bp) of one-hot DNA into predicted Hi-C/Micro-C contact maps at 2,048-bp resolution, learning genome-architecture end-to-end from sequence rather than epigenomic inputs — a sequence-to-structure dna-language-models-style approach that, unlike earlier models, can predict effects of DNA variants.
Learned representations recover an orientation-specific grammar for CTCF sites: inverting all CTCF motifs redistributes rather than abolishes contact patterns, whereas mutagenizing them flattens maps — echoing loop-extrusion halting that underlies topologically-associating-domains and chromatin-loops.
Once trained, predictions run in seconds, enabling saturation mutagenesis, GTEx eQTL interpretation, and prediction of engineered structural variants (model-interpretability).

Notable claims / data

Held-out test-set performance: genome-wide 0.14 MSE, 0.61 Pearson R, 0.56 Spearman R — approaching the noise limit between experimental replicates.
Trained on five human datasets (HFF, H1ESC, GM12878, IMR90, HCT116); multi-task training beat single-dataset models. Training set: 7,008 sequences / 419 validation / 413 test.
CTCF motif mutagenesis had >3x the impact of any other JASPAR motif except CTCFL; 19.9% of high-impact single-nucleotide mutations fell outside standard annotation categories, indicating uncharacterized folding-relevant sequence.
SNPs with higher GTEx causal posterior probability showed significantly larger predicted disruptions, both inside and outside CTCF motifs.
Recapitulated a CRISPR/Cas9 ~25 kb boundary deletion at the Lmo2 locus (redundant CTCF sites) and a ~622 kb inversion at the Eph4A locus; cross-species, a human-trained model predicted mouse folding (median Spearman R 0.50), improving when B2 SINE CTCF sites were masked.

Connections

Directly extends the sequence-to-structure modeling lineage; complements che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome on how genome-architecture is organized and evolves.
Shares CNN-on-DNA methodology with sequence models of the dna-language-models family and offers model-interpretability via in-silico mutagenesis, analogous to interpretation methods used for splicing-code models.

Open questions

How to extend predictions beyond the ~1 Mb window to model A/B compartmentalization and inter-chromosomal contacts?
Can architectures that better encode loop-extrusion biophysics improve accuracy and cell-type specificity?
What are the CTCF-independent DNA determinants (the ~20% of high-impact non-annotated variants) of genome folding?

wiki/sources/gallo-2014-glial-developmen.title: gallo-2014-glial-development-crossroads-regeneration
wiki/sources/gallo-2014-glial-developmen.content:
Glial Development: The Crossroads of Regeneration and Repair in the CNS
One-line: A review arguing that the signaling programs governing developmental astrocyte and oligodendrocyte generation are reused during CNS injury repair and regeneration.
Key takeaways

Glia comprise at least 50% of CNS cells and participate in all injury/disease responses; because regeneration often recapitulates development, developmental gliogenesis illuminates glial repair, complementing our knowledge of neurodevelopment and neurogenesis.
Astrocyte specification hinges on a Sox9/NFIA transcriptional cascade at the “gliogenic switch” (best defined in ventral spinal cord), downstream of Shh and Notch; differentiation is marked by GFAP induction driven by CNTF/LIF/CT-1–gp130–JAK/STAT3, plus Notch and BMP signaling.
Reactive astrocytes (GFAP+, hypertrophic, proliferative) are neither simply “good” nor “bad”: the glial scar walls off damage yet inhibits axon regrowth via CSPGs; ablation studies show they are required for repair.
Oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs) arise from multiple ventral (pMN/Olig2) and dorsal domains in spinal cord and sequential forebrain waves (MGE→LGE→cortex); postnatal SVZ and parenchymal OPCs regenerate myelinating OLs after injury.
Distinct glial subtypes reflect embryonic patterning, echoing how developmental origin shapes neuronal-cell-types; astrocyte lineage stages remain poorly defined for lack of markers.

Notable claims / data

Glia-to-neuron ratio: ~1:6 in C. elegans (56 glia : 352 neurons) vs 1:1–4:1 in vertebrates; a single hippocampal astrocyte contacts ~100,000 synapses (Bushong et al., 2002).
Up to 55% of early postnatal cortical GFAP+ astrocytes are generated after migrating out of germinal centers (Ge et al., 2012).
Nine morphologically distinct adult astrocyte classes identified via GFAP/s100b reporters (Emsley & Macklis, 2006).
SVZ OPC proliferation after hypoxia depends on Cdk4; parenchymal/WM OPC proliferation depends on Cdk2/CyclinE (G1-S transition); dorsally derived OPCs generate 10–50% of dorsal OLs.
SCI conditional KOs of BMPR1a vs BMPR1b have opposing effects on scar formation, gliosis, and recovery; STAT3 deletion in astrocytes impairs scar formation and functional recovery.
Caveat: reactive-astrocyte “NSC-like” neurogenic potential rests on in vitro assays prone to Shh-Olig2 deregulation; in vivo lineage tracing still needed. Review excludes microglia (distinct developmental origin).

Connections

Extends developmental frameworks in stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain and duan-2008-development-of-neural-stem toward the glial (non-neuronal) lineage.
Complements su-2018-alternative-splicing-in-neurogenesis-and on molecular control of neural cell-fate transitions.
Relevant to radial-glia as the shared neural-stem-cell origin feeding both neurogenesis and gliogenesis.

Open questions

Do reactive astrocytes genuinely dedifferentiate into neurogenic NSCs in vivo, or only in culture?
How do injury type, location, and astrocyte heterogeneity jointly dictate beneficial vs detrimental repair outcomes?
Which developmental OPC-proliferation pathways (Cdk2/Cdk4, Wnt, p27Kip1, EGF) are the best therapeutic levers for remyelination after perinatal white matter injury?

wiki/sources/gandal-2022-broad-transcrip.title: gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across
wiki/sources/gandal-2022-broad-transcrip.content:
Broad transcriptomic dysregulation occurs across the cerebral cortex in ASD
One-line: Pan-cortical bulk RNA-seq (725 samples, 11 cortical areas) plus snRNA-seq and methylation profiling showing that autism’s molecular pathology is widespread across the cortex — not confined to frontotemporal association areas — follows an anterior-to-posterior gradient peaking in primary visual cortex, attenuates normal regional identity, and reflects cell-type-specific (mainly excitatory-neuron and glial) gene and isoform dysregulation onto which rare and common ASD risk converge.
Key takeaways

Bulk rna-seq of 725 brain samples spanning 11 cortical areas from 112 post-mortem donors (49 idiopathic ASD, 54 neurotypical controls, plus dup15q cases) — a >3-fold increase in samples and regions over prior frontotemporal studies. Extends the developing/diseased-brain transcriptomics program of the lab (brain-development-neurogenomics).
ASD shows widespread transcriptomic change across the entire cortex with an anterior-to-posterior gradient: the greatest differences are in primary visual cortex (BA17), coincident with attenuation of the normal transcriptomic differences between cortical regions (attenuation of regional identity, “ARI”).
Splitting differential expression by feature type reveals distinct transcript- (isoform-) level signals beyond gene-level signals, with transcript changes of greater effect-size magnitude — supporting a substantial role for alternative-splicing and isoform expression in ASD (splicing-and-disease). Co-expression networks built on isoform quantifications “reflect both alternative splicing and alternative promoter usage.”
Single-nucleus RNA-seq (>250,000 nuclei, 6 ASD / 6 control, frontal/parietal/occipital) and methylation cell-type deconvolution show the bulk signature is driven mainly by within-cell-type gene-expression changes (especially excitatory neurons and glia), not by shifts in cell-type proportions.
Rare and common ASD genetic risk converge on a downregulated synaptic co-expression module (GeneM5), while common variation alone is enriched in an upregulated protein-chaperone/heat-shock isoform module (IsoformM37) — implicating protein homeostasis in ASD.
Directly extends and replicates parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna: GeneM5 overlaps the previously identified downregulated synaptic module CTX.M16, and dup15q again shows a concordant-but-larger-magnitude version of the idiopathic ASD signature.

Notable claims / data

4,223 genes and 9,474 transcripts differentially expressed (DE) cortex-wide at FDR < 0.05 (linear mixed model).
Consistent ASD signature across all 11 regions with highly concordant effect sizes vs the whole-cortex signature; BA17 (primary visual cortex) had the most DE genes (3,264), 59% overlapping the whole-cortex set.
snRNA-seq: 26 cell clusters; cell-type-specific DE shows the same regional gradient — 3–4× more DE genes in occipital/parietal cell types than PFC (23,144 / 17,042 / 5,235 DE genes respectively across clusters at FDR < 0.001), the majority from excitatory neurons (occipital excitatory neurons strongest overall).
Cell-composition shifts are subtle: only nominal increases in astrocytes (AST1) and decreases in lower-layer excitatory neurons (EX7/EX8); none survived FDR. Methylation CTD (n = 36/group) gave high log2FC concordance with vs without proportion covariates (Spearman ρ > 0.7), so proportion changes do not explain the bulk signature.
24 gene co-expression modules (9 down, 15 up in ASD) plus 61 isoform modules (39 non-overlapping with gene modules); 38 modules up/down in ≥1 region, 18 dysregulated cortex-wide. 90 of 96 gene+isoform modules enriched among cell-type-specific DE genes.
Cortex-wide modules: GeneM9 (up, neuronal, non-coding-enriched); GeneM32 (up, reactive astrocytes); GeneM24 (down, endothelial/pericyte, blood-brain-barrier). GeneM5 (down) carries synaptic-vesicle/cytoskeleton/plasticity genes, is enriched for ASD common variants AND rare de novo protein-disrupting mutations (e.g. GRIN2A, MYO5A, BTRC), and overlaps Parikshak et al. CTX.M16. IsoformM37 (up) is enriched for ASD common variants only and carries heat-shock/protein-folding genes (HSP family).
13 regionally-variable modules all track normal anterior-posterior expression gradients; none enriched for ASD risk. BA17-specific examples: GeneM30 (OPC; hubs SOX4, SOX11) and GeneM4 (inhibitory-neuron; e.g. SCN9A).
Caveat: single post-mortem snapshot across ages 2-68 and both sexes cannot establish causality or distinguish primary from compensatory changes; snRNA-seq has few unique donors and poor transcript-level quantitation vs bulk; whether attenuated regional identity is a developmental arealization defect or a consequence of altered circuit/synaptic homeostasis remains open.

Connections

Direct successor to parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (same lab) — scales the frontotemporal ASD cortex transcriptome to a pan-cortical map, reuses the regional-identity-attenuation and dup15q-concordance framework, and recovers the synaptic-downregulation module (CTX.M16 → GeneM5).
Splicing/isoform sibling for splicing-and-disease and alternative-splicing: transcript-level dysregulation exceeds gene-level signal and isoform modules capture splicing + promoter usage; consistent with splicing as an independent axis of ASD molecular pathology.
Core human psychiatric-genetics node for the brain-development-neurogenomics hub — links cortical arealization, cell-type-specific expression, and ASD common+rare risk convergence.
Uses bulk and single-nucleus rna-seq plus methylation-based deconvolution as complementary modalities.

Open questions

Is attenuation of regional identity (ARI) a primary early-developmental arealization defect or a downstream consequence of altered local circuit/synaptic homeostasis propagated across cortex?
Which transcript/splicing-level changes are causal vs compensatory, and how do ASD risk genes mechanistically reshape cortical patterning and connectivity?
Why is primary visual cortex (BA17) the epicenter — does it relate to the pervasive sensory-processing differences in ASD (now in DSM-5)?
How would pan-cortical, cell-type-specific genomic profiling during early human development, or regionally-patterned organoids, test the developmental-arealization hypothesis?

wiki/sources/gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-.title: gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements
wiki/sources/gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-.content:
CRISPR/Cas9-Mediated Scanning for Regulatory Elements Required for HPRT1 Expression via Thousands of Large, Programmed Genomic Deletions
One-line: Introduces scandel, a paired-gRNA CRISPR/Cas9 method that programs thousands of overlapping kilobase-scale genomic deletions tiling a locus, and applies it (with an individual-gRNA comparison and long-read validation) to a 206 kb region around HPRT1 to ask whether any distal regulatory element is required for expression — finding none.
Key takeaways

ScanDel programs kilobase-scale deletions by delivering pairs of gRNAs per cell (each gRNA specifying one deletion boundary), so each base pair of a target locus is covered with high redundancy — overcoming the sparse, uneven coverage and small (1–10 bp) NHEJ indels of conventional single-gRNA noncoding CRISPR screens (which here was the method tested as a comparison; “crispr-screen” / “pooled-crispr-screen” have no wiki page).
Applied to HPRT1 (the housekeeping gene underlying X-linked Lesch-Nyhan syndrome) in near-haploid HAP1 cells, using 6-thioguanine selection for loss of HPRT function as a clean functional readout — all nine HPRT1 exons scored strongly, confirming sensitivity, while no distal intergenic or deep-intronic noncoding-variants region was required.
Concludes that no singular distal cis-regulatory element is required for HPRT1 expression in this assay, suggesting distal mutations are unlikely to contribute substantially to Lesch-Nyhan syndrome burden and that proximal sequences near the TSS/5′UTR suffice.
Apparent positive signal at exon-proximal noncoding sequence (e.g. the promoter, overlapping an ATAC-seq open-chromatin hotspot around exon 1) was shown by long-read (PacBio) genotyping to be an artifact: rare, imprecise “on-target-but-wrong-boundary” deletions that extended into exons drove the selection, not loss of a regulatory element.
Demonstrates a key cautionary lesson for the field: noncoding CRISPR screens (paired- or single-gRNA) need direct, long-read genotyping of edited loci rather than relying solely on indirect counting of integrated gRNA sequences.

Notable claims / data

Library: 4,342 overlapping 1 kb and 2 kb programmed deletions tiling 206 kb centered on HPRT1 (chrX:133,507,694–133,713,798, hg19; 87 kb upstream + 79 kb downstream), with median 27-fold redundancy per base pair.
Construct: paired 20 bp spacers synthesized in cis on a microarray, cloned into lentiGuide-Puro with two PolIII promoters (a U6-H1 design); each gRNA expressed from its own promoter to enable direct sequencing-based quantification. U6-H1 outperformed U6-U6 (20% vs 10% of reads showing the programmed deletion 1 week post-transduction).
Caveat on efficiency: the programmed deletion occurred in only a minority of edited cells (in a test, 32% of edited cells carried the intended deletion; the rest had indels at one/both cut sites retaining the intervening sequence) — handled by redundant independent gRNA pairs, self-pair controls, and negative controls (330 negative-control pairs).
Selection score = log10(after-6TG / before-6TG) normalized read counts; per-base-pair score = mean over all deletions spanning that base; replicate Pearson 0.708. Selection used 5 µM 6TG for 7 days; ≥2,000× library coverage maintained.
Comparison individual-gRNA screen: 12,151 gRNAs over the same region covered only ~70% of bases (PAM sparsity, vs ScanDel’s median 27×); noncoding replicate correlation was poor (Pearson 0.156) vs strong exonic signal (0.781); 86% of exon-targeting gRNAs positively selected. Positively selected gRNAs were enriched near exons (2% intergenic vs 7.5% deep-intronic vs 20.5% proximal-intronic), again traced to exon-extending edits.
HPRT1 has no GTEx eQTLs and sits at the terminus of a ~300 kb topologically associated locus extending ~185 kb beyond the surveyed window — a stated caveat that a critical element could lie outside the interrogated region.
Early episomal reporter-assay studies of HPRT flanking sequence identified a few proximal critical elements; the authors note this discrepancy may reflect cell-type/species differences or genuine differences between episomal vs endogenous regulation (redundancy in the genome).

Connections

A genome-editing, endogenous-context counterpart to episomal massively-parallel-reporter-assay approaches for functionally validating cis-regulatory-elements; complements MPRA-based variant-function work such as deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory, groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at, and zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory, which pair candidate-CRE accessibility maps with allele-resolved reporter assays — where ScanDel instead perturbs the native locus.
Shares the chromatin-accessibility candidate-element framing (ATAC-seq hotspots nominate the HPRT1 promoter) but shows the functionally essential region is only a small part of the broader open-chromatin peak.
The new method is captured as entity scandel.

Open questions

Would a more quantitative readout (e.g. GFP knock-in + FACS stratification by expression) reveal subtle distal regulatory elements that strong 6TG selection misses?
Is the absence of distal requirement specific to housekeeping genes / differentiated HAP1 cells — would developmentally regulated genes, or editing in stem cells before differentiation, behave differently?
Could redundancy among distal elements hide individual requirements, addressable only by combinatorial multi-deletion perturbations or larger/wider tiling libraries?

New concepts referenced (no wiki page yet)

crispr-screen, pooled-crispr-screen, genome-editing (written as plain text per inventory rule).

wiki/sources/gebauer-2020-rna-binding-pr.title: gebauer-2020-rna-binding-proteins-in-human-genetic
wiki/sources/gebauer-2020-rna-binding-pr.content:
RNA-binding proteins in human genetic disease
One-line: A broad review of how mutations in rna-binding-proteins (RBPs) cause human Mendelian and somatic genetic disease, integrating an updated catalogue of ~3,470 human RBPs with disease-association data and emphasizing disordered regions, phase transitions, and emerging RBP-targeted therapeutics.
Key takeaways

RBPs are critical effectors of gene expression, regulating all steps of RNA life including transcription, splicing, modification, trafficking, translation, and decay. Conversely RNA can regulate RBPs (“riboregulation”, e.g. PKR activation by dsRNA).
Unbiased RNA-interactome capture methods have pushed the candidate human RBP count into the four-digit range; the authors assemble a superset of 3,470 RBPs (2,650 detected in ≥2 RIC studies plus previously annotated ones). Only ~25% of these harbour computationally predictable RNA-binding domains (RBDs).
“enigmRBPs” / unconventional RBPs lack discernible RBDs but frequently contain intrinsically disordered regions or mono-/dinucleotide-binding domains; many have cell-biological functions unrelated to RNA (links between intermediary metabolism and RNA regulation).
Crossing the RBP superset with Open Targets disease data: nearly one-third (1,054 RBPs) are mutated in disease, accounting for >20% of all proteins with annotated mutations (vs 10% for transcription factors). Disease-associated RBPs are enriched for metabolism and nervous-system development functions.
Nervous-system disease is the top Mendelian disorder category affected by RBP mutations, with a clear prevalence of RBPs lacking classic RBDs. For somatic mutations the pattern inverts and transcription factors lead (notably in cell-proliferation/cancer). Relevant to splicing-and-neurodevelopment.
RBP mutations frequently fall outside RBDs (in other domains, low-complexity regions, and even UTRs of RBP transcripts), pointing to post-transcriptional de-regulation and cross-regulatory RBP networks.
RBPs, long considered “undruggable”, are emerging as therapeutic targets: see splicing-therapeutics.

Notable claims / data

Integrated candidate RBP count across human cell types: 4,257; stringent superset used for analysis: 3,470; disease-associated: 1,054.
Disease mechanisms (Fig 4): altered expression/isoform ratios; truncation/amino-acid changes altering cofactor/RNA/metabolite binding (e.g. HuR–UDP-glucose, SNAI1 mRNA in cancer); altered enzymatic activity (eIF2B in vanishing white matter, OMIM #603896); mislocalization/aggregation (FUS); and mutations in RBP binding sites (FTL iron-responsive element, hyperferritinaemia-cataract, OMIM #600886).
Phase separation & neurodegeneration: disordered, prion-like, low-complexity regions drive liquid–liquid phase separation; aberrant transitions to solid aggregates underlie neurodegenerative disease. FUS has a QGSY-rich prion-like domain plus RGG R-rich regions; Tyr–Arg interactions govern de-mixing, and mutations accelerate amyloid aggregation/neuronal toxicity (ALS, FTD).
Fragile X (FMR1/FMRP): CGG repeat expansions in the 5′ UTR — 5–44 normal; 55–200 (premutation) → FXTAS via RNA “sinks” and RAN translation of toxic polyglycine peptides; >230 → hypermethylation, gene silencing, FXS. FMRP has two KH domains + RGG; the Ile304Asn KH2 mutation causes FXS at normal repeat number.
splicing-factors in disease: RBM10 (TARP syndrome, OMIM #311900; tumour suppressor/oncogene in lung/colorectal/pancreatic/bladder cancers; regulates NUMB splicing, cross-regulates RBM5). PRP8/PRPF8 and PRP31 — core spliceosome components whose mutations cause retinitis pigmentosa, despite being needed for every intron (PRP8 disrupts BRR2 interaction → ULK1 mis-splicing → impaired mitophagy).
Therapeutics: spliceostatin/pladienolide B/GEX1 inhibit splicing via the SF3B1–PHF5A interface (antitumour); SF3B1 itself is frequently mutated in haematological malignancies (cryptic 3′ splice site activation). ASO nusinersen treats spinal muscular atrophy by masking an hnRNP A1/A2 intronic splicing silencer to promote SMN2 exon 7 inclusion; small molecules also stabilize U1 snRNP recognition of the SMN2 5′ splice site. Familial dysautonomia (IKBKAP intron-20 +6 mutation; OMIM #223900) is targeted by kinetin, EGCG, and RECTAS.

Connections

Core extension of rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors into the disease domain; the strongest topical sibling among existing sources is scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (both center on disease-causing splicing/RNA-processing defects) and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
The therapeutics section (SF3B1 modulators, nusinersen/ASOs, splice-site-stabilizing small molecules) directly grounds splicing-therapeutics.
Nervous-system enrichment of disease RBPs and the FMRP/Fragile X material connect to splicing-and-neurodevelopment.

Open questions

What are the physiological roles, complexes, and dynamics of the >1,000 “disease RBPs”? Pathomechanistic understanding is lacking for nearly all.
How do specific RBP mutations map to phenotypic outcomes mechanistically, and which target RNAs do these RBPs recognize?
What is the true scope of riboregulation, and do disease mutations in newly recognized (RBD-less) RBPs act via RNA binding (e.g. HSD17B10 binding mitochondrial pre-tRNAs)?
Can structural data guide drug development, and can ASOs reliably reach intracellular targets at therapeutic concentrations across tissues?

wiki/sources/gillani-2025-rare-germline-.title: gillani-2025-rare-germline-structural-variants
wiki/sources/gillani-2025-rare-germline-.content:
Rare germline structural variants increase risk for pediatric solid tumors
One-line: Germline WGS of 9373 individuals shows rare structural variants — including sex-biased megabase copy-number changes and noncoding TAD-boundary disruptions — are risk factors for pediatric solid tumors, explaining an estimated 1.1–5.6% of liability.
Key takeaways

Rare germline structural variants (SVs) are a meaningful, previously understudied contributor to the missing-heritability of pediatric solid tumors, beyond the SNVs/indels that prior studies focused on.
Very large (>1 Mb) unbalanced (copy-number-altering) germline SVs are a strongly sex-biased risk factor, with a ~4-fold male enrichment; effect concentrated in males (pan-cancer OR = 3.65) with no significant female enrichment.
Ultrarare noncoding-variants — specifically SVs disrupting chromatin domain boundaries (topologically-associating-domains) — were enriched in neuroblastoma but not Ewing sarcoma, implicating extragenic mechanisms.
Signal is strongest in neuroblastoma: singleton loss-of-function deletions of mutationally constrained, tissue-expressed genes drove risk.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: harmonized short-read germline WGS (median 24×) from 9373 QC-passing individuals: 1765 pediatric cancer patients (Ewing sarcoma n=789, neuroblastoma n=690, osteosarcoma n=286), 943 unaffected parents, 6665 adult controls (MESA + BioMe); 1000 Genomes trios for ancestry/benchmarking.
SV discovery/genotyping/annotation with GATK-SV (existing ensemble pipeline). 284,384 germline SVs; median 7275 per genome; 84.0% rare (AF <1%); median SV size 281 bp.
63 high-confidence de novo SVs; de novo rate (~0.2/genome) not elevated vs population; de novo SVs 4.6× larger and 4.2-fold more gene-disruptive than inherited SVs.
Large (>1 Mb) unbalanced SVs: pan-cancer OR = 2.31 (95% CI 1.48–3.59, P = 2.0×10⁻⁴); no association for balanced SVs (inversions/translocations), showing the effect is dosage-specific.
Category-wide association study (CWAS): neuroblastoma had 29 Bonferroni-significant categories (e.g. singleton LoF deletions of constrained genes OR = 1.81); Ewing sarcoma had none at Bonferroni (only nominal).
Clinically relevant hits: PHOX2B, BARD1 (neuroblastoma), Fanconi anemia genes (Ewing). Estimated rare-SV liability contribution 1.1–5.6%.
Caveat: association study on matched adult controls (not disease-free children); enrichment persisted after excluding 621 known cancer genes, suggesting undiscovered genes / genome-instability mechanisms.

Connections

Directly addresses the missing-heritability problem framed by manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of and zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability, extending it to rare structural (not just common) variation in cancer predisposition.
Complements marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding on the disease relevance of rare noncoding variants; here noncoding SVs act via topologically-associating-domains boundary disruption.

Open questions

Which specific genes/loci drive the sex-biased large-SV risk, and is the male bias due to X-linked dosage, chromosomal instability, or tissue-specific selection?
Do TAD-boundary–disrupting SVs act by rewiring enhancer–oncogene contacts in neuroblastoma, and why is this absent in Ewing sarcoma?
Can germline SV burden be operationalized into clinical predisposition screening for pediatric solid tumors?

wiki/sources/giudice-2024-splicing-regul.title: giudice-2024-splicing-regulation-through-biomolecular-condensates
wiki/sources/giudice-2024-splicing-regul.content:
Splicing regulation through biomolecular condensates and membraneless organelles
One-line: A review of how biomolecular condensates (membraneless organelles formed by liquid–liquid phase separation, LLPS) act as spatio-temporal regulatory platforms for splicing — and conversely how alternative splicing tunes condensate formation — with emphasis on nuclear speckles, co-transcriptional splicing condensates, and disease.
Key takeaways

Biomolecular condensates (a.k.a. membraneless organelles, MLOs) form through weak, multivalent protein/nucleic-acid interactions, often via liquid–liquid phase separation (LLPS). They span a continuous material spectrum from dynamic liquid droplets through gels to solid amyloid aggregates, and their main role is modulating the rate of biochemical reactions, providing a layer of spatio-temporal control over splicing that is distinct from the well-studied biochemistry.
rna-binding-proteins (RBPs) — the main splicing-factors — are enriched in intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs) and low-complexity (LC) domains; these, plus multivalent RNA-binding domains and post-translational modifications (PTMs), drive their phase separation. Organizing/activating RBPs within condensates is emerging as a crucial regulatory layer for splice-site choice and outcomes.
Reciprocal feedback: alternative-splicing of RBPs itself tunes their phase-separation/aggregation propensity. Alternatively spliced exons are enriched for IDR-encoding sequence, so inclusion/skipping fine-tunes condensate formation (e.g. HNRNPA/HNRNPD Gly-Tyr-rich exons are constitutively included in non-mammals but alternatively spliced in mammals, expanding mammalian regulatory capacity).
Nuclear speckles are the most prominent splicing-associated nuclear body; whether they are active splicing sites or storage hubs is still debated. An ‘interface splicing’ model (SRSF1 inside / HNRNPA1 in the outer layer) positions exon–intron junctions at the speckle–nucleoplasm interface where spliceosomes act — supported by super-resolution imaging (‘intra-organelle RNA organization’).
cotranscriptional-splicing converges with condensates: transcription-initiation condensates (transcription factors, Mediator, Pol II) transition to co-transcriptional splicing condensates upon Pol II CTD phosphorylation. Genome organization places highly transcribed regions near speckles, and proximity to speckles raises splicing efficiency (‘economy of scale’).
The spliceosome and its components are more enriched on pre-mRNAs of genes close to nuclear speckles; directing a reporter pre-mRNA to speckles is sufficient to increase its splicing efficiency.
Dysregulated condensate-mediated splicing underlies neurodegenerative disease (TDP43, HNRNPA1/A2B1, FUS in ALS), Rett syndrome (MeCP2–RBFOX co-condensation), cancers (AKAP95, USP42, RBM10), cardiomyopathy (RBM20), and nuclear ‘speckleopathies’ (SON, NKAP, RBM10).

Notable claims / data



95% of human genes are alternatively spliced; most splicing occurs co-transcriptionally.


RBFOX1 needs a Tyr-rich LC domain to form high-order assemblies with the LASR complex; Tyr mutations block assembly (not LASR binding) yet abolish splicing function, linking high-order assembly to function.
AKAP95 (AKAP8): an IDR (residues 101–210) forms liquid-like droplets essential for splicing and tumorigenesis; a Tyr-to-Phe IDR mutation hardens condensates and impairs splicing — biophysical (not just on/off) properties matter.
HNRNPDL isoforms: HNRNPDL1 (both Arg- and Tyr-rich IDRs) phase-separates; HNRNPDL2 (Tyr-rich only) forms amyloid (cryo-EM: stable, non-toxic, RNA-binding fibrils; core encoded by alt exon 6); HNRNPDL3 (neither) is soluble. D378H/N mutations cause limb-girdle muscular dystrophy by accelerating aggregation.
TDP43: alternative 5′ss usage produces short isoforms lacking the Gly-rich LC domain (gain a nuclear export signal); these aggregate cytoplasmically, sequester full-length TDP43, lack splicing capacity, and accumulate in ALS neurons/glia under hyperexcitability. K84 acetylation blocks nuclear import; K136 acetylation impairs RNA binding (reversed by sirtuin 1); A315T/A315E favor irreversible pathogenic aggregation.
CPEB4: neuron-specific microexon 4 (24 nt) tunes condensate reversibility; low inclusion is linked to idiopathic autism spectrum disorder and schizophrenia (microexon-skipping → irreversible aggregation; based partly on a non-peer-reviewed preprint).
U2AF65 + CAPERα (RBM39) + SF3B1 + pyrimidine-rich RNA cooperatively enhance condensation; depletion enhances inclusion of alt exons preceded by pyrimidine-rich sequences — a model for condensation-driven splice-site selection.
Genes near nuclear speckles correspond to genomic regions with high Pol II occupancy; the effect is ‘regional’ (overall regional transcription, not the single gene’s own rate) — Bhat et al. Nature 2024.
Two open caveats stressed throughout (Box 2): almost all evidence is from cell culture / in vitro; there is a lack of rigorous in vivo evidence that MLOs are crucial for cell biology, and ‘phase separation’ is arguably overused.

Connections

Adds a spatial/biophysical mechanistic layer to splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins — explains how the same RBPs (SR proteins vs HNRNPs, with antagonistic exon vs intron binding) are organized in space to regulate splice-site choice.
Tightly coupled to cotranscriptional-splicing: Pol II CTD phosphorylation hands transcripts from transcription-initiation condensates to splicing condensates; speckle proximity governs efficiency. Sibling source choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of covers the co- vs post-transcriptional timing axis and nuclear speckles from a complementary angle (retained/detained introns near speckles).
The spliceosome is recruited and concentrated at speckle interfaces; condensate biophysics modulate where/when assembly and catalysis occur.
Disease links overlap with splicing-and-disease sources: ALS/FTD (TDP43, FUS), muscular dystrophy, autism/schizophrenia (CPEB4), cancers, cardiomyopathy, and developmental ‘speckleopathies’.
Entities already in the wiki that recur here: srsf1, hnrnpa1, ptbp1, sf3b1 — this review supplies their phase-separation context.

Open questions

How to rigorously link condensate formation/properties to splicing function in vivo, given the absence of a method to selectively dissolve specific protein condensates without perturbing other activities?
How are intricate heterotypic MLO substructures built (most in vitro work uses single homotypic proteins, often without RNA)?
How is splicing regulated through condensates amid dynamic genome reorganization in health and disease (e.g. RBM20 splicing foci during cardiogenesis; GATA4 in congenital heart disease)?
Can splicing be therapeutically modulated by pharmacologically targeting condensates (e.g. SMNDC1 Tudor-domain inhibitors that alter nuclear-speckle architecture)?

wiki/sources/gold-2026-scoring-gene-impo.title: gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting
wiki/sources/gold-2026-scoring-gene-impo.content:
Scoring gene importance by interpreting single-cell foundation models
One-line: SIGnature scores each gene’s functional importance to a single cell by computing gradient-based attributions over the latent embeddings of pretrained scRNA-seq single-cell-foundation-models, yielding a standardized, cross-dataset metric that beats expression-based signature scoring and surfaces shared disease mechanisms.
Key takeaways

Reframes gene importance as a model-interpretability problem: applies explainable-AI attribution methods (integrated gradients, input×gradient, DeepLIFT) to single-cell-foundation-models to measure each gene’s contribution to a cell’s position in latent space. Attribution magnitude need not track expression (e.g. high for the TF FOXP3, low for the abundant transcript MALAT1).
Attributions emphasize regulatory/marker genes (including lowly-expressed transcription factors), deemphasize ribosomal/mitochondrial genes, and are robust to technical artifacts (sequencing depth, UMI counts, 50% dropout → 93% top-gene overlap), enabling comparison across experiments against a single standardized FM embedding.
Shipped as the SIGnature Python package (built on Captum) supporting a suite of FMs and explainability methods; in-depth analyses use the SCimilarity FM + integrated gradients. Attributions are precomputed and queried as fast vector operations.
Demonstrated by querying the ~100-gene MS1 monocyte signature (severe-COVID-19/sepsis-linked) across atlas-scale data, revealing MS1 activation in three previously unassociated hyperinflammatory diseases: Kawasaki disease (KD), severe fever with thrombocytopenia syndrome (SFTS), and hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH).
Generated a testable hypothesis that was experimentally validated: serum from KD patients induces the MS1 phenotype in vitro (emergency myelopoiesis, increased HLA-DRlow monocytes).

Notable claims / data

Scores 22 million cells in minutes (vs hours/days for conventional methods); the MS1 query ran across 412 human disease studies, focusing on 2.3M predicted monocytes/macrophages (top 10% MS1 score = “MS1-like”).
Signature-scoring benchmark: mean attributions achieved the highest F1 in 23/32 cell-type prediction tests, beating background-based (ANS, Scanpy) and rank-based (JASMINE, UCell) methods. CD8+ signature: attributions cleanly separate CD8+ from CD4+ T cells across studies.
Cross-study NMF: 90% of attribution configs recovered a Treg-associated factor (98% with FOXP3 in top-10 loadings) vs 58%/4% for expression-based NMF. Benefit is FM-representation-dependent: helps for well-represented cell types (lung/HLCA) but not poorly-represented ones (corneal epithelium).
Benchmarking: seven FMs (scFoundation, scGPT, SCimilarity, scTab-SSL + variants, scVI-CZI) × three XAI methods; MLP-based FMs much faster than transformers; cell-type-label models (SCimilarity, fine-tuned scTab) best boost marker-gene importance.
KD case: MS1-like cells significantly decrease after IVIG (Wilcoxon P<0.01); expression-based consensus NMF independently recovers a KD MS1 factor (S100A9, RETN). IgG alone did not change MS1 induction → IVIG mechanism remains unclear.
Caveats stated: attributions are interpretive (not ideal for clustering), have less-interpretable units than raw counts, and inherit the quality/biases of the underlying FM and XAI method.

Connections

artificial-intelligence, ai-in-genomics, foundation-models — this work is about INTERPRETING single-cell foundation models via attribution, not training a new generative FM.
New concepts: single-cell-foundation-models, model-interpretability (XAI/attribution methods: IG, IxG, DeepLIFT, contrasted with perturbation-based SHAP/LIME).
Tool entity: signature-tool (the SIGnature Python package).
Sibling single-cell methods in the wiki: CMonge and TxPert (perturbation/response modeling) — SIGnature is complementary, providing interpretation/feature-attribution rather than perturbation prediction; relies on existing FMs (SCimilarity, scGPT, scVI, scTab, scFoundation).

Open questions

How well do attributions transfer to FMs/cell types poorly represented in training data, and can task-specific FMs improve them?
What is the mechanism by which IVIG reduces MS1-like cells in KD, given IgG alone showed no effect?
Can the framework extend robustly to perturbation-based explainers (SHAP via gene masking) and other modalities (chromatin accessibility, spatial/multimodal omics)?
How should attribution units (a complex transformation of expression) be made more directly interpretable for biologists?

wiki/sources/groover-2026-mapping-cis-re.title: groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at
wiki/sources/groover-2026-mapping-cis-re.content:
Mapping cis-regulatory mutations at scale in sorghum enables modulation of gene expression
One-line: A protoplast-based massively-parallel-reporter-assay (MPRA) measures the effects of >30,000 CRISPR-mimicking mutations across native promoters and 5′ UTRs of three sorghum photosynthesis genes (PsbS, Raf1, SBPase), yielding base-pair-resolution maps of gene-specific cis-regulatory control and compact, nontransgenic edits that strongly boost protein output.
Key takeaways

Gene expression is most tunable within a ~500-bp “core promoter” window around the TSS (roughly −400 bp onward); the distal promoter (<−400 bp) shows low mutational variance and low replicate reproducibility — an instance of cis-regulatory-grammar.
Mutation effects in the core promoter are reproducible across biological replicates (Pearson r=0.68 overall; r=0.75 for deletions, r=0.62 for insertions) and predictive of protein output (r=0.80 vs orthogonal nanoluciferase assay).
Compact deletions and motif insertions can strongly activate expression (>30-fold over WT), outperforming a 585-bp industry-standard transgenic enhancer (3×ENH).
Mutation–effect relationships are gene specific: the same heuristic does not transfer across PsbS, Raf1 and SBPase — arguing for tailored, per-gene regulatory maps.
A fine-tuned genomic language model (GPN) gives only modest variant-effect prediction (best for disruptive 10–15-bp deletions, r=0.76; weak for activating insertions, r=0.39) and fails to capture hypermorphic effects, underscoring the value of direct MPRA measurement.

Notable claims / data



30,000 mutations total across 3 genes (~10,000/gene): PsbS ~10,096, Raf1 ~7,796, SBPase ~13,974.


Mutation classes emulate CRISPR editing outcomes: deletions (~3,398/library), A>G/T>C substitutions emulating base editors (~4,858/library), and cis-genic motif insertions (8–25 bp) emulating prime editing (~2,179/library).
Workflow: tens of millions of etiolated S. bicolor cv. RTx430 mesophyll protoplasts transfected with barcoded plasmid libraries (full gene context: 2-kb promoter + 5′ UTR + introns + 1-kb downstream + 15-bp barcode); barcode RNA/DNA sequencing; linear regression infers per-variant effect.
PsbS: hypomorphic deletions cluster at −180 to −120 (conserved G-box/I-box light-response motifs); top hypermorphic edit is a 57-bp deletion at −90 (~33-fold), beating the 3×ENH enhancer (~12-fold).
SBPase can only be upregulated by motif insertions (not deletions); a 10-bp insertion at +30 and a 15-bp dual-I-box insertion at −45 both exceed 16-fold and beat 3×ENH (~8-fold).
Hypomorphic PsbS deletions reproduce NPQ phenotypes in rice in planta; hypermorphic edits not yet validated in whole sorghum plants.

Connections

functional-single-cell-genomics — hub topic; this is a functional-genomics MPRA paper.
cis-regulatory-elements — directly maps and dissects cis-regulatory DNA (promoters, 5′ UTRs, G-box/I-box motifs, TFBS).
splicing-methods — shares the MPRA methodology referenced there; this is a plant promoter/UTR MPRA. See massively-parallel-reporter-assay.
New concepts: massively-parallel-reporter-assay (core method), cis-regulatory-grammar (gene-specific position/motif rules); applied toward quantitative trait engineering (QTE) for nontransgenic crop improvement.

Open questions

Do MPRA-identified hypermorphic edits (esp. the 57-bp PsbS deletion at −90) translate to gene-edited whole sorghum plants and yield gains in the field?
How tissue- and condition-dependent are these maps, given protoplasts capture mainly tissue/condition-independent regulation?
Can genomic language models be trained (transcriptome + variant data jointly) to robustly predict activating variants across genes?
What is the mechanism of strong activating deletions — disrupting repressive modules, reconfiguring core enhancers, or creating de novo activating elements?

wiki/sources/grotzinger-2025-mapping-the.title: grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across
wiki/sources/grotzinger-2025-mapping-the.content:
Mapping the genetic landscape across 14 psychiatric disorders
One-line: The third PGC Cross-Disorder study (CDG3) triangulates genome-wide, regional, functional and variant-level analyses across 14 psychiatric disorders (1,056,201 cases) to show that most genetic risk is shared, parsimoniously captured by five genomic factors, with pleiotropic variants acting through early neurodevelopmental processes and specific neuronal/glial cell types — connecting psychiatric genetics to the developing brain (brain-development-neurogenomics).
Key takeaways

Common-variant genetic risk is pervasively shared across 14 childhood- and adult-onset psychiatric disorders; five latent genomic factors explain the majority of each disorder’s genetic variance (~66% on average) and tag 238 pleiotropic loci. This is the psychiatric-genetics anchor of the brain-development-neurogenomics hub.
The five factors are Compulsive (F1: AN, OCD, weakly TS/ANX), Schizophrenia–Bipolar / SB (F2: SCZ, BIP), Neurodevelopmental (F3: ASD, ADHD, weakly TS), Internalizing (F4: PTSD, MD, ANX), and Substance-use disorder / SUD (F5: OUD, CUD, AUD, NIC, weakly ADHD). The first four replicate prior genomic-SEM structure; the SUD factor is new.
Pleiotropic (shared) variants are enriched for broad biological processes (e.g. transcriptional regulation) and are expressed highest at fetal/early-life stages of human brain development, implicating early, fundamental neurodevelopmental processes in transdiagnostic risk — echoing the developmental-window logic of li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis and zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain.
Cell-type specificity: SB-factor genes are strongly enriched in excitatory neurons (strongest: fetal excitatory maturing neurons; also adult deep-layer excitatory neurons), whereas the Internalizing factor implicates oligodendrocyte biology (oligodendrocytes, OPCs, Bergmann glia, MGE interneurons). The p-factor is enriched for fetal excitatory neurons and adult OPCs.
Disorders that load on the same factor are largely indistinguishable at the individual-variant level (>99% of case–case GWAS hits separated disorders on different factors), supporting the factor structure as a neurobiologically grounded reorganization of psychiatric nosology.

Notable claims / data

Scale / update: 14 disorders, 1,056,201 cases; new GWAS for all 8 CDG2 disorders (avg case increase ~165% vs CDG2; ~287% for previously included disorders) plus 6 new disorders (AUD, ANX, PTSD, NIC, OUD, CUD). Main analyses restricted to European-like (EUR-like) genetic ancestry due to uneven ancestral representation; limited EAS-like (MD, SCZ) and AFR-like (AUD, CUD, OUD, PTSD) bivariate results reported — a stated data-quality/generalizability caveat.
Genome-wide correlations (LDSC): pervasive positive rg; MD–SCZ rg in EAS-like (0.45, s.e.=0.09) was double that in EUR-like (0.22, s.e.=0.04). Cross-ancestry generalizability via Popcorn was strong for SCZ (ρgi EAS–EUR = 0.85, s.e.=0.04) but weaker for MD (0.67) and PTSD (0.59); AFR-like LDSC underpowered/not significant.
MiXeR (polygenic overlap): greater overlap than LDSC rgs suggest; shared signal mostly reflects variants with concordant effects, while differentiation is driven by fewer discordant/unique variants.
Factor model fit: five-factor model CFI=0.971, SRMR=0.063; hierarchical p-factor model CFI=0.959, SRMR=0.074. Median residual (factor-unexplained) genetic variance = 33.5%; TS most idiosyncratic (87% unexplained). Internalizing–SUD highest interfactor rg (0.60, s.e.=0.02); Internalizing loads most strongly on p (0.95).
LAVA local correlations: 1,093 LD-independent regions; 458 significant pairwise local rgs; most for MD–ANX (113 regions), MD–PTSD (88), BIP–SCZ (40). Identified 101 rg “hotspots”; the most pleiotropic is on chromosome 11 (NCAM1–TTC12–ANKK1–DRD2 cluster, 17 significant local rgs across 8 of 14 disorders).
Loci: 238 unique factor hits (most for SB n=102, Internalizing n=150), incl. 27 broadly pleiotropic loci; p-factor adds 160 hits (57 new → 295 unique across models); 48 novel vs univariate GWAS (10 entirely novel). QSNP heterogeneity flags disorder-specific signal, e.g. ADH1A/B/C for AUD and the CHRNA3–CHRNA5–CHRNB4 nicotinic cluster for NIC.
CC-GWAS: 75 disorder pairs → 412 loci with divergent effects (294/412 involve SCZ) → 109 LD-independent loci; FST,causal genetic distance inversely correlated with rg (r=−0.79). MD-specific signal enriched in fetal cycling/intermediate progenitors; SCZ-specific in adult endothelial/vascular/upper-rhombic-lip cells.
External traits: p-factor most correlated with suicide attempt (rg=0.87), self-harm (0.74), neuroticism (0.64), loneliness (0.62), stress sensitivity (0.50). Internalizing & SUD uniquely tied to household income (≈−0.40) and cognitive outcomes; Neurodevelopmental uniquely to childhood BMI (0.26) and childhood aggression (0.94).
Stratified genomic SEM: SB enriched at protein-truncating-variant-intolerant (PI) genes × excitatory CA1/CA3 hippocampal neurons; Internalizing at PI-OPC and neural-progenitor annotations; p-factor strongest at fetal male brain H3K4me1 and PI-GABAergic neuron annotations.

Connections

Psychiatric-genetics counterpart to the developing-brain transcriptomic/epigenomic atlases: li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (PsychENCODE/BrainSpan; via psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource) and walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and (prenatal cortical eQTL/sQTL → TWAS) supply the molecular substrate (gene/isoform expression, fetal cell types) onto which this paper maps shared GWAS risk; eQTL + Hi-C from fetal and adult brain were used here to prioritize candidate risk genes.
Complements zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain: Zhou reviews disease genetics and human-cell modeling of brain development; this paper provides the large-scale common-variant evidence that transdiagnostic risk concentrates in early neurodevelopment and in specific neuronal/glial cell types (excitatory neurons, OPCs/oligodendrocytes), reinforcing the cell-type-/stage-specific risk theme.
Cell-type findings touch the neuronal-cell-type backbone of the hub (excitatory vs interneuron vs oligodendrocyte/OPC lineages), linking to developing-brain single-cell sources such as parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (cortical developmental transcriptome) at the level of fetal cortical cell populations.

Open questions

Will the EUR-restricted findings (and the five-factor structure) replicate in better-powered non-European ancestry samples — the paper explicitly flags PTSD/MD as less likely to generalize than SCZ?
Are the implicated cell-type programs (fetal excitatory maturing neurons for SB; oligodendrocyte/OPC for Internalizing) causal, and do they nominate stage- and cell-type-specific therapeutic targets for comorbid presentations?
Can a factor-based (“genomic factor”) nosology outperform DSM/ICD categories clinically, given that same-factor disorders are genetically near-indistinguishable at the variant level?
What drives TS’s largely unique genetic signal (87% factor-unexplained), and why does the chromosome 11 NCAM1–ANKK1–DRD2 hotspot fail to confer transdiagnostic risk for the Neurodevelopmental factor?

wiki/sources/guo-2019-genome-sequencing-.title: guo-2019-genome-sequencing-identifies-multiple-deleterious
wiki/sources/guo-2019-genome-sequencing-.content:
Genome sequencing identifies multiple deleterious variants in autism patients with more severe phenotypes
One-line: Genome sequencing of 180 minimally ascertained ASD families shows that carrying multiple risk variants correlates with more severe cognitive impairment, supporting an oligogenic model.
Key takeaways

Whole-genome sequencing (GS) of a clinically ascertained autism cohort (sage-cohort) captures the full spectrum of de novo/rare SNVs, indels, and CNVs in one assay, outperforming exome sequencing (ES) + microarray for detecting multiple-hit events.
~10% of affected offspring carried more than one NDD risk variant, and these multiple-hit patients had significantly lower IQ, evidence for an oligogenic/multiple-hit model of ASD severity relevant to psychiatric-genetics and neurodevelopment.
Multiplex families show pervasive genetic heterogeneity: no pathogenic/likely-pathogenic variant segregated with all affected members in any family, cautioning against assuming Mendelian inheritance.
High ASD heritability (50-80%) with rare variants explaining only 10-30% of simplex cases motivates GS to close the missing-heritability gap.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 180 families, 578 individuals, 213 patients (149 trios, 23 multiplex quads, multiplex/simplex 4-7 member families); mean GS coverage 35.4x on GRCh38.
40.8% (87/213) of patients carried a pathogenic, likely-pathogenic, or potentially-disorder-related (PDR) variant; 21.1% (45/213) carried a clinically relevant pathogenic/likely-pathogenic variant.
9.9% (21/213) carried >1 validated NDD-gene event; all event-type combinations seen (SNV+SNV, CNV+CNV, SNV+CNV). ES/aCGH could not resolve 52.4% of multiple-hit events.
FSIQ significantly lower in multiple-hit vs single-event offspring (Padj = 0.0004, Mann-Whitney); burden of multiple hits higher in ASD+ID vs ASD-ID (Padj = 0.0003, OR = 12.5, Fisher’s exact).
New/confirmed ASD candidate genes: de novo frameshift in NR4A2, two de novo missense in SYNCRIP (P = 8.7e-7, Padj = 0.02), two missense in SLITRK5, missense in MYT1 (paralog of MYT1L), two missense in TRANK1; NR4A2 and KIRREL3 variants were missed by ES due to low exon coverage.
56 validated de novo disorder-related SNVs/indels (15 LGD, 40 missense) from 52 genes; 46 disorder-related CNVs plus two abnormal karyotypes (48,XXXY; 47,XXY); recurrent 16p11.2 CNV across families. Data in dbGaP phs001740.v1.p1.

Connections

Adds a clinical-GS, phenotype-severity angle to the wiki’s autism and psychiatric-genetics material; de novo variant burden in neurodevelopment risk genes such as chd8 (recurrent ASD gene).
Frames GS as a route to recovered missing-heritability and to the rare/common variant interplay underlying heritability estimates in ASD.

Open questions

How much of the residual heritability lies in noncoding regulatory variants that GS uniquely captures but this study did not systematically test?
Does the oligogenic-severity relationship replicate in larger, non-European cohorts and generalize beyond IQ to other clinical dimensions?
What is the mechanistic basis (additive vs synergistic/epistatic) by which co-occurring hits amplify phenotypic severity?

wiki/sources/gupta-2023-dynamic-regulato.title: gupta-2023-dynamic-regulatory-elements-in-single-cell
wiki/sources/gupta-2023-dynamic-regulato.content:
Dynamic regulatory elements in single-cell multimodal data implicate key immune cell states enriched for autoimmune disease heritability
One-line: Multimodal snRNA/snATAC of inflamed RA synovium maps “dynamic” chromatin peaks whose accessibility tracks cell state, and shows these are enriched for autoimmune heritability.
Key takeaways

Introduces a framework to define cell-state-dependent (“dynamic”) vs cell-state-invariant (“cs-invariant”) chromatin-accessibility peaks by regressing peak counts on transcriptional axes within each cell type.
A multivariable Poisson generalized linear model predicts snATAC peak accessibility from the top ten snRNA-seq principal components, accounting for donor effects and total peak counts.
Dynamic peaks mark cis-regulatory-elements (mostly enhancers) that are enriched for autoimmune disease heritability far more than cs-invariant peaks.
Uses stratified LD-score regression to partition genome-wide-association-study heritability across peak-set annotations, pointing noncoding-variants to specific pathogenic immune cell states.
Implicates CD4+ T peripheral helper (Tph), regulatory T (Treg), dendritic and STAT1+CXCL10+ myeloid cell states as disease-critical.

Notable claims / data

28,674 cells from inflamed synovial tissue of 12 donors (11 RA, 1 osteoarthritis); 22,537 genes and 132,466 peaks across five cell types (7,942 T, 1,543 B, 7,320 myeloid, 9,902 fibroblast, 1,967 endothelial).
90% (119,716/132,466) of peaks were dynamic in ≥1 cell type; 12% of those dynamic in all three immune cell types.
Meta-enrichment across 14 immune-mediated diseases: dynamic-peak enrichment ± SE = 27.7 ± 2.1 (B), 22.0 ± 1.0 (T), 13.5 ± 0.7 (myeloid).
Conditional τ* (dynamic vs cs-invariant): T cells 2.8 ± 0.2 vs 0.2 ± 0.1 — dynamic peaks obviated cs-invariant signal; largest T-cell differences in celiac, hypothyroidism and RA.
Across 23 discrete T cell states, six captured RA heritability disproportionately; Tph (T-7) and CD4+CD25hi Treg (T-8) significant across 10/14 and 11/14 diseases; conditioning on T-7 removed Tph/related signal but retained Treg.
21 traits tested total: 14 autoimmune/allergic (avg GWAS N=187,158) plus 5 blood-cell (positive) and 2 non-immune (negative) controls.

Connections

A single-cell functional-genomics approach to the missing-heritability problem, partitioning where autoimmune genome-wide-association-study signal concentrates in regulatory DNA.
Complements cell-type/tissue heritability-partitioning work such as jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types.
Extends multimodal snRNA/snATAC integration seen in kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and and loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor.

Open questions

Does dynamic-peak heritability enrichment generalize beyond RA synovium once multimodal data exist for other inflamed tissues?
Are the implicated Tph/Treg states drivers of autoimmunity or a fine-grained readout of causal variants — and can they be validated by perturbation?

wiki/sources/han-2026-generalizable-muta.title: han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across
wiki/sources/han-2026-generalizable-muta.content:
Generalizable mutation-effect prediction across adaptive immune recognition via unified multimodal framework
One-line: uniair is a modular, multimodal sequence–structure framework that predicts mutation-induced binding free-energy changes (ΔΔG) generalizably across antibody–antigen and TCR–pMHC immune recognition, reaching state-of-the-art accuracy with minimal task-specific tuning.
Key takeaways

Introduces uniair, a unified framework with three parts: a standardized mutation-centric interface-construction toolkit, the ESSM sequence–structure fusion model (S-Former co-attention transformer coupling a protein language model (ESM2) with geometric structure), and scalable extensions (MoFPE expert mixture + a latent adapter for predicted structures).
Frames mutation-effect-prediction for immune-recognition as a single multimodal problem rather than per-task models, addressing the heterogeneity that limits prior sequence-only or structure-only approaches — relevant to ai-in-genomics and foundation-models.
MoFPE (mixture-of-frozen predictive experts) integrates four frozen structure-pretrained experts (ESSM, GearBind, RDE-DDG, PPIformer) via a structure-aware gating network, avoiding backbone retraining or manual weighting — an instance of multimodal-learning.
UniAIR-LT adds a lightweight latent adapter aligning predicted-structure (ESMFold/OpenFold) embeddings to experimental ones, extending use to cases where experimental structures are unavailable or incomplete.
Demonstrated across antibody maturation (SARS-CoV-2), viral antigen escape (Lassa GPC), TCR–pHLA peptide optimization (KRAS-G12D) coupled with FEP, and zero-shot mutational-pattern analysis.

Notable claims / data

On SKEMPIv2 (n=5,820 mutations, 340 complexes, threefold CV) UniAIR reached state of the art: PCC 0.684, SPC 0.589, RMSE 1.575, MAE 1.077; consistent on single- vs multipoint mutations (PCC 0.682 vs 0.677). ESSM backbone alone: PCC 0.635 / SPC 0.533, beating PLMs ESM2 (0.523/0.410) and SaProt (0.546/0.438).
Robust generalization on two independent, non-overlapping test sets: HER2 antibody–antigen (n=419, PCC 0.521) and TCR–pMHC ATLAS (n=87, PCC 0.736), where competing methods (e.g. RDE-DDG) degraded on one set.
Zero-shot antibody optimization of P36-5D2 vs SARS-CoV-2 spike: of 19 experimentally validated affinity-enhancing mutants, 63.2% ranked in top 10% and 89.5% in top 20%; quadruple mutant T31W/N57L/R103M/L104F ranked top 2.07%.
KRAS-G12D peptide optimization: UniAIR alanine scan correlated with FEP at PCC 0.556 / SPC 0.513; FEP validated 6/13 single-, 3/8 double-, 5/7 triple-point designs with improved peptide–HLA and TCR–pHLA binding, supported by 300 ns triplicate MD.
UniAIR-LT improved predicted-structure ΔΔG (ESMFold PCC 0.590→0.661; OpenFold 0.663→0.673) and rescued the worst mis-predicted complex 5C6T (PCC 0.053→0.421). Caveat: ΔΔG-to-phenotype mapping is system/context dependent; protein–protein interfaces only (no small molecules/nucleic acids).
Beat LLM agent-with-tools workflows on SKEMPIv2; dropping structure-dependent experts roughly doubles inference speed with minimal loss (0.581 vs 0.589).

Connections

Sits in artificial-intelligence; builds on protein-language-models (ESM2) and the foundation-models paradigm applied to ai-in-genomics.
Adaptive-immune mutation focus relates to data-quality-and-bias (structural data scarcity/imbalance for TCR–pMHC, high-affinity dataset bias) and contrasts with agent-based tool aggregation.
Resource/model sibling to other AI-method papers; safety-relevant since it touches viral escape and immunogen design — cf. hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation.

Open questions

Extending beyond protein–protein interfaces to small molecules and nucleic acids that also shape immune recognition.
Calibrating the ΔΔG→immune-phenotype mapping (T-cell activation thresholds, escape) with phenotypic supervision per system.
Prospective wet-lab validation (SPR, T-cell activation assays) and integration with clinical/translational datasets.

wiki/sources/handsaker-2025-long-somatic.title: handsaker-2025-long-somatic-dna-repeat-expansion
wiki/sources/handsaker-2025-long-somatic.content:
Long somatic DNA-repeat expansion drives neurodegeneration in Huntington disease
One-line: Single-cell measurement of HTT CAG-repeat length alongside genome-wide RNA expression shows the repeat is biologically silent until it somatically expands past ~150 CAGs, recasting HD as a decades-long DNA process that becomes acutely toxic only in the final months of a neuron’s life.
Key takeaways

HD pathogenesis is reframed as cell-autonomous somatic DNA-repeat expansion: the inherited HTT allele is innocuous even after decades of expansion, and toxicity is acquired only past a high (~150 CAG) threshold — the elongate-model (extra-long repeats, acquired toxic effect).
Explains the cell-type specificity of neurodegeneration: only striatal projection neurons (SPNs) undergo extreme somatic expansion (to 100-500+ CAGs), while interneurons and glia — sharing HTT expression and even GABAergic lineage — stay near the germline length, tying vulnerability to per-neuronal-cell-types somatic instability rather than differential HTT toxicity.
A new molecular method pairs long-read sequencing of the HTT exon-1 CAG repeat with conventional snRNA-seq in the same barcoded nucleus, letting each cell’s repeat length be matched to its own expression state (a UMI-per-transcript strategy that overcomes PCR bias against long repeats).
Proposes five sequential single-neuron phases (A-E): decades of biologically quiet expansion (A,B), then continuous cell-identity erosion (C, >150 CAGs), a de-repression crisis (D), and elimination (E) — cells traverse them asynchronously, so at any moment most surviving SPNs still carry a harmless huntingtin gene.
Therapeutically pivots away from HTT-lowering (few cells are toxic at any time; those that are may live only months) toward slowing somatic expansion, with a predicted wide therapeutic window.

Notable claims / data

snRNA-seq of 613,670 nuclei from anterior caudate of 56 HD + 53 control donors (mean 5,630 nuclei/donor); SPN loss steepens sharply above CAP score ~400, with >80% SPN loss by CAP >600. iSPNs (D2) lost earlier than dSPNs (D1) (p=8e-6).
HTT expression did NOT explain SPN vulnerability: SPN HTT levels slightly lower than interneurons; dSPN vs iSPN HTT indistinguishable (p=0.56); accelerated SPN loss not associated with higher HTT.
Deep single-cell CAG measurement in 6 manifest-HD donors: 95-98% of SPNs expanded beyond germline (40-43 CAGs) to median 60-73; a “tail” of SPNs reaching 100-842 CAGs. Expansion is allele-specific (cis to the long allele). Armadillo-shaped length distribution.
