No detectable cell-autonomous expression change across 36-150 CAGs; >150 CAGs shifts hundreds of genes, highly reproducible across donors. Phase C: ~203 down (C-) / ~369 up (C+) genes; declining genes (PDE10A, PPP2R2B, PPP3CA, PHACTR1, RYR3, K+ channel subunits) are SPN-identity markers.
Phase D de-represses 173 normally-silent genes — HOXA/B/C/D clusters + lncRNAs (HOTAIR, HOTTIP), ectopic TFs, and senescence/apoptosis genes CDKN2A/CDKN2B (p16/p15); rare (<4%) below 250 CAGs but >50% above 350 CAGs. Mirrors PRC2-inactivation phenotypes.
Rate of SPN loss across HD stages is proportional to the fraction of SPNs showing phase-C transcriptionopathy.
Stochastic simulations reproduce the data with two expansion phases (slow A → fast B, ~10x rate jump over 70-90 CAGs) and predict the inherited-length/age-at-onset relationship arises from bypassing the slowest early lengths, not from graded protein toxicity. Average SPN spends ~96.4% of life below 150 CAGs.

Connections

Companion human-HD source to nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease, which reports mutant-HTT-driven aberrant splicing / TDP-43 dysfunction in the same vulnerable striatal MSNs/SPNs — a downstream RNA-processing phenotype that this paper’s phase-C/D transcriptionopathy framework could contextualize (are splicing changes a >150-CAG, late-phase event?).
Reinforces cell-type-resolved neurodegeneration and single-nucleus rna-seq approaches to disease-vulnerable neuronal-cell-types; the CAG-in-RNA readout relies on long-read-rna-seq of the expanded exon-1 tract.
The DNA-repeat-instability / mismatch-repair (MutSβ, MSH3, FAN1) axis and somatic mosaicism are new to the wiki (plain text — candidates for central concept pages).

Open questions

Does the neuron-autonomous ELongATE dynamic hold in brain regions beyond the striatum, where circuit/connectivity factors have been invoked?
What makes somatic instability so cell-type-specific — why do SPNs (vs lineage-related interneurons) expand extremely?
Do the HD splicing/TDP-43 changes of nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease map onto the >150-CAG phase-C/D window, or precede it?
Can pharmacologically slowing somatic expansion (e.g. modulating MMR genes) postpone onset even in symptomatic patients, and generalize to other repeat-expansion disorders (myotonic dystrophy, SCAs, Friedreich ataxia)?

wiki/sources/hassan-2026-a-safety-centri.title: hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation
wiki/sources/hassan-2026-a-safety-centri.content:
A safety-centric perspective on innovation and risk in the use of artificial intelligence in genomics
One-line: A safety/governance Perspective arguing that generative-ai foundation-models for biological sequences (ai-in-genomics) amplify data bias and dual-use-risk across the whole innovation pipeline, and proposing layered technical safeguards plus an institutional risk–benefit framework to embed ai-safety and ai-governance from project inception.
Key takeaways

foundation-models for DNA/RNA/protein (e.g., Nucleotide Transformer, Evo family) are transformative for ai-in-genomics — identifying regulatory elements, enabling multimodal DNA–RNA–protein design (CRISPR-Cas systems, transposon arrays) and generating the first viable AI-designed bacteriophage genome — but the same capabilities raise dual-use-risk from theoretical to immediate.
data-quality-and-bias: genomic datasets are dominated by European individuals, so models perform poorly on under-represented populations; polygenic risk scores from European cohorts lose 15–20% predictive accuracy in African groups, and the gap persists as datasets grow — a systemic risk that can propagate inequity into clinical tools.
Misuse risk must be analysed across the entire biorisk chain (design–build–test–learn), not as a single point; current bottlenecks (scarce biological data, lab expertise, restricted materials) confine credible end-to-end misuse mostly to state-level or highly resourced actors, while nonstate actors lack means to translate AI designs into physical harm.
ai-safety technical safeguards (data filtering to remove human pathogens, tamper resistance, weight pruning, concept erasure / “SafeGenie”, safety wrappers, Guaranteed Safe AI) are all useful but insufficient alone — they rely on known risk profiles and fail on novel sequences with no natural analogues.
biosecurity framing: the paper deliberately avoids a single ethical/governance framework, instead using a biosecurity perspective grounded in biosafety and dual-use principles, plus a “One Health” approach covering human, animal, and environmental threats.
ai-governance: the EU AI Act classifies AI-driven genomics as “high-risk” (mandating bias assessment, transparency, risk management), yet current FM development largely fails to meet these standards; the authors propose an institutional risk–benefit framework (adapted from the Robert Koch Institute DURC process) applied from project inception.

Notable claims / data

AI adoption: ~1 in 9 industry job postings (as of May 2025) require AI-related skills; the transformer architecture was introduced in 2017; the term “foundation models” was coined in 2021 (GPT/BERT-style).
Distribution-shift failures: Nucleotide Transformer embeddings lose 23% accuracy on rare tumour types absent from pre-training; models trained mostly on European genomes perform 18–41% worse on South Asian regulatory elements; even Evo fails to generalise to eukaryotes, producing nonviable sequences in 94% of yeast chassis trials unless trained across the full tree of life.
Raising the “ceiling of harm”: ML-designed viral libraries yield ~10× more successful variants than traditional NNK libraries; ML-assisted directed evolution finds high-fitness variants more efficiently than standard methods. FMs may overcome evolutionary trade-offs (transmissibility vs virulence), enabling engineered pathogens worse than natural ones — including low-probability, existential-scale threats (caveat: largely speculative).
King et al. created and lab-tested the first AI-generated phages; some showed greater fitness and faster lysis than wild-type — proving genomic design is practically feasible. Fine-tuning has been shown to restore generation of sequences excluded from training data (e.g., Evo excluded eukaryotic viruses, but neither Evo 1 nor Evo 2 documents post-fine-tuning generative capability).
Screening is fragile: AI-designed biosequences are more diverse than natural ones and may evade database-comparison screening; “GeneBreaker” jailbreaks DNA language models via pathogenicity guidance; adversarial changes to mass spectra can make models misclassify cancer biomarkers.
Model opacity: interpretability tools (gradient attribution, in silico mutagenesis, attention visualisation, latent probing) give post-hoc diagnostics but suffer noisy attributions, lack of causality, poor generalisation; architectures like ExplaiNN and ARGMINN embed motif-level priors to make interpretability integral.

Connections

This is the first source of a new artificial-intelligence topic, distinct from the existing splicing topic; it establishes the AI-safety/governance backbone and seeds all eight new-vocab concept pages.
Safety concerns connect across the innovation pipeline: data-quality-and-bias at training → opacity/interpretability at the model layer → dual-use-risk / misuse at deployment → biosecurity screening at the synthesis stage → ai-governance (EU AI Act, Ethics by Design, institutional risk–benefit) wrapping the whole lifecycle.
generative-ai and foundation-models are the technical substrate; ai-safety supplies the mitigation toolkit; ai-governance and biosecurity supply the institutional/regulatory layer — the paper’s thesis is that no single layer suffices.

Open questions

Can interpretability move beyond correlation to genuine causal mechanistic insight, and how do we validate that interpretable features reflect true biology rather than artifacts?
How to resolve the paradox that interpretable AI both exposes population-specific markers for fairness correction and increases misuse/discrimination risk?
Can Guaranteed Safe AI frameworks with quantitative guarantees be implemented for real-world synthesis pipelines and lab automation without inhibiting beneficial innovation?
What institutional decision-making and escalation protocols best handle risks that emerge dynamically during model development?
What empirical evidence distinguishes theoretical risks from demonstrated capabilities in multiobjective biological engineering?
How can FMs be trained across all evolutionary domains to stay functional, and what architectural innovations address eukaryotic genome modelling?

wiki/sources/heberle-2024-mapping-medica.title: heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform
wiki/sources/heberle-2024-mapping-medica.content:
Mapping medically relevant RNA isoform diversity in the aged human frontal cortex with deep long-read RNA sequencing
One-line: Deep Oxford Nanopore long-read-rna-seq of 12 aged human frontal cortices (6 Alzheimer disease, 6 control) maps isoform diversity in 1,917 medically relevant genes, discovers new isoforms (including spliced mitochondrial transcripts) and shows that differential isoform expression reveals disease signals hidden at the gene level (splicing-and-disease).
Key takeaways

Standard short-read RNA-seq collapses each gene’s many isoforms into a single expression value, losing biology; the authors argue that accurate per-isoform quantification matters because individual isoforms can have distinct or even opposite functions (classic example: BCL-X anti-apoptotic BCL-XL vs pro-apoptotic BCL-XS).
They sequenced 12 postmortem dorsolateral prefrontal cortex samples (Brodmann area 9/46; 50% female; all postmortem interval <5 h, RIN ≥9.0) with one PromethION flow cell per sample, reaching a median of 35.5 million aligned reads/sample — far deeper than prior brain long-read studies (which averaged <6 million aligned reads).
Using bambu (a precision-favoring isoform discovery/quantification tool) on Ensembl v.107, they found 28,989 expressed RNA isoforms from 18,041 gene bodies (median CPM > 1).
1,917 medically relevant genes expressed multiple isoforms in frontal cortex, and 1,018 of those expressed isoforms with different protein-coding sequences; 57 of the 1,018 are implicated in brain-related diseases (major depression, schizophrenia, Parkinson’s, Alzheimer’s).
53 new high-confidence RNA isoforms came from 49 medically relevant genes — for some genes the new isoform was among the most highly expressed (e.g. SLC26A1 86%, CAMKMT 61%, WDR4 61%, POLB 28%, TREM2 exon-2-skipping 16%, MAOB 9%) — relevant to interpreting variants in the proper isoform context (splicing-and-disease).
Differential isoform expression between AD and controls surfaced disease signals invisible at the gene level — a direct demonstration of why isoform-resolution alternative-splicing analysis matters for brain disease.
The study links isoform diversity of brain disease genes (APP, MAPT/tau, BIN1, SOD1, SNCA, TARDBP/TDP-43, SHANK3) to neurological and neuropsychiatric disorders, touching themes in splicing-and-neurodevelopment.
Summary data are browsable via a public Shiny web app, brain-rna-isoform-browser-tool.

Notable claims / data

Sequencing: median 35.5M aligned reads/sample (after excluding reads lacking a primer on either end and MAPQ < 10); bambu assigned on average 42.4% of reads uniquely to an isoform and 17.5% spanning a full-length isoform.
Expression threshold: an isoform counted as expressed only if median CPM > 1 (at this depth, CPM = 1 ≈ 24 counts/sample) — a stricter bar than prior studies.
New isoforms from known genes: bambu found 1,534 new transcripts; after the median-CPM > 1 filter, 428 high-confidence remained (303 from protein-coding genes). 297 (69.4%) were unique vs Ensembl v.107, Glinos et al. and Leung et al.
The authors report far fewer new isoforms than Glinos et al. (~70,000) and Leung et al. (~12,000), attributing the gap mainly to discovery tool choice (bambu vs FLAIR vs Cupcake), plus reference-annotation vintage and stringency — when using identical tools/annotation, transcript-discovery overlap with GTEx rose 33.8%.
New high-confidence isoforms: median 761.5 nt (179–3,089 nt), 2–14 exons; splice sites were canonical (94.9% of 5′ and 100% of 3′ matched motifs), supporting biological feasibility.
Validation: 9/17 new isoforms confirmed by PCR/gel (6 of the 8 failures rescued by RT–qPCR); ~98.8% of new high-confidence known-gene transcripts had ≥1 uniquely mapped read in GTEx or ROSMAP; 11 validated at the protein level by mass spec.
Five new high-confidence spliced, mitochondrially encoded isoforms (each two exons) — surprising because all annotated human mitochondrial transcripts are single-exon; four span MT-RNR2 (encodes 16S rRNA / the humanin peptide), the fifth spans MT-ND1/MT-ND2 on the opposite strand. All five validated in GTEx brain data.
New gene bodies: bambu found 1,860 isoforms from 1,676 new gene bodies; 267 high-confidence isoforms from 245 new gene bodies (most just two exons, possibly fragments). 130 overlapped a known gene on the opposite strand; intragenic ones may reflect leaky/spurious transcription linked to aging.
Down-sampling showed depth matters: at 20% of reads only 238 high-confidence known-gene isoforms were found vs 428 at 100%.
Differential expression (DESeq2): 176 differentially expressed genes and 105 differentially expressed isoforms between AD and control; 99 of those 105 isoforms came from genes NOT differentially expressed at the gene level. Example: TNFSF12 was not DE as a gene, yet isoform TNFSF12-219 was up in AD while TNFSF12-203 was up in controls (opposite trends).
Caveats stated by the authors: only 12 samples (DE results are proof-of-concept, not disease conclusions); pronounced 3′ bias; long-read quantification still poor for long, highly similar isoforms (5,695 of 7,418 medically relevant transcripts are >2,000 nt); bulk (not single-cell) so cell-type-specific patterns are obscured; only four MAPT isoforms seen at median CPM > 1 despite six known CNS tau proteins. All samples came from white individuals.

Connections

A deep-coverage human-brain long-read-rna-seq resource and isoform atlas; complements humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas / isomiga, which build a long-read microglia isoform reference and link splicing to AD/PD risk — both argue long-read augmentation of the reference improves disease interpretation, and both flag TREM2 isoform diversity.
Squarely in splicing-and-disease: isoform-level (not gene-level) measurement is needed to interpret variants in medically relevant genes and to detect disease-associated transcriptomic signatures.
Brain disease genes with multiple isoforms (TARDBP, SHANK3, MAPT, etc.) tie into splicing-and-neurodevelopment and neuropsychiatric splicing themes.
Sibling brain/isoform sources here include the cross-ancestry isoform-expression atlas wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform and the developing-cortex expression/splicing studies walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis.

Open questions

What are the functions of the newly discovered isoforms — especially the spliced mitochondrial transcripts (biomarkers? functional? artifacts?) and the new intragenic gene bodies (aging-related vs noise)?
Do the AD-vs-control isoform-switch signals (e.g. TNFSF12) replicate in larger, better-powered cohorts and across ancestries?
Can single-cell long-read sequencing resolve cell-type-specific isoform usage that bulk sequencing obscures, despite current cost?
How much of the discovery-count discrepancy with prior atlases is genuine biology vs tool-driven sensitivity/precision tradeoffs?

wiki/sources/hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pr.title: hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pre-mrna-splicing-methods
wiki/sources/hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pr.content:
Spliceosomal Pre-mRNA Splicing: Methods and Protocols
One-line: An edited Methods in Molecular Biology volume (vol. 1126) that bundles seven introductory reviews on splicing biology with twenty-one bench and computational protocol chapters, serving as a practical handbook for studying spliceosome assembly, catalysis, and alternative pre-mRNA splicing.
Key takeaways

A protocol compendium, not a single study: Part I gives theory across seven chapters (the splicing reaction, diversity/evolution of spliceosomal systems, spliceosomal assembly, alternative splicing, its regulation, cotranscriptional splicing, and chromatin–splicing), while Part II provides 21 step-by-step experimental protocols.
Framing in the preface: of ~25,000 human genes, more than 90% are believed to produce alternatively spliced transcripts; early estimates put ~15% of single-base mutations as splicing-altering, while newer estimates suggest up to 60% of known disease mutations could act through changes in pre-mRNA splicing.
In vitro biochemistry protocols cover preparation of splicing-competent spliceosome nuclear extracts (HeLa) and yeast whole-cell extracts, in vitro splicing assays in mammalian extract, kinetic analysis of splicing in HeLa nuclear extract, coupling RNAP II transcription to splicing/polyadenylation, isolation of spliceosomal assembly intermediates, and U4 snRNA complementation in S. cerevisiae extracts.
Protein/RNA tooling protocols include splinted ligation of synthetic RNA with RNA ligase, expression/purification of splicing-factors from mammalian cells, single-molecule (SNAP-tag/TIRF) approaches to spliceosome assembly and catalysis, and quantifying the stoichiometric ratio of spliceosome components assembled on pre-mRNA.
Cell-based and manipulation protocols cover cell-based splicing of minigenes, antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) methods to modulate splicing (exemplified by splicing correction in spinal muscular atrophy via smn2), and yeast genetics (temperature-sensitive prp/RNA mutants, Synthetic Genetic Array) to dissect splicing mechanisms.
High-throughput / readout protocols cover medium-throughput fluorescently labeled RT-PCR for alternative-splicing, ChIP approaches to establish the cotranscriptional nature of splicing (see cotranscriptional-splicing), iCLIP-Seq for global protein–RNA interaction mapping at single-nucleotide resolution, computational mining of public databases (UCSC Table Browser, SAMtools, liftOver, GenomicTools), linking RNA secondary structure to splicing regulation, and a review chapter classifying RNA-Seq tools for studying splicing.

Notable claims / data

Preface estimates: >90% of human genes alternatively spliced; ~15% of single-base mutations historically estimated to alter splicing; up to 60% of known disease-causing mutations may act via pre-mRNA splicing changes.
Chapter 26 (Alamancos, Agirre & Eyras) reviews and classifies the “plethora” of RNA-Seq tools by operation — read mapping/assignment, event/isoform quantification (with or without annotation), transcript reconstruction/de novo assembly, differential splicing/expression comparison across conditions, and visualization — providing a decision framework rather than a single new algorithm.
Chapter 28 (Barash & Vaquero Garcia) addresses computational prediction of alternative splicing, building on machine-learning “splicing code” work; Part I likewise discusses a computationally derived neuronal splicing code (adapted from Barash et al.) for exon skipping vs. inclusion.
Genome statistics quoted in the evolution chapters (e.g., human and Arabidopsis intron/genome comparisons) ground the cross-species framing.
Caveat: the chapters are presented as guides to commonly used, established methods; the volume’s value is reusable protocol knowledge rather than a single novel hypothesis-driven result.

Connections

Belongs to the splicing-methods cluster as a foundational protocol reference predating modern RNA-Seq event tools; complements method/tool sources such as shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (rMATS / percent-spliced-in) and the tool-survey angle of sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Its review chapters on alternative splicing and its regulation overlap thematically with lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Cotranscriptional/chromatin chapters connect to cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin; ASO/SMA and disease framing connect to splicing-therapeutics, splicing-and-disease, and smn2.
iCLIP and splicing-factor purification chapters connect to rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors; RNA-structure chapter connects to rna-structure-and-splicing.

Open questions

How well do the 2014-era in vitro and RT-PCR readouts integrate with current long-read and single-cell RNA-Seq pipelines?
The RNA-Seq tool classification (Ch. 26) predates many later tools (e.g., rMATS, MAJIQ, SUPPA) — which of its operational categories remain the right taxonomy today?
Which of the biochemical assembly-intermediate and single-molecule protocols have since been superseded by cryo-EM structural approaches to the spliceosome?

wiki/sources/hille-2016-crispr-cas-biolo.title: hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance
wiki/sources/hille-2016-crispr-cas-biolo.content:
CRISPR-Cas: biology, mechanisms and relevance
One-line: A foundational review of CRISPR-Cas as the only known prokaryotic adaptive immune system — its three-stage mechanism (adaptation, crRNA biogenesis, interference), system classification, anti-CRISPR and non-immune roles, and its repurposing into the genome-editing technology CRISPR-Cas9.
Key takeaways

CRISPR-Cas is a prokaryotic adaptive immune system: short invader sequences (“spacers”) from mobile genetic elements are integrated into the CRISPR array, transcribed as small guide crRNAs, and used by Cas proteins to target the invader sequence-specifically upon reinfection.
Immunity proceeds in three stages: (i) adaptation / spacer acquisition (Cas1–Cas2 integrate a protospacer as a new spacer at the leader-repeat boundary; a PAM guides protospacer selection in types I/II/V), (ii) crRNA biogenesis (pre-crRNA is transcribed and processed — by Cas6 in types I/III, by tracrRNA+RNase III+Cas9 in type II, by Cpf1 in type V), and (iii) interference (mature crRNA guides effector complexes to cleave invading nucleic acid).
Systems split into class 1 (multi-protein effector complexes: types I, III, IV) and class 2 (single effector protein: types II, V, VI); six types and multiple subtypes total.
Type I uses Cascade + the nuclease cas3; type II uses cas9 guided by a tracrRNA:crRNA duplex to make a double-strand break; type III (Cas10-Csm/Cmr) can target both DNA and RNA and depends on target transcription; type V uses cpf1 (Cas12a).
The type II CRISPR-Cas9 system, simplified into a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) fusion of tracrRNA and crRNA, was repurposed into a programmable genome-editing tool — underpinning pooled-crispr-screens and single-cell-crispr-screen approaches.
CRISPR-Cas also has functions beyond immunity: DNA repair (Cas1–RecBCD interplay), endogenous gene regulation and virulence control, and roles in genome evolution / self-targeting.

Notable claims / data

Class 2 systems use only one effector protein; type V-C is minimal, comprising only a putative effector (C2C3) and a Cas1 homologue.
In type II adaptation, Cas9 (via its PAM-recognizing domain) selects protospacers and recruits Cas1, Cas2 and Csn2 for integration; the first repeat is duplicated on spacer insertion.
A unique adaptation mode in a type III-B system uses a Cas1 fused to a reverse transcriptase, enabling spacer acquisition from RNA as well as DNA.
Interference self/non-self discrimination: types I/II/V recognize the PAM (upstream in I/V, downstream in II); type III discriminates via the crRNA 5’ tag, which must NOT base-pair with target to enable degradation.
Anti-CRISPR (Acr) proteins: Mu-like phages infecting Pseudomonas aeruginosa encode Acr proteins (AcrF1/AcrF2 bind Cascade subunits; AcrF3 binds and inhibits Cas3). Vibrio cholerae ICP1 phages even encode their own functional type I-F CRISPR-Cas to attack a host anti-phage genomic island.
Genome-editing applications (figure 2): Cas9+sgRNA → double-strand break repaired by error-prone NHEJ (knockouts) or HDR (precise insertion); catalytically dead dcas9 blocks transcription (CRISPRi) or, fused to activators/repressors/epigenetic effectors (e.g. p300, KRAB), enables CRISPRa and epigenome editing.
Cited therapeutic/medical uses: correcting CFTR in cystic fibrosis intestinal stem cell organoids, rescuing hereditary tyrosinaemia in mice, excising latent HIV, and editing CCR5 for HIV resistance; antimicrobial use via engineered CRISPR arrays delivered by phages to kill antibiotic-resistant strains.
Off-target cleavage by Cas9 is a major safety concern; mitigations reviewed include delivering purified Cas9 protein/RNPs, paired nickases with two sgRNAs, and truncated sgRNAs.
Box 2 milestone timeline: CRISPR repeats first described 1987; adaptive-immunity hypothesis 2005; spacer-acquisition proof (Barrangou, S. thermophilus) 2007; DNA as target 2008; Cas9 makes a defined DSB 2010; tracrRNA/RNase III maturation 2011; programmable dual-RNA / sgRNA Cas9 cleavage 2012; eukaryotic (human/mouse) editing 2013.

Connections

Provides the mechanistic and historical foundation for genome-editing, pooled-crispr-screen, and single-cell-crispr-screen — the perturbation toolkit behind functional-single-cell-genomics sources like dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening, and gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements.
The dCas9-based CRISPRi/CRISPRa framework here underlies functional-genomics enhancer/perturbation assays that dissect cis-regulatory-elements.
Charpentier is a co-inventor of the CRISPR-Cas9 editing technology; competing-interests note lists her as co-founder of CRISPR Therapeutics AG and ERS Genomics.

Open questions

Precise biochemistry and conservation of spacer acquisition across CRISPR types remains “the most puzzling event” and poorly understood.
Roles of accessory proteins (Cas4, Csn2) in adaptation still need definition.
How to make Cas9 editing a safe therapeutic tool — controlling off-target cleavage and improving in vivo delivery.
The true evolutionary/fitness balance of maintaining CRISPR-Cas (some studies find costs even without predation; correlation with HGT is contested).

wiki/sources/huang-2023-single-cell-whol.title: huang-2023-single-cell-whole-embryo-phenotyping
wiki/sources/huang-2023-single-cell-whol.content:
Single-cell, whole-embryo phenotyping of mammalian developmental disorders
One-line: Establishes whole-embryo single-cell RNA-seq as a scalable platform for systematically phenotyping mouse genetic models, profiling 1.6M+ nuclei across 22 mutants to detect compositional and expression changes across 52 cell types/trajectories.
Key takeaways

Applies combinatorial-indexing scRNA-seq (sci-RNA-seq3) to 101 E13.5 mouse embryos (22 mutants + 4 wild-type strains) to build the mouse mutant cell atlas (MMCA), a molecular-phenotyping resource for neurodevelopment and organogenesis.
Introduces the lochness-score (local cellular heuristic neighbourhood enrichment specificity score) to detect subtle, label-agnostic shifts in cell-state distributions within a trajectory that gross composition analysis misses.
Frames whole-embryo scRNA-seq as a form of single-cell-perturbation-modeling: each mutant is a genetic perturbation read out simultaneously across all cell types, replacing organ-by-organ phenotyping.
About half the mutants disrupt cis-regulatory-elements rather than coding sequence, including deletions of topologically-associating-domains boundaries near developmental transcription factors.
Demonstrates that developmental pleiotropy may be “decomposable” — some phenotypic changes are shared across otherwise unrelated mutants.

Notable claims / data

103 embryos collected, ~4 replicates/strain; after doublet + outlier filtering, 1,627,857 cells from 101 embryos (16,226 ± 9,289 nuclei/embryo; median 843 UMIs, 534 genes/cell; 75% duplication rate).
13 major trajectories, 8 stratified into 59 sub-trajectories (52–54 sub-trajectories used in composition analyses); validated against the mouse organogenesis cell atlas (MOCA, E9.5–E13.5).
Pseudobulk PCA separates embryos by genetic background (FVB vs C57BL/6/G4/BALB/c), not by mutant — no mutant showed severe global aberration.
Beta-binomial regression across 22 mutants flagged 300 nominally significant sub-trajectory composition changes (uncorrected P < 0.05); Sox9 regulatory INV altered 30/54 sub-trajectories, TAD-boundary KOs very few.
Ttc21b KO: near-complete loss of retinal neuron (log2 ratio −7.16), lens (−2.40), retina epithelium (−1.65) trajectories, confirmed by H&E (missing retinal neurons, lens, optic nerve in homozygotes).
Gli2 KO: floor-plate depletion (~60% fewer cells) plus an unexpected increase in roof-plate-derived choroid plexus (Ttr+), the latter detected only by lochNESS, not by composition analysis.
Atp6v0a2 KO, Atp6v0a2^R755Q and Gorab KO share mesenchymal/epithelial phenotypes mirroring overlapping human connective-tissue disorders; only Atp6v0a2 shows a neural-tube (CNS) phenotype.
Sox9 regulatory INV (1-Mb upstream inversion relocating enhancers + a TAD boundary into the Kcnj2 TAD) causes mesenchymal stalling — excess limb mesenchyme at the expense of osteoblasts/chondrocytes/connective tissue.
Caveat: limited replicate embryos per strain restricts power to definitively call changes, especially in small trajectories.

Connections

Uses the same combinatorial-indexing lineage and MOCA reference from the Shendure/Cao/Trapnell groups; conceptually adjacent to pooled genetic-perturbation single-cell readouts like dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits and, more loosely, single-cell-crispr-screen approaches — here perturbations are germline mutant genotypes rather than a pooled CRISPR library.
Complements single-cell atlases of prenatal development such as velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal by adding matched mutant genotypes for phenotyping.
The TAD-boundary and enhancer-deletion mutants tie cis-regulatory-elements and topologically-associating-domains disruption to concrete cell-type-level developmental consequences.

Open questions

Can further scaling (more mutants, more replicates) make developmental pleiotropy fully “decomposable” into shared modular effects?
How much of the missing statistical power for small trajectories can be recovered with deeper per-strain replication or spatial validation?
Do TAD-boundary KOs with near-absent gross phenotypes harbour consistent subtle cell-state shifts detectable only by lochNESS-like methods?

wiki/sources/humphrey-2025-long-read-rna.title: humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas
wiki/sources/humphrey-2025-long-read-rna.content:
Long-read RNA sequencing atlas of human microglia isoforms elucidates disease-associated genetic regulation of splicing
One-line: A long-read RNA-seq isoform atlas of human microglia (isomiga) that discovers 35,879 novel isoforms and, combined with a 555-sample short-read splicing-qtl meta-analysis, links isoform/splicing regulation to Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease risk loci (splicing-and-disease).
Key takeaways

Microglia (CNS innate immune cells) are genetically implicated in neurodegeneration, but short-read sequencing makes detecting genetic effects on splicing hard. This study uses PacBio long-read RNA-seq to build an isoform-centric microglia genomic atlas (isoMiGA) and augment the GENCODE reference.
From 89,285,858 full-length long reads across 30 postmortem microglia samples (hybrid-assembled with matched short reads), they identified 128,436 isoforms in 25,956 genes; 35,879 isoforms and 2,238 genes were novel (absent from GENCODE v.38).
Novel isoforms include new categories of regulatory transcripts: intron retention (highly abundant vs GENCODE), readthrough fusions, antisense, and intergenic. Notable fusion examples at AD risk genes TREM2/TREML1 and CD33.
Functional relevance shown via stimulation response (iPSC-derived microglia ± IFNγ/LPS) and brain-region specificity (subventricular zone vs cortex/thalamus), using differential isoform usage (DIU).
A multi-ancestry splicing-qtl meta-analysis of 555 short-read samples from 391 donors mapped eQTLs and multiple sQTL types (junction usage via leafcutter, transcript usage, and SUPPA splicing events). The augmented GENCODE+Novel reference increased transcript-usage and splicing-event QTL discovery.
Colocalization with the latest AD and PD GWAS nominated risk genes acting through splicing rather than expression (splicing-and-disease), highlighting PLCG2 (AD) and SIPA1L2 (PD).

Notable claims / data

89,285,858 long reads; 99.73% uniquely aligned to hg38; median read lengths 558–3,792 bp (28/30 samples >2 kbp).
Isoform classification (SQANTI): 92,557 full splice match, 18,727 novel-in-catalog, 11,607 novel-not-in-catalog, 2,142 incomplete splice match, 1,725 fusion, 731 intergenic, 621 antisense, 326 genic. Of 590 readthrough fusion genes, 36 were in the Conjoined Genes database.
Predicted coding ability varies by class: incomplete splice match highest at 82% protein-coding, novel-in-catalog 57%; many other classes predicted noncoding or NMD-sensitive.
Proteomics (mass spec on 68 microglia samples; 137,704 unique peptides) gave limited but real novel-isoform support: 69 peptides matched exclusively to 112 novel isoforms in 65 genes (incl. RNA-binding-protein genes HNRNPK, HNRNPH1, SRRM1, FXR2). Novel peptides were less abundant / in fewer samples — caveat: peptides spanning exon junctions are technically disfavored.
QTL cohort: 555 samples / 391 donors, multi-ancestry (64 non-European donors), 8.5M common variants (MAF >1%); multi-ancestry mixed-model meta-analysis. Identified 456 novel genes with an eQTL and 5,658 novel isoforms (3,545 genes) with a transcript-usage QTL at q<0.05.
Augmented reference RAISED transcript-usage and splicing-event QTL discovery but REDUCED eQTL discovery (8,409 → 5,928 eGenes), attributed partly to increased multiple-testing burden from added novel genes.
MESC heritability mediation: microglia eQTLs/sQTLs mediated nonzero AD and PD heritability but NOT schizophrenia; bulk-brain QTLs mediated all three.
AD: 32–37 of 78 loci had a colocalized expression or sQTL (PP4>0.5); 14 loci had ≥1 sQTL colocalization at PP4>0.9 (vs only 3 with caQTL). TREM2, ACE, CD33, MS4A6A favored splicing; BIN1, ECHDC3 favored expression. ~28 AD risk genes nominated.
PLCG2 (AD): a lead common SNP rs12446759 upstream of the canonical TSS overlaps lncRNA RP11-960L18.1; novel readthrough fusion isoforms (PLCG2_RP11-960L18.1) detected; a 5′ splice-site juQTL sits 13 bp from the GWAS SNP. spliceAI/Pangolin scores were small but variants in high LD may act multiplicatively. ~15 PD risk genes nominated.
PD: more sQTL colocalizations (34–35) than eQTL (14–16). SIPA1L2 implicated via a noncoding 5′-UTR cassette exon (exon 4); lead juQTL SNP rs16857578 weakens a 5′ splice site (spliceAI donor-loss 0.28; Pangolin 0.78) — but exon-4 skipping doesn’t alter CDS, so protein consequence unclear.
Caveats stated: microglia isolation can induce activation; imperfect short/long correlation; long-read isoform assembly still challenging; colocalization is not causality and sQTL variant validation remains hard.

Connections

A human-tissue splicing-qtl resource squarely in splicing-and-disease; uses leafcutter (psi_2019 branch) for junction-usage QTLs and SUPPA for event decomposition.
Variant-effect interpretation leans on spliceai (and Pangolin) for splice-site delta scores.
Sibling to existing microglia/brain sQTL sources in this wiki such as zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (sQTL sharing across tissues/cell types is a recurring theme here).
Methodologically contrasts junction-ratio sQTL mapping (leafcutter) — which cannot pinpoint isoforms or capture intron retention/poly(A) — with isoform-level and event-level quantification; motivates the long-read-augmented reference.

Open questions

How do AD variants mechanistically alter RP11-960L18.1 / PLCG2 expression and fusion-isoform usage — splice-site change, promoter/TF-binding change, or NMD modulation?
Are PD sQTLs (e.g. SIPA1L2) genuinely microglial or proxies for other CNS cell types, given sQTL sharing across tissues?
Can single-cell long-read quantification resolve isoform usage across microglial states without needing short reads?
Functional/experimental validation of nominated sQTL variants, which remains harder than for eQTLs.

wiki/sources/hwang-2016-dynamic-regulati.title: hwang-2016-dynamic-regulation-of-rna-editing
wiki/sources/hwang-2016-dynamic-regulati.content:
Dynamic regulation of RNA editing in human brain development and disease
One-line: A 2016 Nature Neuroscience Resource mapping the genome-wide landscape of A-to-I RNA editing across human cortical development (39 post-mortem brains, fetus to old age) — a sibling RNA-processing layer to splicing — which identifies three developmental editing-rate patterns (stable high, stable low, increasing) and links the brain-selective “increasing” pattern to neuronal maturation, ADAR expression, RNA secondary structure, and disease.
Key takeaways

This is about A-to-I RNA editing (adenosine→inosine, read as guanosine by the cell), catalyzed by ADAR enzymes on double-stranded RNA — a distinct co-/post-transcriptional RNA-processing layer that runs alongside, and can intersect with, alternative-splicing (editing in introns can modulate splice-site usage; the GluR-B/GRIA2 paradigm couples editing to splicing).
Surveys editing genome-wide in 33 fully-sequenced developing human brains (of 39 individuals), defining three developmental A-to-I editing patterns: stable high (~0.9), stable low (~0.1), and increasing (rising from fetal to adult). 748 sites differed significantly across six age groups; 742 of them followed the increasing pattern.
The increasing pattern is brain-selective and neuron-associated: strongest in brain vs heart/liver/lung/kidney/muscle; persists after correcting for neuron/glia composition; reproduced in fetal-vs-adult neurons (single-cell data), in vitro hESC/mESC corticogenesis, and primary mouse neuron culture; emerges with deep cortical-layer formation and maturation markers (TBR1, MAPT, SYN1/SYN2).
Regulation is both cis and trans: trans = temporal ADAR1/ADAR2 (ADAR/ADARB1) mRNA expression, which correlates selectively with the increasing pattern; cis = the degree of RNA double-strandedness around the site (increasing sites have intermediate double-strandedness). ADAR knockdown most strongly reduces editing at increasing-pattern sites.
Functional and disease relevance: editing in CDS can recode proteins (75% of CDS increasing sites nonsynonymous); 3’UTR editing correlates with mRNA abundance and can perturb predicted miRNA binding; GO enrichment in vesicle/organelle membranes and glutamate signaling. The increasing pattern is selectively disrupted in glioblastoma and (mouse) spinal cord injury.
Frames itself explicitly as a step toward a spatiotemporal atlas of RNA editing in the human brain, emphasizing primate/human-specific, developmentally regulated sites that mouse models miss (A-to-I editing is enriched in primate-specific Alu repeats).

Notable claims / data

267,766 RNA variant sites identified across 33 samples; the two known editing types (A-to-I and C-to-U) account for 94.3% of variants; A-to-I disproportionately enriched. 85.5% of A-to-I sites (115,336 / 134,914) were previously reported in other human tissues — pipeline validation.
A-to-I editing concentrated in introns, 3’UTRs, and Alu repeats; but a large fraction of CDS and 5’UTR sites lie outside Alu, so editing there is not merely Alu expansion.
742 increasing sites; 86% seen in ≥10 samples; cluster into 5 groups by fetal baseline. Representative groups for mechanism analysis: group I low (65 sites), group II increasing (58), group III high (40).
ADAR correlation with increasing pattern: mean Spearman ρ = 0.56 (ADAR1) and 0.51 (ADAR2). Double-strandedness differs among groups (ANOVA n=163, F2,160=34.66, P=3.11×10⁻¹³); group II intermediate between low (I) and high (III).
ADAR1 siRNA knockdown (B-cell data) sharply drops group II vs group III editing (t34.8=−13442 [sic], P=2.4×10⁻¹⁵); ADAR2 knockdown similar but weaker (P=0.024) — increasing sites are most ADAR-dependent; high sites least affected (suggesting additional mechanisms).
Cross-tissue: brain has by far the largest fetal→adult editing-rate increase (brain F2,147=331.1, P<2×10⁻¹⁶); heart, kidney not significant. Only brain raises both ADAR1 and ADAR2 in adults vs fetus.
Conservation: of 742 human increasing sites, 95 conserved in mouse genome; of 64 with adequate depth, 12 showed an increasing pattern in mouse brain (emerging ~E15–18 into early postnatal) — pattern conserved though numerically limited in mouse.
Molecular consequences: 21/28 CDS increasing sites nonsynonymous (incl. 1 stop-codon loss, 6 “damaging”); a human-specific PDZD7 site abolishes a stop codon (UAG→UGG; Usher-syndrome/cilia gene). Edited mRNAs had lower predicted miRNA binding energy (n=10,207 vs 10,001, t=−14.293, P<2.2×10⁻¹⁶). 3’UTR editing–abundance correlations bimodal and distinct from CDS (KS P=0.029).
Disease: glioblastoma selectively lowers group II editing vs adjacent non-tumor tissue (patient samples provided by the neurosurgery co-author); mouse spinal cord injury lowers increasing-pattern CDS sites as injury progresses. Clinical hook: GRIA2 R/G and COG3 I/V editing sites (both increasing) reported to affect cancer drug sensitivity.
Cohort/data: 39 individuals fetus→old age; six age bins (fetal, infant, child, teen, middle, old). Data at NCBI SRA BioProject PRJNA322318 (SRP076099). Caveat: heavy reliance on computational prediction (miRNA binding, protein-damage, double-strandedness measures whose biochemical meaning the authors say “remains to be explored”); disease evidence partly from mouse/reanalysis.

Connections

Sibling RNA-processing layer to alternative-splicing: A-to-I editing is presented here as its own epitranscriptional regulatory layer, but the paper notes editing↔splicing crosstalk (intronic editing altering splice-site usage; the GluR-B/GRIA2 editing-splicing paradigm) — a natural complement to the splicing focus of this wiki.
Closest sibling on developmentally regulated RNA processing in brain: kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated makes the analogous argument for splicing transitions across development (CELF/MBNL, NOVA, RBFOX), including brain glutamate-receptor and Dscam examples — same conceptual frame (which dynamic RNA-processing events are functional?), different mechanism.
Human-brain cohort/atlas siblings: humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (brain/microglia isoforms) and zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain sQTL) — all map a regulatory RNA layer across human brain; this one adds the editing layer.
Disease angle parallels scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (mis-splicing in disease) and the general splicing-and-disease theme: here it is mis-editing (loss of the increasing pattern) in glioblastoma and spinal cord injury.
Methodologically RNA-seq–based (see rna-seq); ADARs are rna-binding-proteins acting on dsRNA, conceptually adjacent to rna-structure-and-splicing given the central role of RNA secondary structure / double-strandedness.

Open questions

What is the biochemical meaning of the “degree of double-strandedness” measure, and how does distance-to-hairpin act synergistically with it to set editing rate?
What maintains the stable-high pattern in fetal life despite low ADAR expression and (sometimes) low double-strandedness — cofactors or unknown facilitators?
Which of the many primate-/human-specific increasing sites are genuinely functional, and can targeted (not just global) assays resolve their role in human-specific brain development?
Does loss of the increasing editing pattern causally drive dedifferentiation in glioblastoma, or is it a downstream marker?
How tightly does A-to-I editing actually couple to alternative splicing at these brain-developmental sites — i.e., where do the editing and splicing layers mechanistically intersect?

wiki/sources/jagadeesh-2022-identifying-.title: jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types
wiki/sources/jagadeesh-2022-identifying-.content:
Identifying disease-critical cell types and cellular processes by integrating single-cell RNA-sequencing and human genetics
One-line: Introduces sc-linker, a framework that links GWAS disease heritability to cell types and cellular processes by turning scRNA-seq-derived gene programs into SNP annotations (via tissue-specific enhancer–gene maps) and scoring them with stratified LD-score regression (S-LDSC).
Key takeaways

A functional-single-cell-genomics method connecting single-cell expression to human disease genetics: scRNA-seq → gene programs → SNP annotations (through enhancer–gene links) → heritability enrichment via S-LDSC, conditional on the baseline-LD model.
Three families of gene programs capture different facets of cells: (1) cell-type programs (genes specific to one cell type vs others), (2) disease-dependent programs (same cell type in disease vs healthy tissue), and (3) cellular process programs (genes co-varying within or across cell types, recovered by non-negative matrix factorization / NMF).
Key methodological claim: programs are mapped to SNPs using tissue-specific enhancer–gene links (Roadmap Enhancer-Gene Linking and the Activity-by-Contact / ABC model) rather than naive gene windows; tissue specificity of both the program and the enhancer–gene strategy was necessary for correct linking.
Analyzes a single tissue in isolation can mislead (e.g. an enteric neural gene looks colon-specific until CNS cells are added), motivating a multi-tissue framework.
Recovered known biology (proof of principle on blood-cell traits) and surfaced novel, biologically plausible cell–disease links, including GABA-ergic neurons in major depressive disorder, a disease-dependent M-cell program in ulcerative colitis, and a disease-specific complement cascade in multiple sclerosis.
For autoimmune disease, both healthy and disease-dependent immune-cell programs were enriched, but only disease-dependent epithelial programs were prominent — suggesting epithelial cells act in disease response/development rather than initiation.

Notable claims / data

Scale: 17 scRNA-seq datasets across 11 tissues + 6 disease conditions (MS, AD, ulcerative colitis, asthma, IPF, COVID-19); 209 individuals, 1,602,614 cells, 256 annotated cell subsets; 60 GWAS traits (genetic correlation <0.9, average N ≈ 297,000).
Benchmarking: sc-linker outperformed MAGMA gene-set analysis on a sensitivity/specificity index across five blood-cell traits matched to immune cell types (erythroid→RBC count, megakaryocytes→platelet count, monocytes→monocyte count, B/T cells→lymphocyte %).
Brain/psychiatric (prefrontal cortex, k=73,191, n=10): MDD and BMI enriched specifically in GABA-ergic neurons; insomnia, schizophrenia, intelligence in glutamatergic neurons; neuroticism in both. MDD GABA-ergic E-score 4.0 (P=1.0×10⁻⁴); top genes TCF4 (rank 1), PCLO (rank 4). Finer NMF programs localized signal to neuron subtypes (MDD→SST+/PVALB+; BMI→LAMP5+/VIP+).
Disease-dependent programs differed substantially from healthy or disease cell-type programs alone (avg Pearson r=0.16 healthy, 0.29 disease). MS: enrichment in disease-dependent GABA-ergic neurons and microglia (microglia top genes MERTK rank 2, TREM2 rank 4); AD microglia disease-dependent E-score 9.1 (PICALM, APOE, APOC1).
Reproducibility: results recapitulated in independent datasets — immune programs avg correlation 0.78 (same cell type) and disease enrichments 0.86 (E-score); brain programs 0.77 same-type / −0.21 otherwise.
Caveats stated by authors: cell types/states not exhaustive; enhancer–gene links limited to Roadmap + ABC; focus is genome-wide heritability rather than individual loci; the sensitivity/specificity index can penalize biologically real “other” enrichments.

Connections

A core functional-single-cell-genomics integration method; complements single-cell-only or GWAS-only approaches by joining them through epigenomic enhancer–gene maps.
Brain/psychiatric findings (GABA-ergic neurons in MDD; microglia in MS/AD) are relevant to developing-brain and neuropsychiatric-genetics siblings such as walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis.
Contrasts with gene-window gene-set methods (MAGMA, RSS-E, LDSC-SEG) by substituting enhancer–gene SNP linking.

Open questions

Can finer/more exhaustive cell states and improved enhancer–gene-linking strategies (beyond Roadmap/ABC) sharpen or shift the enrichments?
Which specific causal genes and loci (rather than genome-wide heritability) drive each program–disease link, and can perturbation experiments validate them?
Does the epithelial “response not initiation” pattern generalize across more autoimmune diseases and tissues?

wiki/sources/jeste-2014-disentangling-th.title: jeste-2014-disentangling-the-heterogeneity-of-autism
wiki/sources/jeste-2014-disentangling-th.content:
Disentangling the heterogeneity of autism spectrum disorder through genetic findings
One-line: A clinical review arguing that genetic findings can parse the phenotypic heterogeneity of autism spectrum disorder into more homogeneous, treatment-relevant subgroups.
Key takeaways

Frames autism as “the autisms”—a heterogeneous group whose biological variability can be dissected using psychiatric-genetics, moving from gene to brain to behaviour.
Argues that rare, large-effect mutations (not common variants) are the most robustly identified genetic risks, and that single-gene (Mendelian) disorders offer tractable models of ASD neurodevelopment and drug targets.
Reviews three organizing themes: single-gene disorders (TSC, fragile X, Rett, neurofibromatosis), the male sex bias / female-protective effect, and neurological comorbidities (epilepsy, motor impairment, sleep disturbance).
Emphasizes the heritability of ASD (recognized since the earliest twin studies) and that endophenotypes preceding diagnosis could feed back to identify common genetic syndromes.

Notable claims / data

Contributory mutations identified in >20% of individuals with ASD; 10–20% carry de novo mutations detectable by current testing; no single mutation accounts for >1% of cases (extreme genetic heterogeneity).
Male:female ratios range 1.33:1 to 15.7:1, dropping toward 1:1 in severely intellectually disabled populations; female-protective effect (FPE) supported by greater genetic load in affected females and higher symptom scores among siblings of female probands.
Neurological comorbidity prevalences: insomnia 53–78%; epilepsy 5–46% (epileptiform EEG abnormalities up to 60%); ASD in up to 50% of TSC children.
TSC (TSC1/TSC2) upregulates mTORC1; mTOR inhibitors (e.g., rapamycin) reverse cognitive/social deficits in mouse models; everolimus FDA-approved (2010) for subependymal giant cell astrocytoma in TSC.
Clinical genetic-testing guidance: chromosomal microarray (aCGH or SNP array) as first-tier for all ASD; ACMG-estimated diagnostic yield ~40%, with clinical exome sequencing adding an estimated 15–25%.
Sibling recurrence risk highly variable: ~6% average (Danish twin study) up to 32% for a next male child in a family with two affected boys.

Connections

Provides clinical-genetics context for autism as a wiki topic and complements molecular/transcriptomic ASD work in the brain-development-neurogenomics bucket (e.g., schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism, gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across, parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna).
The heritability / rare-vs-common-variant framing links to missing-heritability and twin-and-family-study-design discussions in psychiatric-genetics.
CHD8 and other high-confidence ASD risk genes discussed elsewhere (nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8) exemplify the single-gene-disorder-to-mechanism strategy this review advocates.

Open questions

Can genetic classifiers for diagnosis/prognosis/treatment stratification be validated once sample sizes are adequate, without population-structure confounding?
Are sex differences in ASD driven by biological mechanisms (FPE, sex chromosomes, fetal testosterone) or ascertainment/diagnostic bias?
Does early, mechanism-based or antiepileptic intervention (e.g., prophylactic vigabatrin in TSC) improve long-term cognitive/social outcomes?

wiki/sources/kalsotra-2011-functional-co.title: kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated
wiki/sources/kalsotra-2011-functional-co.content:
Functional consequences of developmentally regulated alternative splicing
One-line: A 2011 Nature Reviews Genetics review surveying how regulated alternative splicing networks reshape transcriptomes during development, differentiation, and cell stress across yeast, worms, flies, and vertebrates, and what physiological consequences those splicing transitions actually have.
Key takeaways

Frames the central question of the field: of the ~19,000 alternatively spliced human genes, what fraction of mRNA isoforms have a real functional impact? Much diversity is low-abundance, non-conserved stochastic noise, but a growing set of transitions are physiologically determinative.
Distinguishes two roles for alternative-splicing: determinative (the splicing change itself drives the biological outcome) versus permissive (splicing provides mRNA/protein variability that a downstream mechanism then uses).
Splicing transitions accompanying physiological change are disproportionately conserved (>40% of heart/muscle transitions conserved between mammals and birds, vs <20% genome-wide human-mouse), arguing for functional importance.
Genome-wide studies repeatedly find two largely separate gene sets: one regulated mainly at the level of total mRNA expression, the other mainly at the level of splice-isoform choice without changing total output.
A splicing regulatory network is defined as the set of events directly regulated by one RNA-binding protein; ~50 mammalian auxiliary regulators are characterized, each governing hundreds of events but determinative for only a subset.
Reviews concrete networks across biology: yeast meiosis (Mer1/Nam8/Tgs1), cell cycle/apoptosis (SRSF1, BCL-X, MCL1), stem-cell pluripotency (OCT4, RBFOX2, PTB/nPTB), EMT (SRSF1, ESRP1/2, FGFR2), heart development (CELF/MBNL/SR switch), brain development (DSCAM1, ROBO3, NOVA, RBFOX1), and stress responses (heat shock, genotoxic stress).

Notable claims / data

Alternative splicing affects ~90% of human genes, ~60% of Drosophila genes, ~25% of C. elegans genes (RNA-seq estimates).
In Drosophila development (27 stages) >60% of alternative splicing undergoes developmental change; in C. elegans (17 stages) >30% does, often in coordinated co-regulated sets.
Budding yeast: only 5% of genes (290 of 6,000) have introns, but introns are enriched in highly expressed genes — >1/4 of the ~38,000 RNAs transcribed per hour are spliced. ~45 intron-containing genes are inefficiently spliced in vegetative growth; 13 of 20 intron-containing meiotic genes are efficiently spliced only during meiosis. At least three overlapping meiotic splicing programs (Mer1 activates 4 single-intron genes; Nam8- and Tgs1-dependent subnetworks).
Cell cycle: a genome-wide RNAi screen with BCL-X/MCL1 splicing reporters found knockdown of 52 genes induced pro-apoptotic splicing of both; aurora kinase A loss degrades SRSF1; importantly the splicing response preceded mitotic arrest (parallel, not secondary).
ESCs: splice-isoform diversity is high in undifferentiated cells and decreases on differentiation. OCT4 yields three isoforms (OCT4A/B/B1) with distinct functions; RBFOX2 is essential for human ESC viability but not for differentiated/transformed cells. PTB represses neuronal nPTB via NMD-coupled exon-10 skipping; miR-124 silences PTB — first miRNA→splicing-network hierarchy.
EMT: SRSF1 upregulation produces pro-invasive ΔRON (RON exon-11 skipping); ESRP1/2 downregulation switches CD44v→CD44s (essential for EMT); FGFR2 IIIb/IIIc mutually exclusive choice is steered by ESRPs, RBFOX2, and PTB recruited via the H3K36me3 mark through adaptor MRG15 — linking the histone code to splicing.
Heart: postnatal development shows three temporally coordinated splicing-transition sets; >40% conserved mammal-to-bird. CELF1/CELF2 drop >10-fold postnatally while MBNL rises ~4-fold; forcing CELF gain + MBNL loss in adult hearts reverts >half the transitions to the embryonic pattern. miR-23a/miR-23b drive the >10-fold CELF1/CELF2 downregulation (Dicer1 deletion re-expresses fetal isoforms). SR-protein KOs (SRSF1, SRSF2 conditional → dilated cardiomyopathy by ~8 weeks; SRSF10 constitutive → mid-gestation lethality) each misregulate only a few unique transcripts.
Brain — permissive: Drosophila Dscam1 produces >19,000 extracellular variants; individual neurons express 14-50 stochastically; a single isoform suffices for homophilic repulsion but >1,000 isoforms are required population-wide for self/non-self discrimination. ROBO3’s two isoforms act as a binary midline-crossing switch (spatial restriction is post-transcriptional, not splicing-based).
Brain — determinative: NOVA regulates ~700 splicing events; uses a conserved YCAY positional activity code shared with Drosophila Pasilla; NOVA2 KO disrupts Dab1/reelin-pathway splicing causing neocortical migration defects. RBFOX1 (A2BP1) controls neuronal excitation; linked to epilepsy, intellectual disability, and autism.
Stress: heat shock inhibits splicing partly via rapid SRSF10 dephosphorylation (reversed within ~1 h) and partly via nuclear stress bodies (peak ~3 h, reversed over 10-12 h). Genotoxic stress: UV-induced MDM2/MDM4 exon skipping removes the p53-binding region (activating p53); two co-transcriptional mechanisms — reduced EWS-RNAPII coupling (more skipping) and slowed RNAPII elongation (more inclusion).
AS-NMD (alternative splicing coupled to nonsense-mediated decay): general mammalian rule — a stop codon >50 nt upstream of the last intron triggers NMD. SR and hnRNP gene families autoregulate via ultraconserved-element AS-NMD switches with “polar” feedback (SR activators: activation→NMD; hnRNP repressors: repression→NMD).
Caveat (Box 3): many RBP binding sites may be non-functional “sinks” that sequester the regulator rather than directing splicing — paralleling the miRNA-target paradox. Cell-heterogeneity caveat: <20% of heart cells are cardiomyocytes, so whole-tissue splicing transitions can be ambiguous; single-cell transcriptomics is proposed as the fix.

Connections

Foundational/contextual companion to existing review-type sources in this wiki: lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — this 2011 review predates and underpins much of their framing of regulatory networks and physiological splicing.
Deep on splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins and the splicing-code; the SRSF1, hnRNPA1, PTBP1, and DSCAM entities all appear as central examples: srsf1, hnrnpa1, ptbp1, dscam.
Touches splicing-and-chromatin (FGFR2/H3K36me3/MRG15), cotranscriptional-splicing (RNAPII elongation rate, EWS), splicing-and-evolution (conserved YCAY code, NOVA functional divergence), and splicing-and-disease (RBFOX1/autism-epilepsy, cardiomyopathy, cancer/EMT).
Complements method-oriented sources capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the and sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying: it relies heavily on CLIP + splicing microarrays/RNA-seq + computational network inference (Box 1), the experimental substrate those methods/tools formalize.

Open questions

Which binding sites of an RBP are genuine “physiological targets” versus inert molecular “sinks,” and what criteria (conserved sites, robust loss/gain-of-function response, regulation during conserved physiological change) reliably distinguish them?
For each network, is a natural change in RBP activity actually used to drive the transition in vivo, versus the regulator merely being permissive/required?
How are multiple stress mechanisms with different kinetics (e.g., SRSF10 dephosphorylation vs nuclear stress bodies) integrated during recovery?
Is alternative splicing “reset” during somatic-cell reprogramming to pluripotency, as transcription is?
How will single-cell transcriptomics resolve which cell type within a heterogeneous tissue actually undergoes a measured splicing transition?

wiki/sources/kampmann-2020-crispr-based-.title: kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for
wiki/sources/kampmann-2020-crispr-based-.content:
CRISPR-based functional genomics for neurological disease
One-line: A single-author review arguing that CRISPR-based genome editing and gene-expression control (CRISPRi/CRISPRa), deployed in massively parallel genetic screens in human iPSC-derived neurons, glia and organoids, is the key to moving from disease-associated genetic variants to causal mechanisms and therapeutic targets in neurological disease.
Key takeaways

The bottleneck in neurological disease is not variant discovery but functional interpretation: genome-wide-association-study catalogues grow fast, yet the causal genes, cell types and mechanisms lag behind. The review frames seven “key mechanistic questions” (which gene? which process? loss vs toxic gain of function? which cell types? what sensitizes/protects cell types? when in life does the variant act? how do modifiers/environment interact?).
noncoding-variants are hard to interpret: the nearest coding gene is often wrong. Example — an Alzheimer disease risk locus first attributed to CELF1 was later shown to affect SPI1 (PU.1); enhancer variants act cell-type-specifically (e.g. BIN1 in microglia but not neurons/astrocytes).
CRISPR provides a programmable toolkit spanning DNA and RNA: homology-directed repair, NHEJ knockout, dual-cut deletions, base editing, prime editing, CRISPRi (dCas9-KRAB) and CRISPRa (dCas9-activator), and RNA-targeting Cas (Cas13) for knockdown, splice manipulation and RNA modification.
These perturbations scale into pooled genetic screens (survival/proliferation, insult-sensitivity, FACS-based, single-cell RNA-seq / perturb-seq, optical) and arrayed screens (needed for non-cell-autonomous / morphological / electrophysiological phenotypes).
The most powerful application couples CRISPR screens to human iPSC-derived disease models — patient vs isogenic-control neurons, glia, brain-organoids and assembloids — enabling causal tests in the relevant human cell type rather than in cancer lines or mouse models.
Modelling disease-associated gene-expression changes with CRISPRi/CRISPRa can pinpoint the causal changes among many correlated ones, and modifier screens can reveal mechanisms and determinants of cell-type-selective vulnerability.

Notable claims / data

Limitations of prior models are argued in detail: human vs mouse differences (dopamine levels in nigral neurons; von Economo neurons absent in mice; microglial expression differences; SNCA A53T causes PD in humans but is wild-type residue in mice), and the correlative, non-perturbable nature of post-mortem human tissue. Non-human primates model genome/circuitry better but are costly and ethically constrained.
CRISPRi can achieve stringent knockdown (>99% in some cases) of coding and non-coding RNAs, is inducible/reversible, and has substantially fewer off-target effects than RNAi; it acts at transcription start sites so partial knockdown enables study of essential genes.
CRISPRa (dCas9-activator, or dCas9-p300 for distal enhancers) gives gain-of-function; CRISPRi and CRISPRa often yield complementary/opposite phenotypes (e.g. sensitization vs desensitization to toxins).
Example screens with neurological relevance: a screen for modifiers of toxic dipeptide repeats (C9orf72 ALS/FTD) pointed to nucleocytosolic transport and to protective knockdown of TMX2; tau-spreading screens implicated heparan sulfate proteoglycans, LRP1, and the ESCRT complex; α-synuclein modifiers included DCLK1 and THAP11; DDX3X as repressor of C9orf72 RAN translation; TM2D3 in Aβ-aggregate uptake.
iPSC CRISPRi screens in neurons found the neuronal essential-gene set is distinct from cancer/iPSC essential genes; recovered MAP3K12 (DLK) as a neuronal death regulator; UBA1 is broadly expressed yet its loss-of-function links to neurodegeneration. CRISPRa screens found loss of prosaposin causes neuron-specific lipofuscin formation, iron trapping and ferroptosis.
Author states his group established the crisprbrain data commons to compare functional-genomics screens across differentiated human cell types, and implemented the first large-scale CRISPR screens in iPSC-derived neurons.
Unexpected finding: applying CRISPRi/a to hundreds of neurodegeneration-associated genes showed increased NQO1 expression is neurotoxic rather than neuroprotective.

Connections

Extends single-cell-crispr-screen / perturb-seq and pooled-crispr-screen concepts specifically into iPSC-derived neurons and glia; complements dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening, gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements.
Connects genome-editing and noncoding-variants interpretation to disease-causal gene identification via genome-wide-association-study.
Touches splicing-therapeutics / splicing manipulation: notes RNA-targeting dCas can alter alternative splicing (e.g. tau).
Relevant to brain-development-neurogenomics and psychiatric-genetics / autism via iPSC modelling of neurodevelopmental disorders and schizophrenia-variant eQTL screens.

Open questions

Which iPSC-derived cell types can we actually make? Many disease-relevant types lack differentiation protocols; iPSC-neurons resemble young neurons while ageing is a top risk factor.
Transgene silencing during iPSC differentiation threatens durable CRISPRi/a machinery expression.
How to model non-cell-autonomous, circuit-level and tissue-context phenotypes at screen scale (organoids/assembloids are promising but immature)?
Can prime editing enable large-scale introduction of patient variants into iPSCs for phenotypic screens?

wiki/sources/karollus-2023-current-seque.title: karollus-2023-current-sequence-based-models
wiki/sources/karollus-2023-current-seque.content:
Current sequence-based models capture gene expression determinants in promoters but mostly ignore distal enhancers
One-line: A benchmark showing that state-of-the-art sequence-to-expression deep models (esp. Enformer) capture causal promoter signals but massively under-weight distal enhancers and eQTLs.
Key takeaways

Confronts state-of-the-art sequence-based models of transcription (Enformer, Basenji1/2, Xpresso, Expecto) with two observational RNA-seq datasets and five deep perturbation assays via in silico “reproductions” of experiments, probing whether they learn genuine cis-regulatory-grammar rather than evolutionary correlations.
Enformer captures causal determinants of human promoters well — including single-nucleotide variants, TF-binding-site effects, and core-promoter-motif perturbations from a massively-parallel-reporter-assay — demonstrating real signal about promoter cis-regulatory-elements.
Models fail to capture the causal effect of enhancers and distal noncoding-variants; the predicted impact decays as ~1/distance, so most validated enhancers and eQTLs have negligible influence on predictions.
Uses in silico mutagenesis and window-masking as a model-interpretability probe: Enformer’s distal two-thirds of its 196 kb receptive field adds only ~1% explained variance.
Diagnoses the root cause as an escalating class imbalance — the ratio of true regulatory elements to enhancer-like candidates worsens rapidly with distance from the TSS.

Notable claims / data

Enformer predicts endogenous between-gene expression well: median GTEx tissue r = 0.79; Cardoso-Moreira developmental r = 0.77; between-tissue log-fold-change r = 0.64. Mean-absolute error ~2-fold vs ~1.5-fold typical between-tissue variation.
A tiny 1001 bp window (~0.5% of receptive field) already recovers ~two-thirds of full-sequence explained variance; expanding 1→40 kb helps, distal two-thirds adds only ~1%.
Promoter tasks: Weingarten-Gabbay MPRA promoter r = 0.68; TF-motif backgrounds r = 0.75/0.73; TATA-shift RPLP0 r = 0.9.
Enhancer failure: Bergman et al. plasmid MPRA — promoter strength r = 0.81 but enhancer strength r = 0.137; promoters drive 90% of predicted variance vs 54% of measured. CRISPRi (crispri-crispra) validated enhancers — measured median knockdown effect ~20% but Enformer predicts only ~4%; for ~60% of genes no candidate exceeds 10% predicted effect.
Distal eQTLs: predicted effect <1% beyond 1 kb (median 0.5% change) with no such decay in measured GTEx normalized effect sizes.
Enformer’s promoter-enhancer logic is largely multiplicative (77% of variance as product of promoter × enhancer × background strength), qualitatively matching Bergman/Hong experimental findings.
Enformer beats Basenji2 even when restricted to the same ~40 kb window and on receptive-field-irrelevant tasks (plasmid saturation mutagenesis), suggesting parameter count + transformer architecture — not receptive field — drives gains.

Connections

A skeptical evaluation of sequence-based deep models complementary to broader dna-language-models and foundation-models framings in the wiki’s AI bucket; relevant to whether such models learn causal grammar vs correlation.
Enhancer-vs-promoter and long-range folding limits connect to genome-architecture and chromatin-conformation-capture (paper proposes adding Hi-C prediction as an auxiliary task).
The model-interpretability via in silico mutagenesis parallels interpretation-based scoring approaches elsewhere in the wiki (e.g. gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting).
eQTL / variant-effect angle relates to mutation-effect-prediction and noncoding-variant prioritization work.

Open questions

Is the distal-element failure driven by the “enhancer code” (identifying functional elements) or the “folding code” (linking enhancers to target genes)?
Can new data — more cell types, more species, or scaled perturbation assays — plus Hi-C integration or ABC-style hybrids fix long-range prediction?
Does the observed locus-specific miscalibration of variant effects reflect the model or the plasmid assay?

wiki/sources/katz-2010-analysis-and-desi.title: katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna
wiki/sources/katz-2010-analysis-and-desi.content:
Analysis and design of RNA sequencing experiments for identifying isoform regulation
One-line: Introduces MISO (mixture-of-isoforms), a Bayesian statistical model that quantifies alternative-splicing and isoform expression from RNA-seq with confidence intervals, detects differential regulation, and yields design guidelines for paired-end RNA-seq experiments.
Key takeaways

MISO recasts isoform quantification as a Bayesian inference problem: it models the generative process of RNA-seq read sampling (an isoform is chosen by its length-rescaled abundance, then a read is sampled uniformly along the mRNA) and infers the posterior over isoform abundances Ψ.
Beyond the standard splice-junction estimator (Ŷ_SJ), MISO’s estimator Ŷ_MISO also uses reads on flanking constitutive exons (latent information) and paired-end insert-length distributions, both unbiased estimators of percent-spliced-in.
Incorporating mRNA fragment (insert) length distribution from paired-end data substantially improves splicing estimates and gives concrete RNA-seq design guidelines (favor paired-end, longer inserts of ~300+ nt, low dispersion, and matched insert sizes across compared libraries).
MISO detects differentially regulated exons/isoforms via a Bayes factor (BF) computed from the posterior over ΔΨ using the Savage-Dickey density ratio.
Applied to hnRNP H knockdown human cell data, MISO implicated the splicing factor hnRNP H1 in regulation of alternative cleavage and polyadenylation (APA), validated by CLIP-seq.
Exon-centric (two-isoform) case has an analytic solution; the general isoform-centric case uses a hybrid MCMC sampler (Metropolis-Hastings + Gibbs with a logistic-normal proposal on the simplex). Implements miso.

Notable claims / data

For a gene at median GA2 coverage (~220 RPK), the s.d. of estimated Ψ dropped more than twofold, from 0.21 (Ŷ_SJ) to 0.09 (Ŷ_MISO).
Against qRT-PCR for 52 alternatively skipped exons, Ŷ_MISO gave Pearson r = 0.87; restricting to high-confidence exons with 95% CI width < 0.25 (n = 23) raised it to r = 0.96.
Differential-splicing detection: 100% of exons (6/6) with BF ≥ 20 were confirmed differentially regulated by qRT-PCR vs 21% (4/19) with BF < 20 (P < 0.0004, Fisher’s exact). Overall ~15% of alternative exons changed at BF ≥ 20 on hnRNP H knockdown.
CLIP-seq validation: percentage of hnRNP-H-enhanced exons among those with flanking-intron CLIP tags rose from 60% to >90% as BF threshold increased, plateauing near BF = 5 (5:1 odds); effect stronger for downstream-intron binding and reversed for exonic CLIP tags.
Paired-end with insert-length info reduced Ψ error by ~2-5x; at 200 RPK, error fell from ~8% (standard) to ~4%, and to ~2% at higher coverage.
Low-dispersion paired-end length distributions raised the fraction of assignable reads from ~15% to >50%, potentially tripling splicing information at fixed depth. Libraries here had dispersion d between 0.6 and 1.5.
Caveat: read-coverage fluctuations along exons depend on fragment size, so comparisons are most accurate when compared libraries share similar insert lengths; very short mRNAs (a few hundred bases) lose ~20-40% coverage in longer-insert libraries. Genes with multiple distant alternative events still need longer reads/inserts to resolve.

Connections

A foundational isoform-quantification method predating rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); both estimate percent-spliced-in and differential splicing but rMATS uses a replicate-aware likelihood-ratio framework whereas MISO uses single-sample Bayes factors.
Fits the methods/tools strand of the wiki (splicing-methods); relevant to alternative-splicing quantification and splicing-factors mechanism studies.
The hnRNP H application connects splicing-factor biology to alternative cleavage and polyadenylation; complements RBP-mechanism sources.
Builds on the earlier Jiang & Wong (2009) alternative-splicing quantification method, extending it with paired-end information.

Open questions

How well does the single-sample Bayes-factor differential test hold up versus replicate-based approaches when biological variability is high?
What is the practical magnitude/impact of increased local read-coverage fluctuations at insert lengths well beyond 300 nt?
Can short-read paired-end data, even with insert-length modeling, ever resolve isoforms in genes with many distant alternative events, or is long-read sequencing strictly required?

wiki/sources/kelley-2021-human-brain-org.title: kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular
wiki/sources/kelley-2021-human-brain-org.content:
Human brain organogenesis: Toward a cellular understanding of development and disease
One-line: A Cell Leading Edge review (Stanford, Pașca lab) arguing that the convergence of single-cell genomics and stem-cell-based 3D tissue modeling (organoids and assembloids) is enabling a new cellular/molecular understanding of human-specific brain-development-neurogenomics — progenitor expansion, neurogenesis/gliogenesis, brain evolution, and neuropsychiatric disease.
Key takeaways

The human brain forms from a neuroepithelial tube into ~170 billion cells of diverse types over a uniquely protracted developmental course; most neurodevelopmental principles were extrapolated from non-primate models because human tissue (especially late gestation) is inaccessible. Two technological waves — single-cell molecular profiling and iPSC-based 3D culture — are now opening human-specific biology.
Progenitor expansion is a key driver of human cortical enlargement. Ventricular radial glia (vRG) give rise to intermediate progenitor cells (IPCs) and outer radial glia (oRG); the outer subventricular zone (oSVZ), populated by oRG, is the defining feature of large mammalian cortices and its thickness correlates with cortex size. scRNA-seq has assigned molecular identities to morphologically-defined progenitors (oRG, vRG, IPCs) across cortex, hippocampus, striatum, midbrain, cerebellum.
Neurogenesis is protracted and regionally staggered (begins ~4 PCW in brainstem/spinal cord, continues postnatally in cerebellum); non-neuronal cells (oligodendrocytes, astrocytes, microglia, vascular) are ~half of adult CNS cells and are generated largely after neurogenesis. In humans (not rodents) oRG are a source of OPCs via a “pre-OPC” state, coordinating oligodendrogliogenesis with expanded neurogenesis.
Human cellular innovations: cell types are broadly conserved across human/mouse, but humans show compositional shifts (relative increase in cortical GABAergic interneurons; expanded cerebellar nuclei) and putative human/primate-enriched types (rosehip layer-1 interneurons, TAC3+ striatal interneurons, von Economo neurons, Betz cells, enlarged interlaminar astrocytes). Human supragranular pyramidal neurons are larger, more branched, and show novel dendritic calcium waves and HCN1-mediated membrane differences.
Molecular regulation: bulk transcriptomes show ~two-thirds of protein-coding genes are developmentally regulated, with two major temporal transitions (early-fetal PCW 6–10 regional-identity establishment; a massive late-fetal transition spanning prenatal→postnatal). Brain gene expression evolves slowly; many bulk human-vs-macaque “differences” prenatally reflect cellular composition rather than cell-intrinsic molecular change.
Molecular innovations in human brain evolution come via (1) human-specific gene duplications (NOTCH2NL, ARHGAP11B, TBC1D3 → progenitor/oRG expansion; SRGAP2C → protracted spine maturation) and (2) human accelerated regions (HARs) and divergent regulatory enhancers (e.g. FZD8 enhancer, PPP1R17 via HARs; PDGFD, mTOR signaling enriched in human RG).
3D modeling: iPSC-derived organoids (guided vs unguided differentiation) and assembloids (fusion of regionalized organoids and multi-lineage assembly) recapitulate progenitor diversity, regional identity, gliogenesis, myelination, circuit formation, and migration — and can model neuropsychiatric/neurodevelopmental disease (brain-development-neurogenomics).
Disease: rare coding/CNV variants (ASD, epilepsy, developmental delay) converge on maturing glutamatergic/GABAergic neurons; common noncoding variants are annotated via eQTLs and chromatin maps (the psychENCODE Consortium linked schizophrenia variants to adult excitatory-neuron genes; AD signals enrich in microglial regulatory regions). Organoid/assembloid models reveal disease phenotypes (Timothy syndrome, 22q11.2, Phelan-McDermid, Miller-Dieker, microcephaly, Huntington’s, Down syndrome).

Notable claims / data

~170 billion cells of diverse types in the human brain; ~two-thirds of protein-coding genes developmentally regulated in neocortex (Li et al., 2018).
