RG scaffold becomes discontinuous ~17–24 PCW (vRG truncate to tRG; oRG become the primary migration scaffold).
Human-specific duplications: NOTCH2NLB overexpression clonally expands cortical progenitors via Notch activation; NOTCH2NL deletion → premature neuronal maturation. ARHGAP11B (mice/marmoset) and TBC1D3 increase oRG and neocortical expansion/folding. SRGAP2C in mice extends dendritic-spine formation and increases intracortical connectivity + sensory learning.
HAR-linked PPP1R17 modulates progenitor cell-cycle progression (mirrors human protracted development); a human-accelerated FZD8 enhancer (WNT) increased brain size in mice.
TAC3+ striatal interneurons ≈ one-third of sampled interneurons in marmoset and human, with no apparent mouse homolog (Krienen et al., 2020). Rosehip layer-1 GABAergic interneuron lacks a mouse homolog (Boldog et al., 2018).
In vitro maturation tracks in vivo timing: organoid electrical spiking noted after ~3 months; cortical organoids cultured up to 21 months show postnatal hallmarks (e.g. NMDA-subunit composition changes); transplanted human neurons assayed to 11 months. A reported organoid metabolic/stress signature (Bhaduri et al., 2020) was argued to be culture-microenvironmental and homeostatic, not deleterious cell stress (Gordon et al., 2021).
Disease modeling examples: cortico-spinal-muscle assembloids form a functional di-synaptic circuit driving muscle contraction (Andersen et al., 2020); Timothy syndrome (CACNA1C) assembloids show inefficient interneuron migration (Birey et al., 2017); 22q11.2 neurons show increased excitability/impaired Ca2+ partly via DGCR8 loss; Phelan-McDermid cortico-striatal assembloids show reduced network synchronization only after cortical fusion; human fetal Huntington’s tissue shows progenitor cell-cycle/polarity/ciliogenesis deficits (Barnat et al., 2020).
Caveats stressed throughout: bulk-vs-single-cell composition confounds in cross-species comparisons; organoids lack microglia/vasculature/sensory input and show batch variability (especially unguided); colocalization/association ≠ causality; functional validation of common-variant effects remains hard and difficult to scale to hundreds of variants.

Connections

The NEW topic hub: this is a foundational developmental-neurobiology review for brain-development-neurogenomics — covers cell types, neurogenesis, brain evolution, and psychiatric-disease genetics in one synthesis.
Cites and is conceptually upstream of bulk developmental-transcriptome work in this wiki: li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (psychENCODE/BrainSpan; the “two-thirds developmentally regulated” and late-fetal transition claims), walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and (fetal-brain eQTLs for annotating disease common variants), parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (ASD cortical molecular changes).
Relates to single-cell developmental brain atlas siblings here, e.g. wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of and zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory, and to cis-regulatory disease mapping in groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at.
The psychENCODE work it cites underlies the existing resource page psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource.
Touches splicing-adjacent themes (alternative splicing analyses contribute to the late-fetal molecular transition) but is not a splicing paper.

Open questions

What underlies the protracted human developmental time course, and is the massive late-fetal molecular transition driven by birth/environment or pre-programmed intrinsic programs?
Are prenatal cross-species cell-type differences genuinely smaller than adult ones, or an artifact of sample size/coverage?
For most human-enriched differentially expressed genes, the causal noncoding regulatory changes remain unknown — how to systematically map HAR/enhancer effects to cell-type expression?
Can in vitro systems model the specified-neuron → mature-circuit transition (synaptic plasticity, experience-dependent wiring, complex morphology) and scale common-variant functional testing (e.g. “village-in-a-dish”)?

wiki/sources/kernohan-2024-expanding-dia.title: kernohan-2024-expanding-diagnostic-toolbox-for-rare
wiki/sources/kernohan-2024-expanding-dia.content:
The expanding diagnostic toolbox for rare genetic diseases
One-line: A Nature Reviews Genetics review of the current and emerging technologies used to diagnose rare genetic diseases — from targeted, exome and short-read genome sequencing through long-read sequencing, optical genome mapping and functional multi-omics (RNA-seq, methylation, metabolomics, lipidomics, proteomics, MAVEs) — framing how to pick, interpret and integrate tests when >50% of patients still remain undiagnosed.
Key takeaways

Rare diseases (often <1 in 2,000) collectively affect >400 million people worldwide, ~80% are genetic, and OMIM lists >6,300 disease–gene associations; yet fewer than 50% of patients obtain a molecular diagnosis with current methods. Diagnosis matters clinically (prognosis, targeted treatment, stopping ineffective therapy, reproductive/predictive counselling).
Rare-disease genetics has moved through eras — karyotyping (1950s) → Sanger (1970s) → triplet-repeat/mtDNA/imprinting (1990s) → CNV microarray/MLPA (2000s) → next-generation sequencing (NGS, 2010s). Today a mix of traditional cytogenetics and NGS is standard, with long-read sequencing and optical genome mapping emerging.
Variant classes each carry distinct detection/interpretation challenges: SNVs, indels (1–50 bp), short tandem repeats (STRs), and structural variants (SVs ≥50 bp, incl. CNVs, rearrangements, mobile-element insertions). The diagnostic path is a four-step process: clinical consultation → test selection/sampling → lab testing + bioinformatics/reporting → clinical correlation.
Three NGS testing choices — small gene panels vs exome sequencing vs genome sequencing — traded off against cost and pretest probability. Defined phenotypes suit panels; genetically heterogeneous or unclear presentations favour genome-wide exome/genome. Testing strategy also spans singleton vs trio (trio speeds analysis and interpretation but can expose non-parental relationships).
Emerging DNA-interrogation tools push past short-read limits: long-read genome sequencing (PacBio ~10–100 kb; Oxford Nanopore ~10–30 kb; 10x linked reads ~50 kb) enables mapping of non-unique regions, repeat-expansion sizing, SV detection, phasing, de novo haplotype-resolved assembly, and simultaneous epigenetic-mark detection; optical genome mapping (OGM) is an imaging (not sequencing) method for SVs from ~500 bp to whole chromosomes.
A parallel wave of functional assays interprets variants of unknown significance (VUS) rather than finding them: RNA sequencing (expression outliers, aberrant splicing, allele-specific expression), epigenomic/dna-methylation episignatures, metabolomics, lipidomics, proteomics, and multiplexed assays of variant effect (MAVEs). The authors argue these omics succeed mainly as secondary functional assays, not primary variant discovery.
The forward vision is integrated multi-omics (not just parallel cross-referencing), AI-assisted cross-omic variant nomination, and global data sharing + matchmaking (Matchmaker Exchange) to solve the unsolved ~50%.

Notable claims / data

Coverage of coding bases: exome ~95%, genome ~98%; typical read depth exome ~100×, genome ~30×, mosaic panels ~500–1,000×. Genome sequencing detects SVs but generally only >10 kb, and short reads (~50–300 bp) cannot reliably resolve repeat expansions.
Diagnostic-yield gain of genome over exome sequencing is described as “thus far underwhelming” — most diagnoses and novel gene discoveries remain achievable with current exome technology — yet genome sequencing is expected to supersede exome for its noncoding reach and shorter processing time.
~15% of rare-disease-causing variants alter mRNA splicing, a portion outside the canonical splice site (>±2 bp) where effects are hard to predict — motivating RNA-seq. Published RNA-seq cohort studies report diagnostic yields of 7.5–36.0%; the main utility is splicing analysis / VUS resolution (expression and untargeted analyses are hampered by high variability and weak control cohorts).
Metabolomics: one untargeted study accurately diagnosed 16 inborn errors of metabolism; coupling exome sequencing with metabolomics gave clarity in ~43.5% of cases and raised diagnostic yield by 12.3%. >2,000 “metabolic genes” and >19,000 small molecules; >100 rare diseases relate to lipid synthesis/remodelling.
DNA-methylation “episignatures” defined for many disorders (e.g. Sotos, Kabuki, CHARGE, Rubinstein-Taybi, ATR-X, 16p11.2 deletion) can both reclassify a VUS as pathogenic/benign and flag candidate diagnoses in unsolved patients.
MAVEs (saturation mutagenesis + multiplexed phenotyping) build variant-effect maps; the Atlas of Variant Effects aggregates data from >400 researchers across >30 countries.
Control-cohort scale for DNA is far ahead of other omics: gnomAD v2.1.1 has 125,748 exomes + 15,708 genomes (GRCh37), v3.1.2 has 76,156 genomes (GRCh38); RNA/protein/metabolite reference sets lag and are confounded by demographics/environment. Genome-in-a-Bottle provides DNA truth sets but comparable benchmarks are missing for the new omics.
Data sharing / matchmaking: >60 million people projected to have had exome/genome sequencing for healthcare by 2025; the Matchmaker Exchange connects 8 matchmakers, >15,000 unique genes submitted from 105 countries representing 137,000 unsolved cases, with ≥600 novel disease–gene discoveries and ~15% of submitted genes leading to publications. Reanalysis of prior exome/genome data is a strongly recommended, high-yield first step for negative cases.

Connections

This is the diagnosis half of the genome-medicine (crispr topic) diagnosis→therapy arc. It pairs with the therapy-side reviews pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of (CRISPR editing toolbox) and stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of (clinical interpretation of editing off-targets); together they trace a rare-disease patient from molecular diagnosis to genome-editing-based treatment.
Extends noncoding-variants: the review repeatedly names deep-intronic and intergenic SNVs as the frontier where annotation is “incredibly limited,” positioning RNA-seq (aberrant splicing/expression outliers) and ENCODE-type regulatory maps as the interpretive bridge — the clinical-diagnostics counterpart to the wiki’s splicing/splicing-qtl and regulatory-variant threads.
Reinforces dna-methylation as a clinical readout (episignatures) beyond its regulatory-biology role elsewhere in the wiki.
Touches the transcriptomics thread relevant to long-read-rna-seq: the review notes long-read platforms can detect epigenetic marks alongside sequence and enable haplotype-resolved assembly, and frames RNA-seq splicing analysis as the highest-yield functional assay.

Open questions

Will integrated (vs merely coordinated/sequential) multi-omics pipelines — likely AI-assisted — become scalable enough for routine clinical use, or is the per-sample analysis time an insurmountable bottleneck?
Noncoding interpretation: can regulatory-region annotation advance enough for genome sequencing to realize its promise beyond being “an improved-coverage exome”?
Control cohorts for RNA/protein/metabolite/lipid data lag far behind gnomAD and lack age/sex-matched, tissue-relevant references and truth sets — how are these built, and how are underrepresented ancestries included?
Which emerging technology belongs where in a standardized “rare disease diagnostic algorithm,” and how is equitable global access achieved given that millions still have no genetic testing at all?
Health-economics/real-world-evidence gaps block reimbursement — how is clinical utility demonstrated for tests from small, potentially unstable start-up manufacturers?

wiki/sources/kilpinen-2013-coordinated-e.title: kilpinen-2013-coordinated-effects-of-sequence-variation
wiki/sources/kilpinen-2013-coordinated-e.content:
Coordinated Effects of Sequence Variation on DNA Binding, Chromatin Structure, and Transcription
One-line: Allele-specific profiling in human lymphoblastoid cell lines shows sequence variants coordinately perturb TF binding, histone marks, and transcription, with TFs as the primary drivers.
Key takeaways

Genetic variation at noncoding-variants in cis-regulatory-elements produces abundant allele-specific (AS) activity that is coordinated across regulatory layers, not just gene expression.
Transcription factors are the primary mediators of sequence-specific regulation; histone modifications more often reflect, rather than define, the primary regulatory event (context-dependent behavior).
AS effects show local, short-range, and long-range (haplotypic) coordination, and are enriched at DNase I sensitivity QTLs (dsQTLs) and eQTLs, linking them to chromatin-accessibility and allele-specific-open-chromatin.

Notable claims / data

Profiled LCLs from two parent-offspring trios (+8 unrelated individuals): ChIP of 5 histone PTMs (H3K4me1/me3, H3K27ac/me3, H4K20me1), 3 TFs (TFIIB, PU.1, MYC), POLR2B; plus GRO-seq, DNase I-seq, CTCF ChIP-seq; mRNA for all 14.
AS activity: 11-12% for TFs, 6-30% for hPTMs at accessible heterozygous sites; fewer AS effects in mRNA (5%) than in nascent GRO-seq transcripts (27-28%), reflecting posttranscriptional buffering.
Most TF AS binding is motif-independent (70% PU.1, 97% MYC not explained by consensus-motif disruption); homotypic motif clusters buffer effects of motif-disrupting SNPs (41% vs 25% allelic effects; P=0.0087).
Significant parental transmission for all three TFs (r=0.44-0.75, P≤0.02) and mRNA/GRO-seq (r=0.46-0.50); weaker for active histone marks (r=0.12-0.21), stronger near eQTLs/dsQTLs (r=0.31-0.57).
Long-range haplotypic coordination extends up to ~200 kb (H3K4me1/H3K27ac with PU.1); >90% of coordination events are positive (same haplotype). Companion report (McVicker et al.) maps >1200 histone-mark/Pol II QTLs in Yoruba LCLs.

Connections

Complements MPRA and eQTL-mapping work on how regulatory SNPs act; shares the allele-specific-signal logic used in allele-specific splicing/chromatin studies in the wiki.
Relates to functional-genomics annotation of cis-regulatory-elements and to interpretation of noncoding-variants via chromatin-accessibility.

Open questions

To what extent are histone-mark changes causal vs. consequential across cell types beyond LCLs?
Can the motif-independent majority of AS TF binding be explained by cooperativity/chromatin context, and does this generalize to disease-relevant loci?

wiki/sources/kim-2020-extraction-of-dist.title: kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell
wiki/sources/kim-2020-extraction-of-dist.content:
Extraction of Distinct Neuronal Cell Types from within a Genetically Continuous Population
One-line: In mouse primary visual cortex (V1), layer 2/3 cortico-cortical projection neurons that look like a single transcriptomic cluster split into distinct cell types once anatomical projection target and feedback connectivity are added — showing gene expression alone cannot fully annotate cortical cell types and linking to brain-development-neurogenomics.
Key takeaways

Single-cell transcriptomics yields >100 putative cortical cell-type clusters, but cortico-cortical projection neurons (CCPNs, the intratelencephalic class) collapse into surprisingly few clusters despite projecting to >10 different higher visual areas (HVAs) — a mismatch between genetic clusters and anatomical diversity.
Combining projection-dependent single-nuclei RNA-seq with monosynaptic rabies tracing, the authors show V1→AL versus V1→PM L2/3 neurons differ systematically in gene expression even though they form ONE transcriptomic cluster (“continuous variability,” not discrete clusters).
These neurons obey a “like-to-like” feedback rule: each population receives long-range cortical feedback selectively from the same HVA it projects to.
Central conclusion: connectivity is an essential feature for cell-type identification; gene-expression analysis in isolation is insufficient to define cortical neuron types — a caution for purely transcriptomic atlases of the brain.
Developmental framing: L2/3 V1 neurons share birthdates and neurogenic environment yet acquire distinct adult expression+connectivity, suggesting genetic programs and activity-dependent mechanisms jointly establish the gene-expression/connectivity relationship.

Notable claims / data

Prior transcriptomic taxonomy (Tasic et al., 2018): 111 V1 clusters (60 inhibitory, 34 excitatory); excitatory split into 11 SCPN (5 PT + 6 CT) and 14 CCPN/IT clusters. Inhibitory neurons and SCPNs show cluster–anatomy concordance; CCPN clusters map to layers, not within-layer anatomy.
Triple retrograde CTB tracing: most V1 neurons project to a single area (single-color labeling: V1→AL 76.01%±2.09%, V1→PM 84.08%±3.89%, V1→LM 81.14%±4.31%). Among double-labels, AL+LM most common (19.47%±3.29%), AL+PM rare (3.84%±0.90%) — so V1→AL vs V1→PM are largely non-overlapping populations.
Laminar bias: V1→AL/V1→RL (anterior-targeting) enriched in lower L2/3 and rare in L5/6; V1→PM/V1→P/POR (posterior-targeting) enriched in upper L2/3 and most abundant in L5. Two-way ANOVA p<0.0001; 55,742 retrograde-labeled neurons across 12 mice.
snRNA-seq: AAVretro-Cre + Sun1-sfGFP reporter, FANS-sorted GFP+ nuclei, SMART-seq v4 / Nextera XT, Illumina HiSeq 4000, ~1.6M uniquely mapped reads/nucleus. tSNE shows two clusters = L2/3 vs L5+6; V1→AL and V1→PM do NOT form sub-clusters, but enrich toward opposite ends of the single L2/3 cluster.


800 genes differentially expressed between L2/3 V1→AL and V1→PM (Zinbwave-EdgeR / Zinbwave-DESeq2 zero-inflated negative binomial models). Four neuronal genes (Astn2, Kcnh5, Grm1, Cntn5) validated by retrograde labeling + smFISH; differences persisted even for intermingled neurons at matched depth (100–200 µm from pia in L2/3upper), ruling out pure laminar artifact. Caveat: substantial overlap in expression distributions — differences are statistical, not categorical.


These projection-based differences do NOT correspond to previously reported unsupervised L2/3 clusters — i.e. clustering without projection labels misses them.
Rabies feedback tracing (SepW1-Cre for L2/3, Tlx3-Cre for L5; EnvA+RVdG-eGFP, oG glycoprotein): L2/3 neurons received ~half their HVA feedback from their own target area (V1→AL 50.99%±4.47% from AL; V1→PM 40.97%±11.49% from PM). V1→PM received virtually no input from AL; V1→AL got only 10.28%±5.08% from PM. L5 mirrored this (V1→PM L5 54.47%±21.82% from PM; V1→AL L5 no detectable PM input). Feedback interpreted as consistent with activity-dependent Hebbian “like-to-like” wiring.

Connections

brain-development-neurogenomics — core caution for the topic: transcriptomic cell-type atlases of the developing/adult cortex under-resolve neuron types that connectivity reveals.
Complements transcriptome-vs-other-modality discordance themes seen in wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (mRNA an imperfect proxy for protein there; here mRNA an imperfect proxy for connectivity-defined identity).
Conceptual sibling to single-cell/sn-RNA-seq atlas efforts (Tasic 2018 V1 taxonomy) — argues clusters must be cross-validated against anatomy/connectivity.

Open questions

Does gene expression instruct long-range connectivity, or does connectivity (and shared activity among co-circuit neurons) shape gene expression? The paper leaves causality open and proposes loss-/gain-of-function tests at relevant developmental stages.
How are these feedforward+feedback CCPN networks established during embryonic/postnatal development, given shared L2/3 birthdates?
Do other HVA-targeting V1 populations with similar receptive fields or projection motifs also separate transcriptomically?
Can candidate genes (e.g. Astn2, Grm1, Cntn5, Kcnh5) be shown causal for HVA-specific projection routing?

wiki/sources/kim-2024-leveraging-single-.title: kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and
wiki/sources/kim-2024-leveraging-single-.content:
Leveraging single-cell ATAC-seq and RNA-seq to identify disease-critical fetal and adult brain cell types
One-line: Integrates GWAS summary statistics for 28 brain-related diseases/traits with single-cell chromatin accessibility (scATAC-seq) and gene expression (scRNA-seq) profiles from 83 fetal and adult brain cell types (3.2M cells) via stratified LD score regression, finding that scATAC-seq-derived cis-regulatory-elements annotations are surprisingly more powerful than scRNA-seq for prioritizing disease-critical cell types.
Key takeaways

A functional-single-cell-genomics study that builds cell-type SNP annotations from 4 single-cell datasets — fetal scATAC-seq (14 cell types), fetal scRNA-seq (34), adult scATAC-seq (18), adult scRNA-seq (17) — and tests heritability enrichment with S-LDSC across 28 brain traits (average N = 298K).
scATAC-seq annotations use chromatin-accessible peaks (MACS2 peak regions = accessible cis-regulatory-elements / TF-binding sites); scRNA-seq annotations use the sc-linker pipeline to score SNPs linked to cell-type-specifically-expressed genes via brain-specific enhancer-gene (ABC + Roadmap S2G) links.
Primary metric is the standardized effect size τ* (per-SNP heritability change per 1-SD annotation increase, conditional on others), assessed at per-dataset FDR < 5%; analyses condition on the baseline model plus the union of open chromatin regions (scATAC) or union of enhancer-gene links (scRNA) for cell-type specificity.
scATAC-seq was the more informative modality in these data: significant in 22 (fetal) / 23 (adult) of 28 traits, vs 8 / 17 for scRNA-seq.
Recovers known associations (excitatory/glutamatergic neurons in SCZ, MDD, ADHD; GABAergic inhibitory neurons in SCZ) and proposes novel ones (fetal photoreceptor cells in MDD/insomnia; fetal ganglion cells in BMI; fetal astrocytes in ADHD; fetal Purkinje neurons in insomnia; adult VGLUT2 excitatory neurons in SCZ).
Disease-critical cell types can be shared OR developmental-stage-specific: fetal vs adult scATAC results correlated (r = 0.52 for -log10 p), yet fetal astrocyte ADHD enrichment was absent in adult astrocytes (p = 0.0065 for the fetal-vs-adult τ* difference), supporting a fetal-specific astrocyte role.

Notable claims / data

3.2 million scATAC-seq + scRNA-seq profiles, 83 cell types, 4 datasets (Domcke fetal scATAC; Cao fetal scRNA; Corces adult scATAC; Velmeshev adult scRNA). Fetal donors aged 72–129 days (scATAC) / 89–125 days (scRNA); “adult” = child+adult donors past fetal stage (scATAC age 38–95 yr; scRNA age 4–22 yr).
Significant disease-cell-type pairs: fetal scATAC 152/392; fetal scRNA 9/952; adult scATAC 168/504; adult scRNA 64/476.
Notable τ* results (Table 2): fetal photoreceptor cells in MDD τ*=0.67 (p=5.26×10⁻⁴); fetal ganglion cells in BMI τ*=1.05 (p=9.68×10⁻⁴); fetal astrocytes in ADHD τ*=0.45 (p=3.93×10⁻²); adult VGLUT2 excitatory neurons in SCZ τ*=0.55 (p=6.14×10⁻¹⁰); fetal BDNF excitatory neurons in MDD (scRNA) τ*=0.98.
For 13 fetal cell types in both modalities, matched scATAC vs scRNA annotations were ~uncorrelated (r=0.01–0.06) yet their -log10(p) values were moderately correlated (r=0.24), confirming shared biology while showing scATAC gives more significant p-values.
Conditioning matters: dropping the union-of-open-chromatin condition in adult scATAC inflated hits from 168 to 246 significant pairs — the extra condition is a conservative cell-type-specificity step.
Brain-specific enhancer-gene links beat ±100kb window linking for adult scRNA (64 vs 28 significant pairs) but, oppositely, underperformed for fetal scRNA (9 vs 22) — direction is data-dependent.
Control (non-brain) traits yielded no significant enrichments, and Q-Q plots confirmed a null τ* distribution.
Limitations stated by authors: cannot universally rank scATAC vs scRNA (scRNA datasets had more donors/nuclei here); resolution limited to annotated cell types; cannot claim causality (may tag an unmeasured causal cell type); no significant cell types for autism, anorexia, ischemic stroke, Alzheimer’s; failed to recover known microglia–Alzheimer’s link, attributed to conservative conditioning + stringent multiple testing.
Released cell-type annotations (alkesgroup “Kim_ATAC”); code at github.com/buutrg/Kim_ATAC_code; analysis uses S-LDSC (github.com/bulik/ldsc) and GREAT for gene-set enrichment.

Connections

A functional-single-cell-genomics application: turns single-cell readouts into genetic-heritability annotations, complementary to MPRA/perturbation methods in this topic. Methodologically central is the use of accessible-peak cis-regulatory-elements (scATAC) vs enhancer-gene-linked expression (scRNA) as the SNP-to-function bridge.
Brain developmental-genomics sibling to li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (human brain development + neuropsychiatric risk) and to the psychiatric-genetics framing in walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and — but this paper’s deliverable is cell-type heritability prioritization rather than an eQTL/isoform atlas.
Shares the cell-type-prioritization-from-single-cell goal with chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory (single-cell regulatory DNA-protein mapping), though Chi maps TF binding directly while Kim infers disease relevance statistically.
Builds on the sc-linker framework (Jagadeesh et al. 2022) and S-LDSC / specifically-expressed-genes heritability lineage (Finucane et al.).

Open questions

Would simultaneous ATAC+RNA profiling (SHARE-seq, 10x Multiome) on matched cells resolve whether scATAC’s apparent advantage is biological or a dataset artifact of donor/nuclei counts?
Can per-cell (rather than per-annotated-cell-type) enrichment methods recover rare disease-critical cell types missed here (e.g., microglia in Alzheimer’s)?
How to fine-map causal cell types — distinguishing a truly causal cell type from a correlated tag — analogous to variant/gene fine-mapping?
Why does brain-specific enhancer-gene linking help adult but hurt fetal scRNA power, and does this reflect maturity of fetal enhancer maps?

wiki/sources/kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rn.title: kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rna-sequencing-of
wiki/sources/kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rn.content:
Nanopore direct RNA sequencing of human transcriptomes reveals the complexity of mRNA modifications and crosstalk between regulatory features
One-line: Uses high-throughput Oxford Nanopore long-read direct RNA sequencing (dRNA-seq, PromethION) to simultaneously capture m6A methylation, poly(A) tail length, isoform/splicing, abundance, and stability on the same mRNA molecules in human MOLM13 leukemia cells, producing a deep validated data resource (PRJNA1135158) for studying crosstalk between RNA regulatory features.
Key takeaways

dRNA-seq sequences the native mRNA, preserving chemical modifications, so a single assay yields m6A frequency (single-nucleotide, single-molecule stoichiometry), poly(A) tail length, isoform-level abundance, mRNA half-life proxies, and splicing — letting the authors measure correlations between features that previously had to be inferred across separate assays/samples.
The key enabling claim is depth: PromethION runs gave ~34M reads total across 6 samples (3–7M/sample), far beyond the ~1M reads/sample of typical MinION human-cell datasets, providing the coverage needed for genome-wide correlative analyses.
m6A calling used m6Anet (de novo, neural-network multiple-instance-learning); cross-validated against miCLIP-seq, CHEUI, and iM6A. m6A distribution reproduced the canonical metagene pattern (3’ UTR / near-stop-codon enrichment).
Reproduced the splicing-relevant observation that m6A is depressed ~200 nt around splice sites (exclusion of m6A near spliced junctions), and found m6A presence correlates with exon length.
Splicing analysis: Isoquant for isoform quantification, FLAIR for differential alternative splicing between control and METTL3-knockdown; four event classes (A3’SS, A5’SS, cassette exon ES, intron retention IR), with ES and IR showing the most change upon METTL3 depletion.
Headline biology: a negative correlation between poly(A) tail length and both mRNA abundance and mRNA stability — challenging the textbook assumption that long poly(A) tails mean more stable / more abundant transcripts. METTL3 depletion effects on m6A, poly(A), expression, and splicing are pathway-specific, not global.

Notable claims / data

m6A sites (control, 4 replicates): 25,125 / 11,200 / 24,118 / 18,526 at m6Anet prob ≥0.7; 11,266 / 4,748 / 11,110 / 8,327 at ≥0.9. A consensus set of 12,716 sites across 3,825 genes was detected in ≥2 of 4 replicates.
Overlap with prior MOLM13 miCLIP-seq: ~28% (≥0.7) and ~16.6% (≥0.9) of dRNA-seq sites recovered ~26% of miCLIP sites — concordance comparable to known cross-method variability (e.g. miCLIP vs m6ACE-seq). 6,564 sites shared between m6Anet (≥0.7) and CHEUI on DRACH motifs, with strongly correlated stoichiometry.
METTL3 KD: only ~5–10% global drop in methylation level but a clear drop in number of confident m6A sites; GGACT (most-enriched DRACH motif) showed the greatest reduction. 606 differentially modified DRACH positions across 510 genes (600 down, 6 up); GO enrichment in ribonucleoprotein complex biogenesis, RNA splicing, mRNA processing.
Abundance vs m6A is weak globally (Spearman R = −0.071 mean / −0.0643 sum) and depends on both stoichiometry and number of sites. Methylated mRNAs had significantly shorter half-lives; ≥5 m6A sites gave a steep stability drop. m6A also anticorrelated with ribosome association (Ribo-STAMP / RPS2-APOBEC EPKM).
Poly(A): median tail length 92.1 nt, full range from a few nt to ~400 nt; high replicate reproducibility (Pearson R 0.70–0.78); tailfindR vs Nanopolish estimates correlated (Spearman R 0.65–0.67). ~45% of genes have transcripts spanning all three (short <98 nt / medium 99–139 / long >139) length groups. Longer CDS/5’UTR/3’UTR and higher %AU → longer tails. Expression vs median poly(A): Spearman R = −0.261; half-life vs poly(A): R = −0.106 (both p < 2.2e-16).
m6A–poly(A): modified transcripts had slightly longer tails; more m6A sites in CDS/3’UTR → longer tails, but higher summed modification ratio in 3’UTR → shorter tails. Upon METTL3 KD, 186 transcripts lengthened and 100 shortened their poly(A) tails — coordinated regulation affects only a small subset of targets, not globally.
Splicing: 233–4,217 events per category; mapped to 45–599 isoforms and 16–121 genes; 171 genes with differential AS between KD and control (GO: cytoplasmic translation, nucleotide biosynthesis). Isoforms with 20–60% m6A modification and <5 m6A sites carried the most AS events; cassette-exon (ES) group behaved distinctly, and m6A-to-splice-site distance patterns differed across event types.
Caveats: analyses are correlative (not functional); poly(A) enrichment was used (claimed not to bias tail-length detection vs total-RNA control); SQK_RNA002 kit (newer SQK_RNA004 / FLO-PRO004RA noted as improvement); m6Anet favors DRACH motifs, likely missing non-DRACH sites.

Connections

A long-read counterpoint to the short-read splicing-methods in this wiki: where rmats/leafcutter/suppa infer splicing from split reads, here FLAIR + Isoquant call alternative splicing directly from full-length native-RNA reads, and intron retention is a first-class event class (cf. intron-retention).
Splicing classes here (A3’SS, A5’SS, cassette exon, intron-retention) are the same alternative-splicing event taxonomy used across the wiki’s PSI/event-based tools.
Reinforces the splicing-and-chromatin / co-transcriptional theme indirectly: m6A is excluded near splice junctions and m6A-depleted genes are enriched for RNA-splicing GO terms, linking the epitranscriptome to spliceosome activity.
Distinct from the rest of the catalog in that the primary deliverable is a dataset, not an algorithm — see entity molm13-drna-seq.

Open questions

Do multiple m6A decorations on one mRNA additively increase YTHDF2-mediated decay or YTHDF1-mediated translation? Single-molecule dRNA-seq is positioned to test combinatorial m6A effects directly.
Functional (not just correlative) validation of the poly(A)-length ↔ abundance/stability inversion and of m6A–splicing coordination.
Extending the same raw fast5 data to other modifications (pseudouridine, m5C, inosine) as dRNA-seq callers mature; head-to-head total-RNA vs poly(A)-enriched input; re-running on the newer SQK_RNA004 chemistry.

wiki/sources/klie-2023-predictive-analys.title: klie-2023-predictive-analyses-of-regulatory-sequences
wiki/sources/klie-2023-predictive-analys.content:
Predictive analyses of regulatory sequences with EUGENe
One-line: A FAIR, PyTorch-based toolkit (eugene) that streamlines the full deep-learning workflow — data loading, model training, and interpretation — for regulatory DNA/RNA sequences.
Key takeaways

Introduces eugene, organized around three stages: ETL (data), IIT (model training), EI (evaluation/interpretation), with standalone subpackages SeqData, SeqPro and SeqExplainer.
Targets the cis-regulatory-grammar problem — learning biologically meaningful sequence features by predicting functional-genomics readouts — while following FAIR (findable, accessible, interoperable, reusable) software principles.
Ships a rich model-interpretability suite: convolutional-filter annotation (TomTom), attribution methods (DeepLIFT, InputXGradient, GradientSHAP, saturation mutagenesis), global importance analyses, TF-MoDISco, and in silico sequence evolution.
Validated on three tasks spanning massively-parallel-reporter-assay (plant STARR-seq promoters), rna-binding-proteins specificity (DeepBind), and DNA-protein binding classification (JunD ChIP-seq).

Notable claims / data

Plant promoters: trained on 79,838 170-bp promoters (Arabidopsis/maize/sorghum) assayed by plant STARR-seq in tobacco leaves and maize protoplasts; reproduced Jores et al. performance, and 78 first-layer filters were initialized with plant TF/core-promoter PWMs. Inserting a consensus TATA box motif raised predicted leaf activity by ~147% on average across positions.
RBP task: reimplemented DeepBind and trained 244 single-task models over 241,357 RNA probes, plus one multitask model predicting 233 RBP specificities in a single pass; also loaded 89 pretrained Kipoi models. Number of multitask filters annotated to an RBP correlated with that RBP’s predictive performance.
JunD task: ~1,013,080 200-bp bins from ENCODE ChIP-seq (chr2 validation, chr3 test); double-stranded CNNs beat dsHybrid and Kopp21CNN models on AUPRC; the dsFCN (no convolutions) immediately overfit. Recovered JunD and CTCF motifs.
Infrastructure: SeqData reads CSV/TSV, FASTA, BED, BAM, BigWig into XArray/Zarr datasets with Dask out-of-core support; many large datasets fit in <32 GB RAM. Training uses PyTorch Lightning; RayTune for HPO.

Connections

Creates the entity eugene (the toolkit itself).
Positioned against prior packages (Kipoi, Selene, Janggu) as more end-to-end and interpretation-complete; complements deep splicing/regulatory models like spliceai by providing training + interpretation scaffolding rather than a single model.
Interpretation-first framing overlaps with the broader model-interpretability literature and connects to dna-language-models as a future direction (the authors flag language modeling, generative and adversarial LightningModules as planned extensions).
STARR-seq application ties into the massively-parallel-reporter-assay concept and its use to decode cis-regulatory-elements.

Open questions

No dedicated support yet for protein or multimodal inputs, nor single-cell-resolution regulatory syntax — both flagged as high-priority gaps.
How well do bespoke, leakage-safe preprocessing pipelines (a common source of ML pitfalls in this field) integrate into a standardized toolkit?
Can the model/dataset library scale to consortium-scale data (ENCODE Phase 4, IGVF) while staying FAIR?

wiki/sources/lau-2019-splice-junction-ba.title: lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of
wiki/sources/lau-2019-splice-junction-ba.content:
Splice-Junction-Based Mapping of Alternative Isoforms in the Human Proteome
One-line: An RNA-seq-guided proteomics method (and open-source tool, jcast) that translates alternative splice-junction pairs into compact tissue-specific protein databases to detect noncanonical and undocumented protein isoforms by mass spectrometry across 12 human tissues and during iPSC-cardiomyocyte differentiation.
Key takeaways

Bridges the transcript-to-protein gap in alternative splicing: most splicing isoforms detected by RNA-seq have never been confirmed at the protein level, and standard MS protein databases (SwissProt ~1.1 isoforms/gene) preclude their identification.
Builds junction-centric databases by extracting AS events with rMATS (rmats) — A3SS, A5SS, MXE, SE (skipped exon), RI (retained intron) — filtering on skipped-junction read counts (Gaussian mixture model cutoff, ~4 reads in heart) and replicate consistency (p ≤ 0.01), then enforcing one-frame in-silico translation (annotated frame or a frame avoiding premature termination codons) to avoid the false-positive inflation of 3-/6-frame translation.
Translated junction pairs are stitched back to full-length SwissProt canonical sequences via a 10-aa overhang so that junction-spanning peptides are searchable; orphan slices are discarded.
Reanalyzing ~80 million public mass spectra (5 ProteomeXchange datasets) across 12 human tissues recovered >1,500 noncanonical protein isoforms, including ~400 sequences undocumented on TrEMBL/RefSeq.
Demonstrates a functional consequence of splicing: alternative isoform regions preferentially overlap intrinsically disordered regions and post-translational-modification (PTM) sites, suggesting AS regulates protein function via disordered-region rewiring and PTM-site toggling.

Notable claims / data

From ENCODE RNA-seq of 12 GTEx tissues: median 72,194 AS events/tissue (adrenal fewest 66,160; testis most 91,895). AS-type distribution: SE 65.1%, A3SS 10.8%, RI 10.1%, MXE 7.3%, A5SS 6.7%.
Custom databases are far sparser than standard ones: heart database 13,816 entries vs SwissProt human 42,237 (20,225 canonical + 22,012 isoform), TrEMBL 93,555, RefSeq 113,620 — only 6.3%–17.0% the size of RefSeq. Mean across 12 tissues 11,911 entries (pancreas fewest 6,309; testis most 19,285).
Reanalyzed 1,927 MS files / 79.6 million MS2 spectra. In heart left ventricle, 13,900 distinct peptides at 1% FDR (reverse decoy + Percolator); 397 (2.9%) absent from SwissProt canonical, 142 not in SwissProt canonical+isoform, 39 not in TrEMBL.
Overall: 3,418 distinct uniquely-mapped peptides not in canonical SwissProt → up to 1,555 noncanonical isoforms in 1,189 genes. 1,385 peptides (681 genes) absent from SwissProt(canonical+isoform); 566 also absent from TrEMBL; 453 (366 genes) not in RefSeq. Testis and heart most enriched in noncanonical isoforms.
MXE has higher-than-expected translational rate (16.3% of peptides vs 7.2% of RNA events); RI lower (5.6% vs 10.0%); SE proportionate (~64.6% vs 64.8%). Titin (TTN) yielded the most isoforms.
Validation lines of evidence: 70.8% of undocumented peptides not matchable to RefSeq even with 2 mismatches (so not SAAVs); MSFragger open search (–200/+400 Da) on left ventricle found only 1 of 51 undocumented peptides confidently at 1% FDR, while 267/394 (67%) TrEMBL-noncanonical were matched — indicating added identification power. Targeted PRM with heavy synthetic standards verified PKM1/PKM2 and detected 6/12 undocumented endogenous junction peptides in heart lysate.
Example: a MYBPC3 SE isoform (peptide RTDSHEDTGILDFSSLLK) differs from canonical by only 10/1,274 residues but lies in a disordered region enriched for PKA phosphorylation sites (Fisher p=0.02; S275/S284/S304), with cardiac diastolic relevance. MYOM1 SE isoform region shows higher disorder (Mann-Whitney p=3.3e-50). Proteome-wide, alternative regions are significantly more disordered.
iPSC-cardiomyocyte differentiation (original 10-plex TMT shotgun proteomics, 3 iPSC lines, 14-day protocol): 87 quantified noncanonical isoforms (14 not in SwissProt), with stage-specific regulation (e.g., ACTN4, TPM1 isoforms incl. TPM1-kappa, NCAM1 N-CAM 140/180, PDLIM5). Transcript-vs-protein isoform logFC correlated (Pearson r 0.57–0.74 noncanonical; 0.52–0.57 canonical).
Caveats stated by authors: label-free single-junction-peptide quantification is imprecise; short-read RNA-seq produces combinatorially redundant/ambiguous junctions; bottom-up MS cannot resolve full-length isoforms; some undocumented matches with unexpected retention times are likely false positives; long-read RNA-seq and top-down MS would mitigate.

Connections

Method is built directly on rMATS (rmats) for AS event quantification and uses the percent-spliced-in (PSI) framework; complements PSI-based transcript tools by adding a protein-level readout.
A proteomics counterpart to other splicing-methods sources in this wiki (e.g., shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); where most catalogued tools quantify splicing at the RNA level, this one asks which junctions are actually translated.
Supports the broader theme of alternative-splicing expanding proteome diversity and links splicing to protein structure/function via disordered regions and PTMs.
The named software deliverable is jcast.

Open questions

How many additional translatable junctions remain hidden due to short-read combinatorial ambiguity, and how much would long-read RNA-seq + top-down/middle-down proteomics recover?
Can elution-time (retention-time) prediction be improved enough to adjudicate borderline undocumented isoform peptides and lower false-positive risk?
Are MXE events genuinely more translatable, or is the apparent enrichment a reading-frame-conservation artifact?
Better NMD prediction from frameshifted/PTC-containing junctions to decide which transcripts truly yield protein.

wiki/sources/lee-2015-mechanisms-and-reg.title: lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative
wiki/sources/lee-2015-mechanisms-and-reg.content:
Mechanisms and Regulation of Alternative Pre-mRNA Splicing
One-line: A broad review of how the spliceosome assembles and catalyzes intron removal as a ribozyme, how alternative-splicing is regulated by cis-elements, RNA-binding proteins, RNA structure, and chromatin, and how splicing defects cause disease and offer therapeutic targets.
Key takeaways

splicing removes introns via the spliceosome, a ~4.8 MDa dynamic RNP machine of five snRNPs (U1, U2, U4/U6, U5) that assembles stepwise (E → A → B → Bact/B* → C) on every intron and recycles.
The spliceosome is a ribozyme: the catalytic center is RNA (the U2–U6 snRNA base-paired complex), structurally and mechanistically analogous to self-splicing group II introns; metal-ion-rescue experiments in yeast U6 snRNA show RNA, not the prp8 protein, coordinates the catalytic divalent metals.
Alternative splicing scales with organismal complexity and expands the proteome; recent RNA-seq estimates indicate >95–100% of human multiexon genes produce at least two isoforms (see numbers below).
alternative-splicing is set up by cis-acting splicing-regulatory-elements (exonic/intronic enhancers and silencers) bound by splicing-factors — SR proteins (activators) and hnRNPs (repressors), plus tissue-specific rna-binding-proteins (Nova, PTB, Rbfox, Muscleblind/CELF) whose effect (activator vs. repressor) is position-dependent.
Splice-site choice can also be dictated by RNA–RNA base pairing (Drosophila DSCAM docking/selector sites) and, in trans, by a snoRNA (HBII-52) regulating the serotonin receptor 2C transcript.
splicing-and-chromatin: histone marks, “adapter” proteins (CHD1–U2 snRNP; MRG15–PTB), and RNA polymerase II elongation speed couple chromatin state to cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing outcomes; some transcripts are spliced post-transcriptional-splicing.
splicing-and-disease: mutations at splice-sites or in silencers/enhancers cause disease (β-globin thalassemia, SMN2/spinal muscular atrophy, ALS via hnRNPA1/TDP-43/FUS prion-like domains); recurrent somatic mutations in 3′-splice-site factors drive myeloid cancers.
splicing-therapeutics: small molecules (spliceostatin targeting SF3b), antisense oligonucleotides (TSUNAMI correcting SMN2), and CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing are emerging interventions.

Notable claims / data

Human spliceosome: ~4.8 MDa, 40–60S; >200 proteins in metazoans, ~100 in yeast. Transition B → Bact loses U1/U4 snRNAs and ~35 proteins while adding 12; Bact → C loses 2 proteins and adds 9.
Average human gene: ~8 exons, 7 introns, ≥3 alternatively spliced isoforms; RNA-seq estimates average ~7 mRNA isoforms per gene.
Ensembl (pre-RNA-seq) alternative-splicing fractions of multiexon genes: C. elegans 25%, Drosophila 45%, mouse 63%, human 88%; ~63% of mouse genes vs. >95–100% of human genes show alternative splicing.
Drosophila DSCAM can generate >36,000 isoforms (~3× the fly gene count); exon 6 cluster has 48 mutually exclusive exons selected by docking/selector RNA base pairing, with hnRNP hrp36 enforcing mutual exclusivity.
Cancer: recurrent somatic mutations in U2AF1 (U2AF35), SRSF2 (SC35), SF3B1 (SAP155), ZRSR2 (URP) in myelodysplastic syndrome/CLL; SRSF1 (ASF/SF2) overexpression acts as an oncogene in mice (mTORC1-pathway targets).
U1 snRNP is the most abundant snRNP (~1 million copies/cell) and suppresses premature cleavage and polyadenylation (“telescripting”); blocking U1 causes premature polyadenylation within ~1 kb of transcription start sites.
RNA pol II elongation ~3–4 kb/min; “slow” polymerase mutants alter splicing patterns.
Structural milestones cited: U1 snRNP reconstituted/crystallized (5.5 Å; native 4.4 Å), U6 snRNP core (1.7 Å), U4 snRNP core; prp8 has RNase H- and reverse-transcriptase-like domains near the active site.
Caveat: deep sequencing may detect low-abundance isoforms that could be spliceosome errors rather than functional products.

Connections

Foundational support for spliceosome, splicing, and alternative-splicing as a single integrated topic; ties splicing-factors and splicing-regulatory-elements to the position-dependent splicing-code (Nova RNA map).
Links splicing-and-chromatin and cotranscriptional-splicing via RNA pol II speed and histone marks; connects post-transcriptional-splicing for slow/long introns.
Connects splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics (cancer splicing-factor mutations → SF3b-targeting drugs; SMN2 → antisense).
Supports splicing-and-evolution: spliceosome evolved from group II self-splicing introns; alternative splicing correlates with organismal complexity.

Open questions

Which specific “causal” mis-spliced mRNAs (not just global pattern shifts) drive particular cancer phenotypes?
How prevalent is trans-acting small-RNA / Argonaute control of alternative splicing beyond HBII-52, given nuclear Ago-2 binds chromatin and splicing targets?
How much detected low-abundance alternative splicing is functional versus spliceosomal noise?
How does in vivo transcriptome RNA structure (SHAPE/DMS probing) shape splicing, and how do disease mutations disrupt it?

wiki/sources/lee-2024-massively-parallel.title: lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric
wiki/sources/lee-2024-massively-parallel.content:
Massively parallel reporter assay investigates shared genetic variants of eight psychiatric disorders
One-line: A massively parallel reporter assay tests ~17,800 cross-disorder GWAS risk variants from eight psychiatric disorders in human neural progenitors to decode why pleiotropic variants act broadly versus disorder-specific variants, finding pleiotropy is mediated by broad excitatory-neuronal regulation and high protein-protein network connectivity.
Key takeaways

Applied a massively-parallel-reporter-assay to 17,841 cross-disorder risk variants (from a meta-genome-wide-association-study of 8 psychiatric disorders, 136 loci) in human neural progenitor cells (HNPs), assaying both element-level enhancer activity and allelic activity.
Identified 683 expression-modulating variants (emVars, FDR < 0.05) across 103 loci — averaging 6.6 emVars per locus from ~98 tested variants — greatly refining candidate causal noncoding-variants within each locus.
Pleiotropic (mDisorder3, ≥3 disorders) emVars show broad chromatin-accessibility spanning multiple excitatory neuronal cell types, whereas disorder-specific (mDisorder1, 1 disorder) emVars have accessibility confined to single cell types.
Pleiotropic emVars alter binding motifs of transcription factors with higher protein-protein interaction (PPI) network connectivity, and their target genes are likewise more network-connected and mutation-intolerant (higher LOEUF constraint).
emVars were mapped to 488 putative target genes via meta-eQTL eGenes, the activity-by-contact (ABC) model, and Hi-C chromatin interactions; emVar genes are enriched for synaptic/transcriptional-regulator functions and for genes hit by rare protein-truncating variants in autism and developmental disorders, linking common and rare risk.
CRISPR perturbation (CROP-seq in hiPSC-derived neurons and in vivo mouse brain) validated variant-gene links (rs301804→RERE, rs4513167→DCC) and showed a pleiotropic gene (Anp32e) yields neuron-biased, highly network-connected dysregulation versus a disorder-specific gene (Kmt5a).

Notable claims / data

15,902 variant-harboring elements passed QC; 1,478 (9.3%) were MPRA-active vs 10,675 (67.1%) MPRA-inactive. Replicate reproducibility was high (mean Pearson r = 0.985 across 22 biological replicates).
MPRA-active elements were unexpectedly NOT enriched in brain cis-regulatory-elements overall, but were strongly/selectively enriched for Alu repeats; “active-noAlu” elements were enriched in CREs of every developing-brain cell type while “active-Alu” elements were depleted. All active classes were enriched in enhancer-promoter RNA interactions (EPRIs).
Only 3 of 100 tested GWAS index SNPs were emVars; GWAS p-values did not distinguish emVars from non-allelic variants, though GWAS effect size (|log2OR|) did. MPRA allelic activity and GWAS effect size were uncorrelated (r = -0.007).
Statistical fine-mapping (FINEMAP, SuSiE, CAVIAR) agreed with MPRA mainly in loci with small credible sets / simple LD; 438 emVars were in no credible set (nonFM-emVars), 198 in all three (FM-emVars). SuSiE PIPs did not separate emVars from non-allelic variants.
62% of emVars overlapped MetaBrain meta-eQTL (vs 48% non-allelic, 26% background); IDE and colocalization highest in excitatory neurons / layer 2-3 ENs. Pleiotropic vs disorder-specific emVars did NOT differ by eQTL overlap, eGene count, or cell-type breadth — so pleiotropy is not explained by number of target genes.
In vivo CROP-seq: Anp32e perturbation gave 46 DEGs (neuron-biased), Kmt5a gave 244 DEGs (glia-biased); despite fewer DEGs, Anp32e DEGs had higher PPI node degree/connectivity, supporting a network-connectivity mechanism for pleiotropy.
Limitations stated: episomal MPRA ignores native chromatin context and the 150-bp window; design misses non-enhancer effects such as alternative splicing; CRISPR validation covered only a few variants; mDisorder1 variants were 81.7% SCZ-derived (power-biased); emVar status does not prove causality (endogenous genome editing needed).

Connections

Direct methodological sibling to deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory (MPRA of regulatory variants) and shares the emVar/MPRA framework; both probe regulatory grammar of noncoding-variants.
Complements MPRA/CRE-mapping work in zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory and groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at on cis-regulatory variant function.
Connects to psychiatric cross-disorder genetics in grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across (genomic SEM landscape across disorders) and transcriptomic convergence in gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across; situates within psychiatric-genetics more broadly.

Open questions

Does the broad chromatin accessibility / high PPI connectivity signature generalize to disorder-specific variants beyond SCZ, once better-powered GWAS exist for the other seven disorders?
Are the proposed pleiotropy mechanisms (multi-stage neurodevelopmental regulation, mutational constraint, network connectivity) causal at endogenous loci, as opposed to episomal-MPRA artifacts?
What fraction of cross-disorder risk acts through regulatory modalities this enhancer-focused MPRA cannot capture (e.g., alternative splicing, distal/3D-dependent effects)?

wiki/sources/li-2018-annotation-free-qua.title: li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna
wiki/sources/li-2018-annotation-free-qua.content:
Annotation-free quantification of RNA splicing using LeafCutter
One-line: Introduces leafcutter, an annotation-free, intron-excision-centric suite for quantifying RNA splicing from short-read RNA-seq that detects differential splicing and maps splicing QTLs at far greater scale and power than contemporary methods.
Key takeaways

LeafCutter quantifies splicing by focusing on intron excisions inferred from split (junction) reads, not isoforms or exons — obviating transcript annotations and sidestepping the statistical difficulty of isoform/exon abundance estimation in complex events.
It builds intron clusters: overlapping introns sharing a donor or acceptor splice site are connected into a graph whose connected components are clusters of alternatively excised introns; usage is summarized as counts/proportions within a cluster.
Two downstream analyses: (1) differential splicing between user-defined groups via a Dirichlet-multinomial generalized linear model (likelihood-ratio test); (2) sQTL mapping by feeding intron-excision proportions to standard QTL tools (fastQTL, MatrixEQTL).
Ships with LeafViz, an interactive R Shiny browser app for visualizing differential-splicing output, labeling clusters “annotated” vs “cryptic”.
Applied at scale to GTEx (2,192 samples, 14 tissues) and GEUVADIS lymphoblastoid cell lines; substantially boosts sQTL discovery and the interpretation of GWAS/disease-associated variants.

Notable claims / data

Identified 1.4–2.1× more sQTLs than contemporary methods; in GEUVADIS, 1.36–1.46× more than Cufflinks2 and 1.83–2.06× more than Altrans.
In 372 CEU GEUVADIS samples: 42,716 intron clusters, 5,774 sQTLs at 5% FDR (vs 620 trQTLs in the original GEUVADIS study — ~9× more), 4,543 at 1% FDR.
>10% of alternatively spliced introns per tissue were unannotated (10.8% pancreas to 19.3% spleen; testis an outlier at 48.5%); 31.5% overall unannotated. 86% of novel junctions were reproduced in independent SRA samples (Intropolis). 4,616 (~15–25%) novel splice sites conserved across vertebrates (phastCons ≥ 0.6).
Speed/scale: completed all YRI/CEU comparisons within 1 h, while Cufflinks2, rMATS, and MAJIQ took up to 7.8 h, 55.7 h, and 66.2 h on the largest comparison; <400 MB RAM vs MAJIQ’s >50 GB — an order-of-magnitude+ improvement over MAJIQ.
Better P-value calibration (near-uniform under permutation, like rMATS; Cufflinks2 overly conservative, MAJIQ posteriors miscalibrated) and lower false-negative rates in simulation.
5,070 tissue-regulated GTEx clusters at 10% FDR (|effect| > 1.5); detects differential splicing even with few replicates (885 clusters from 4 vs 4 samples; 98% replicated in a 220-sample analysis). Tissue-biased intron excisions are broadly conserved across mammalian organs.
sQTLs are highly shared across tissues (75–93% replication across pairs), and S-PrediXcan over 40 complex-trait GWASs increased associated disease genes at 5% FDR by an average 2.1-fold vs gene expression alone (e.g., CD40 newly implicated in rheumatoid arthritis).
Caveats: does not capture alternative transcription-start sites or polyadenylation (not reflected in intron excision); WASP filtering of allele-specific reads is needed before sQTL mapping; long reads may help resolve clusters for gene families with multi-mapping.

Connections

Defines the leafcutter entity; sits in the splicing-methods / percent-spliced-in family of differential-splicing callers alongside rmats (which it benchmarks against, finding comparable P-value calibration) and the isoform-assembly tool cufflinks (Cufflinks2, benchmarked and outperformed on sQTLs).
Avoids the library-size normalization required by dexseq’s exon-count model via the implicit normalization of its multinomial likelihood.
Its intron-centric, split-read view is shared with MAJIQ; LeafCutter’s main edge is scalability and adaptation to sQTL mapping.
Heavily relevant to splicing-and-disease and population/sQTL genetics: links genetic variation to mechanism via GWAS interpretation.

Open questions

How would LeafCutter’s intron-excision representation extend to long-read technologies, especially for resolving clusters in multi-mapping gene families?
What fraction of the thousands of conserved novel splice sites are genuinely functional vs splicing noise, and how would one test this?
How do intron-cluster quantifications compare to event-based ΔPSI callers when there is no one-to-one mapping between methods’ splicing events?

wiki/sources/li-2018-integrative-functio.title: li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis
wiki/sources/li-2018-integrative-functio.content:
Integrative functional genomic analysis of human brain development and neuropsychiatric risks
One-line: The PsychENCODE/BrainSpan developmental flagship — a multimodal (mRNA-seq, smRNA-seq, scRNA-seq/snRNA-seq, DNA methylation, histone/CTCF ChIP-seq, genotype) atlas of 60 postmortem human brains spanning 5 postconceptional weeks to 64 years, revealing a cup-shaped transcriptomic trajectory anchored on a “late fetal transition” and showing how neuropsychiatric GWAS risk converges on specific developmental coexpression modules and cell types; alternative splicing is one of the molecular layers tracked across development.
Key takeaways

Generated and integrated genomics across 16 brain regions (11 neocortical areas + HIP, AMY, STR, MD thalamus, CBC) and the full prenatal-to-adult span: 1230 bulk samples from 48 brains plus 18,288 single cells/nuclei from 12 brains, across 60 de-identified, histopathologically unremarkable donors of both sexes and multiple ancestries.
A global “cup-shaped” (hourglass-like) transcriptomic pattern: high inter-/intra-regional variation in embryonic–mid-fetal ages, a sharp late fetal transition (window W5, ~35 PCW–0.3 PY) of collapsing regional differences, then renewed divergence in childhood–adolescence. Most protein-coding genes are temporally (67.8%) or spatially (54.5%) differentially expressed.
Alternative splicing tracks this developmental program: global splicing (the exon inclusion ratio, percent spliced in / PSI; see percent-spliced-in) is higher prenatally than postnatally, and genes with the highest interregional expression variation in a given window also showed higher PSI — leading the authors to argue the late fetal transition “may be amplified by alternative splicing.”
68 RNA-binding proteins selected for their roles in RNA splicing (cf. the rna-binding-proteins / splicing-factors layer) themselves show a late fetal transition in expression by hierarchical clustering, consistent with a developmentally regulated splicing-regulatory program.
Neuropsychiatric GWAS risk converges developmentally: integrating GWAS + Hi-C + H3K27ac and WGCNA coexpression modules, disease risk genes (MEF2C, SATB2, SOX5, TCF4, TSHZ3, ZNF184, NR4A2, etc.) concentrate in a small number of spatiotemporally dynamic modules — most prominently module ME37, enriched for SCZ, IQ, neuroticism, ASD and broader neurodevelopmental-disorder risk and specific to deep-layer neocortical excitatory neurons. This grounds the source in splicing-and-disease’s neuropsychiatric wing (risk → developmental context), even though the disease mechanism here is largely regulatory/coexpression rather than splice-variant-based.

Notable claims / data

Sampling: transcriptome from 607 high-quality tissue mRNA-seq samples (16 regions, 41 brains, 8 PCW–40 PY); smRNA-seq on 278 QC-passing samples (16 regions, 22 postnatal brains); scRNA-seq from 1195 cells (9 brains, 5–20 PCW); snRNA-seq from 17,093 nuclei of adult DFC/DLPFC (3 brains). Epigenome: HumanMethylation450 in 269 postnatal samples; ChIP-seq for H3K4me3, H3K27me3, H3K27ac, and CTCF in DFC and CBC subsets.
Splicing-specific evidence is descriptive, not QTL-based. This paper quantifies global PSI dynamics and splicing-RBP expression across development (figs. S18–S19, table S7); it does not itself run eQTL/sQTL/TWAS. The companion comprehensive PsychENCODE resource paper (Wang et al., Science 362, eaat8464, 2018) houses the eQTL/isoform-QTL/cis-QTL integration — only referenced indirectly here.
Late fetal transition is supported by MDS/AC-PCA on gene and isoform levels, intra-/inter-regional Pearson correlation, and cell-type deconvolution; W5 samples cluster with early-postnatal rather than late-mid-fetal samples. Cerebellum (CBC) is the most transcriptomically distinct region and, once excluded, the cup-shape sharpens.
Disease convergence: partitioned LD-score regression found SNP heritability for SCZ, IQ and neuroticism enriched specifically in DFC- (not CBC-) specific H3K27ac elements, but no enrichment for non-neural traits (height, HBA1C/diabetes). 7/73 modules captured early-onset disorder risk (ADHD, SCZ, MDD); 5 modules captured adult-onset (AD, PD), the latter enriched for glial signatures (ME3, ME7 with AD risk).
Caveat raised by the authors: little overlap between GWAS-risk modules and modules differentially expressed in SCZ/BD/ASD postmortem brains — implying a possible decoupling of primary genetic causes from secondary postmortem expression changes, and emphasizing the value of studying neurotypical development.
Cross-species: a companion macaque study (Zhu et al., Science 362, eaat8077) shows the same global pattern, framing the late fetal transition as a conserved catarrhine-primate feature.

Connections

This is the developmental/spatiotemporal anchor for the brain-splicing cluster: it provides the prenatal-vs-postnatal PSI dynamics that brain-sQTL atlases sit on top of. Directly complements zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (GTEx brain-region sQTLs) and park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative (population splicing variation) — Li 2018 supplies the developmental axis those QTL studies lack.
The “developmentally regulated splicing program” framing connects to kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmental splicing switches) and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (AS as a developmental regulator).
Its neuropsychiatric-risk integration links to splicing-and-disease; the regulatory/coexpression-module mechanism here is a contrast to mis-splicing-centric disease accounts such as scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.
Introduces the psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource entity (development.psychencode.org / brainspan.org), a reusable multimodal human-brain data resource.
Because the paper itself does not generate sQTL/TWAS but motivates them, it bridges to splicing-qtl conceptually rather than methodologically.

Open questions

The paper measures global PSI but not which exons switch or which trans-regulators drive them developmentally — what are the specific developmental splicing events, and do they overlap with the brain sQTLs reported in zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls?
Does the prenatal-high PSI signal reflect genuine isoform regulation or shifting cell-type composition (progenitor- vs neuron-enriched isoform programs) that bulk tissue confounds?
Could splice-variant QTLs (rather than the regulatory-element/coexpression signals used here) explain additional GWAS heritability for SCZ/ASD in the ME37 deep-layer-excitatory-neuron context?
Given the decoupling of GWAS-risk modules from postmortem-DEX modules, are disease-relevant splicing changes primary (developmental) or secondary (reactive) — and at which developmental window do they act?

wiki/sources/li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-.title: li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-m6a-promotes-local
wiki/sources/li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-.content:
Super-enhancer RNA m6A promotes local chromatin accessibility and oncogene transcription in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
One-line: In pancreatic cancer, m6A methylation of super-enhancer RNAs recruits an H3K4 methyltransferase that opens local chromatin-accessibility and drives oncogene transcription.
Key takeaways

Maps the landscape of super-enhancer RNA (seRNA) m6A modification in PDAC and defines a CFL1–METTL3–seRNA m6A–YTHDC2/MLL1 regulatory axis linking the epitranscriptome to the epigenome at cis-regulatory-elements.
The actin-depolymerizing protein CFL1, overexpressed in tumor, acts as a non-canonical METTL3 cofactor guiding m6A deposition onto seRNAs — an unexpected role for an RNA-binding cytoskeletal protein in the nucleus.
m6A seRNAs are read by YTHDC2 (not YTHDC1), which recruits the H3K4 methyltransferase MLL1 (KMT2A) to catalyze H3K4me3 cotranscriptionally, augmenting local chromatin-accessibility and downstream oncogene expression.
Provides a disease-context example of noncoding-RNA m6A modification as a layer of gene-regulatory control over super-enhancer function.

Notable claims / data

18,311 super-enhancers annotated from H3K27ac ChIP-seq; 17,467 seRNAs expressed in >10% of samples; 5,375 (30.77%) carry m6A. m6A-seq run on 65 PDAC (33 paired + 32 unpaired) and 33 normal tissues.
Of 410 differential m6A seRNAs, 398 (97.07%) were hyper-m6A in PDAC vs normal; CFL1 RNA scored higher than all known m6A writers for correlation with hyper-m6A seRNA levels.
CFL1-KO in PANC-1 lowered m6A on 3,217 (77.07%) seRNAs, overlapping 69.10% of PDAC hyper-m6A seRNAs; METTL3-KO hypomethylated 83.99% of seRNAs. CFL1 binds METTL3 via its ZnF1 domain (aa 259–298), RNA/DNA-independent.
CFL1-KO reduced H3K4me3 at 2,382 peaks (262 increased); 66.54% of CFL1-related hypo-m6A seRNAs overlapped decreased-H3K4me3 regions. YTHDC2-KO decreased 2,229 H3K4me3 peaks; YTH-pocket mutation W1360A abolished m6A-seRNA recognition.
ATAC-seq: 12,201 / 16,832 / 22,201 regions lost accessibility on CFL1 / YTHDC2 / MLL1 KO, coregulated at 10,300 shared ATAC-loss regions (intron / distal intergenic / promoter). 539 target genes enriched in hypoxia, mTORC1, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, glycolysis, EMT pathways.
Clinical: high CFL1 associated with shorter overall survival across two patient sets (combined n=227; HR 2.58, 95% CI 1.85–3.59); 16 hyper-m6A seRNAs correlated with shorter progression-free survival. CFL1/METTL3/YTHDC2 KO suppressed proliferation, migration, invasion, metastasis.
Caveat: total m6A-seq captures only steady-state RNA, missing degraded nascent seRNAs; authors flag MINT-seq as needed for precise nascent seRNA methylome profiling.

Connections

Extends the theme of super-enhancers and cis-regulatory-elements regulating gene expression, adding an RNA-modification control layer.
Complements work on chromatin-accessibility as a readout of local epigenetic state, here driven by seRNA m6A rather than DNA sequence.
Illustrates rna-binding-proteins (CFL1 as writer cofactor, YTHDC2 as reader) mediating chromatin remodeling.

Open questions

Does seRNA m6A modulate R-loop / three-stranded DNA–RNA hybrid homeostasis at enhancers (as suggested by AANCR/APOE eRNA precedent)?
How general is the CFL1–YTHDC2/MLL1 axis across other cancers with CFL1 overexpression, and is it therapeutically targetable?
What determines seRNA-specific vs mRNA-specific METTL3 targeting given CFL1’s selective effect on seRNA (not global mRNA) m6A?

wiki/sources/li-2026-the-contribution-of.title: li-2026-the-contribution-of-short-tandem
wiki/sources/li-2026-the-contribution-of.content:
The contribution of short tandem repeats to splicing variation in the human cortex
One-line: A human dorsolateral-prefrontal-cortex (DLPFC) sQTL study that, for the first time at scale and including homopolymers, maps short tandem repeats (STRs) whose copy number controls nearby alternative splicing — yielding the splicestr catalog of 146,969 STR–splice associations, fine-mapped candidate-causal repeats, an RBP-binding mechanism, and co-localization with brain-disorder GWAS.
Key takeaways

Built a brain splicing-qtl resource from 336 HBCC DLPFC samples (194 controls, 142 bipolar/schizophrenia cases) with both SNP genotypes and deep RNA-seq; imputed 445,720 autosomal STRs from a SNP-STR reference panel and tested STR dosage vs exon percent-spliced-in (PSI) for STRs within 100 kb of a splice event’s constitutive exons.
Identified 146,969 splicestrs at 5% FDR (51,343 unique STRs × 25,230 splicing events) from 6,143,556 tests; splicing quantified with rmats across SE/MXE/A3SS/A5SS/RI event classes (see rna-seq).
Key contribution over prior brain STR–splicing work: it retains homopolymers (~50% of all STRs), which Cui et al. excluded, and uses larger/deeper data than the GTEx-based Hamanaka et al. study — so the majority of associations are novel.
Three orthogonal fine-mappers (ANOVA, FINEMAP, Lasso) prioritized candidate-causal repeats: 21,893 leniently (≥1 method) and 1,313 strictly (all three) fine-mapped spliceSTRs. Candidate-causal repeats are longer, T-rich, and enriched within 100 bp upstream of the affected exon — consistent with shaping the polypyrimidine tract.
A mechanistic subset acts via rna-binding-proteins: fine-mapped spliceSTRs overlap RBP eCLIP binding sites, and repeat length predicts RBP binding probability — recapitulating HNRNPL–(AC)n and TARDBP–(GT)n relationships.
Co-localization with GWAS for 6 brain disorders linked spliceSTRs to disease, recapitulating a (GT)n spliceSTR for PLEKHA1 (Alzheimer’s) and identifying a novel intronic (AGG)n spliceSTR controlling SEPTIN3 exon-2 skipping co-localized with schizophrenia (see splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

Replication: of HBCC spliceSTRs testable in 4 GTEx tissues (n=34,828 overlapping), effect sizes correlate strongly (Pearson r > 0.7, P < 1×10⁻¹⁰⁰ all tissues); strongest with GTEx brain frontal cortex BA9 (r=0.79), weakest with whole blood (r=0.70). Cross-study overlap was small (1,067–4,825 shared) due to differing TR-callers (rmats + HipSTR here vs GangSTR / intron-based elsewhere) but shared effect sizes still correlate (r > 0.47).
Of 25,230 events with a significant spliceSTR, all but 44 also had a significant SNP signal; those 44 STR-only events were weak and dropped — motivating joint STR+SNP fine-mapping.
Fine-mapping counts: 17,524 pass ANOVA (FDR<10%), 4,647 pass FINEMAP (PIP>0.5), 7,830 pass Lasso. Strict set most enriched within target-gene transcribed regions (OR=2.2, P=5.3×10⁻³⁶) and especially ≤100 bp upstream of the affected exon (OR=25.9, P=2.8×10⁻¹⁵). CG-rich units (CCG OR=10.6, CGG OR=5.7) enriched; consistent T-rich strand bias on the coding strand.
RBP binding (eCLIP, 129 RBPs, K562+HepG2): leniently fine-mapped spliceSTRs enriched at binding sites of 32 RBPs (6 for the strict set). 15 repeat-unit–RBP pairs (4 units, 13 RBPs) enriched; (T)n most common (7 RBPs). Length→binding associations: 17/98 pairs significant; HNRNPL–(AC)n positive (adj P=3.1×10⁻³⁰), TARDBP–(GT)n positive (adj P=9.9×10⁻⁵); some negative associations possibly mapping artifacts.
GWAS co-localization (PP.H4>50% + spliceSTR in LD r²>0.1 with lead variant): 6,111 event–trait pairs tested → 96 gene-trait pairs (11 strict); schizophrenia had the most (60). PLEKHA1 exon-12 skipping ↔ AD (PP.H4=88.97%; (GT)n at chr10:122429495, FINEMAP PIP=0.96, r²=0.71 with rs7908662). SEPTIN3 exon-2 skipping ↔ SCZ (PP.H4=65.3%; intronic (AGG)n at chr22:41978894, strictly fine-mapped, 2.7 kb upstream); exon-2 PSI and the excluding isoform differ between cases and controls (Mann-Whitney P=1.3×10⁻³ and P=0.02). The (AGG)n repeat overlaps no analyzed RBP site but AGG repeats can form g-quadruplex structures, a proposed alternate mechanism.
Methods stack: Beagle 5.2 STR imputation (hg19→hg38 LiftOver); STAR 2.7.9a + GENCODE v38 alignment, RSEM isoform quantification, rmats v4.1.2 PSI; OLS (statsmodels) with age/sex/read-count/10 genotype-PCs/20 PEER-factor covariates; GTEx replication via HipSTR + TRtools genotyping.
Caveats (authors): single brain region, modest n, limited power for rare events; mostly European ancestry; imputation limited to short-read-spannable repeats (no large expansions / VNTRs); RBP mechanism needs experimental validation; eCLIP positional biases may inflate downstream-shift patterns.

Connections

STR-based counterpart to the SNP/indel brain sQTL atlas zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls and the population-blood sQTL→pQTL→disease resource tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants (which it shares a near-identical title with); together these extend splicing-qtl from SNPs toward repeats. See also park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative.
Splicing quantified event-wise with rmats (PSI), unlike the intron-ratio approaches in the studies it benchmarks against — a methodological axis affecting cross-study overlap.
Mechanistic core (repeat length tunes rna-binding-proteins affinity, e.g. HNRNPL/TARDBP; polypyrimidine-tract length) connects to splicing-factors and to RBP-binding resources like encode-rbp-map / van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional.
Disease relevance (Alzheimer’s, schizophrenia) sits in splicing-and-disease; the SEPTIN3 (AGG)n → g-quadruplex hypothesis offers a non-RBP, structure-based route.
Introduces the splicestr entity (the catalog/resource and the term itself).

Open questions

Will direct STR genotyping from WGS/long reads (vs short-read-limited imputation) recover large expansions and VNTRs missed here, and change the spliceSTR landscape?
Does the SEPTIN3 (AGG)n spliceSTR drive schizophrenia risk through G-quadruplex formation, and can this be validated experimentally?
How much of the catalog replicates in cell-type-resolved or multi-region brain data, given the single-region, bulk DLPFC design?
Beyond RBP-affinity, how many spliceSTRs act via DNA/RNA secondary structure or folding energy — a mechanism the authors flag as untested genome-wide?

wiki/sources/liang-2026-single-cell-mult.title: liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron
wiki/sources/liang-2026-single-cell-mult.content:
Single-cell multiomics of neuron activation reveals context-specific genetics of brain disorders
One-line: Single-nucleus RNA+ATAC multiomics of iPSC-derived excitatory and inhibitory neurons from 100 donors, stimulated with KCl (0/1/6 h) to model neuronal activation, showing that most genetic effects relevant to neuropsychiatric disorders (NPD) are context-specific — eQTLs, caQTLs, allele-specific open chromatin (ASoC), and cTWAS-prioritized risk genes appear or strengthen only upon activation, with caQTLs explaining a far larger share of NPD heritability than eQTLs and activation implicating cholesterol/lipid metabolism in disease.
Key takeaways

Models activity-dependent gene regulation at population scale: glutamatergic (iGlut) + GABAergic (iGABA) neurons differentiated from 100 human iPSC lines, cocultured with rat astrocytes, depolarized with 53 mM KCl for 1 or 6 h, then assayed by single-nucleus multiomics (snRNA-seq + snATAC-seq) at 0/1/6 h — the resting post-mortem brain misses biology that only switches on with activity.
1,053,422 nuclei (651,012 passed QC) resolve three neural subtypes — GABA, NEFM⁺ Glut (npglut), NEFM⁻ Glut (nmglut) — each showing widespread activation-induced transcriptomic + epigenomic change: early response genes (ERGs, peak ~1 h, e.g. FOS, NPAS4) then late response genes (LRGs, peak ~6 h, e.g. BDNF, VGF).
Gene expression and chromatin accessibility are often discordant — “epigenetic memory” (open chromatin persists after expression drops) and “chromatin priming” (accessibility opens before expression rises), complicating the activation regulatory code (chromatin-accessibility).
ERG clusters are enriched for cholesterol-biosynthesis genes and NPD GWAS signal; LRG clusters for axon guidance / synaptic processes — surfacing lipid metabolism as an activation-regulated axis of NPD.
Activation-dependent gene regulatory networks (GRN inference) highlight ASD-risk transcription factors MEF2C, TCF4, RORB, MLXIP; CRISPR knockout of RORB, MEF2C, TCF4 validated predicted ASD-target dysregulation (autism).
Mapped stimulation-specific molecular QTLs: eQTLs, chromatin-accessibility QTLs (caQTLs), and ASoC variants — many detectable only after activation. caQTLs explained ~25% of SCZ heritability vs ~10% for eQTLs, and stimulation-specific QTLs carried stronger GWAS enrichment.
Causal-TWAS (ctwas) integrating these QTLs with GWAS prioritized putatively causal NPD risk genes predominantly under stimulated conditions (e.g. CPT1C for SCZ), and SCZ patient–derived neurons up-regulated cholesterol synthesis upon activation.

Notable claims / data

Cohort/assay: 100 iPSC lines → Day-35 iGlut (NEUROG2) + iGABA (ASCL1+DLX2) neurons + rat astrocytes; 53 mM KCl, 0/1/6 h; 10x Genomics Single-Cell Multiome (GEX+ATAC). Subtype counts: GABA n=251,501; npglut n=159,735; nmglut n=137,564. iGlut/iGABA most resemble second-trimester–to–2-year human neurons.
OCR–gene links: 8,522 OCR–gene pairs (4,017 genes) in GABA, 6,921 (3,625) nmglut, 7,492 (3,651) npglut; 43–49% of “coactivation” OCR–gene pairs overlap activity-by-contact (ABC) enhancer–gene pairs (>2.5-fold enrichment). ~150,000–300,000 OCR peaks across types/times, 10–22% stimulation-specific; only DA peaks at 1/6 h (not static peaks) showed NPD GWAS enrichment.
GRN/TF: 145 candidate early-response TF regulators (half shared across all cell types); only 6 of 64 late-response TFs shared across cell types (late response is more cell-type-specific, as with LRGs). GABA-specific TCF4 (SCZ master regulator) elevated even before stimulation.
eQTL: 1,316–4,113 eGenes per context (cell type × time); 8–11% of eGenes only in stimulated states; eGene numbers larger from stimulated conditions; 19–61% of eGenes shared with GTEx brain; effect sizes correlate r ≈ 0.71–0.76 with GTEx cerebellum. Dynamic eGenes: 288–976 across contexts; dynamic eQTLs more cell-type-specific and less shared with GTEx than static — i.e. activation reveals QTLs missed by bulk brain.
caQTL/ASoC: 1,800–11,600 cPeaks (≥1 caQTL) per context, >2× more upon stimulation; 5,461–9,832 caQTLs dynamic. 4,900–18,700 ASoC variants across nine contexts; caQTL vs ASoC effect sizes r=0.82; ASoC enriched 30–80-fold in eQTLs of the matching context. Stimulation-specific ASoC enriched in enhancers and AP-1 (FOS/JUNB) motifs at 1/6 h vs ASCL1/DLX1/2, CUX2 at 0 h.
Heritability: caQTLs explained substantially larger NPD h²g than eQTLs (≈25% vs ≈10% for SCZ cPeaks), even controlling for eQTL effects — chromatin variants capture regulatory effects expression QTLs miss.
cTWAS: SCZ had most candidate genes (48 at PIP>0.5; 19 high-confidence PIP>0.8). Most SCZ/NPD cTWAS genes are “dynamic” (PIP higher in stimulated states). Only a few overlap GTEx-brain-derived TWAS genes (e.g. FOXN2, NPIPB2, TDRD6, ZNF823) — stimulation-driven eQTLs discover risk genes static brain QTLs miss. MADL1L SCZ cPeak driven by a single caQTL.
Cholesterol/SCZ: CPT1C (carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1C) is a SCZ cTWAS gene whose eQTL effect appears chiefly at 1 h stimulation in nmglut; it maintains neuronal lipid metabolism for synaptic activity.
