SCZ C6 ERG module (cholesterol-biosynthesis-enriched, NPD-GWAS-enriched): 5 of 6 cholesterol genes (e.g. INSIG1, HMGCS1, MSMO1, HMGCR, CYP51A1) differentially expressed in SCZ neurons only upon activation; SREBF1/SREBF2 TFs (lipid/cholesterol regulators) have targets enriched for ASD genes.
Caveats (authors): iPSC QTL mapping may be underpowered; KCl is a strong, possibly non-physiological proxy for in vivo neural activation; only two stimulation time points; some activity-dependent genes (e.g. FOS) show diurnal rhythmicity that could confound; statistical prioritization ≠ definitive causal inference — functional validation still required.

Connections

Activation as the missing context. This is the wiki’s first population-scale activity-dependent genetics dataset — it operationalizes neuronal-activation and supplies the dynamic / context-specific QTL evidence that static post-mortem brain QTL atlases (e.g. bryois-2022-cell-type-specific-cis-eqtls-in-eight, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls) structurally cannot see.
Chromatin > expression for heritability. caQTLs/ASoC explaining ~2.5× more SCZ h²g than eQTLs extends chromatin-accessibility as the prime substrate for noncoding-variant interpretation, and speaks to the missing-heritability debate (context-specific regulatory variance is part of the “missing” share).
Causal-gene prioritization. ctwas here is the disease-mapping engine, a cousin of TWAS/isotwas; it complements direct functional assays (massively-parallel-reporter-assay, pooled-crispr-screen) — and the paper’s own CRISPR-KO validation of RORB/MEF2C/TCF4 closes that loop for autism.
Cholesterol thesis. cholesterol-and-brain-disorders connects an unexpected metabolic axis (lipid/cholesterol synthesis, CPT1C) to SCZ/ASD, joining the synaptic/developmental story that dominates psychiatric-genetics.
A resource-tagged iPSC-neuron activation atlas, sibling to the chromatin-accessibility / single-cell atlases the artificial-intelligence topic’s models learn from.

Open questions

Does KCl depolarization faithfully recapitulate physiological/in-vivo neural activity, and would patterned or receptor-driven stimulation (and more time points) reveal additional dynamic QTLs?
Is cholesterol/lipid dysregulation a cause or consequence of NPD-relevant neuronal activation — and could cholesterol-targeting drugs (the CPT1C/SREBF axis) act on clinical symptoms?
How much further NPD “missing heritability” is hidden in other unmodeled contexts (developmental stage, other cell types, other stimuli)?
Will functional validation confirm the cTWAS-prioritized causal genes (e.g. CPT1C, MADL1L, ZNF823) beyond statistical prioritization?

wiki/sources/lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-compre.title: lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method
wiki/sources/lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-compre.content:
PSI-Sigma: a comprehensive splicing-detection method for short-read and long-read RNA-seq analysis
One-line: Introduces PSI-Sigma, a splicing-detection tool with a new PSI index (PSIΣ) that quantifies Percent Spliced-In for individual alternative exons across all isoforms, benchmarked against rMATS, SUPPA2 and Whippet using real (non-simulated) RNA-seq data from synthetic RNA Sequins.
Key takeaways

PSI-Sigma computes percent-spliced-in using a modified PSI (Σ) equation that considers splice-junction reads of all isoforms between two constitutive exons, so a single alternative exon can carry multiple PSI values depending on the isoform it belongs to.
Unlike two-isoform tools (rmats, MISO) and isoform-resolution tools (SUPPA2), PSI-Sigma handles multiple-exon-skipping (MES) events and intron-retention (IR) events, where other tools fail.
Benchmarking used real RNA-seq data from spliced synthetic genes (RNA Sequins, 164 polyA RNAs in two mixtures with known concentration ratios = ground truth), avoiding the in silico-simulation limitation of prior splicing-methods evaluations.
PSI-Sigma also works on nanopore long-read RNA-seq (the other three tools are short-read only), demonstrated on U87 glioblastoma RNA spiked with RNA Sequins.
Tool entity: psi-sigma. Software at github.com/wososa/PSI-Sigma.

Notable claims / data

Short-read RNA Sequins benchmark (DPSI ≠ 0 criterion): recall — PSI-Sigma 1.0, Whippet 1.0, rMATS 0.64, SUPPA2 0.50; precision — PSI-Sigma 0.53, Whippet 0.40, rMATS 0.56, SUPPA2 0.52. rMATS/SUPPA2 missed up to ~50% of AS genes (cannot detect MES; actual recall for MES events was 0 for both).
DPSI accuracy vs ground truth: PSI-Sigma most accurate (r = 0.9441, P = 3.93e-30); SUPPA2 least accurate (r = 0.5700). For 8 IR events, PSI-Sigma highest correlation (r = 0.9465).
False positive rate at 10% DPSI cutoff: PSI-Sigma 0.12, Whippet 0.28, rMATS 0.08, SUPPA2 0.18; at 30% cutoff: PSI-Sigma 0, Whippet 0.04, rMATS 0, SUPPA2 0.10. PSI-Sigma FPRs differ significantly from Whippet (P=0.0002) and SUPPA2 (P=3.09e-8) but not from rMATS (P=0.0721).
Human transcriptome (Ensembl 94): PSI-Sigma identified MES events in 7029 genes (90.18% protein-coding) and IR events in 9175 genes vs only 3574 for rMATS v4.0.2; PSI-Sigma recovered 98.35% of rMATS’s IR genes plus 5660 additional. Validated AFMID exon-6 isoform switch in HCC (TCGA-LIHC, 50 normal + 369 HCC) where rMATS/Whippet could not report the two opposite DPSI values.
Long-read: nanopore GridION runs gave 2.20M (Run 1) and 2.51M (Run 2) reads; error rate ~12.5% vs <0.2% short-read; only 48 reads (0.0022%) of Run 1 ambiguous between human/Sequins genomes. Long-read DPSI vs concentration r=0.9245; short- vs long-read gene-expression r=0.9045. ~0.76M long reads covered ≥1 read for 11,420 protein-coding genes; ~1M long reads ≈ 10–100× fewer reads needed than short-read for PSI analysis.
Speed/caveat: PSI-Sigma single-threaded (~10 h for a 3-vs-3 comparison on pre-aligned BAMs) vs Whippet ~5 h and rMATS v4.0.2 ~30 min (8 threads). Whippet generates no P-values; PSI-Sigma and rMATS give adjusted P-values for multi-sample statistical comparison.

Connections

Direct benchmark competitor to rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); both perform similarly on single-alternative-exon events, but PSI-Sigma extends to MES/IR.
Methodological sibling to other splicing-quantification tools catalogued under splicing-methods; central concept is percent-spliced-in (PSI/DPSI).
Touches splicing-and-disease / cancer biomarkers via AFMID, TERT, SRSF2 examples and TCGA-LIHC analysis; mentions splicing factors srsf1 (SRSF1 last-intron IR) and SRSF2.
Methods/tools-survey context overlaps with sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.

Open questions

Does PSI-Sigma’s per-isoform PSI generalize to whole human cohorts at scale, given its lack of multithreading?
How does the ~12–13% nanopore error rate cap reliable IR/MES detection as long-read depth and base-calling improve?
Could rMATS/SUPPA2 close the MES/IR gap by adopting the PSIΣ index, as the authors suggest?
How robust is the RNA Sequins ground truth as a general benchmark for newer splicing tools beyond the four compared here?

wiki/sources/lindhout-2024-a-molecular-a.title: lindhout-2024-a-molecular-and-cellular-perspective
wiki/sources/lindhout-2024-a-molecular-a.content:
A molecular and cellular perspective on human brain evolution and tempo
One-line: A Nature Perspective arguing that human brain specializations arise through a progressively slowed developmental tempo — for which the authors coin the terms “bradychrony” (slowed) and “tachychrony” (accelerated) — acting together with human-specific genetics, gene-regulatory rewiring and cellular innovations across distinct stages of neurodevelopment.
Key takeaways

Frames human brain evolution as a multi-stage developmental process (neural stem cell proliferation → neurogenesis → neuronal maturation), where most differences from other species are quantitative shifts in conserved, homologous cell types rather than wholly novel cells.
Introduces two new terms — bradychrony (slowed tempo of a developmental event between species or brain regions) and tachychrony (accelerated tempo) — to replace the loosely-used “neoteny,” arguing developmental delay is not merely retention of juvenile traits but a pervasive slowing throughout embryonic and postnatal development.
Human developmental tempo has become progressively more bradychronic along the lineage (rodents → primates → great apes → humans), and the degree of slowing correlates with brain size, cell number/diversity and connectivity; tempo differences appear to be cell-intrinsic.
Later neuronal-maturation stages (synaptic pruning, myelination) are the most disproportionately protracted in humans, suggesting this extreme bradychrony is a relatively recent evolutionary event.
Molecular drivers are dual: human-specific protein-coding changes/gene duplications (TKTL1, NOTCH2NL, SRGAP2C, ARHGAP11B) and non-coding gene-regulatory changes (human-accelerated regions/HARs, hCONDELs, enhancer rewiring), validated increasingly via in vitro models, comparative functional genetics and high-throughput single-cell profiling.
A central theme: extended developmental windows give more room for environmental influence, behavioural plasticity and cultural transmission, but also heighten susceptibility to neuropsychiatric disease.

Notable claims / data

The human brain is ~3× larger than chimpanzee/gorilla/bonobo brains; hominin endocranial volume rose stepwise from ~450 cm³ (Australopithecus) → ~600 cm³ (early Homo) → ~1,000 cm³ (H. erectus) → 1,400–1,500 cm³ (H. sapiens / Neanderthals).
The human neocortex underwent ~50% more expansion relative to the rest of the brain vs other primates; white matter expands disproportionately (universal power law across mammals), with extra human-specific prefrontal white-matter increase implying more connectivity.
Interneuron fraction: ~15% of cortical neurons in mouse (invariant across areas) vs 30–40% in primate association cortex; humans exceed marmosets in motor-cortex interneuron number, indicating a recent lineage change.
Tempo metrics: at birth the human brain is 28% of adult size vs 40% in chimpanzee; in vivo neurogenic window ~4 months in humans (~2× macaque, ~10× mouse); neural-stem-cell/tangential-expansion phase ~4 weeks human vs 2.5 weeks macaque vs 2 days mouse. Full brain development (gastrulation→myelination completion) takes ~30 years in humans (~half the lifespan) vs ~30% of lifespan in chimp/macaque and ~5% in mouse.
Maturation tempo: initial synaptogenesis ~5 yr (human) / 4 yr (chimp) / 3 mo (macaque); late maturation (pruning + myelination) ~30 yr (human) / 10 yr (chimp) / 4.5 yr (macaque). Within humans, prefrontal cortex is slowest (synaptogenesis peaks ~15 months postnatal, pruning to ~30 yr) vs auditory cortex (peak ~3 months, pruning to ~12 yr).
Tempo is cell-intrinsic: species-specific timing is preserved when human neurons are xenografted into mouse or co-cultured with NHP neurons; candidate mechanisms include protein stability/turnover, mitochondrial metabolism (its stimulation accelerates human neuronal maturation), and a chromatin-modifier epigenetic barrier (EZH2, EHMT1/2, DOT1L) gating maturation timing.
Protein-coding human-specific drivers: TKTL1 (Lys→Arg, post-Neanderthal) boosts basal radial glia output; NOTCH2NL paralogues expand progenitors and are linked to brain-size/autism structural variants; SRGAP2B/C modulate synaptic maturation and prolong SYNGAP1 upregulation (bradychronic spines); ARHGAP11B enlarges/gyrifies neocortex in mouse and marmoset.
Regulatory drivers: HARs are the fastest-evolving conserved non-coding elements, many act as neurodevelopmental enhancers (e.g. HARE5 enhancer of FRIZZLED8 accelerates progenitor cell cycle and enlarges mouse brain); a hominin-specific SOX5-binding-site deletion in a CBLN2 enhancer drives human-specific prefrontal CBLN2 upregulation and spine formation. Caveat: even the strongest human adaptive-trait cases have largely relied on correlative evidence, now beginning to shift toward functional validation.

Connections

brain-development-neurogenomics — this Perspective is a foundational conceptual anchor for the topic: it organizes brain evolution by developmental stage and supplies the bradychrony/tachychrony vocabulary.
Pairs with single-cell atlases of the developing human brain as the conceptual/evolutionary framing for their cell-type findings — e.g. the protein-level companion wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (which independently flags the intermediate-progenitor→neuron transition as disease-vulnerable, consistent with this paper’s emphasis on conserved-cell-type shifts and neurodevelopmental disease susceptibility).
Touches developmental gene-regulation themes shared with the developing-brain expression/isoform siblings walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna.

Open questions

What are the global vs stage-specific molecular clocks setting tempo, and how much of human bradychrony follows allometric scaling vs is slower than expected for our size?
Are human-specific maturation features (extended axons, dendritic arborization, spine number) shared with other great apes or truly human-specific, given the ethical/technical limits on great-ape fetal tissue?
How do structural variants and 3D-genome reorganization (enhancer hijacking near HARs) causally rewire neurodevelopmental gene regulation in their endogenous context, beyond reporter-assay evidence?
Do cerebellar and thalamic (non-cortical) cell types diversify in human evolution, and how does neuronal activity feed back to shape these protracted developmental processes?

wiki/sources/liu-2024-single-cell-rna-se.title: liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral
wiki/sources/liu-2024-single-cell-rna-se.content:
Single-cell RNA sequencing of peripheral blood links cell-type-specific regulation of splicing to autoimmune and inflammatory diseases
One-line: A population-scale single-cell splicing study of ~1 million PBMCs from 474 healthy Eastern/Southeastern/South Asian donors (the aida cohort) that maps cell-type-specific, sex-biased and ancestry-biased alternative splicing plus 11,577 cis-sQTLs, and links these to autoimmune/inflammatory disease heritability — validating an Asian-specific TCHP sQTL for Graves’ disease.
Key takeaways

Uses 10x Genomics 5′ scRNA-seq plus the endogenous “exon painting” phenomenon (stochastic mRNA cleavage and recapping creating multiple 5′ ends per isoform) to gain exon coverage that standard 3′-biased droplet scRNA-seq lacks, enabling splicing-qtl mapping at single-cell resolution across 21 PBMC subtypes from 474 donors.
Two-level alternative splicing (see alternative-splicing) quantification: SpliZ at the single-cell level (median 1,146 AS genes/cell, 4.3× the OneK1K estimate of 267) and leafcutter at the pseudobulk level (median 7,721 AS genes/pseudobulk, saturating at ~11,500 genes; R²=0.92 sigmoid fit to cell count).
Identifies 11,577 independent cis-sQTLs (10,874 protein-coding + 703 lncRNA sGenes, FDR<0.05; ~39.9% of tested genes), 607 trans-sGenes (FDR<0.01) and 107 dynamic sQTLs along B cell differentiation; most differential splicing and many sQTLs are cell-type-specific.
A cell-type-specific trans-regulatory relationship: a cis-eQTL for HNRNPLL (a master regulator of T cell activation-induced splicing) colocalizes with a trans-sQTL for PTPRC/CD45 (rs6751481, H4≈1.00 in naive CD4+ T), with higher HNRNPLL expression driving shorter PTPRC isoforms — recapitulating the CD45RA→CD45RO isoform switch in T cell development.
cis-sQTL effects are enriched in autoimmune/inflammatory disease heritability (S-LDSC: SLE, atopic dermatitis, Graves’ disease, RA highest); colocalization with 20 Asian-focused GWAS traits nominates 563 putative effector genes (see splicing-and-disease).
Functional validation: an Asian-specific sQTL (rs74416240) disrupting the 5′ splice site of TCHP exon 4 colocalizes with Graves’ disease GWAS; a K562 minigene assay confirmed the alternative (A) allele induces intron retention while the reference (G) allele yields the normal isoform.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: AIDA Data Freeze v.1 — 474 donors (Japan, Korea, Singapore: Singaporean Chinese/Malay/Indian), avg 1,959 cells/donor, avg 53,846 reads/cell of which 46.9% overlapped a splice junction; median 85.3% of exonic bases covered per gene (IQR 51.8–97.3%).
QC/replication: 59.3% of pseudobulk introns canonical in GENCODE v.32; 85.9% of short-read junctions replicated in PacBio MAS-seq long-read data (4 donors); ~93%/98.8%/99.5% of junctions seen in >1,000/>100/>10 Snaptron samples. cis-sQTLs replicated at π1 ≈ 0.70–1 across BLUEPRINT, DICE, GTEx whole-blood/LCL and ImmuNexUT, with ~14.3% new vs ImmuNexUT (largest existing PBMC sQTL set).
Sex/ancestry splicing: 48 sex-biased differential splicing events (DSEs) across 32 genes (e.g. female-biased FLNA isoform ENST00000498491 in T/NK cells); 1,031 ancestry-biased DSEs across 509 genes. SNP rs11064437 disrupts the 3′ splice site of SPSB2 exon 2 (AG|C→AA|C) with MAF declining EAS 30.7% → SEA 13.9% → SAS 3.4%; the EAS-biased alt intron was absent from GENCODE.
sQTL context-dependence: 27 sex-biased sQTLs across 20 genes; 8 Malay-biased and 19 Indian-biased cis-sQTLs; allelic heterogeneity up to 16.1% of sGenes with ≥2 independent variants. Sample size (r=0.95) and library size (r=0.96) independently drive sQTL discovery.
Dynamic sQTLs across B-cell pseudotime (naive → IGHMhi memory → IGHMlo memory, 52,964 cells): 865 dynamic introns (3 modes: stepwise/linear/quadratic; e.g. PAX5A↓/PAX5B↑, PTPRC exon 4↓, DOCK8 exon 1 transient) and 107 dynamic sQTLs (62 strengthening, 37 weakening, 11 transient); CD83 dynamic sQTL colocalizes with RA (H4=0.980), BCL2A1 with lymphocyte count (H4=0.848).
trans-sQTLs are more cell-type-specific than cis: 393/607 (64.7%) appear in only one cell type; 17 cis-eQTL↔trans-sQTL colocalization events (H4>0.75). A lead-SNP switch between naive CD4+ T (rs6751481, promoter) and CD4+ TEM (rs74258942, enhancer; Asian-biased) confirmed by SuSiE fine-mapping; SMR links HNRNPLL expression to memory CD4+ T proportion (P=9.21×10⁻⁷, HEIDI P=0.103).
TCHP/Graves’: lead GWAS risk variant rs74416240 has an East-Asian-biased minor allele (AIDA + 1000 Genomes), sits in the TCHP exon 4–5 5′ splice site, and shows cell-type-specific GD-GWAS/sQTL colocalization across seven cell types.

Connections

A single-cell, immune-cell, population-genetics application built on existing splicing-quantification tooling — pseudobulk via leafcutter (cf. li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna) rather than a new algorithm; the disease-genetics angle parallels zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain-region sQTLs) but resolves cell types instead of tissue regions and adds ancestry diversity.
Extends splicing-qtl and splicing-and-disease into single-cell, multi-ancestry territory; complements the population-splicing line of park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative and disease-splicing reviews (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer).
Mechanistically centers RNA-binding-protein trans-regulation (HNRNPLL→PTPRC) relevant to rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors; splice-site-disrupting variants (SPSB2, TCHP) connect to splice-sites.
Introduces the aida atlas as a reusable multi-ancestry immune single-cell + sQTL resource.

Open questions

Does the East-Asian-biased TCHP exon 4 sQTL causally raise Graves’ disease risk in vivo, and through what TCHP isoform function?
How much of the disease heritability enrichment attributable to sQTLs is orthogonal to eQTLs in these same cell types, and which effector genes among the 563 are druggable?
Can full-length single-cell technologies eventually replace the “exon painting” trick, and would they recover isoforms missed by 5′ coverage?
How well do sQTLs and ancestry-biased splicing events discovered in Asian donors transfer to (or differ from) European-ancestry cohorts like OneK1K?

wiki/sources/lord-2020-autism-spectrum-d.title: lord-2020-autism-spectrum-disorder
wiki/sources/lord-2020-autism-spectrum-d.content:
Autism spectrum disorder
One-line: Authoritative Primer synthesizing the epidemiology, genetics, neurobiology, diagnosis, and lifespan management of autism spectrum disorder (ASD), framing it as a highly heritable, heterogeneous neurodevelopmental condition.
Key takeaways

ASD is a highly heritable neurodevelopmental disorder defined by impairments in social communication plus restricted/repetitive behaviours and sensory anomalies beginning early in life; the wiki concept page is autism.
Genetics is dominated by rare, de novo, heterozygous coding mutations (single-base to large CNVs) rather than the common-variant architecture typical of adult-onset psychiatric-genetics; >100 genes/loci now confidently associated.
Autism risk genes converge on two broad functional buckets — synaptic structure/function and chromatin modification/transcriptional regulation — and on a mid-fetal glutamatergic cortical neuron nexus (spatiotemporal convergence).
Prospective “baby sibling” studies show brain-structural and EEG differences at 6 months, before behavioural symptoms emerge, hinting at cascading developmental mechanisms rather than deterministic gene→synapse→symptom models.

Notable claims / data

Worldwide prevalence just under 1% (2010 Global Burden of Disease: ~52 million people, 1 in 132); higher estimates in high-income countries; male:female ratio 2:1 to 5:1 (~4:1).
No clear evidence of a real increase in community prevalence 1990–2010; apparent rises reflect administrative case-finding, not true incidence. Prevalence in mental-health inpatient settings 4–9.9%.
heritability estimated ~40–90% from twin/family studies; among the most heritable common medical conditions.
De novo mutations contribute ~3% of population-level risk but are markedly enriched clinically: ~10–20% of autistic people carry a contributory de novo point mutation or CNV, rising to ~20–30% with added risk factors (female, intellectual disability, multiple unaffected siblings, seizures).
A single de novo germline loss-of-function point mutation can convey more risk than the top decile of schizophrenia polygenic risk. Monogenic syndromes (fragile X, tuberous sclerosis, Rett/MECP2, Angelman) account for <10% of clinical cases.
Highest-confidence idiopathic-autism gene-therapy targets named: SCN2A and CHD8 (see chd8).
Neurobiology: greater cortical surface-area growth 6–12 months and larger brain volume 12–24 months in later-diagnosed toddlers; EEG/MEG converge on excitation/inhibition imbalance and reduced long-range functional connectivity; no MRI or EEG measure is yet a validated biomarker (replication/heterogeneity problems).
Environmental associations (contributory, not causal): advanced parental age, birth hypoxia, maternal obesity, gestational diabetes, short interpregnancy interval, prenatal valproate. Clearly NOT associated: vaccination.

Connections

Complements chd8-focused developmental work in nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8 and SCN2A restoration in tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related.
Provides clinical/genetic framing for autism transcriptomic and developmental studies: schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism, zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of, velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal.
Genetic-architecture themes connect to psychiatric-genetics and cross-disorder overlap discussed in grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across.

Open questions

How do rare large-effect mutations mechanistically produce human social disability given their pleiotropy across brain regions and development, and why the strong sexual dimorphism?
Can spatiotemporal convergence (mid-fetal glutamatergic neurons, striatum) be leveraged for shared-mechanism therapeutics across genetically distinct cases?
Which early (pre-symptomatic) MRI/EEG signatures are genuine endophenotypes versus average-brain artefacts, and can large-scale collaboration yield generalizable biomarkers?

wiki/sources/loupe-2024-multiomic-profil.title: loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor
wiki/sources/loupe-2024-multiomic-profil.content:
Multiomic profiling of transcription factor binding and function in human brain
One-line: A large multiomic ChIP–seq resource (braintf) mapping occupancy of >100 transcription factors directly in human postmortem brain tissue and FANS-sorted neuronal/glial nuclei, integrated with ATAC-seq, RNA-seq and DNA methylation to model TF function and to link specific TFs (notably TBR1, PKNOX1) to neuropsychiatric disease risk.
Key takeaways

First and largest direct measurement of TF binding in human postmortem brain: ChIP–seq for 106 total TFs (96 in bulk, 76 in sorted nuclei) plus histone marks across nine brain regions from four neurotypical donors, paired with ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, phased WGS and DNA methylation — packaged as the braintf resource.
FANS sorting (NeuN+ neurons, Olig2+ oligodendrocytes, NeuN−/Olig2− microglia+astrocytes) de-confounds cell-type signal versus bulk homogenate; for nearly all TFs profiled, no prior brain binding data existed in ENCODE.
Identifies high-occupancy target (HOT) sites — loci bound by many TFs — that are promoter/CpG-island biased and largely cell-type-shared; they carry little TF-specific information and bias both motif discovery and computational TF-binding predictors. Removing them sharpens motif recognition for low-GC factors.
Neuronal TFs SATB2 and TBR1 bind unique, HOT-depleted cis-regulatory-elements and promote neuronal gene expression; 31% of TF-bound regions have no ENCODE cCRE annotation and are enriched for neuron-specific regulatory elements/pathways.
Stratified LD score regression links TF binding sites — especially in NeuN+ neurons (e.g. PKNOX1, TBR1, BCL11A) — to heritability for neuropsychiatric disorders (schizophrenia, bipolar), more strongly than histone marks or ATAC-seq; Alzheimer’s risk localizes to NeuN−/Olig2− (e.g. REST).

Notable claims / data

Union of TF peaks across the four large regions: 239,361 distinct regions spanning 6.94% of hg38 (text also cites 236,311 in Fig. 1); ~49% distal enhancer-like (dELS), 8% promoter-like (PLS), 31% unannotated (“none”). Promoters shared across more cell types than enhancers (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶).
“None” regions enriched for neuron-specific elements: odds ratio 2.9 for overlap with NeuN+ vs bulk ATAC-seq (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶) vs 1.1 (Olig2+) and 1.5 (NeuN−/Olig2−).
TF co-association: PCA on ChIP signal (first 20 PCs = 83.76% variance) yields three clusters — Cluster 1 low promoter-preference/high signal, Cluster 3 promoter-biased (H3K4me3-overlapping), Cluster 2 intermediate (TBR1–SATB2 node). Recovers known interactions (CTCF–RAD21 cohesin, EHMT2–REST, NFIB–NFIC).
HOT sites = top 10% of merged loci by number of bound TFs; 66% are promoter-like CpG-island sequences (OR = 3.81, P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶); shared HOT sites enriched for housekeeping-gene promoters (OR = 8.245). SATB2 binds few-TF regions; HCFC1 binds predominantly HOT sites.
Motifs: from JASPAR 2022, peak motif-match proportion ranged 0.8–86.5%; of 49 factors, 24 were most enriched for their expected motif. Common motifs (e.g. THAP11, NRF/SP families) are GC-rich; removing HOT sites improved enrichment for low-GC motifs (e.g. NFIB motif rose to top-ranked MEME call).
Predictive models (Virtual ChIP–seq, 16 overlapping TFs): bulk-DLPFC auPRs correlated with GM12878 cell-line auPRs (r = 0.69, P = 0.0093); CTCF/NRF1 well predicted, EGR1/REST poorly. auPRs drop sharply when HOT sites removed — models are biased toward easily-predicted promoter/HOT sites and miss neuron-enriched “none” CREs.
Gene-expression modeling (NeuN+ linear models controlling for cobound TF count): most TFs positively affect expression, with largest effect at non-HOT promoters; at HOT promoters individual TFs have minimal effect. TAF1 most enriched for upregulated genes; EHMT2/SIRT1 repressive; TBR1/ZBTB7B dual activator/repressor.
Cell-type-biased TFs: SATB2/TBR1/BCL11A (neurons), OLIG2/SOX8 (oligodendrocytes), MITF (microglia/astrocytes). Example: a NeuN+ PKNOX1 peak overlaps a schizophrenia risk locus at the 3′ end of TSNARE1, linked to neuronal ADGRB1 and ARC expression.
Caveats: only four donors; ChIP–seq quality/skewness covaries with peak count and signal-to-noise; HOT sites should NOT be universally excluded (unlike ENCODE excluded-regions list) since they capture real GC-rich TF behavior; target-gene/distal links rely on a prior single-cell multiomic study (Anderson et al. 2023).

Connections

A functional-single-cell-genomics / regulatory resource: complements zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory and groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at — all interpret noncoding cis-regulatory-elements, but this one supplies direct TF occupancy maps (ChIP–seq) rather than MPRA-measured activity.
Sibling human-brain regulatory/psychiatric-genetics source to li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (PsychENCODE) and zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory; uses cell-type FANS sorting like the sorted-nuclei strategy in kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell.
Conceptually adjacent to TF/RBP binding-map efforts such as encode-rbp-map (van Nostrand RBPs) — both build occupancy maps to assign regulatory function, but BrainTF targets DNA-binding TFs in brain tissue rather than RNA-binding proteins.
Relevant to the developing/diseased-brain hub brain-development-neurogenomics via neuronal TFs (SATB2, TBR1, BCL11A, NEUROD1) with established roles in cortical neuron development.

Open questions

Can the BrainTF occupancy maps improve in silico TF-binding predictors for the neuron-enriched, unannotated (“none”) CRE category that current models miss?
Do TF–disease enrichments replicate with more donors and additional disease GWAS, and can dual-role TFs (TBR1, ZBTB7B) be resolved into activator vs repressor contexts?
How do these bulk/sorted-nuclei TF maps relate to single-cell-resolution regulatory states, and to allele-specific binding from the phased WGS generated here?

wiki/sources/manolio-2009-finding-the-mi.title: manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of
wiki/sources/manolio-2009-finding-the-mi.content:
Finding the missing heritability of complex diseases
One-line: The landmark review that named and framed the “missing heritability” problem — common-variant GWAS explain only a small fraction of the heritability estimated from family/twin studies — and catalogues candidate explanations and research strategies.
Key takeaways

Defines the central puzzle of human missing-heritability: hundreds of common variants found by GWAS confer only small risk increments (typically odds ratios ~1.1–1.5) and together explain only a small portion of the heritability estimated from family and twin studies.
GWAS rests on the “common disease, common variant” hypothesis — that common diseases are partly attributable to allelic variants present in >1–5% of the population. The review argues this hypothesis is incomplete, not wrong: common variants matter but do not account for the bulk of heritability.
Catalogues candidate sources of the missing variance: (1) many more common variants of even smaller effect; (2) rarer variants (MAF 0.5–5%, or <0.5%) of possibly larger effect, poorly tagged by SNP arrays; (3) structural variation / copy-number variants (CNVs); (4) gene–gene (epistasis) and gene–environment interactions; and (5) inadequate accounting for shared environment or overestimated heritability.
Frames the contributions to phenotype using risk-allele frequency vs effect size (Fig. 1): GWAS so far has only populated the common-variant/modest-effect corner, leaving low-frequency and rare-but-large-effect regions largely unexplored.
Proposes concrete strategies: larger GWAS and meta-analyses, targeted and whole-genome sequencing (e.g. 1000 Genomes, extreme-phenotype sequencing), better CNV detection, family-based designs (which enrich for rare variants and enable identity-by-descent and parent-of-origin analyses), and improved phenotyping including non-European populations.
Notes the heritability denominator itself can be questioned: narrow-sense heritability can be inflated by non-additive effects (dominance, epistasis) and shared environment — though IBD-based estimates (e.g. height ~0.8 from sib IBD sharing) agree with traditional ones, suggesting at least some estimates are not over-inflated.

Notable claims / data

Human height: estimated heritability ~80%, yet at least ~40 loci identified at the time explained only ~5% of phenotypic variance despite studies of tens of thousands of people.
Table 1 examples of heritability explained by identified loci: age-related macular degeneration ~50% (5 loci), Crohn’s disease ~20% (32 loci), systemic lupus erythematosus ~15% (6 loci), type 2 diabetes ~6% (18 loci), HDL cholesterol ~5.2% (7 loci), height ~5% (40 loci), early-onset myocardial infarction ~2.8% (9 loci), fasting glucose ~1.5% (4 loci).
Most common variants individually confer small risk (OR ~1.1–1.5; “large effect” defined loosely as ≥twofold).


80% of associated variants fall outside coding regions, emphasizing non-coding and regulatory variation.


The vast majority of GWAS were done in European-ancestry populations; populations of recent African ancestry (greater genetic diversity, shorter LD blocks) could increase yield of rare variants and narrow association regions.
Age-related macular degeneration is highlighted as the best example of a common disease whose heritability is substantially explained by a small number of common variants of large effect; Crohn’s disease is the opposite case (many variants, modest fraction explained).
Rare CNVs implicated in neuropsychiatric/developmental conditions (autism, schizophrenia, epilepsy, intellectual disability) can have very large effect sizes (Table 2 ORs up to ~40–100), and de novo CNVs would contribute to “environment,” not family resemblance/heritability.
Sample size for detecting associations scales roughly with 1/MAF and rises sharply as odds ratios approach 1, so low-frequency and rare variants require much larger samples.

Connections

This is the canonical statement of the missing-heritability problem within biometrical-genetics, setting up the gap between pedigree/twin-based heritability and variance explained by measured genotypes.
zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability directly challenges one of this review’s assumptions: it argues that epistasis inflates the heritability denominator, producing “phantom heritability” — i.e. part of the gap may be an artifact of how heritability is estimated rather than truly hidden variants. Manolio et al. raise but largely set aside this possibility (citing IBD agreement for height).
The review’s heritability estimation discussion connects to the variance-components-model / quantitative-genetics machinery used in rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and, mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models and pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and, which formalize partitioning phenotypic variance into additive genetic and environmental components — the very quantities GWAS attempts to recover with measured markers.
For disease (binary) traits, the heritability figures rely implicitly on a liability-threshold-model mapping continuous liability to disease status.

Open questions

How much of the missing heritability is due to many common variants of tiny effect vs rare variants of larger effect vs structural variation vs interactions — the relative weighting is left unresolved.
Is the family/twin heritability denominator itself accurate, given possible inflation by dominance, epistasis and shared environment? (Later sharpened by zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability.)
Can sequencing and family-based designs recover the rare-variant fraction, and how should rare variants be pooled/tested for association?
Will explaining missing heritability actually improve disease prediction, prevention or treatment — or is mechanistic insight (drug targets) the real payoff regardless of variance explained?

wiki/sources/mao-2024-structurally-diver.title: mao-2024-structurally-divergent-and-recurrently-mutated
wiki/sources/mao-2024-structurally-diver.content:
Structurally divergent and recurrently mutated regions of primate genomes
One-line: Long-read, haplotype-resolved assemblies of eight nonhuman primate genomes yield an atlas of lineage-specific structural variation and structurally divergent regions that spawn new genes and predispose to human disease.
Key takeaways

Multi-platform (PacBio CLR + HiFi + ONT) sequencing and hifiasm assembly of chimpanzee, bonobo, gorilla, orangutan, gibbon, macaque, owl monkey, and marmoset produced HiFi assemblies of QV 42–58 and contig N50 19–104 Mbp, resolving paralogous and allelic variation previously collapsed in “squashed” references — a genome-scale advance in genome-architecture.
Structurally divergent regions (SDRs) act as cradles of gene innovation and recurrent gene loss, with several affecting brain-expressed and neuronal genes relevant to human-brain-evolution (ASTN2 exapted exon, TAAR2 loss, NAT16, marmoset-specific VPS36/NEK5/CKAP2 paralogs).
Recurrent segmental duplications drive homoplasy and disease susceptibility (e.g., RGPD family on chr2, C4 copy-number in the MHC where higher hC4A expression is linked to schizophrenia synaptic pruning).

Notable claims / data

1,338,997 lineage-specific fixed SVs (441,453 deletions, 897,544 insertions) disrupting 1,561 protein-coding genes and intersecting 136,932 regulatory elements; includes the most complete set of human-specific fixed differences (7,169 human-specific changes reassessed).
~819.47 Mbp (27.2%) of the genome has been subjected to structural variation across the nine lineages; fixed insertions outnumber deletions ~2:1 (524.8 Mbp vs 294.68 Mbp).
1,607 validated SDRs (average 127.4 kbp, 94.3% validation, 3.6-fold enriched for segmental duplications); 184 recurrent-SV hotspots, 103 corresponding to 631 genes including CFHR, RHD, LPA, APOL, AMY1, and MHC (C4, MICA/MICB).
Recurrent, independent CARD-family deletions (Pan, gibbon, owl monkey) indicate relaxed selection; RGPD family shows ~20 independent mutation events (1.2 Mbp) — an extreme homoplasy example.
Marmoset: 5.13M full-length Iso-seq cDNAs across 10 tissues plus 11,797 single-nuclei prefrontal-cortex RNA-seq reveal species-specific expression shifts of newly duplicated paralogs (THSD1, VPS36).
Mean autosomal NHP-to-human divergence 1.3–9.83%; owl monkey evolves significantly slower than marmoset; only 52.7% of 302,575 gene trees support the accepted topology (ILS at African/great-ape nodes).

Connections

Extends structural/architectural genome studies alongside che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome in the 3d-genome topic, from a comparative-primate structural-variation angle.
Provides lineage-specific structural context for human-brain-evolution and genome-architecture: neuronal-gene-disrupting SVs and neuroglia/neuron expression of species-specific paralogs.

Open questions

What are the neurobiological functions of newly minted marmoset- and ape-specific duplicated genes (VPS36, NEK5, CKAP2) in species-specific brain wiring?
How do recurrent SDR/segmental-duplication hotspots mechanistically predispose to human microdeletion/microduplication disorders (e.g., MEIG1, C4)?
Can complete haplotype-resolved primate assemblies now reconstruct the evolutionary history of every base pair of the human reference, including centromeric and subtelomeric regions?

wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.title: marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative
wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.content:
The physiology of alternative splicing
One-line: A review arguing that most of the transcriptomic complexity generated by alternative-splicing in multicellular eukaryotes is reflected at the protein level and shapes diverse physiological and pathological processes, from tissue differentiation to thermal regulation, cancer and approved splicing-correcting therapies.
Key takeaways

alternative-splicing occurs in ~95% of human genes and is “more a rule than an exception” in multicellular organisms; the central question is how much is functional versus noise. The authors argue most is functional, citing four decades of single-gene studies.
The apparent contradiction between sensitive RNA-seq and low-sensitivity mass spectrometry (which suggested ~85% of alternative exons are irrelevant) is attributed to technical limits of splicing proteomics (“absence of evidence is not evidence of absence”); ribosome profiling shows ~75% of medium/high-abundance skipped-exon mRNAs are ribosome-engaged.
cotranscriptional-splicing: splice-site choice depends on cis-elements (splicing-regulatory-elements) AND trans splicing-factors AND coupling to Pol II elongation. Kinetic coupling model — slow elongation promotes inclusion of class I exons and skipping of class II exons. splicing-and-chromatin: histone marks (H3K9ac, H3K9me2) modulate elongation and thus splicing outcomes.
Global alternative-splicing changes drive cell proliferation, cancer (splicing-and-disease), ASD and thermal regulation; gene-specific switches drive neuron self-avoidance, infrared sensing, telomere maintenance and the Warburg effect.
splicing-therapeutics: mastering splicing control yielded clinically approved ASO/small-molecule drugs for SMA (nusinersen, risdiplam) and DMD (eteplirsen).
splicing-and-evolution: alternative splicing per se has adaptive value as a readily available pool of mRNA variability that can be co-opted without eliminating gene functions or making new genes; it prevails in K-selected multicellular eukaryotes.

Notable claims / data

Drosophila Dscam can generate up to 38,000 mRNA variants (19,000 distinct extracellular domains) — more than the ~15,000 genes in the fly genome — via mutually exclusive exons in 4 regions; basis of stochastic neurite self-avoidance via homophilic repulsion. Vertebrates achieve self-avoidance differently, via clustered protocadherin promoters (~60 variants), no splicing.
CLK1 is a body-temperature sensor: activity is >5-fold higher at 35 °C than 38 °C; its 25-aa activation domain becomes flexible at high temperature (domain-swap with temperature-insensitive SRPKs confirms the activation domain is the thermosensor). Drives global SR-protein phosphorylation and splicing. Conserved in poikilotherms (alligators, turtles, Drosophila); links to fly circadian tim siesta behavior.
Warburg effect: PKM E9/E10 mutually exclusive exons (each 56 aa) → PKM1 (constitutive tetramer) vs PKM2 (low-activity, favors anabolic intermediates, cancer/embryonic). Regulated by hnRNPA1, hnRNPA2, PTBP1, whose genes are activated by MYC.
Vampire bat (Desmodus rotundus) infrared sensing: trigeminal-ganglion-specific TRPV1 splice variant (E14a, truncated C-terminus) lowers heat threshold from 43 °C to 30 °C — “sees” prey while retaining noxious-heat protection.
Telomeres: hTERT E2 skipping (vs SON-promoted inclusion) is a developmental switch controlling telomerase; an individual with a SON mutation had shorter telomeres.
ASD: neural microexons (3–27 nt, usually multiples of 3) are highly conserved, promoted by SRRM4 (reduced in ASD); examples in eIF4G1/eIF4G3 and CPEB4 (microexon 4). Gut-microbiota transplant from ASD donors into germ-free mice altered >500 splicing events (52 autism-related) with few gene-expression changes.
Cancer: SRSF1 (SF2/ASF) is a proto-oncogene; SRSF2/U2AF1/SF3B1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes; PRMT5 methylates SRSF1 (AML target) and removes detained introns.
SMA: SMN1/SMN2 differ in 11 nt (key C>T in SMN2 E7 causes skipping). Nusinersen (FDA 2016/EMA 2017) blocks intron-7 ISS bound by hnRNPA1/A2 → E7 inclusion; first splicing-corrective drug. Marasco et al. found SMN2 E7 is a class II exon, and the ASO paradoxically induces H3K9me2 — HDAC inhibitors (VPA) synergize by relieving the elongation roadblock. Risdiplam = small molecule affecting snRNA–5′SS pairing.
DMD: E49–E50 deletions = 13% of cases; eteplirsen (30-mer ASO) targets an ESE in E51 to force E51 skipping, restoring frame (Becker-like internally truncated dystrophin).
Textbook example CALCA: one gene → calcitonin (thyroid C cells, E4 skipped) vs CGRP1 (neurons, E4 included) via alternative splicing + cleavage/polyadenylation + cell-specific proteolysis. FOXP1 mutually exclusive E18/E18b switches between differentiation and pluripotency (FOXP1-ES), upstream-controlled by MBNL1/MBNL2.

Connections

Vertebrate organ transcriptomes: alternative-splicing profiles track species identity more than organ type (primates highest complexity); PTBP1 isoform reshapes splicing across species — links to splicing-and-evolution.
Coupling of alternative-splicing to NMD: ~15,000 human variants annotated as NMD targets (Ensembl 2019); post-transcriptional-splicing control of protein output.
Tissue/organ-specific: RBM20/titin in dilated cardiomyopathy; endothelial flow; obesity; pancreatic circadian exocytosis — rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors as drivers of homeostasis and splicing-and-disease.
G9a (EHMT2) E10 inclusion (Kornblihtt group) creates an H3K9me2-driven positive feedback loop reinforcing neuronal differentiation — direct tie to splicing-and-chromatin.

Open questions

How much RNA-seq-detected isoform diversity is truly functional vs too low-abundance to matter? Authors suggest long-read (third-generation) sequencing to resolve the transcriptomic/proteomic discrepancy.
Why do mutations in constitutive splicing-factors (U2AF1, SF3B1) preferentially perturb specific alternative events rather than global splicing? (Partly redundancy; partly late-recruited regulatory roles of core spliceosome components.)
Molecular mechanism of the bat TRPV1 dorsal-root vs trigeminal splicing switch remains unknown.
Whether ASD gut-microbiota metabolites reverse brain splicing changes, and whether HDAC inhibitors also boost risdiplam efficacy, remain to be tested.

wiki/sources/marderstein-2026-decoding-c.title: marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding
wiki/sources/marderstein-2026-decoding-c.content:
Decoding common and rare noncoding variant effects across cellular and developmental contexts
One-line: A deep-learning resource of ~3 billion ChromBPNet chromatin-accessibility predictions across 132 fetal and adult brain/heart cell contexts that reveals common variants are more cell-type-specific while ultra-rare variants are larger and broader (strongest purifying selection in fetal neurons), and introduces flare to prioritize constrained noncoding variants in development and disease.
Key takeaways

Trained per-context ChromBPNet convolutional sequence models of chromatin-accessibility on pseudobulk scATAC-seq across 132 cellular contexts; model interpretation (DeepLIFT + TF-MoDISco) recovers cell-type- and developmental-stage-specific TF motifs driving chromatin accessibility — an ai-in-genomics / cis-regulatory-grammar approach to mutation-effect-prediction for noncoding variants.
A common-vs-rare dichotomy: common variants are more cell-type-specific; ultra-rare variants have larger, broader effects across cell types, with the strongest evidence of purifying selection in fetal brain neurons (links heritability / selection to cis-regulatory-elements).
Context-matched models prioritize fine-mapped GWAS and eQTL variants in disease-relevant cell types (e.g. adult microglia for Alzheimer’s; nominated rs113706587 → RASGEF1C via a created ZEB/SNAI motif, validated by CRISPRi).
New tool flare (Functional Lasso Analysis of Regulatory Evolution) integrates ChromBPNet variant effects + accessibility + genomic context to predict evolutionary conservation (PhyloP), prioritizing rare deleterious noncoding variants.
flare generalizes across applications: de novo mutations in autism and congenital heart disease, rare-variant outliers in adult brain expression, and common-variant schizophrenia heritability (psychiatric-genetics).

Notable claims / data

~3 billion predictions generated; 15,106,800 variants from 3,202 whole genomes in 1000 Genomes scored — 6,349,771 common (MAF > 5%) and 8,757,029 ultra-rare (MAF < 0.1%) SNPs.
132 contexts: 30 fetal brain, 24 adult brain, 33 fetal heart, 45 adult heart, from 5 scATAC-seq studies.
Among top-1% large-effect SNPs, 46.9% of cell-type-shared variants sit in verified promoters vs 0.5% of cell-type-specific; shared variants disrupt broad TFs (CTCF, SP/KLF, NF-Y, MAZ), specific variants disrupt lineage TFs (ATOH1, NDF1/2, OLIG2).
Adult-specific neuron variants disrupt EGR (43.3%) and AP-1 (17.3%) motifs vs 1.5% / 0.02% of fetal-specific; fetal-specific variants disrupt NDF/ATOH, SP/KLF, ZEB/SNAI, NRF1, ATF. Curated 381 adult- and 1,236 fetal-specific ultra-rare variants in excitatory neurons.
FLARE-fb trained on 8,757,029 ultra-rare variants; 14 of the 16 highest-scoring de novo mutations near syndromic ASD genes were in cases (case t-test P = 6.8e-3); excess burden ~34 case vs 10.8 control de novo mutations (~1.2% of probands, CI 0.5–1.9%). CADD, PhyloP, TSS distance and single-cell-type ChromBPNet scores did NOT enrich.
CHD: FLARE-h placed 7 of top 10 variants near high-confidence CHD genes in cases (P = 0.016); top variants near ARID1B with endocardial-specific effects.
Adult-brain expression outliers (ROSMAP, 791 individuals): top 0.1% FLARE near genes with z < −3 gave OR = 15.4 (CI 7.0–30.3, P < 4.4e-9); enrichment driven by TSS-proximal variants.
Schizophrenia S-LDSC: at top 1% threshold FLARE-fb showed 19.1-fold heritability enrichment (τ = 4.4e-7, P = 2.7e-18) and FLARE-h 4.7-fold (P = 0.026), outperforming the best fetal-brain peak annotation.

Connections

Core artificial-intelligence / deep-learning genomics METHOD: a per-context sequence-to-accessibility model plus an evolutionary-constraint integrator (flare).
Cross-links to functional-single-cell-genomics — uses single-cell ATAC atlases as training data; conceptually adjacent to sc-linker (S-LDSC over single-cell-derived annotations) and to kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and and jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types.
Cross-links to biometrical-genetics — common-vs-rare effect-size contrast, purifying selection and heritability partitioning bear on heritability and missing-heritability (cf. manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of, zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability).
Cross-links to brain / psychiatric-genetics — fetal-neuron constraint, ASD de novo mutations, schizophrenia heritability; complements brain transcriptomic dysregulation work (gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across).
Methodologically related to other cis-regulatory variant-effect / MPRA efforts: deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory, groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at, yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions.

Open questions

How far do conclusions extend beyond brain/heart to other organs and to indels/structural variants (only SNPs scored)?
Linking prioritized noncoding variants to target genes remains imperfect (most-but-not-all closest-gene assignments); needs paired RNA-seq.
Can FLARE-style constraint integration improve common-variant fine-mapping and heritability beyond region-based annotations across more traits?
What experimental validation scales beyond single CRISPRi loci to confirm predicted causal mechanisms?

wiki/sources/masland-2004-neuronal-cell-.title: masland-2004-neuronal-cell-types
wiki/sources/masland-2004-neuronal-cell-.content:
Neuronal cell types
One-line: A Current Biology Primer arguing that a complete inventory of functionally distinct neuronal types — the brain’s “parts list” — is foundational to understanding brain function, and surveying how cell types are distinguished across the cerebellum, neocortex, and retina.
Key takeaways

Distinct neuronal cell types are the brain’s elementary computational components; assembling a “parts list” of cell types is a prerequisite for any bottom-up understanding of brain function. This connects to the broader brain-development-neurogenomics program of cataloguing neural cell diversity.
Cell types are defined by the premise that different structure indicates different function (“structure” including both morphology and expression of functionally important proteins). The most common discriminator is cell shape, because shape is a direct reflection of a neuron’s synaptic connections — the level of dendritic/axonal stratification defines which cells can contact each other.
A core taxonomic distinction is projection neurons (axon leaves the structure; “principal cells”) vs intrinsic neurons (synapse only locally; “interneurons”). The distinction applies only to axonal projection, not to the source of inputs.
Nomenclature is unstandardized and inconsistent: terms like “class,” “type,” “subclass,” “subtype” are used loosely; “type” is reserved here for the terminally differentiated level. Like genes, cells acquire whimsical and multiple names.
The retina is now known with unprecedented precision and serves as the proof-of-concept that a complete CNS cell-type inventory is achievable; the neocortex remains “a much harder nut to crack.”
Genome-screening probes will increasingly help sort cells, but truly cell-type-specific gene expression is rare; the practical future method combines partial molecular specificity + morphology (an arborization marker), which is more robust than morphology or expression alone.

Notable claims / data

Cerebellum: one projection neuron type (Purkinje cell, essentially 2-D / leaf-shaped) and five main intrinsic types. Granule cells have tiny somata (5–8 µm) and small dendritic arbors (~50 µm diameter) but mm-long axons, and are the most numerous single neuron type in the nervous system. Other interneurons: basket, stellate, and (larger) Golgi cells.
Neocortex: projection neurons (pyramidal cells) make up ~80% of all cortical neurons — opposite ratio to cerebellum/retina — possibly because cortical pyramidal cells send local axon collaterals (local-circuit + long-range function). The striate cortex of the macaque monkey is estimated to contain >40 morphologically identifiable interneuron types. Only interneurons expressing specific calcium-binding proteins reach the classification certainty of cerebellar/retinal cells.
Retina: ~five major neuron classes divided into ~60 individual types: retinal ganglion cells (projection; ~12 types), horizontal cells (2 types), bipolar cells (~12 types), amacrine cells (~30 types). Example of molecular/functional coupling: ON bipolar cells depolarize to light, arborize deep in the inner plexiform layer, express metabotropic mGluR6; OFF bipolar cells hyperpolarize, arborize high, express ionotropic AMPA/kainate receptors.
Mosaic regularity (law of coverage): neurons of a given type maintain a minimum exclusion distance from same-type neurons, whereas spacing relative to different types is entirely random — a powerful confirmation that a type is correctly identified. The mechanism for self-type recognition (possibly ~60 cell-type-specific recognition signals) is unknown.
The retinal survey is judged essentially complete (~98% of retinal neurons; no major players missed), with a few orphan wide-field types remaining.
Cortex extrapolation: from spacing, cell number, and dendritic-field assumptions, one observer estimated ~1,000 cortical cell types, highlighting a large gulf between that figure and current understanding of cortical diversity.
Caveat: this is a 2004 Primer/opinion piece grounded in morphology and early genomics; “few instances of truly cell-type-specific gene expression have been discovered so far” — partial (mixed) specificity was the norm at the time.

Connections

Sits under the new brain-development-neurogenomics hub as a foundational, pre-single-cell-era statement of the cell-type cataloguing problem.
Conceptual precursor to modern single-cell atlas efforts that this wiki tracks (e.g. proteomic and transcriptomic landscapes of brain tissue such as wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of) — Masland’s “parts list” goal is what high-throughput single-cell/-omics now attempts to deliver at molecular resolution.
The emphasis on cell-type-specific gene/protein expression as a defining feature links to expression-genomics studies of developing brain such as li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis and walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and.

Open questions

How do neurons of the same type recognize and exclude one another to form regular mosaics — are there dozens of distinct cell-type-specific recognition signals?
How many neuronal types does the neocortex truly contain (~100? ~1,000?), and can the retina’s combined morphology + molecular + mosaic approach be scaled to crack cortical taxonomy?
Will genome-wide screening eventually yield genuinely cell-type-specific markers, or will partial specificity plus morphology remain the practical standard?

wiki/sources/mazin-2012-widespread-splic.title: mazin-2012-widespread-splicing-changes-in-human
wiki/sources/mazin-2012-widespread-splic.content:
Widespread splicing changes in human brain development and aging
One-line: RNA-seq of human prefrontal cortex and cerebellum across the whole lifespan (2 days–98 years) shows that splicing changes affect more than a third of expressed brain genes, with a dominant “down-up” inclusion pattern that links infant and elderly brains via NMD-targeting segment inclusion.
Key takeaways

Two human RNA-seq data sets (DS1: paired-end, 30 individuals pooled into 6 PFC + 6 CBC samples; DS2: strand-specific single-end, 13 PFC individuals) cover the entire postnatal lifespan; age-related splicing changes affect close to 40% of genes expressed in the human brain.
Most splicing variation (~70%) occurs during development (0–14 years), much less during aging (25–98 years) — mirroring the known kinetics of gene expression change but, unlike expression, splicing shows strong directional asymmetry.
A single dominant “down-up” pattern (segment inclusion decreasing in development, increasing in aging) accounts for ~62% of age-related changes; these segments are enriched for retained introns, are poorly conserved, and are predicted to trigger nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) — a process related to intron-retention and AS-NMD (poison-exons).
Five of six NMD-pathway genes with age-related expression (UPF2, UPF3b, SMG1, SMG5, SMG6; CASC3 being the exception in direction) mirror the down-up profile (low in development, high in aging), supporting NMD as a driver of the shared infant/elderly splicing state.
Age-related splicing patterns associate with expression profiles of splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins — notably ptbp1 and PTBP2 plus several hnRNPs (hnrnpa1, hnRNPH1/H3/F) — providing a candidate regulatory mechanism; >50% of changes could be linked to SF expression changes.
Changes were cross-validated against an independent human data set, rhesus macaque RNA-seq, Affymetrix exon arrays, RT-PCR, and label-free mass spectrometry at the protein level, indicating robustness and evolutionary conservation.

Notable claims / data

Sequencing: 181,555,729 read pairs (DS1) + 274,927,771 reads (DS2), avg ~18M reads/sample; ~64% mapped (≤3 mismatches), 93% within gene boundaries. 21,644 novel junctions found, implying ≥10% of brain transcript isoforms are unannotated.
Significant age-related splicing: 22% of genes in DS1 (3132 segments / 1456 genes) and 38% in DS2 (6114 segments / 2588 genes); 1484 segments / 721 genes significant in both data sets (the core analysis set).
DS1/DS2 age-related changes overlap significantly (Fisher P<0.0001) and correlate strongly; exon-array validation P<0.0001 in both regions; RT-PCR: 24/30 tested segments gave expected bands, all matching RNA-seq direction (change ρ=0.93; estimate ρ=0.88).
Directional bias: 75% of segments show decreased inclusion in development, 77% increased inclusion in aging. Of the 1484, 970 significant in development vs 310 in aging when tested separately.
“Down-up” segments significantly enriched for retained introns (P<0.0001) and NMD targets (P<0.001), and significantly less conserved (phastCons, Wilcoxon P<0.0001, holds even for retained introns alone).
Protein-level confirmation: 24 segments / 19 genes had sufficient mass-spec peptides; RNA–protein splicing-change correlation significant vs permutation (P=0.0004 integrated). Protein-confirmed genes implicated in neural function/disease: MAPT, NDRG2, NPTN, PALM, PTPRZ1.
Macaque reproducibility: of 1484 segments, 496 mappable/covered; human–macaque trajectories more positively correlated than chance (permutation P<0.005); development 321 and aging 131 segments consistent (Fisher P<0.0001 dev, P<0.005 aging).
Eight clusters (CL1–8, 1422/1484 segments); CL6 and CL8 enriched for neural GO terms (synaptogenesis, learning, behavior, regulation of neurogenesis/synaptic transmission). CL1 = the down-up pattern; 10 SFs with binding-motif enrichment, 8 positively / 2 negatively correlated with the CL1 profile.
Regional difference: 15% of DS1 age-related segments differ significantly between PFC and CBC (q<0.05); affected neural genes include APP (Alzheimer’s), BIN1, and protocadherin gamma (PCDHG, with PFC pattern resembling developing mouse brain).
Caveats stated by authors: only two brain regions and a small number of (pooled) individuals → likely underestimate; SFBM identification is computationally unreliable → SF role likely underestimated; un-annotated intron retention could not be detected; functional consequences of the inferred NMD increase in infancy/old age remain untested. Sex and ethnicity could not be assessed but showed no detectable confounding; tissue composition and RNA degradation ruled out as drivers.
Data: deposited at Sequence Read Archive, study accession SRP005169. Methods: Ensembl v57 annotation, hg19, Bowtie mapping, binomial GLM with logit link on age^0.25; analysis released as a standalone Java application + R package (bioinf.fbb.msu.ru/~mazin).

Connections

A foundational human-brain-lifespan companion to gene-expression work from the same group (Somel et al, 2010, reused here for protein data and expression context).
Closely related to kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated splicing) as a developmental-splicing sibling, and provides organism-level human-brain context for scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (NMD / mis-splicing in disease) and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Brain/sQTL siblings in the wiki: zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (region-specific brain sQTL), park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative, wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (developing human brain proteome), humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (brain microglia isoforms).
Mechanistic links to ptbp1, PTBP2, and hnRNPs (hnrnpa1) as candidate regulators of the down-up pattern; methodological relation to intron-retention quantification and AS-NMD (poison-exons).

Open questions

Are the inferred NMD-targeting inclusion events in infants and the elderly functionally beneficial, deleterious, or neutral — and do they actively downregulate their host genes?
Why do infant and elderly brains converge on the same splicing state mechanistically — is it fully explained by the shared SF/NMD expression trajectory?
How do these lifespan splicing changes shape the establishment and deterioration of human cognition, and how much is missed by sampling only PFC and cerebellum at low individual numbers?

wiki/sources/mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-.title: mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models
wiki/sources/mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-.content:
Mixed-Effects Variance Components Models for Biometric Family Analyses
One-line: Shows that the biometric path-analysis model (PAM) and the biometric variance-components model (VCM) are algebraically equivalent for twin/family data, and that the VCM (but not the PAM) can be fit with off-the-shelf linear-mixed-model software like SAS PROC MIXED.
Key takeaways

For twin-and-family-study-design data the structural-equation-model-style path model (PAM) and the variance-components-model (VCM) are two re-parameterizations of the same model: PAM uses a fixed “design of correlations” with estimated coefficients (paths), whereas VCM uses a fixed “design of coefficients” (weights) with estimated variances. Given matching constraints they yield identical estimates, fit, and inferences.
The core algebraic trick: two correlated genetic factors (A1, A2) for a pair are re-expressed as one shared factor (AC) common to the pair plus two unique factors (AU1, AU2), all assigned the same variance σ_a², with fixed weights encoding relatedness (MZ: w_ac=1, w_au=0; DZ: w_ac=w_au=√(1/2)). This orthogonal-component form makes the model a linear-mixed-model with fixed weights on the design and random components with estimated variances.
Because of this, the VCM is programmable in standard mixed-model software (sas-proc-mixed, and PROC NLMIXED), while the PAM is not — it needs structural-equation software (Mx, Mplus, LISREL, AMOS). The authors label the two software families “MEMA” (mixed-effects model algorithms → linear-mixed-model) vs “SEMA” (structural-equation model algorithms → structural-equation-model).
They verify equivalence on simulated MZ/DZ twin data (N=1001 pairs/group) by fitting four ways — SEMA-PAM, SEMA-VCM, MIXED-VCM, NLMIXED-VCM — getting essentially identical ML estimates of heritability and the ACE components (e.g., a²≈46%, c/s²≈15%, e²≈39%).
The VCM/MEMA route extends naturally to measured covariates, observed-variable interactions (e.g., sex-limitation tests), and varying numbers of relatives per family (sibships, adoption, twin-sibling designs) by just adding fixed weight columns to the data and RANDOM statements — no new software needed.

Notable claims / data

Historical lineage of quantitative-genetics biometric models: Wright (path analysis, 1918/1921), Fisher (1918 variance decomposition; 1925), Cattell MAVA (Multiple Abstract Variance Analysis, 1953/1960), Jinks & Fulker (1970), Loehlin (1965/1978); Li (1968) noted Fisher’s and Wright’s approaches were already equivalent.
This paper responds to Guo & Wang (2002), who fit complex correlations with MEMA but did not directly compute biometric estimates or allow straightforward hypothesis testing of h² and c²; McArdle & Prescott close that gap by programming the biometric VCM directly in MIXED.
The two basic design-matrix requirements: (1) genetic weights in each individual’s row sum (of squares) to 1; (2) the correlation of two individuals on a component equals the product of their weights — reproducing r_a=1 for MZ, 0.5 for DZ, etc.
SAS implementation: one RANDOM INTERCEPT/SUBJECT=famid TYPE=VC for the shared environment (S), plus a second RANDOM on the AC/AU weight variables with TYPE=TOEP(1) (single-band Toeplitz → equal-variance, uncorrelated genetic components, all forced to σ_a²). Sub-models (A+E, S+E, E-only) fit by commenting out RANDOM lines or PARMS/HOLD.
PAM and VCM diverge only at boundaries / misspecification: SEMA-PAM imposes a lower bound of 0 on variances (σ²≥0 by construction), whereas VCM/MEMA can allow negative variance estimates — which is actually useful as a diagnostic (e.g., negative σ_xa² flags which sex has higher genetic variance; negative components can flag D×D epistasis / model misspecification).
Multiple-relatives extension: TYPE=TOEP(1) RANDOM statement handles sibships of size 1–5 (mix of biological + adopted); a twin-sibling design adds an AM component (MZ-pair-specific genetic) to restore r_a=1.0 for MZ twins, and an ST component for a special twin environment.
For continuous outcomes all four programs gave identical estimates; for binary outcomes (probit/logit via GLMM-style NLMIXED BINARY, or Mplus probit), results were close but not exactly equal across MEMA and SEMA — exact equivalence for categorical traits is left for future work.

Connections

Companion to rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and, which likewise casts twin/family biometric models as generalized linear mixed models (including binary traits) using gllamm — same MEMA-as-biometrics program, more general software.
Companion to pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and, which handles binary traits via GLMM; this paper’s binary-outcome section (NLMIXED probit/logit, the noted non-equivalence with SEMA) is the same problem these two address more fully.
Sits under biometrical-genetics and grounds the equivalence of variance-components-model, structural-equation-model, and linear-mixed-model formulations of heritability estimation in twin-and-family-study-design data.

Open questions

For binary/ordinal outcomes the MEMA and SEMA estimates are not exactly equal — what causes the discrepancy (link function, integration/quadrature, threshold parameterization) and can exact equivalence be established?
When does allowing negative variance components in VCM give genuinely better insight into misspecification (epistasis, sex differences) versus simply an inadmissible solution?
How far does the fixed-weight MEMA approach scale to large, unbalanced extended-family pedigrees and multivariate/longitudinal biometric factor models before SEMA flexibility is preferable?
Can newer mixed-model packages (GLLAMM, HLM, MLwiN, WinBUGS, R/S-Plus) reproduce the variance constraints (equal, uncorrelated, possibly negative components) imposed here via the Toeplitz device in SAS?

wiki/sources/meng-2023-assembloid-crispr.title: meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact
wiki/sources/meng-2023-assembloid-crispr.content:
Assembloid CRISPR screens reveal impact of disease genes in human neurodevelopment
One-line: Couples human forebrain assembloid models with pooled CRISPR loss-of-function screens to systematically map 425 neurodevelopmental-disorder (NDD) genes onto human cortical interneuron generation and migration, uncovering LNPK/endoplasmic-reticulum dynamics as a previously unappreciated driver of interneuron migration.
Key takeaways

Two-stage CRISPR screen over 425 NDD genes (354 ASD + 71 other NDD) in human assembloids built from hiPS cells, reading out generation and migration of ventral-forebrain interneurons.
Interneurons are generated ventrally (subpallium) and migrate long distances to the dorsal cortex; this protracted process extends into the postnatal period in humans and is implicated in ASD and other NDDs.
Model system: subpallial organoids (hSO, ventral forebrain) fused with cortical organoids (hCO, dorsal) to make a forebrain assembloid (hFA); a Dlxi1/2b::eGFP reporter labels MGE-derived interneurons that migrate from the hSO into the hCO.
Generation screen (in hSO): 13 candidate genes, top hits CSDE1 and SMAD4 (also PPP2R1A, PPM1D, FOXG1, SYNCRIP). Validated KOs of SMAD4 and CSDE1 impair subpallial differentiation (reduced DLX2, disrupted MGE identity).
Migration screen (in hFA, >1,000 assembloids): 33 candidate genes, enriched for cytoskeleton/cell-migration genes (NCKAP1, SEMA5A, PRKD1, RAC1) plus the ER-related gene LNPK and the shelterin/telomere gene TERF2.
Mechanism: during interneuron migration the endoplasmic reticulum is displaced forward along the leading neuronal branch before nuclear translocation (nucleokinesis); loss of LNPK disrupts this ER displacement and produces abnormal saltatory migration.
Effect scale: 46 of 425 NDD genes (~11%) interfere with human interneuron development — demonstrating the platform’s power to systematically link disease genes to developmental stages.

Notable claims / data

Statistical framework: hits called with casTLE maximum effect size + score; candidates required ≥2 sgRNAs with effects beyond roughly twice the s.d. of negative controls (generation: ≤ −1.57 / ≥ +1.57; migration: ≤ −2.5 / ≥ +2.5).
sgRNA library: five sgRNAs per gene (425 NDD + 13 known interneuron/safe-harbour genes) plus 218 safe-harbour negative-control sgRNAs; ~1,000× coverage; delivered by lentivirus co-expressing mCherry, selected by FACS.
Cell lines: knock-in Tet-Off CAG::Cas9 hiPS line (parental 1205-4); CAG::Cas9;Dlxi1/2b::eGFP line; 2242-1 and 1208-2 control lines for hCO; SEC61B-mEGFP line (AICS-0059 cl.36, Allen Institute) used to visualize ER.
SMAD4 and CSDE1 KO hSO: reduced proportion of Dlxi1/2b::eGFP+ cells (****P < 0.0001), reduced DLX2 and MGE TF expression (NKX2.1, LHX6, LHX8, SOX6), increased CGE-related TFs (NR2F2, NR2F1, PROX1); organoids consistently smaller.
LNPK KO and TERF2 KO reduce migration of interneurons into the cortical (hCO) side of hFA (LNPK P = 0.0428; TERF2 P = 0.0225) without affecting interneuron generation.
LNPK KO interneurons show reduced saltation length, fewer saltations/hour, and slower speed (live imaging); antisense-oligonucleotide (ASO) knockdown of LNPK (85% mRNA, 70% protein reduction) acutely reproduces the migration defect, arguing for an acute rather than purely developmental role.
Mouse in vivo validation: in utero-style electroporation of Lnpk-targeting sgRNA-Cas9 into E13-14 mouse ganglionic eminences reduces Lnpk and impairs tangential interneuron migration.
ER visualization (SEC61B-mEGFP): a large fraction of cellular ER is displaced along the leading branch, taking a linear conformation, before compacting in front of the nucleus prior to nucleokinesis; observed in ~75-88% of saltatory migrating cells; LNPK KO reduces the fraction of cells showing ER displacement (P = 0.004). Loss of ATL1 (atlastin-1, another ER-shaping protein) similarly reduces ER-displacement and saltation length, supporting an ER-tubule requirement.
LNPK loss-of-function mutations are reported in patients with severe epilepsy; TERF2 regulates telomere length; the study proposes ER-tubule–microtubule interaction as the link between LNPK and cytoplasmic dilation in the leading process.

Connections

A functional-single-cell-genomics / pooled CRISPR screen deployed inside 3D human organoids, extending screening beyond neural proliferation into later interneuron generation and migration.
Both top generation-screen hits are RNA-associated: CSDE1 (RNA-binding, post-transcriptional program) and SYNCRIP; connects autism/NDD genetics to translational/RNA-regulatory and TGF-β (SMAD4) control of neural differentiation.
Reinforces the role of GABAergic interneuron development in ASD and epilepsy pathophysiology; complements imaging/postmortem findings of altered interneuron density.
Methodologically related to other CRISPR-screen sources in the wiki (adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening, gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements) though applied to organoid developmental phenotypes rather than transcriptomic/regulatory readouts.

Open questions

Do the ~11% of NDD genes converge on shared molecular pathways (cytoskeleton, ER dynamics, RNA regulation), and can convergent vs divergent mechanisms across ASD subtypes be defined?
hSO models MGE-derived interneurons; what are the cell-type-specific effects on the diversity of GABAergic interneurons (e.g. CGE-derived) not captured here?
Non-cell-autonomous and non-neuronal contributions (vascular endothelial cells, oligodendrocytes) to migration are not captured — how much do NDD genes act through them?
How exactly does LNPK-dependent ER-tubule structure couple to microtubules and cytoplasmic dilation to drive nucleokinesis, and is this a general mechanism in human vs rodent migration?
Can automation and maturation-accelerating strategies expand the number of screenable genes and enable circuit-assembly-stage screens?

wiki/sources/michielsen-2026-decoding-ex.title: michielsen-2026-decoding-exon-inclusion-in
wiki/sources/michielsen-2026-decoding-ex.content:
Decoding exon inclusion in the human brain reveals more divergent splicing mechanisms in neurons than glia
One-line: Trains logistic-regression and deep-learning models to predict cell-type-specific exon inclusion (PSI) in human neurons vs glia from long-read single-nucleus data, finding that neuronal splicing of variable exons is consistently harder to predict and mechanistically diverges further from the standard sequence/RBP grammar than glial splicing.
Key takeaways

Uses long-read single-nucleus isoform RNA-seq (SnISOr-Seq) from human hippocampus (6 individuals) and frontal cortex (2 individuals) to compute cell-type-specific percent-spliced-in values for neurons and glia; “variable exons” are those with |ΔΨ(glia−neur)| > 0.25 (2,244 in HPC, 943 in FC).
Three model families predict exon inclusion: logistic regression on ENCODE eCLIP rna-binding-proteins binding sites (122 RBPs × 6 positional bins = 732 features), a deep-learning model adapted from Saluki using pre-mRNA sequence, and a combined sequence+RBP model — directly probing the splicing-code for alternative-splicing.
Central finding: glial PSI is much easier to predict than neuronal PSI across all models and both regions (median Spearman 0.54 vs 0.23 in HPC; 0.57 vs 0.10 in FC), implying neuronal splicing mechanisms in variable exons have diverged further from the general grammar.
Model interpretation (LR coefficients, in-silico saturation mutagenesis, TF-MoDISco) pinpoints cell-type-specific regulators — most strikingly ptbp1 and QKI in glia — and shows the sequence immediately around the splice sites dominates predictions.
The DL models also predict and prioritize the effects of splicing-qtls from GTEx, correctly predicting the expected direction for 83% of variants with a predicted effect.

Notable claims / data

Glia vs neuron predictability: median Spearman correlation 0.54 (glia) vs 0.23 (neuron) in HPC, and 0.57 vs 0.10 in FC; gap persists across LR, DL-sequence, and DL-sequence+RBP models.
Primary sequence is more informative for neurons: DLall-seq beats the best RBP-based LR model on neuronal variable exons (0.29 vs 0.23 in HPC); adding RBP channels helps glia (best model, 0.57 HPC / 0.65 FC) but hurts neurons.
RBP binding profiles of variable vs non-variable exons differ significantly more in neurons than glia (paired Wilcoxon, both HPC and FC). Splicing factors u2af1, U2AF2, SF3B4 mark high-PSI non-variable exons at the 3’ splice site, but this signal disappears in neuronal variable exons.
QKI has the strongest cell-type-specific LR coefficient in HPC (−0.15 glia vs +0.12 neuron at 3’ splice site), is significantly higher expressed in glia (Wilcoxon adj. p < 2.2e-16), and shows known position-dependent regulation (3’ SS represses, downstream promotes inclusion); variable exons are enriched for QKI sites (Fisher adj. p = 1.6e-13).
Neuron-specific exon features: of 82 variable microexons (≤27 bp), 80.5% have higher PSI in neurons; variable exons are enriched for frame-consistency (49.6% vs 37.3%), and frame-consistent variable exons skew toward neuronal inclusion (58.1%).
sQTL prioritization: of 326 GTEx hippocampus sQTL variants in range, an effect (|ΔISM| > 0.005) was predicted for 71; 83% (59/71) matched the expected direction; 74.6% of predicted-effect variants lie within 15 bp of a splice site. ISM showed only the ~50 bp upstream of the 3’ SS and ~150 bp downstream of the 5’ SS carry effects > 0.1.
Cross-validation across mouse data and ENCODE4 PGP1-derived astrocytes/neurons reproduces the glia > neuron predictability gap, arguing it is not a protocol artifact. Controls rule out PSI-distribution, intra-cell-type heterogeneity, and cross-individual variance as explanations.
