sickle-cell treatments — foreshadowing HbF reactivation as the therapeutic strategy.
The therapeutic “prize” is a short non-coding regulatory sequence (the BCL11A
erythroid enhancer) — it encodes no protein, underscoring the importance of
noncoding regulatory DNA.

Connections

The human-story companion to the wiki’s technical CRISPR-therapy sources:
stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of analyzes Casgevy’s
off-target profile (variant-aware nomination), while
villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome and
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of cite BCL11A-enhancer disruption as the
archetypal approved ex vivo Cas9 therapy.
Reinforces the crispr topic’s mechanism → tool → therapy arc from the patient/
clinical end, and pairs with kernohan-2024-expanding-diagnostic-toolbox-for-rare as
the therapy side of the diagnosis→therapy story.

Open questions

Cost and access: ex vivo HSC editing is a bespoke, expensive procedure — how does an
approved CRISPR cure reach the African populations who bear most of the disease burden?
Durability and safety of enhancer-disruption knockouts over a patient’s lifetime
(addressed technically in the peer-reviewed sources above, not this chapter).

wiki/sources/parikshak-2016-genome-wide-.title: parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna
wiki/sources/parikshak-2016-genome-wide-.content:
Genome-wide changes in lncRNA, splicing, and regional gene expression patterns in autism
One-line: Post-mortem transcriptome study of the largest autism cortex cohort to date, showing that ASD molecular pathology converges across lncRNA dysregulation, downregulated alternative splicing of neuron-specific/activity-dependent exons, and attenuated frontal–temporal regional gene-expression differences.
Key takeaways

rRNA-depleted RNA-seq of 251 post-mortem samples (frontal cortex, temporal cortex, cerebellum) from 48 individuals with ASD and 49 controls; differential gene expression (DGE) in cortex replicated prior microarray data (R² = 0.60).
ASD cortex shows 584 upregulated and 558 downregulated genes (FDR < 0.05, linear mixed-effects model); downregulated set enriched in neuronal genes (e.g. PVALB, SYT2 interneuron markers), upregulated set enriched in microglia/astrocyte genes. No significant DGE in cerebellum — cortex is selectively vulnerable.
A comprehensive alternative-splicing analysis found 1,127 differential splicing events in 833 genes in cortex (none in cerebellum). Most events are exclusion of neuron-specific exons; the differential splicing signature is not driven by DGE, consistent with splicing being an independent link to disease risk (splicing-and-disease).
Differential splicing correlates with cortical expression of neuronal splicing factors and overlaps experimentally defined targets of Rbfox1, SRRM4, and ptbp1 (PTBP1); events are enriched for neuronal activity-dependent exons, supporting an excitation/inhibition-balance model of ASD.
60 lncRNAs are in the DGE set; many are brain-enriched and primate-specific (e.g. LINC00693, LINC00689, both upregulated in ASD but normally downregulated during development; several interact with miRNA-processing complexes or FMRP).
Attenuated cortical patterning (ACP): 523 genes differ between frontal and temporal cortex in controls but not in ASD; the transcription factor SOX5 (sole ACP-set member among enriched TFs) loses its correlation with predicted targets in ASD, and SOX5 overexpression in human neural progenitors induced synaptic genes and repressed proliferation.
A genetically defined ASD subtype, chromosome 15q11.2–13.1 duplication (dup15q, n = 9), shows cis upregulation of the 15q region plus genome-wide (trans) DGE and differential splicing highly concordant with idiopathic ASD (R² = 0.64–0.65) but of greater magnitude — biological validation of the shared signature.
WGCNA identified 24 modules; 6 ASD-associated (3 up / 3 down). Downregulated modules are synaptic/neuronal, upregulated modules immune/glial (microglial), and the two anticorrelate. CTX.M19/CTX.M20 trajectories diverge between ASD and controls over the first two decades of life.

Notable claims / data

Cortex DGE replication R² = 0.60 (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶) against independent microarray samples; signal present in >2/3 of ASD samples.
1,127 differential splicing events / 833 genes in cortex; reproduced with an alternative pipeline (OLego-based) and validated by RT–PCR.
Splicing-factor perturbation overlaps: Rbfox1 knockout, SRRM4 overexpression, and PTBP1 knockdown all significantly overlap ASD differential splicing (e.g. fold enrichments ~2.0–2.4, P down to 1.7 × 10⁻⁵ for inclusion/exclusion subsets).
PC1 of protein-coding DGE, lncRNA DGE, and differential splicing are highly correlated (R² > 0.8), indicating molecular convergence is a “unitary phenomenon.”
ACP set: 551 vs 51 genes show frontal–temporal DGE in controls vs ASD respectively; SOX5 DGE set enriched in ACP set (P = 1.1 × 10⁻⁷, Fisher’s exact). Δmedian SOX5–target correlation R drops by 0.24 in ASD.
dup15q vs ASD effect-size concordance: DGE R² = 0.64 (slope 3.7), differential splicing R² = 0.65 (slope 3.2), both P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶.
Module CTX.M24 enriched for ASD rare mutations and lncRNAs and fetal-cortex genes; CTX.M20 (overlapping the ACP set) enriched for ASD common-variant polygenic risk (FDR < 0.1), linking polygenic risk to regional attenuation.
Caveat: many observed changes may be compensatory/consequential rather than causal; ASD GWAS at the time was underpowered, limiting common-variant conclusions.

Connections

Direct disease sibling for splicing-and-disease — splicing alterations link to common disease risk independently of expression changes.
Brain/neurodevelopment context shared with kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated AS) and the developing-brain proteome work wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of.
Splicing-factor regulatory programs (Rbfox1, PTBP1, neuronal microexons via SRRM4) connect to baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and mis-splicing-in-disease reviews scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.
Brain-region splicing variation relates to zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain sQTL) and microglial isoform work humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia being the upregulated cell type here).
Uses percent-spliced-in (ΔPSI) metrics and rna-seq throughout.

Open questions

Which splicing/lncRNA changes are causal vs compensatory responses to a primary genetic/prenatal insult?
How would larger, better-powered ASD GWAS sharpen the link between common polygenic risk and the CTX.M20/ACP regional-attenuation module?
What are the functions of the primate-specific brain lncRNAs (LINC00693, LINC00689) and their role as candidate ASD risk loci?
Do the activity-dependent splicing changes reflect altered excitation/inhibition balance mechanistically, and can the early-developmental trajectory window (first decade) be targeted for intervention?

wiki/sources/park-2018-the-expanding-lan.title: park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative
wiki/sources/park-2018-the-expanding-lan.content:
The Expanding Landscape of Alternative Splicing Variation in Human Populations
One-line: A review (from the Xing lab) of how population-scale RNA-seq, sQTL/ASAS analysis, massively parallel reporter assays, and machine/deep learning have revealed widespread naturally occurring genetic variation in alternative splicing across human populations, and its link to GWAS traits and disease.
Key takeaways

Genetic variation in splicing-regulatory-elements is pervasive in human populations; many sQTL SNPs are enriched at the 5’ and 3’ splice-sites but a large fraction lie outside them, modifying exonic/intronic enhancers/silencers or rna-binding-proteins binding sites.
Splicing quantitative trait locus (sQTL) analysis treats percent-spliced-in (PSI) values as quantitative traits tested against genotypes; allele-specific alternative splicing (ASAS) is a complementary cis-only approach using heterozygous SNPs to compare the two haplotypes within one individual.
sQTLs frequently colocalize with GWAS signals, arguing that alternative-splicing is a primary causal mechanism (not a secondary effect) linking genetic variation to complex traits and disease — comparable to or greater than eQTL effects (Li et al. 2016).
Massively parallel reporter assays (massively-parallel-reporter-assay) plus minigene reporters allow rapid screening of which variants causally alter splicing; deep learning models the “splicing code” to predict PSI and variant effects.
RNA-seq-based splicing analysis is emerging as a diagnostic and prognostic tool (rare-disease diagnosis, cancer survival prediction).

Notable claims / data

cis splicing mutations are estimated to constitute 15%–60% of human disease mutations.
Population sQTL studies (Table 1): Montgomery et al. (LCLs, n=60, 110 sQTL events); Pickrell et al. (LCLs, n=69, 187 sQTL genes at 10% FDR); Lappalainen/Geuvadis (LCLs, n=462, 639 trQTL genes); Battle et al. (whole blood, n=922, 1,370 sQTL genes at 5% FDR, 159 in high LD with GWAS SNPs); Fadista et al. (pancreatic islets, n=89, 371 sQTLs in T2D loci); Li et al. family study (LCLs, n=17, 261 sQTLs at 50% FDR, enriched for rare variants); GTEx pilot (43 tissues, 1,641 samples/175 individuals, ~1,900 sQTL genes/tissue with Altrans and ~250 with sQTLseekeR; tissue-specific sQTLs only 7%–21%); Chen et al. (immune cells, n=197, >2,000 sQTL genes/cell type); Pala et al. (Sardinia leukocytes, n=624, 6,768 sQTLs); Takata et al. (brain prefrontal cortex, n=206, 1,595 sQTLs enriched for schizophrenia loci).
Worked sQTL/GWAS examples: SP140 exon 7 (silent exonic SNP rs28445040; T allele lowers inclusion; in LD with GWAS SNPs for CLL, MS, Crohn, IBD) and ERAP2 exon 10 (intronic 5’SS SNP rs2248374 activating a cryptic 5’SS → PTC → NMD; linked to Crohn/IBD). Both causal variants are silent-exonic or intronic and missed by common variant-annotation tools.
MaPSy (Soemedi et al.) tested 4,964 exonic disease mutations: ~10% perturbed splicing in both in vivo and in vitro assays vs ~3% of common SNPs (likely a lower-bound, single cell type HEK293). Rosenberg et al. tested >2 million synthetic minigenes for alt 5’/3’ splice site selection.
Deep learning splicing-code models (Frey/Xiong et al.) achieved R²=0.65 between predicted and empirical PSI on independent data and predicted misregulated splicing in 19 ASD candidate genes.
Rare-disease diagnosis proof-of-concept (Cummings et al.): muscle RNA-seq of 63 patients vs 184 GTEx controls yielded genetic diagnoses for 35% of previously undiagnosed individuals (e.g., recurrent COL6A1 deep-intronic GC→GT creating a novel 5’SS).
Notes the FDA approval of nusinersen (antisense oligonucleotide) correcting splicing in spinal muscular atrophy.

Connections

Methodologically anchors event-based tools cited elsewhere in the wiki: miso, rmats, MAJIQ, suppa, and PSI (percent-spliced-in); transcript-level quantitation via kallisto/bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification.
Complements deep-learning splice-prediction work centered on spliceai and the splicing-code; this review predates SpliceAI but reviews its lineage (Barash splicing code, AVISPA, Leung/Xiong deep models, BRIE).
Extends splicing-and-disease and splicing-and-evolution into the population-genetics regime; relates to cis-regulatory-elements and ai-in-genomics.
Same Xing lab as shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (rMATS); ships only the helper rmats2sashimiplot as a Web Resource, not a new analysis tool.

Open questions

How many GWAS signals across all complex traits are ultimately splicing-causal, once full ASAS + long-read coverage and additional tissues are layered in?
Can predictive models incorporate chromatin state, epigenetic marks, and 3D genome organization to capture co-transcriptional splicing regulation?
How well do MPRA/HEK293 reporter results generalize to the cell-type-specific splicing of disease-relevant tissues?
What is the contribution of non-SNP variants (indels, short tandem repeats) to population-scale splicing variation?

wiki/sources/park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-.title: park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing
wiki/sources/park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-.content:
In vivo neuronal gene editing via CRISPR–Cas9 amphiphilic nanocomplexes alleviates deficits in mouse models of Alzheimer’s disease
One-line: A non-viral, amphiphilic peptide (R7L10)–Cas9 ribonucleoprotein nanocomplex edits Bace1 in post-mitotic neurons of the adult mouse brain, lowering amyloid-beta pathology and rescuing cognitive deficits in two Alzheimer’s disease mouse models with minimal off-target effects.
Key takeaways

Demonstrates efficient genome-editing in post-mitotic adult neurons in vivo — a setting where HDR-based correction fails and only NHEJ-mediated indel gene disruption is practical — using a delivered Cas9 protein–sgRNA RNP rather than a virus.
Delivery vehicle is the amphiphilic peptide R7L10 (a poly-arginine/poly-leucine peptide) that self-assembles with Cas9-sgRNA into ~125 nm spherical nanocomplexes (cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex); this avoids viral integration and the persistence of Cas9 expression.
Target gene is Bace1 (beta-secretase 1), required to generate amyloid-beta (Aβ); knocking it down in neurons is a therapeutic strategy for Alzheimer’s disease.
Therapeutic efficacy shown in two models: the 5xfad-mouse-model (five familial AD mutations) and an App knock-in model (humanized App with Swedish/Iberian/Arctic mutations) — Aβ plaque and Aβ42 reduced, cognitive/memory deficits alleviated.
Comprehensive off-target profiling (WGS, WES, digenome-seq, Cas-OFFinder) found no genome-wide elevation of mutation rates from Cas9 nanocomplex activity.

Notable claims / data

Nanocomplex physical characterization: ~125 nm uniform spheres (DLS/AFM/SEM); STEM-EDS confirmed N (peptide) + P (sgRNA) colocalization; positive ζ-potential and stable size across R7L10:Cas9-sgRNA weight ratios.
Cas9 protein persisted ~1 week post-injection and declined to undetectable by 3 weeks in brain (transient exposure, unlike viral expression).
In vitro editing: on-target indels at Bace1 and Th with very low off-target; also achieved HDR-mediated 2A-eGFP-IRES-Puro knock-in at the TH locus in human NSC-derived neurons.
In vivo editing efficiency (Surveyor / deep sequencing): ~26% indel at Bace1 and ~31% at Th in WT hippocampus/midbrain; ~45% editing rate at Bace1 target region by deep sequencing in nanocomplex-treated brains.
In 6-month-old 5XFAD hippocampus: ~70% decrease in Bace1 expression in CA3; significantly decreased APP β-cleavage products (βCTF, C99); reduced Aβ plaque and Aβ42 secretion.
Off-target (Table 1, n=3): control vs Cas9-Bace1 brains had similar SNV/indel counts; e.g. WGS 686 vs 775 unique SNVs and 347 vs 408 indels; none of these fell in top-100 predicted off-target sites; no large-scale rearrangements or CNVs; Digenome-seq showed uniform genome-wide cleavage patterns with clean on-target and no off-target double-peaks validated.
Behavior: Cas9-Bace1-treated 5XFAD mice showed increased contextual fear-conditioning associative learning, rescued Y-maze alternation, shorter Morris water maze escape latency, and more time in target quadrant vs 5XFAD controls.
Multiple deliveries into hippocampal CA3 more efficiently suppressed Bace1 and disease phenotypes than a single injection, suggesting multi-targeting for translation.
No increase in inflammation (Iba1, Tnf-α, Il-12A, Itgam, Cd86, Cd68), toxicity (blood urea nitrogen), or apoptosis (cleaved caspase-3) in nanocomplex-treated brains.
Aβ suppression by Cas9-Bace1 was more efficient than by chemical BACE1 inhibitors.

Connections

Extends genome-editing into non-dividing neural tissue via non-viral RNP delivery — complements virus-mediated CRISPR approaches by removing integration/long-term-expression risks.
Relevant to neurodegeneration and Alzheimer’s therapy; contrasts with pharmacological BACE1 inhibition.
The R7L10 amphiphilic peptide delivery (cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex) is a reusable method for CNS RNP delivery beyond this paper.
Off-target validation stack (digenome-seq + WGS/WES + Cas-OFFinder) is a template for rigorous in vivo editing safety assessment.

Open questions

Gene editing is irreversible — authors caution about rare/very rare off-target or long-term effects; longer-term and non-human primate safety studies are needed.
Delivery cannot target widespread neuronal dysfunction, so the approach is proposed as best suited to focal neurodegenerative disease (e.g., Parkinson’s), not late-stage widespread AD.
Cell-type-specific delivery systems are needed to restrict editing to desired brain cell types.
Optimal timing, dosing, and number of deliveries for durable phenotype rescue remain to be defined.

wiki/sources/patowary-2024-developmental.title: patowary-2024-developmental-isoform-diversity-in-the
wiki/sources/patowary-2024-developmental.content:
Developmental isoform diversity in the human neocortex informs neuropsychiatric risk mechanisms
One-line: A PsychENCODE2 long-read-rna-seq (PacBio Iso-Seq) full-length isoform atlas of the developing human neocortex (mid-gestation, progenitor vs neuron zones, tissue + single-cell), cataloguing 214,516 isoforms (72.6% novel) and using this isoform-resolved annotation to reprioritize rare de novo neurodevelopmental-disorder variants — the Gandal/de la Torre-Ubieta developmental counterpart to bulk short-read brain studies. (sciso-portal)
Key takeaways

Long-read full-length transcriptome of microdissected germinal zone (GZ, progenitor-enriched) and cortical plate (CP, neuron-enriched) from PCW15–17 human neocortex (6 donors bulk + 3 donors single-cell, >7,000 cells via ScISOr-Seq) — the first systematic isoform-diversity map of developing human cortex.
214,516 isoforms, 72.6% novel vs Gencode v33; >7,000 novel spliced exons; the transcribed genome extended by ~27 Mb; 92,422 isoforms with protein-coding potential, 35,467 with mass-spec peptide support (new proteoforms).
Isoform switching drives cell fate. GZ→CP differential transcript usage (DTU) in 2,679/10,809 multi-isoform genes; 1,669 genes switch isoform with no change in overall gene expression — a regulatory layer invisible to gene-level analysis (alternative-splicing, cf. percent-spliced-in). Switches alter ORF length, NMD sensitivity, and 3′UTR length (DPUI lengthening in neurons).
RBP-driven networks: isoform-usage networks are organized by rna-binding-proteins rather than cell type — PTBP1, SRRM4, RBFOX1/2/3, ELAVL1/2, CELF2 — and implicate understudied RBPs (SAFB, UTP18, SRSF9) in corticogenesis (splicing-factors).
Disease mechanism: isoform number/complexity per gene strongly predicts NDD-risk-gene status; dynamic isoform expression/usage is the feature most enriched across neuropsychiatric disorders; the isoform-resolved annotation reprioritizes thousands of rare de novo ASD/ID/NDD variants to more-severe consequences and reveals more cryptic splice variants than previously reported (splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

Technology: PacBio HiFi Iso-Seq (>33M CCS reads), >99% full-length alignment vs ~85% for short-read; median read 2.99 kb. Single-cell via ScISOr-Seq (Drop-seq + UMIs), barcodes matched to prior short-read scRNA-seq.
Validation: ~80% of novel TSSs supported by CAGE/ATAC; ~91% of novel 3′ ends by polyA sites/motifs; 74% of novel junctions in Intropolis; RT-PCR validation of novel exons.
NDD-risk genes have significantly more isoforms (OR 1.56–1.8, controlling for length/expression). 319 multiexonic genes match no existing model (256 antisense, 63 intergenic), e.g. an 18-exon antisense gene to ASD-risk DPYSL2.
Single cell: early-stage excitatory neurons show greatest isoform diversity; isoform-centric clustering reveals previously uncharacterized cell states across 16 cell types.
Resource portal: sciso.gandallab.org/ (sciso-portal).

Connections

The developing-brain, long-read complement to the wiki’s short-read brain work: it directly addresses the cell-type-confounding and isoform-resolution open questions that bulk studies (walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis) leave open — here splicing is resolved to full-length isoforms and to GZ/CP + single cells.
Same lab lineage as isoform-level disease mapping (bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic, isoTWAS) and the ASD cortical transcriptome (gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across); shares the long-read-isoform-atlas goal with heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform (aged cortex) but maps development.
RBP-network thread connects to encode-rbp-map and splicing-factors; variant-reannotation connects to splice-variant prediction (spliceai, absplice2).

Open questions

Does isoform diversity at later developmental stages and across cortical regions differ from this mid-gestation GZ/CP snapshot?
How many of the 150k+ novel isoforms are functional vs low-abundance noise — and which novel proteoforms are translated in vivo?
Can isoform-resolved variant reannotation raise the diagnostic yield for unsolved NDD cases (cryptic splice variants), and does it generalize beyond ASD/ID gene sets?
Would matched genotypes/WGS turn this isoform atlas into developmental isoform-QTL / sQTL maps?

wiki/sources/pawitan-2004-estimation-of-.title: pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and
wiki/sources/pawitan-2004-estimation-of-.content:
Estimation of genetic and environmental factors for binary traits using family data
One-line: A general GLMM/liability-threshold method, fitted by likelihood over arbitrary extended-family structures, for partitioning a binary trait into maternal, foetal, and environmental variance components — demonstrated on Swedish birth-registry pre-eclampsia data.
Key takeaways

Treats a binary disease phenotype as a thresholded unobserved normal “liability”, fitting it directly as a probit-link generalized-linear-mixed-model — a liability-threshold-model embedded in a variance-components-model. This handles arbitrary family structures (not just twins/relative-pairs) and incomplete outcome data.
Motivated by the limits of classic twin-and-family-study-design: twinning is rare so twin studies are underpowered for common conditions (PE prevalence ~4%), and nuclear families alone cannot separate maternal vs foetal genetic effects or genetic vs shared-environment effects. Extended family data (e.g. families of sib-pairs) breaks these confoundings.
Inference is by direct (exact) likelihood: the random effects are integrated out, reducing each family’s contribution to an orthant probability of a multivariate normal, evaluated via a fast Cholesky-based Monte Carlo algorithm (Genz). Parameters estimated by a derivative-free simplex; the authors note ongoing work on a hierarchical-likelihood approach to avoid the integration.
heritability is defined on the liability scale as h² = σ²_g/(σ²_g + σ²_e + 1), consistent with standard biometrical-genetics quantitative-genetics; with the probit link it is the proportion of liability variance due to additive genetic effects.
Families of sib-pairs (mothers who are full sisters, fathers unrelated, etc.) let the model separate maternal genetic (m), foetal genetic (f), common-family-environment (e), and common-sibling-environment (s) components, and can even split the foetal effect into maternal- vs paternal-transmitted contributions (f_m, f_p).

Notable claims / data

Twin study (Ros et al., ref [6]) of PE: 917 MZ + 1199 DZ Swedish female twin pairs, only 8 concordant MZ / 2 concordant DZ affected pairs → heritability estimate 0.57 but with 95% CI (0.0–0.71) — illustrating the power problem.
Familial clustering far exceeds chance: among women with 2 pregnancies, 570 had PE in both vs 68 expected under randomness; among 3-pregnancy women, 21 PE-in-all-three vs <1 expected. Clustering by fathers is similar (536 vs 63), showing nuclear data cannot separate maternal from paternal genetic contribution.
Application data: all Swedish pregnancies 1987–1997, first 3 known same-partner pregnancies; 239,193 family-pairs, 774,858 pregnancies, 20,358 pre-eclamptic. Mother–mother, father–father, and mother–father sib-pair groupings used.
PE risk is much higher in first (13,072/320,512 = 4.1%) than subsequent (7,286/454,346 = 1.6%) pregnancies; modelled via separate prevalence parameters φ₁, φ₂ orthogonal to the variance components.
Model 2 estimates (liability variance components): maternal σ²_m = 1.08 (0.99–1.18), foetal σ²_f = 0.63 (0.32–0.81), family-env σ²_e = 0.40 (0.33–0.51), sibling σ²_s = 0.02 (0.0–0.03). Maternal heritability h²_m = 1.08/(1.08+0.63+0.40+0.02+1) = 35%; foetal 20%, family-env 13%, sibling 1%.
Model 3 (split foetal effect) shows no evidence of unequal maternal vs paternal foetal contribution (Δ −2logL = 2.5, 1 df). Model 4 (later-pregnancy genetic/family interaction terms) is not a significant improvement (Δ = 0.1).
Conclusion: PE has both maternal and foetal genetic origin (part of the foetal effect from paternal genes), plus a real common-family environment effect but no detectable sibling-environment effect; goodness-of-fit (χ² = 656.3, 434 df for patterns with expected freq > 5) shows Model 2 explains most but not all variation.

Connections

Extends the same GLMM/liability logic used in rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and (which fits twin/family biometrical models with gllamm) from twins/relative-pairs to arbitrary extended pedigrees, and replaces approximate quadrature with exact orthant-probability likelihood.
Shares the variance-components-model foundation that mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models frames as the path-analysis ↔ VCM equivalence; here the same additive-genetic correlations (MZ=1, DZ/full-sibs=0.5, cousins=0.125) are encoded directly in random-effect correlation (R) matrices rather than path diagrams.
The probit/liability-threshold framing makes binary biometrical-genetics a special case of the generalized-linear-mixed-model, and the heritability definition aligns with classical quantitative-genetics (Falconer; Sham 1998).

Open questions

Can the hierarchical-likelihood (h-likelihood) formulation, flagged as ongoing work, deliver the same estimates without the Monte Carlo integration and known to fail for small clusters under PQL/APHL?
How to incorporate continuous fixed covariates without the data-grouping step that the current efficient likelihood computation requires?
Non-additive genetic effects (dominance, gene–gene, maternal×paternal interaction) are confounded with the family-environment effect here — what design or data resolves them?
Given that the estimated genetic components still leave PE largely unexplained, how do specific molecular PE polymorphisms map onto these liability-scale variance partitions?

wiki/sources/perochon-2023-early-detecti.title: perochon-2023-early-detection-of-autism
wiki/sources/perochon-2023-early-detecti.content:
Early detection of autism using digital behavioral phenotyping
One-line: A multiclinic prospective study showing a tablet app (sensetoknow) that quantifies autism-related behaviors via computer vision achieves high, demographically fair diagnostic accuracy for toddler autism screening in primary care.
Key takeaways

Combines multiple digital behavioral phenotypes (gaze, facial dynamics, head movements, response to name, blink rate, motor/touch) rather than a single signal, better capturing the heterogeneous presentation of autism.
Deployed the sensetoknow app during routine pediatric well-child visits, targeting the toddler age when screening is normally done.
Uses XGBoost over 23 phenotypes with SHAP-based model interpretability, giving individualized, auditable explanations for each prediction.
Sensitivity was consistent across sex, race and ethnicity, addressing known disparities of questionnaire-based screening.

Notable claims / data

Sample: 475 toddlers (17–36 months; 269 boys, 206 girls); 49 diagnosed autistic, 98 developmental/language delay (DD–LD), 328 neurotypical.
Autism vs neurotypical: AUC = 0.90 (CI 0.87–0.93), sensitivity 87.8%, specificity 80.8%, NPV 97.8%, PPV 40.6%; rises to AUC 0.93 on high-confidence administrations.
Autism vs nonautism (DD–LD + neurotypical): AUC = 0.86, sensitivity 81.6%, specificity 80.5%.
Combining app + M-CHAT-R/F: AUC = 0.97, sensitivity 92.1%, specificity 91.8%.
Nine autistic children who screened M-CHAT-R/F-negative were correctly flagged by the app.
Top SHAP predictors: facing forward during social movies (|SHAP| 11.2%), percent gaze at social stimuli (11.1%), response-to-name delay (7.1%); feature interactions contributed 35.4% of prediction.
Context: prior M-CHAT/F primary-care study (>25,000 children) had sensitivity 39.0%, PPV 14.6%; eye-tracking-alone screening had specificity 98.0% but sensitivity 17.0%.

Connections

Applies model-interpretability (SHAP) and multimodal-learning to a clinical autism screening problem.
Complements molecular/genetic autism work such as schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism with a behavioral-phenotyping detection angle.
Relates to early-brain-development framing in courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development.

Open questions

PPV remains modest (40.6%) at population prevalence — how to triage app-positive children without over-referral?
Generalizability beyond a single US health system, and to home/non-clinic administration.
Whether digital phenotypes track intervention response or predict subtypes, not just presence of autism.

wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.title: peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes
wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.content:
Emerging role of G-quadruplexes in alternative splicing regulation
One-line: A mechanistic review arguing that RNA G-quadruplexes (rG4s) are an emerging class of secondary-structure regulators of alternative-splicing, acting position-dependently near splice-sites to recruit or block splicing factors, with strong links to cancer and neurodegeneration and a therapeutic angle via G4 ligands.
Key takeaways

g-quadruplex structures are noncanonical four-stranded folds in guanine-rich nucleic acid: guanines pair via Hoogsteen (not Watson–Crick) bonds into stacked G-quartets, stabilised by K+ cations. They form in both DNA (dG4s) and RNA (rG4s) and act as rna-structure-and-splicing elements distinct from sequence motifs.
rG4s regulate alternative-splicing in a position-dependent way: located near donor (5’ ss) or acceptor (3’ ss) sites, they either recruit rG4-binding splicing-factors that redirect the spliceosome to proximal sites, or remodel pre-mRNA to mask/expose splice sites — functioning as enhancers or silencers (splicing-regulatory-elements).
This is a structure-based complement to the splicing-code: rG4s overlay sequence-defined splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements, and their effect depends on dynamic folding equilibria rather than a fixed consensus.
Many examples span all five major ASE types (exon skipping, intron retention, alternative 5’/3’ ss, mutually exclusive exons); rna-binding-proteins including the hnRNP family, FMRP, AFF family, SRSF1, and RBM25 read these rG4s.
dG4s also influence splicing indirectly via cotranscriptional-splicing: by slowing RNA Pol II elongation (and via R-loops on the non-template strand), they alter exon inclusion (Box 3), separate from the direct rG4 effects.
rG4-controlled splicing is heavily implicated in splicing-and-disease, especially cancer (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x, BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) and neurodegeneration (BACE1/Alzheimer, FMR1/Fragile X).
G4 ligands (e.g. PhenDC3, pyridostatin, GQC-05) are model splicing-therapeutics that stabilise/unfold rG4s or block G4-binding proteins; effects across ASEs are often contradictory, so no general rule yet exists.

Notable claims / data

Scale: ~100,000 proteins from ~20,000 human genes via AS. ~60% of human transcripts contain ≥1 putative quadruplex-forming sequence (PQS); ~1,200,000 PQSs (300,000 unique motifs) across transcripts and splice junctions; canonical folding rule d(GGG+N1–7)x4 predicts ~400,000 dG4s in the genome.
A threefold enrichment of G4s near splice junctions, preferentially on the non-template (coding) strand (implying the corresponding rG4 matters); G4-at-ss enrichment is conserved in vertebrates but not invertebrates (possibly because AS matters less there).
FMR1 (Fragile X): two rG4s in exon 15 inhibit inclusion; FMRP binds them via its RGG motif and modulates its own splicing, favouring minor ISO2/ISO3 isoforms (autoregulatory feedback). ~15 loop mutations (ΔG4) altered exon-15 splicing without changing translation.
AFF family (AFF1–4; AFF2/FMR2 → FRAXE intellectual disability) bind rG4s via a conserved C-terminal domain (not RGG), localise to nuclear speckles, and broadly modulate AS.
CD44: intron-8 rG4 (element I-8) promotes the epithelial CD44v isoform; hnRNPF regulates it (depletion accelerates EMT). Alkaloids emetine and cephaeline modulate inclusion by unfolding rG4s (~60% of emetine-affected ASEs are skipped exons).
EWS-FLI (Ewing sarcoma): exon-8 rG4 promotes exon exclusion (keeping the oncoprotein); hnRNPH1 destabilises the rG4. Pyridostatin (PDS) blocks hnRNPH1 binding and reduces viability of EWSR1-fusion cells.
BCL-2: exon-3 rG4 favours skipping → proapoptotic BCL-2β; interplay with three nearby m6A sites; SRSF1 (an rG4-binding factor) resolves the rG4. Bcl-2β sensitises breast cancer cells to chemotherapy.
hTERT: intron-6 rG4s act as intronic splicing silencers; ligands Bisquinolinium 12459, CX-5461, and PhenDC3 shift splicing toward the inactive β transcript (telomestatin/BRACO19 had no effect — ligand selectivity).
TP53: intron-3 rG4 promotes intron-2 retention → dominant-negative ΔNp53/Δ40p53; ligand 360A shifts toward full-length p53. A common intronic polymorphism disrupting this rG4 is linked to increased cancer risk.
BCL-x (“rG4 saga”, Figure 1): exon-2 GQ-2/Q2 rG4 (overlaps the Gt16 G-tract) downstream of the alternative 5’ ss controls Bcl-xL (antiapoptotic) vs Bcl-xS (proapoptotic) balance. Splicing factor RBM25 binds Q2 directly via its arginine–glutamate (RE) domain — a novel G4-binding domain — and this binding is required for Bcl-xS production. Ligands GQC-05, PhenDC3, and the novel PhenDH8/PhenDH9 promote Bcl-xS; a genome screen for the Gt16-like G-tract found 3374 exons in 2707 genes.
Other cases: PAX9 intron-1 rG4 (positively regulates splicing; conserved in placental mammals; affects dentition — splicing-and-evolution); IGFN1 intron-15 (renal/breast cancer, PDS); CD133 introns 3/7 (cancer stem cells, RHPS4); BACE1 exon-3 alternative 5’ ss (hnRNPH/hnRNPL; Alzheimer); TRα/THRA alternative 3’ ss (G30 element, only suggestive evidence); vertebrate mirtron 5’-arm rG4s aiding mirtron biogenesis.
Mechanism (Box 6, splicing-and-chromatin/condensate angle): rG4–protein interactions may nucleate liquid–liquid phase separation (LLPS) at nuclear speckles, concentrating splicing factors — proposed but not yet demonstrated for the validated rG4 cases.

Connections

Structure vs sequence: extends splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements from a sequence-motif view to a secondary-structure view — rG4s are folded ESE/ISE/ESS/ISS-like elements whose activity is position-dependent and dynamic, complementing the splicing-code.
Sits in the same RNA-structure thread as Lee 2015 (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative), which discusses RNA secondary structure masking splice sites; this review supplies the G4-specific molecular detail and the new [[rna-structure-and-splicing]] entity.
splicing-and-disease: a curated cancer-centric catalogue (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x/BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) plus neurodegeneration (BACE1, FMR1).
splicing-therapeutics: positions G4 ligands as splicing modulators; relates to BH3-mimetic (venetoclax) and SF3B1-modulator strategies, and to G4-inducing ASOs (G-ASOs) for exon skipping in Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
cotranscriptional-splicing: dG4/R-loop effects on Pol II elongation (Box 3) tie G4 biology to the transcription–splicing coupling thread.

Open questions

The molecular mechanisms of rG4-mediated AS, and the role of phase separation, remain largely unproven (mostly hypothesised models).
Which methods can confirm that an in-silico PQS actually forms a functional rG4 in cellulo and controls a specific gene’s AS (single-RNA-molecule monitoring is lacking).
G4 ligand selectivity is a major limitation: hundreds of thousands of competing dG4s/rG4s exist, effects across ASEs are often antinomic, and no general predictive “G4–ligand code” yet exists.
Is rG4-mediated splicing a general feature of apoptosis-regulating genes with opposing isoforms (e.g. BCL-x, MCL-1)?
Systematic identification of the full set of rG4-binding RBPs that reshape the AS landscape, and the influence of surrounding chemical RNA modifications (e.g. m6A), are still needed.

wiki/sources/pigini-2026-poison-exon-spl.title: pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in
wiki/sources/pigini-2026-poison-exon-spl.content:
Poison exon splicing in the human brain: a new paradigm for understanding and targeting neurological disorders
One-line: A genome-wide catalog of human poison exons (PEs) — alternative exons whose inclusion creates premature stop codons and triggers NMD — mapping their brain-specific and developmental splicing and 1,625 pathogenic variants that disrupt them, with CRISPR prime-editing validation in iPSC-derived neurons.
Key takeaways

Poison exons are conserved exons that, when included, introduce a premature termination codon (PTC) and trigger nonsense-mediated decay (NMD), down-tuning protein output. They are a specialized case of alternative-splicing coupled to NMD (the “AS-NMD” / poison-exon mechanism) and a recurring theme in splicing-and-disease.
A comparative analysis of 957 eukaryotic transcriptomes (Ensembl) shows Homo sapiens has the highest enrichment of NMD-targeted isoforms; 39.6% of human protein-coding genes give rise to >=1 predicted NMD isoform. The authors release the prediction pipeline as the tool evonmd.
Systematic in-silico annotation against Gencode v26 identified 9,814 putative human PEs (17.8% of 55,150 annotated AS events); 59.2% arise from cassette exons, the rest from alternative 3’/5’ splice-site usage.
Using rna-seq from GTEx and BrainSpan, they found 83 PEs uniquely detected in brain, 973 PEs differentially spliced in brain vs other tissues, and 1,284 PEs differentially spliced across brain development (before vs after birth). Brain-expressed genes are depleted (not enriched) for PEs, suggesting tight regulatory pressure.
Integrating ClinVar pathogenic variants with spliceai predictions flagged 1,625 pathogenic variants predicted to affect splicing of 743 PEs, enriched for seizures/epilepsy, intellectual disability, neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative disorders.
CRISPR prime editing in HEK293T and iPSC-derived i3N neurons functionally validated a subset; the deep-intronic NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T (Leigh syndrome) variant increased PE inclusion, reduced NDUFAF6 protein, and impaired mitochondrial membrane potential.

Notable claims / data

Evolutionary scan: average 2.67% of coding genes per genome produce >=1 NMD isoform; vertebrates (3.7%) and plants (3.7%) highest, fungi (0.6%) and protists (0.1%) lowest. In humans, NMD isoforms = 20.1% of all protein-coding isoforms (14.3% under stringent TSL 1-3 filtering); 39.6% of genes have >=1 NMD isoform (30.3% under TSL 1-3). Robust to BUSCO completeness >=90% filtering — not an annotation-depth artifact.
PE definition (stringent, to minimize NMD escape): exon <=300 bp; not overlapping 5’/3’ UTR; on inclusion generates a PTC >150 bp downstream of TSS, >50 nt upstream of the last exon-exon junction, and outside the terminal exon.
CHX (cycloheximide) NMD-stabilization assay confirmed 87.5% (14/16) of putative PEs trigger NMD in HEK293T cells; same 87.5% rate confirmed in CHX-treated neurons. Non-responders (SMPD4, WDR45) reflect transcript-context dependence — downstream exon inclusion restores frame.
Disease ontology enrichment (UniProt KW): PE-containing genes enriched in epilepsy (137 genes), intellectual disability (313), neurodegeneration (181). KEGG: metabolic pathways (572 genes, FDR=0.00069), spliceosome (61), mRNA surveillance (43) — consistent with splicing factors autoregulating via PE inclusion (SRS/SF3 factors).
GTEx: 17,382 libraries, 54 tissues, 13 brain regions, 838 donors. BrainSpan: 607 libraries, 41 individuals, 16 brain regions, 10 pcw to 40 yr. PE inclusion correlated inversely with host-gene expression. Splicing factors RBFOX1, NOVA1, UPF3B enriched for brain-specific PEs (p=4e-6, hypergeometric). NOVA1 PE inclusion high in iPSCs, declines during neuronal maturation (validated by RT-PCR + CHX).
Variant pipeline: SpliceAI score cutoff |0.2| (positive = increased PE inclusion); 45,691 intronic vs 22,991 exonic candidate variants. Pangolin cross-validation showed strong concordance; SpliceAI higher sensitivity. ODiseA ontology mapping: 596 variants linked to seizures, 262 to epileptic syndromes.
Prime editing: 13 pegRNAs across 12 genes; 10/13 >5% efficiency, 9 >10% (avg 22.9%). Five representative validated variants — TAFAZZIN/TAZ c.284+110G>A (SpliceAI 0.57, +0.12 PSI), NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T (0.23, +0.13 PSI), SLC12A6 c.2633-1G>A (0.99, +0.04 PSI), TSC2 c.4850-1G>A (0.99, +0.06 PSI), COQ9 c.521+1del (0.67, -0.28 PSI).
NDUFAF6 deep dive: c.420+784C>T retains 124 intronic nt, creating a PTC in exon 4. Clonal i3N neurons: WT PSI 0.13, heterozygous (C/T) 0.37, homozygous (T/T) 0.94. Homozygotes showed reduced NDUFAF6 protein (Western), impaired mitochondrial membrane potential (TMRM), and smaller/fragmented cell clusters; heterozygotes intermediate. Authors state this is the first functional characterization of NDUFAF6 deficiency from PE mis-splicing in a human neuronal model.

Connections

Direct sibling to disease/mechanism sources: scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (NMD-inducing cryptic exons repressed by TDP-43; this paper extends to a genome-wide PE catalog) and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (AS as a regulator of gene expression).
Developmental-splicing angle complements kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated AS) and the brain-proteome work wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of.
Brain transcriptomic siblings: humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia isoforms), zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (splicing-qtl in brain).
Methodologically anchored by spliceai (variant-effect prediction; Pangolin used as cross-check) and introduces the new tool evonmd.
Therapeutic framing (NMD-sensitive PEs as targetable defects via RNA-based correction, e.g. ASOs) ties to splicing-therapeutics; the SCN1A “20N” PE / Dravet syndrome and SMN2 precedent sit in this lineage (smn2).

Open questions

How many of the 9,814 putative PEs are bona fide regulatory switches vs annotation artifacts of low-abundance isoforms (the authors caution annotation depth inflates human counts)?
Short-read PSI measures only overall exon inclusion, not transcript-context PE usage — long-read RNA-seq could resolve which isoforms actually carry the PTC.
Why does the heterozygous NDUFAF6 line show intermediate PE inclusion and MMP deficit but no significant protein reduction? What sets the dosage threshold for phenotype?
Can PE-targeting ASOs that block aberrant inclusion correct deep-intronic variants like NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T in patient-derived neurons?

wiki/sources/pollex-2024-enhancer-promot.title: pollex-2024-enhancer-promoter-interactions-become-more
wiki/sources/pollex-2024-enhancer-promot.content:
Enhancer–promoter interactions become more instructive in the transition from cell-fate specification to tissue differentiation
One-line: Capture-C on ~600 activity-characterized Drosophila enhancers/promoters shows E–P regulation shifts from permissive (proximity uncoupled from activity) during cell-fate specification to instructive (proximity coupled to activity) during terminal tissue differentiation.
Key takeaways

Directly tests the relationship between enhancer–promoter proximity and activity by starting from cis-regulatory-elements with characterized in vivo activity, then measuring their proximity as they transition OFF-to-ON or ON-to-OFF — an orthogonal design that avoids biasing toward already-active or already-proximal loops.
During specification (6–8 h), myogenic and neuronal cells have remarkably similar E–P topologies (1,157 shared invariant interactions vs 245 differential), i.e. chromatin-loops are largely permissive and uncoupled from lineage-specific activity.
During terminal differentiation (10–12 h), many new, more distal interactions emerge whose proximity changes track activity changes — an instructive mode confirmed by concordant chromatin-accessibility (DHS) and H3K27ac signal at the interacting “other end.”
Most E–P interactions stay within a TAD, though some cross ten-plus boundaries as megabase-scale loops.
Insulator binding (CTCF, BEAF-32, Su(Hw)) does NOT explain differential E–P interactions — 74–79% of significant interacting regions bind none of the three, and interaction changes are uncorrelated with insulator-binding changes.

Notable claims / data

583 baits passed QC (303 enhancers, 276 promoters, 4 both); Capture-C with a 4 bp cutter, 2 replicates × 5 developmental contexts = 20 datasets; nuclei FANS-purified to >95% (Mef2+ myogenic, Elav+ neuronal).
24,012 high-confidence interactions (18,252 unique), defined by CHiCAGO score ≥5 + DHS overlap + 2 kb–10 Mb distance filter.
Interaction complexity rises with development: median 1 interaction/bait at 6–8 h vs 7 at 10–12 h; total high-confidence interactions ~1,000→3,000 (2–3 h→6–8 h) and ~3,000→9,000 (6–8 h→10–12 h).
Stringent differential set: 4,348 differential interactions (2,858 unique; 5% FDR, >0.7 log2FC) and 6,853 constant (3,164 unique). 68% (myo) and 74% (neuro) of differential interactions occur in the 6–8 h→10–12 h transition; gains dominate (78% muscle, 91% neurons).
Dendrogram/PCA: myoblasts and neuronal cells are closer to each other at 6–8 h than to their own 10–12 h differentiated counterparts.
DHS and H3K27ac changes at the “other end” are concordant with interaction-frequency changes, but a DHS/accessibility gain alone does not force a new loop (interactions often skip a DHS) — binding of specific factors, not mere accessibility, regulates loops.
Motif analysis implicates Trl/GAF and Clamp broadly, and lineage TFs (Mef2 in muscle, l(3)neo38 in neurons) in instructive loop formation; insulator motifs not enriched.

Connections

Extends the permissive-vs-instructive loop debate for cis-regulatory-elements and chromatin-loops using chromatin-conformation-capture (Capture-C), complementing wiki pages on 3D genome architecture and topologically-associating-domains.
Pairs with chromatin-accessibility as a regulatory readout; relevant to studies linking chromatin state to gene regulation across development.

Open questions

Which specific factors (beyond Trl/GAF, Clamp, lineage TFs) actively drive instructive loop formation during differentiation, and how?
Why is the permissive mode favored during specification and the instructive mode during terminal differentiation — does permissive poising enable cell-fate plasticity while instructive coupling ensures tissue diversification?
Do these Drosophila-derived modes generalize to vertebrate developmental systems and to inducible/stimulus-driven gene expression?

wiki/sources/quinones-valdez-2025-long-r.title: quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis
wiki/sources/quinones-valdez-2025-long-r.content:
Long-read RNA-seq demarcates cis- and trans-directed alternative RNA splicing
One-line: Introduces isolaser, a single-sample long-read RNA-seq method that calls variants, phases haplotypes, and tests allelic linkage of splicing to cleanly separate cis-directed (genetically driven) from trans-directed alternative splicing events — applied to ENCODE human/mouse data, HLA genes, and Alzheimer’s disease brains.
Key takeaways

Splicing is jointly controlled by cis-regulatory elements and trans-acting factors (mainly rna-binding-proteins); separating events that are primarily cis- vs. trans-directed in a given sample clarifies the genetic basis of disease. Long reads phase a heterozygous SNP into two haplotypes (H1/H2), and cis-directed exons show exon inclusion almost exclusively in one haplotype while trans-directed exons show similar inclusion across both.
isolaser (isoform-Level analysis of Allele-Specific processing of Exonic Regions) is a one-stop tool: (1) de novo variant calling via de Bruijn graph local reassembly + multi-layer-perceptron false-positive filter, (2) gene-level phasing via weighted k-means read clustering (haplotagging), (3) linkage test between haplotypes and exonic parts using adjusted mutual information (AMI). Crucially it needs NO pre-phased whole genome.
Works on a single sample (a major advantage for allele-specific splicing); an isoLASER-joint mode merges multiple samples to prioritize candidate functional variants (Splicing-Associated Variants, SAVs) and recover cis-events untestable in one sample (e.g. driven by homozygous variants), effectively achieving an sQTL-type analysis with very small cohorts.
Operates on exonic parts (non-overlapping exonic segments from collapsing isoforms, ~100–200 nt) rather than whole isoforms — granular, event-level resolution that is more sensitive than isoform-level allelic methods like LORALS when a gene has many combinatorial AS events.
Striking biological finding: clustering samples by AMI of splicing linkage segregates them by donor identity, not tissue of origin — even though PSI splicing profiles cluster by tissue. Genetic background shapes an individual’s overall splicing linkage; cis-directed events are individual-specific and maintained across that donor’s tissues.
Resolves allele-specific splicing in the highly polymorphic HLA system (hard with short reads), enables HLA typing from RNA, and surfaces cis-directed events in Alzheimer’s-relevant genes (MAPT, BIN1, MS4A6A, HLA-DQA1).

Notable claims / data

Variant calling: MLP classifier training AUC 0.92–0.99 (ROC) / 0.86–0.99 (PR). On HG002 long-read RNA-seq, isoLASER matched or beat GATK HaplotypeCaller, DeepVariant, and Clair3 on F1 but with superior precision.
Phasing: >99% of heterozygous variants phased by HapCUT2 or the HG002 diploid (T2T) assembly were consistently phased by isoLASER; switch-error rates 0.15% (vs HapCUT2) and 0.1% (vs HPRC). No variants phased by isoLASER only.
Cis-event definition: AMI above 99th percentile of a simulated unlinked background (coverage-dependent cutoff fit by 3rd-degree polynomial) AND |delta PSI| > 5% between haplotypes; trans = below 95th percentile; in between = ambiguous.
Scale: 2–946 cis-directed events per sample. Human tissues 2047 unique cis exonic parts / 1203 genes; mouse tissues 4679 / 2341; human cell lines 3312 / 1703; mouse cell lines 1341 / 778. ~13% of cis exonic parts are novel (not in GENCODE). F1 hybrid mice show more cis-events than F0 inbred (more heterozygosity).
isoLASER identified substantially more allele-specific-splicing genes than LORALS in both ENCODE and HG002 data; example WASHC2C has one isoLASER cis exonic part but none of its 4 transcripts showed LORALS-detectable allelic bias.
Considering all human tissues jointly, 37% of testable events were cis-directed in ≥1 tissue, 46% trans, 17% ambiguous (single-sample: 15% cis / 68% trans). 4–29% of single-sample trans events became cis at the joint level.
SAVs of cis-events had significantly lower GTEx splicing-qtl p-values than matched controls; their delta PSI correlated with the GTEx sQTL genotype beta (R = -0.37, p = 4.5e-08; inverse because GTEx/LeafCutter uses intron usage). 44 SAVs were known to alter RBP binding (allele-specific-binding study); motif enrichment near SAVs implicated HNRNP D/L/K, KHSRP, SFPQ; DeepRiPe predicted SAVs significantly alter RBP-RNA binding vs controls (KS p = 4.79e-12).
HLA: isoLASER HLA typing concordant with HG002 diploid MHC assembly and arcasHLA on 70 short-read samples. HLA-C alleles show allele-specific patterns (C04/C06 exon 5 skipping; C03 alt 3’ splice site in exon 6 ~25% usage; C07 none). AlphaFold shows cis-events reshape the protein (MHC-I C-terminus elongated by 6 aa; exon-5 exclusion deletes a transmembrane helix).
Alzheimer’s: in ENCODE PacBio DLPFC (5 AD / 4 control) plus an Oxford Nanopore frontal-cortex set (6 AD / 6 control), 7 HLA genes showed AD allele-specific splicing. SAVs in MS4A6A, BIN1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DQA1 passed the AD GWAS significance threshold (5e-8). UBC had the highest delta-delta PSI (ddPSI): an intron showed 100% retention with the T allele in AD but the linkage was reversed in controls (C allele 100% retention); the event removes one ubiquitin domain. ~15% (1201) of SAVs were rare/ultra-rare (gnomAD MAF < 1e-2 / 1e-3), a regime sQTL studies cannot address.

Connections

A long-read, single-sample counterpart to short-read allele-specific-splicing tools like pairadise (see demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing) — both detect rare-variant effects that population splicing-qtl mapping misses, but isoLASER needs no near-exon SNP on the same short read.
Benchmarked against LORALS (isoform-level allelic linkage) and against GTEx sQTLs computed with LeafCutter (see li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna); positioned as a viable small-cohort alternative to sQTL mapping (cf. zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls).
Quantifies events with percent-spliced-in (delta PSI between haplotypes) and centrally analyzes intron-retention (UBC event).
RBP-binding interpretation connects to the allele-specific-binding / rna-binding-proteins literature (DeepRiPe, RBNS); related to large RBP binding maps (see van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional).
Tool entity: isolaser. Builds on the same lab’s scAllele variant-calling framework.

Open questions

Do non-consecutive cis-directed events within one gene share a common causal variant (genetic co-regulation of splicing)?
Older Nanopore chemistries force SNP-only analysis with external validation — how much sensitivity is lost, and does newer chemistry close the gap with PacBio HiFi?
Can isoLASER-joint with much larger cohorts yield shorter, more robust SAV lists (fine-mapping toward causal variants)? Not evaluated here.
Functional validation: do the predicted HLA/UBC protein-structure changes have measurable cellular or disease consequences?

wiki/sources/rabe-hesketh-2008-biometric.title: rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and
wiki/sources/rabe-hesketh-2008-biometric.content:
Biometrical Modeling of Twin and Family Data Using Standard Mixed Model Software
One-line: Shows that biometrical variance-components-model decomposition of twin and family data into additive-genetic (A), common-environment (C), dominance (D), and unique-environment (E) components can be fitted with standard mixed-model software (Stata’s gllamm, R/S-PLUS) rather than specialized SEM software, via parameterizations needing only a few random effects so the approach extends to categorical phenotypes.
Key takeaways

Twin/family designs are usually fit as structural-equation-models in specialized software like mx-software; this paper recasts the same biometrical models as linear-mixed-models (continuous phenotypes) and generalized-linear-mixed-models (categorical phenotypes), making them accessible in Stata, R/S-PLUS, SAS, SPSS.
The core challenge is imposing the covariance restrictions predicted by quantitative-genetics (e.g., MZ twins correlate 1 on additive genetic effects, DZ twins 1/2) within software that does not allow random-effect covariance matrices to depend on covariates or nonlinear constraints on parameters.
Two parameterizations are proposed. Parameterization 1 reproduces the genetic covariance structure directly using dummy-variable tricks (3 random effects for ACE/ADE), giving a one-to-one correspondence between random-effect variances and the A/C/D/E variance components.
Parameterization 2 is a plain three-level random-intercept model needing only 2 random effects, achieved by letting a level-2 random-effect variance be shared by MZ twins (via an artificial cluster identifier) but unique to DZ individuals; variance components of interest are then linear combinations of model variances.
Fewer random effects matter most for categorical/dichotomous phenotypes, where the GLMM likelihood has no closed form and random effects must be integrated out numerically — dimensionality of the integral is often far smaller than in the SEM approach for large families.
Applied to continuous birth weight (ACE model on Norwegian nuclear families) and dichotomous depression (ACE/ADE models on Virginia Twin Registry twin-pairs).

Notable claims / data

ACDE model is not identified from twins reared together alone; in practice the special cases ACE and ADE (and nested AE, CE) are fitted.
Birth weight (1000 nuclear families, Medical Birth Registry of Norway): fit with Stata’s xtmixed / R-S-PLUS lme. Additive-genetic SD σ_A = 315.06 g (SE 16.12), unique-environment SD σ_E = 365.40 g (SE 12.41); heritability ĥ² = σ²_A/(σ²_A+σ²_E) = 0.43; 95% CI 0.35–0.50 via probit transformation and delta method.
Depression (DSM-III-R, Virginia Twin Registry, Caucasian female twin-pairs): estimated by maximum likelihood with adaptive quadrature in gllamm. ACE common-environment variance from parameterization 2 was negative (−0.12) and constrained to exactly zero (AE model) in parameterization 1; AE heritability ĥ² = σ²_A/(σ²_A+1) = 0.43, 95% CI 0.31–0.53. ADE gives σ²_A = 0.54, σ²_D = 0.25.
A practical feature: parameterizations with direct correspondence between variance components and parameters let software that forbids negative variances enforce the genetically required nonnegativity; parameterization 2 does NOT impose nonnegativity, so a negative σ²_C estimate signals the ACE model is inappropriate and suggests switching to ADE without re-estimation.
Also gives parameterizations for siblings-plus-cousins (sharing grandparents) and nuclear families (mother, father, two children), with the relevant A and C covariance matrices.
Categorical phenotypes handled via a probit threshold/liability model; estimation needs numerical or Monte Carlo integration. Notes adaptive quadrature (gllamm, SAS PROC NLMIXED) is superior to ordinary quadrature, and warns penalized quasi-likelihood (PQL) performs poorly for family data with large/unequal cluster sizes.
Caveat: likelihood-ratio tests for variance components do not follow the conventional χ² null; correct null is a mixture of χ² distributions (cites Dominicus et al. 2005).

Connections

Explicitly builds on mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models: McArdle & Prescott (2005) propose a similar parameterization to “parameterization 1,” but theirs requires four random effects for twin models whereas this paper’s needs three (parameterization 1) or only two (parameterization 2) — a deliberate reduction in random-effect dimensionality for computational efficiency.
Positions itself against pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and: Pawitan et al. (2004) wrote a custom program for a nonstandard GLMM allowing random-effect correlations to depend on covariates; this paper instead achieves the same restrictions within standard GLMM software. It notes Pawitan et al. nicely demonstrate dominance and maternal effects, which these standard-software parameterizations can also incorporate.
Contrasts with two other prior attempts: Guo & Wang (2002) ignore the restrictions to use standard LMM software; van den Oord (2001) uses standard LMM software but needs complicated constraints, parameters that do not map to quantities of interest, and risks theoretically impossible (improper) models.
Strongly tied to twin-and-family-study-design and heritability estimation; both example analyses report ĥ² for the additive-genetic component.

Open questions

The mixture-χ² null for variance-component LRTs is flagged but not applied to the worked examples — how much would correctly testing C=0 (ACE vs AE) in the depression data change conclusions?
Extensions noted but not developed: incorporating measured genotypes at specific loci, genotype-environment interaction, epistasis (genotype × genetic), and higher-level environmental random effects (e.g., neighborhoods) — all within standard mixed-model software.
Mx (mx-software) still uniquely supports sibling-interaction, homogamy, and complex parent–child resemblance models the authors say cannot be fit with standard GLMMs; what is the boundary of what the mixed-model reframing can reach?
For large families with categorical phenotypes, how far does the reduced integral dimensionality of parameterization 2 actually extend feasible model size versus SEM/Mx?

wiki/sources/rajderkar-2023-cell-type-an.title: rajderkar-2023-cell-type-and-tissue-specific
wiki/sources/rajderkar-2023-cell-type-an.content:
Cell Type- and Tissue-specific Enhancers in Craniofacial Development
One-line: A tissue- and single-cell-resolution catalogue of ~14,000 human craniofacial enhancers, cross-validated in mouse, to interpret noncoding variants behind facial variation and birth defects.
Key takeaways

Builds a comprehensive map of cis-regulatory-elements active during human face development (Carnegie stages 13-23, gestational weeks 4-8), combining H3K27ac ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, and RNA-seq from primary human embryonic face tissue.
Pairs bulk human enhancer calls with single-cell mouse data (scanfacex transcriptome atlas + ScanFaceN enhancer catalog) to assign cell-type-specific in vivo activity to enhancers via chromatin-accessibility.
Intersects the enhancer set with facial-shape and craniofacial-disease genome-wide-association-study signals, nominating noncoding-variants in enhancers as candidate contributors to normal variation and orofacial clefts.

Notable claims / data

13,983 reproducible human candidate enhancers (H3K27ac in >=2 samples); 6,718 of 10,893 CS18-23 regions (61.7%) also show accessible chromatin.
Transgenic mouse reporter assays: 60 candidates tested, 16 previously-unknown craniofacial enhancers validated in vivo; 153 predicted enhancers overlap prior VISTA craniofacial elements.
87% (12,179/13,983) of human enhancers are sequence-conserved to mouse; 59% of those (8,257) show mouse enhancer signatures (functionally conserved), while 28% are human-specific by epigenomic signature. Overall 56% of human enhancers are functionally conserved.
scRNA-seq: 57,598 mouse face cells (e11.5-e13.5), 16 annotated cell types (ScanFaceX). snATAC-seq: 41,483 cells, 20 clusters, 16,564 DARs (10,038 retained after label transfer).
GWAS: 1,404 lead SNPs -> 27,386 LD SNPs; 209 enhancers overlap 605 LD SNPs (43 enhancers/102 disease SNPs; 176 enhancers/515 normal-variation SNPs).
hs2656 (near POP1/NIPAL2/KCNS2, 8q22.2 cleft region) drives nasal/maxillary activity for the human but not the mouse ortholog (mm2280), confirming a human-specific enhancer.

Connections

Extends the general theme of cis-regulatory-elements and enhancer catalogues; complements MPRA-style and single-cell ATAC approaches to regulatory annotation elsewhere in the wiki.
Uses chromatin-accessibility (snATAC-seq) plus label transfer to give cis-regulatory-elements cell-type resolution, a strategy shared with other single-cell regulatory-genomics sources.
The genome-wide-association-study intersection to prioritize noncoding-variants parallels disease-critical cell-type and TWAS work in the collection.

Open questions

Which of the 209 GWAS-overlapping enhancers are causal for specific clefts or facial traits, and via which target genes?
Do the 28% human-specific (non-mouse-conserved) enhancers reflect true human regulatory innovation, or limits of the mouse assay?
Can enhancer-reporter + scRNA-seq scale beyond three enhancers to systematically resolve in vivo cell-type specificity genome-wide?

wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.title: rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing
wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.content:
Regulation of pre-mRNA splicing: roles in physiology and disease, and therapeutic prospects
One-line: A review of recent advances in the molecular mechanisms that distinguish introns from exons and regulate alternative-splicing, connecting these to human disease and to the first generation of splicing-therapeutics.
Key takeaways

Intron removal is carried out by the spliceosome, comprising 5 snRNP complexes (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) plus more than 150 additional proteins, through a two-step transesterification reaction; cryo-EM has now captured snapshots of splicing at many assembly and catalytic steps. A principle of accuracy is that intron boundaries are recognized multiple times.
splice-sites in higher eukaryotes are remarkably diverse and often resemble non-splice-site sequences, so recognition depends on base pairing of splicing-factors/snRNPs plus context; U1 snRNA base-pairing defines 5’ss “strength”, and BP/PPT/3’ss recognition by SF1, U2AF1/U2AF2 and U2 snRNP (SF3B1/PHF5A) defines the 3’ end.
splicing-regulatory-elements (ESE/ESS, ISE/ISS) recruited by rna-binding-proteins (SR proteins, hnRNPs, RBM, CELF) tune splice-site recognition and exon definition; the same motif can act positively or negatively depending on position (the splicing-code).
Mutations in pre-mRNAs and core splicing-factors drive splicing-and-disease: genetic disorders, neurodegeneration and cancer (SF3B1, U2AF1/2, U1 snRNA mutations).
splicing-therapeutics are now clinical reality: antisense oligonucleotides (nusinersen, eteplirsen), the small molecule risdiplam, engineered U1 snRNAs, and SF3B1/RBM39-targeting drugs in oncology.
cotranscriptional-splicing is widespread but not universal; Pol II elongation rate, splicing-and-chromatin (histone marks, nucleosome positioning, CTD), nuclear compartmentalization, RNA modifications and RNA structure all shape outcomes — alongside post-transcriptional-splicing (e.g. detained introns).

Notable claims / data

Massively parallel assay of all 32,768 possible NNN/GYNNNN 5’ss sequences confirmed U1-base-pairing relevance but revealed strong context effects; ~90% of BRCA2 5’ss mutations in breast cancer affect splicing. G at +1 is required for catalysis; pyrimidine at +2 present in 99.6% of 5’ss.
Spinal muscular atrophy: caused by loss of SMN1; nusinersen (approved 2016) blocks an ISS to boost SMN2 exon 7 inclusion via quarterly intrathecal injection. Risdiplam (FDA approval 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone, “repairs” the U1–SMN2 exon 7 bulge by aiding U1C zinc-finger contact.
Familial dysautonomia: IKBKAP intron 20 +6 mutation causes exon skipping; therapeutic strategies include ASOs, modified U1 snRNAs, kinetin, and RECTAS (enhances SRSF6 phosphorylation).
SF3B1 mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts, 15–36% of eye melanomas, and are among the most common single mutations in CLL; K700E correlates with worse prognosis in CLL but better in MDS. SF3B1 mutations activate cryptic 3’ss 10–30 nt upstream and converge on repressing tumour suppressor BRD9 via a “poison exon”.
U2AF1 S34F/Y vs Q157P/R mutants change which flanking-nucleotide 3’ss are selected. SF3B1 inhibitors (H3B-8800, in trials; pladienolide B; spliceostatin A; sudemycins) block the open→closed BP-recognition conformational change; indisulam degrades RBM39 (synthetic lethality).
Splicing modulators generate tumour neoantigens, combinable with anti-PD1 checkpoint blockade. Eteplirsen targets ESEs to skip a mutant dystrophin exon in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, restoring an in-frame shorter functional protein. Pladienolide B reprograms mouse pluripotent cells to totipotent blastomere-like cells.
Two-thirds of all possible mutations in an alternative exon affect inclusion (vs constitutive exons). Internal exons are constrained to ~50–250 nt by exon definition; microexons (~3–27 nt) use SRRM4-coordinated recognition relevant to autism.
m6A regulates splicing: at 3’ss AG it inhibits U2AF1 binding (SAM-synthetase feedback in C. elegans and via METTL16 in humans); readers YTHDC1 (recruits SRSF3, antagonizes SRSF10) and hnRNPG/RBMX modulate exon inclusion.

Connections

alternative-splicing diversifies the proteome and regulates expression via PTC introduction and NMD (~30% of AS events introduce PTCs); links to splicing-and-evolution (joint evolution of splicing and protein codes; circular RNAs from back splicing).
splicing-code prediction: decades of tools from SpliceSiteFinder (1987) through SpliceAI, MMSplice, Pangolin; saturation mutagenesis and AI methods are the path forward.
splicing-and-chromatin: MRG15 links H3K36me3 to PTB recruitment; genome-edited histone marks induce splicing changes during EMT.

Open questions

How widespread is the functional relevance of alternative-splicing versus transcriptome noise, and how much variability exists cell-to-cell?
Do introns have autonomous functions beyond being removed by-products?
How general is cotranscriptional-splicing, and do cell-type- or substrate-specific spliceosomes exist?
How can splice sites be reliably predicted from genomic sequence, and how do lncRNAs contribute to splicing regulation?

wiki/sources/rolland-2023-phenotypic-eff.title: rolland-2023-phenotypic-effects-of-genetic-variants
wiki/sources/rolland-2023-phenotypic-eff.content:
Phenotypic effects of genetic variants associated with autism
One-line: Across 13,000+ autistic and 210,000+ undiagnosed individuals, rare loss-of-function variants in autism genes carry small but significant effects on cognition and socioeconomic status even in people without an autism diagnosis.
Key takeaways

Reframes autism genetics away from a categorical diagnosis: the same rare loss-of-function (LoF) variants that raise autism odds also shift cognitive and socioeconomic traits in undiagnosed carriers, arguing for studying variant effects beyond the clinical label.
Uses whole-exome sequencing across four cohorts (SSC, SPARK, iPSYCH, UK Biobank; 226,649 individuals of European ancestry) to estimate per-gene autism odds ratios for stringent LoFs (S-LoFs) in 185 autism-associated genes plus 2,492 LoF-constrained genes.
Links to psychiatric-genetics and neurodevelopment: autism OR is highest for synaptic and neuronal-module genes and correlates positively with early/mid-fetal cortical expression.
Rare-variant (S-LoF) and common-variant (autism polygenic score) effects on autism-related traits are largely independent, with no detected interaction.

Notable claims / data

S-LoFs in autism genes seen in 4% of autistic individuals (n=523), 1.13% of siblings/parents, and 0.58% of UK Biobank (n=1,090).
14 autism genes (e.g. SCN2A, CHD8, GRIN2B, SYNGAP1) had all variants de novo with zero S-LoF carriers among 213,558 undiagnosed individuals (autism OR = Inf.); 134 genes had ≥1 undiagnosed carrier, implying lower penetrance.
Female autistic carriers enriched for S-LoFs vs males (OR 1.72, P=1.4×10⁻⁴), consistent with the female protective effect; no such skew in undiagnosed relatives/population.
In undiagnosed UK Biobank carriers, top phenome-wide hits were unemployment, income, qualifications and Townsend deprivation index (corrected P<1×10⁻⁵). Fluid intelligence β = −0.19 (OR≤10 genes) and −0.37 (OR>10 genes); qualification OR 0.82 / 0.49; income OR 0.62 / 0.51.
Autism polygenic score moved in the opposite direction for cognition (positively associated with fluid intelligence and qualification) but shared the deprivation-index direction with S-LoFs.
Brain MRI in 21,040 UK Biobank participants showed no significant overall anatomical difference between S-LoF carriers and non-carriers.

Connections

Extends autism gene-discovery / dysregulation work such as gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across and the developmental-genetics framing in zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain.
Complements genome-wide-association-study-based polygenic scoring and the rare/common variant partitioning central to psychiatric-genetics and quantitative-genetics.
Neuronal/synaptic coexpression-module enrichment connects to gene-coexpression analyses of autism cortex (parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna).

Open questions

Are the socioeconomic associations (income, deprivation) causal consequences of the variants, ascertainment/collider artifacts, or mediated by cognition?
Why do rare LoF and polygenic autism risk push cognition in opposite directions, and what does that imply for a unified liability model?
How do these European-ancestry effect estimates generalize across ancestries and to non-LoF variant classes?

wiki/sources/roohani-2024-predicting-tra.title: roohani-2024-predicting-transcriptional-outcomes-of-novel
wiki/sources/roohani-2024-predicting-tra.content:
Predicting transcriptional outcomes of novel multigene perturbations with GEARS
One-line: A knowledge-graph-augmented deep learning model that predicts single-cell transcriptional responses to single- and multi-gene perturbations, including combinations of genes never experimentally perturbed.
Key takeaways

gears integrates a graph neural network with two prior-knowledge graphs — a gene coexpression graph and a Gene Ontology similarity graph — to do single-cell-perturbation-modeling from Perturb-seq data.
The knowledge-graphs prior is what lets GEARS generalize to genes with no experimental perturbation data, addressing the combinatorial explosion of multigene combinations.
Each gene gets two learnable embeddings (gene + perturbation), composed with a ‘sum’ operator so the model extends to perturbation sets of any size.
GEARS predicts five non-additive genetic interaction subtypes (synergy, suppression, neomorphism, redundancy, epistasis), moving beyond linear additive models.
A Bayesian formulation outputs an uncertainty score inversely correlated with accuracy, flagging poorly graph-connected genes.

Notable claims / data

Evaluated across seven perturbation datasets; single-gene screens from Replogle et al. with 1,543 (RPE-1) and 1,092 (K562) perturbations, each >170,000 cells, via the perturb-seq assay.
30–50% m.s.e. improvement over baselines (CPA, gene-regulatory-network propagation adapted from CellOracle, no-perturbation) on top-20 differentially expressed genes; >2x better Pearson correlation across all genes.
Two-gene evaluation (Norman et al., 131 combinations): >30% m.s.e. improvement across all generalization classes, up to 53% when both genes were unseen.


40% precision@10 improvement for four of five interaction subtypes (>90% for redundancy and epistasis); ~2x accuracy on the ten strongest interactions.


Predicted all 5,151 pairwise combinations of 102 genes, recovering known phenotypic clusters and proposing a novel high-erythroid-marker phenotype; GI predictions validated against a combinatorial cell-fitness screen (P < 0.0013 synergy, P < 4e-5 buffering).
Caveat: requires training on the same cell type/condition and on combinatorial data for accurate multigene prediction; sensitive to cell-cycle and gene-editing-success confounders.

Connections

Same problem space as Txpert (wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge), which extends knowledge-graph-guided perturbation prediction, and the optimal-transport approach of driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable.
Builds directly on Perturb-seq screens introduced in dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits and adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening.
The coexpression prior connects to gene coexpression network (gene-coexpression-network) concepts; training data comes from pooled-crispr-screen experiments.

Open questions

Can context-specific networks replace generic GO/coexpression graphs to improve prediction for poorly connected genes?
How reliably does the uncertainty score gate real experimental design decisions?
Does the novel erythroid phenotype prediction hold up under experimental validation?

wiki/sources/rossi-2026-coexpression-bas.title: rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl
wiki/sources/rossi-2026-coexpression-bas.content:
Co-expression-based models improve eQTL predictions for transcriptome-wide association studies and highlight new schizophrenia-associated genes
One-line: Two co-expression-network models, INGENE & MODULE, add distal (trans) regulatory signal to conventional cis-eQTL predictors, expanding the pool of genetically imputable genes across six human brain regions and uncovering 641 novel schizophrenia-associated genes in a PGC3 coexpression-informed TWAS.
Key takeaways

Most psychiatric risk variants are noncoding and act by regulating expression, but standard transcriptome-wide association studies (TWAS) model only local cis-eQTLs (±1 Mb), leaving most regulatory heritability unexplained; this work adds trans/co-expression effects.
Introduces two trans-informed elastic-net models (INGENE & MODULE): INGENE models trans effects of cis-regulated coexpression partners on a target gene; MODULE finds SNPs (co-eQTLs) associated with a gene’s coexpression-module eigengene (first PC).
Trained on LIBD postmortem RNA-seq across six brain regions (caudate, dACC, sACC, amygdala, DLPFC, hippocampus), with cross-dataset replication in GTEx and CommonMind (CMC); benchmarked against cis models CIS (elastic-net PrediXcan-style) and EpiXcan.
Combining cis and trans predictors significantly improved gene-expression imputation (maximum likelihood estimation, α = 0.05) for 18,744 genes when pooling regions; trans models nearly doubled the count of predictable genes versus cis-only in GTEx.
Applied to 62 Psychiatric Genomics Consortium wave 3 (GWAS) cohorts (102,613 individuals), the coexpression-informed TWAS (coTWAS) found 766 schizophrenia-associated genes (FDR < 0.01), 641 (83.7%) novel; novel hits were enriched among trans-only predictions, implicating distal regulation in discovery.
Conceptually parallels isoform-resolution TWAS extensions such as isotwas — both argue that conventional gene-level cis TWAS misses regulatory signal — but here the added dimension is trans/co-expression rather than isoform.

Notable claims / data

Pooled training counts: CIS predicted 26,285 genes, EpiXcan 25,702, INGENE 18,403, MODULE 16,721; INGENE/MODULE shared 16,103 predictions (96% of MODULE, 88% of INGENE).
Cross-validated adjusted R² (15,232 common genes): MODULE significantly outperformed all others — vs INGENE ΔR² = 0.0464 (P = 5.04×10⁻¹⁶), vs CIS ΔR² = 0.0721, vs EpiXcan ΔR² = 0.0705.
In independent GTEx testing, cis models explained more per-gene variance (CIS 7.1–7.8%, EpiXcan 7.2–9.4% adjusted R²) than trans models (INGENE 2–3.3%, MODULE 2.7–4.9%), but INGENE predicted up to 1.8× more genes than CIS and 1.7× more than EpiXcan.
MODULE trans-SNP weights correlated more strongly with SCZ variant effect sizes (r = 0.28–0.42) than CIS/EpiXcan (r = 0.14–0.18); ~40% of MODULE trans-eQTLs also act as cis-eQTLs in GTEx tissues, and up to 29% of MODULE genes have a cis-SNP regulating an INGENE coexpression partner.
coTWAS: 96,535 conditional tests across 315 genomic regions → 1,162 significant associations, 766 independent genes (693 non-MHC); 381 upregulated / 414 downregulated; 556 replicable by leave-site-out (mean concordance 69.4% ± 5.1%).
Of 766 genes, 125 previously reported and 641 novel; known genes recovered by cis/cis–trans models, novel ones enriched in trans-only (51%).
