Recovered loci include MAPK3 and INO80E (16p11.2), GATAD2A, GNL3, SNX19, C4A; GO enrichment showed prefrontal synaptic (AMPA receptor clustering) and distributed immune/MHC processes.
Caveat: all training/validation cohorts are European ancestry; trans models showed lower cross-cohort effect-size correlation (e.g. MODULE r = 0.14–0.17 in CMC vs cis r = 0.43–0.68) despite higher gene-set overlap.

Connections

Schizophrenia transcriptomic context: relates to gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across (the Gandal gene set was the strongest enrichment, P = 2.98×10⁻²¹) and cortical expression architecture in dear-2024-cortical-gene-expression-architecture-links.
TWAS methodology family: complements isoform-level TWAS — bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic / isotwas and the isoform atlas of wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform — by adding an orthogonal trans/co-expression axis rather than isoform resolution.
Speaks to the missing-heritability problem and the limits of cis-only models in explaining heritability of complex psychiatric traits.
New entity INGENE & MODULE is the reusable modeling contribution.

Open questions

Does trans/co-expression imputation generalize beyond European-ancestry cohorts, given the lower cross-cohort effect-size correlation of the trans models?
Are the 641 novel trans-driven schizophrenia genes causal regulators or downstream coexpression passengers — can they be validated functionally (e.g. in vitro perturbation)?
How much of the MODULE “trans” signal is genuinely distal versus cis effects propagated through coexpression networks, given ~40% overlap with known cis-eQTLs?

wiki/sources/ruzzo-2019-inherited-and-de.title: ruzzo-2019-inherited-and-de-novo-genetic
wiki/sources/ruzzo-2019-inherited-and-de.content:
Inherited and De Novo Genetic Risk for Autism Impacts Shared Networks
One-line: Whole-genome sequencing of 493 multiplex ASD families (the ihart-cohort) surfaces rare inherited risk, 16 new risk genes, and a shared PPI network converging with de novo genes.
Key takeaways

Rare inherited variants are a real but low-penetrance source of autism risk that is enriched in multiplex families and had been largely missed by prior simplex-family studies — a direct attack on ASD’s missing-heritability.
Inherited and de novo ASD genes hit distinct pathways (inherited = ion transport, cell cycle, microtubule cytoskeleton; de novo = transcriptional/chromatin regulation) yet converge on a common protein-protein interaction network, arguing the two variant classes impact shared biology.
The 69 TADA risk genes are enriched in a cortical-neurogenesis co-expression module (Parikshak module M2), FMRP and RBFOX1 targets, and mid-gestation glutamatergic projection neurons, tying inherited risk to neurogenesis and psychiatric-genetics.
Non-coding regulatory deletions in the promoters of NR3C2 and DLG2 fall in developing-brain open-chromatin regions, illustrating that WGS can implicate noncoding-variants (though no genome-wide non-coding burden was detectable).
NR3C2 loss defines a novel syndromic ASD form, modeled in zebrafish (social-preference deficit + disrupted sleep) that phenocopies human core and comorbid features.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 2,308 individuals in 493 AGRE multiplex families (~30x WGS, hg19); 960 affected + 217 unaffected children with both parents sequenced.
No global excess of rare inherited PTVs/missense/SVs in affected vs unaffected children — consistent with reduced penetrance and lower effect size of inherited risk.
High-risk inherited approach (variants transmitted to all affected, no unaffected, in constrained genes pLI ≥ 0.9): 98 genes, 62 PTVs + 40 SVs; forms significant direct (p < 0.008) and indirect (p < 0.002) PPI networks enriched for the BAF/SWI-SNF complex (ARID1B, SMARCC2, SMARCA4; OR = 5.9).
TADA mega-analysis (iHART + Sanders 2015): 69 genes at FDR < 0.1, 24 passing genome-wide Bonferroni, 16 novel (e.g. UIMC1, PCM1, MYO5A, GRIA1, RAPGEF4); novel genes enriched for inherited-PTV-driven signal.
DLG2 promoter deletion (2.5 kb): 3/484 affected vs 0/2,889 controls (Fisher p = 0.003), arose independently on different haplotypes.
ARC (Artifact Removal by Classifier), a 48-feature random forest trained on 76 MZ twin pairs, removes LCL-derived artifacts; ROC AUC 0.99 train / 0.98 test; restores expected de novo rate (60.1 RDNVs/child) and paternal-age effect (+1.46 RDNVs/year).
De novo PTV rate in multiplex affected children (0.07) was ~half the simplex rate (0.13) and equal to unaffected iHART siblings — evidence for de novo depletion in multiplex families.

Connections

Extends and reuses the parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna cortical co-expression framework (module M2) for gene-set enrichment.
Complements gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across (coauthor Gandal) on ASD molecular convergence, and schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism on autism cortical development.
CHD8 (chd8) is the top-ranked ASD gene here (lowest simulation p = 9e-7); DSCAM (dscam) is among the 69; contrast with the CHD8 neurogenesis mechanism in nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8.
Single-cell enrichment analyses connect to prenatal-brain atlases such as velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal; broader ASD/brain genetics context in zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain.
Speaks to heritability and family-based (twin-and-family-study-design) architecture of neurodevelopmental disorders.

Open questions

Can larger multiplex cohorts push lower-penetrance inherited PTVs (genome-wide transmission trend p = 0.08) past significance and classify genes as de novo / inherited / both?
Do inherited-vs-de-novo pathway differences (ion transport/cytoskeleton vs chromatin) map onto phenotypic severity subtypes?
What non-coding constraint metrics (analogous to pLI) are needed to detect regulatory risk given the null genome-wide promoter burden?
Does the NR3C2 mineralocorticoid-receptor axis generalize as a syndromic ASD mechanism beyond zebrafish?

wiki/sources/ryan-2009-the-origin-and-ev.title: ryan-2009-the-origin-and-evolution
wiki/sources/ryan-2009-the-origin-and-ev.content:
The origin and evolution of synapses
One-line: A review arguing that the molecular machinery of the synapse predates neurons and metazoans, tracing a “protosynapse” in unicellular ancestors through comparative proteomics and genomics.
Key takeaways

Proteomic and comparative-genomic mapping of the postsynaptic proteome reveals an ancestral “protosynapse” in unicellular eukaryotes that predated the evolution of neurons and metazoans, reframing views on the origins of the brain and its bearing on human-brain-evolution.
Synaptic signalling components were co-opted from pre-existing environmental-response machinery, so much of the synapse’s molecular toolkit was in place before morphological synapses or neurodevelopment existed.
Synapse complexity and diversity — including the range of neuronal-cell-types and synapse types — expanded via clade-specific gene-family duplications, especially in deuterostomes/vertebrates.

Notable claims / data

The mouse postsynaptic proteome comprises ~1,500 proteins; the postsynaptic density (PSD) is built from multiprotein complexes (ion channels, receptors, adhesion/cytoskeletal proteins, kinases, phosphatases, scaffolds).
Against the yeast S. cerevisiae genome, >21% of MASC genes and 25% of PSD genes had direct orthologues, defining them as protosynaptic proteins of pre-metazoan ancestry; ~15% of yeast PSD orthologues function in signal transduction.
Sponges (e.g. A. queenslandica, O. carmela) express co-localized protosynaptic complexes (Dlg, GKAP, GRIP, Homer, CRIPT) plus inhibitory GABA receptors, KIR channels and metabotropic glutamate receptors; inhibitory GABA receptors preceded excitatory ionotropic glutamate receptors.
Ionotropic NMDA/AMPA receptors and neuroligin first appear in cnidarians (N. vectensis), ~200 Myr before bilaterians; ursynapse dated ~1,020 Mya (av.).
Vertebrate NR2 (NMDA-receptor) subunits carry intracellular C-terminal domains ~5x larger (~600 aa) than protostome NR2 (~100 aa); two rounds of chordate whole-genome duplication yielded four NR2 (A–D) and four Dlg paralogues, expanding potential NR2–Dlg interactions ~12-fold.
Mouse MASC is enriched for recently evolved “upstream” signalling components (>60%) vs 25% in Drosophila MASC; 71% of Drosophila MASC components have yeast orthologues vs 21.2% for mouse.

Connections

Complements the neurodevelopmental and cell-type framing in this wiki’s brain-development corpus, providing the deep-evolutionary backdrop to neurodevelopment and the diversification of neuronal-cell-types.
Molecular-evolution lens on human-brain-evolution: synaptic complexity as a substrate for later expansion in the vertebrate/human lineage.

Open questions

Do protosynaptic components in yeast and choanoflagellates assemble into functional multiprotein complexes as they do in neurons?
Comprehensive PSD-gene-set analyses of choanoflagellate genomes remained unreported at time of writing; transgenic tests in unicellular organisms and sponges could validate proposed protosynaptic roles.
What are the actual (proteomically defined) contents of protostome PSD/MASC complexes, beyond orthology inference?

wiki/sources/sanders-2015-insights-into-.title: sanders-2015-insights-into-autism-spectrum-disorder
wiki/sources/sanders-2015-insights-into-.content:
Insights into Autism Spectrum Disorder Genomic Architecture and Biology from 71 Risk Loci
One-line: A combined CNV + exome analysis of the full Simons Simplex Collection that nominates 71 ASD risk loci (6 CNV regions + 65 genes) and shows small de novo deletions, unlike large CNVs, each harbor a single high-effect risk gene.
Key takeaways

Extends the tada-method framework to integrate small de novo deletions with de novo SNVs/indels, yielding 65 ASD risk genes (FDR ≤ 0.1) plus 6 CNV loci = 71 risk loci.
De novo mutations are a large driver of autism risk in simplex families, contributing in ~11% of cases; the study is a landmark of psychiatric-genetics gene discovery.
The 65 genes split into two protein-protein interaction subnetworks enriched for chromatin regulators vs. synaptic/neuronal proteins, echoing neurodevelopment convergence points (e.g., chd8, dscam).
Small de novo deletions behave like point mutations (one critical gene), whereas large de novo CNVs likely carry multiple modest-effect genes — a genomic-architecture distinction relevant to missing-heritability.
De novo mutations confer ASD risk even at high IQ, decoupling ASD liability from intellectual disability.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 10,220 individuals in 2,591 SSC families (2,100 quartets, 491 trios); 166 validated autosomal dnCNVs (97% qPCR confirmation).
dnCNV burden: 0.052 per proband vs 0.016 per sibling (ratio 3.1, p = 6.0×10⁻¹¹); proband dnCNVs are more frequent, larger (median 875 kbp vs 147 kbp), and more gene-rich.
Six genome-wide-significant SSC CNV loci (1q21.1, 3q29, 7q11.23, 16p11.2, 15q11.2-13.1, 22q11.2); +AGP adds NRXN1 and SHANK3/22q13.33 among 8 total CNV loci.
Female protective effect: 1.9 genes within dnCNVs per female proband vs 0.8 per male; de novo mutations contribute to ASD risk in 17% of female vs 10% of male probands.
Contribution estimates: ~70% of proband dnCNVs and ~46% of dnLoF mutations carry ASD risk; 4% of cases have a risk dnCNV, 7% a risk dnLoF, 11% either.
Phenotype associations: lower non-verbal IQ, seizure history, and head-circumference deviation >1 SD all raise de novo burden; paternal/maternal age and developmental regression do not.
Gene ontology: 3.2-fold enrichment for chromatin regulation (ARID1B, CHD8, KMT2C/E, DNMT3A); synaptic subnetwork 9.5-fold enriched, chromatin subnetwork 6.6-fold.

Connections

Extends and depends on the tada-method (He et al. 2013) statistical framework it introduces as an entity here.
Names recurrent ASD convergence genes catalogued elsewhere in the wiki: chd8 (chromatin, ChIP-seq target enrichment) and dscam (synaptic adhesion).
Foundational to later ASD systems-genomics and neurodevelopmental splicing/expression work; relevant background for autism- and neurodevelopment-tagged sources.
Frames the genomic-architecture question underlying missing-heritability and psychiatric-genetics.

Open questions

Which specific gene(s) within large multi-genic de novo CNVs drive risk, given the “multiple modest-effect genes” model?
How do inherited (rather than de novo) rare and common variants combine with de novo events to reach full ASD heritability?
What convergent developmental cell types and time windows unify the chromatin vs synaptic subnetworks?

wiki/sources/saunders-2023-embryo-scale-.title: saunders-2023-embryo-scale-reverse-genetics
wiki/sources/saunders-2023-embryo-scale-.content:
Embryo-scale reverse genetics at single-cell resolution
One-line: A whole-organism, individual-embryo scRNA-seq framework that phenotypes 23 F0 CRISPR perturbations across zebrafish development, delivering the zscape atlas and cell-type-abundance statistics for reverse genetics.
Key takeaways

Couples three technologies—combinatorial-indexing scRNA-seq (sci-RNA-seq3), sci-Plex oligonucleotide hashing to resolve individual embryos, and F0 CRISPR–Cas9 mutagenesis (crispants)—into a scalable single-cell-perturbation-modeling pipeline for whole vertebrate embryos.
Reads out genome-editing phenotypes as organism-wide shifts in cell-type composition rather than by gross morphology, an arrayed alternative to pooled single-cell-crispr-screen designs where each embryo is one perturbation replicate.
Builds zscape (“zebrafish single-cell atlas of perturbed embryos”): a high-time-resolution, individual-resolved reference atlas plus a perturbation dataset spanning neurodevelopment and diverse neuronal-cell-types.
High replication (≥8 embryos/condition) lets the study model baseline variance in cell-type abundance and detect perturbation-dependent deviance, resolving genetic dependencies of rare cell types (<1% of the embryo).

Notable claims / data

Scale: single-cell transcriptomes from 1,812 individually resolved embryos, 19 timepoints (18 hpf → 96 hpf, mostly 2 h resolution), 23 genetic perturbations, ~3.2 million cells total.
Reference atlas: ~1,167 individuals / ~1.2M cells; oligo hashing recovers embryo-of-origin for ~70% of QC-passing cells. Cells hierarchically classified into 33 major tissues, 99 broad cell types, 156 subtypes (annotated against ZFIN markers).
Perturbation set: 23 genes/gene-pairs across mesoderm, CNS/PNS, and neural-crest lineages; 2–3 gRNAs/gene injected as RNPs at the one-cell stage; ~804 barcoded embryos, ~2.7M cells; average 1,470 DEGs per perturbation summed across cell types.
Statistics: cell-type-abundance variance modeled with a gamma-response GLM (significant excess variance at P<0.05); differentially abundant cell types (DACTs) called by beta-binomial regression (q<0.01).
Biology: tbx16/tbx16l/msgn1 crispants cooperatively expand MPC and posterior spinal-cord progenitor lineages from the NMp pool. Captured ~30,000 cranial sensory ganglia neurons (~20/embryo) resolving epibranchial, trigeminal, statoacoustic, and lateral-line branches; foxi1 vs phox2a crispants dissect their genetic hierarchy.
Notochord-like cells (NLCs) appearing in tbxta crispants (epyc+, col2a1a+, shha+) are transcriptionally near-identical to notochord sheath cells but are actually parachordal cartilage, suggesting a shared notochord–cartilage program and new hypotheses on skull origins.

Connections

Extends single-cell perturbation profiling from cell lines to whole organisms; conceptual lineage from dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits and adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening (in-vitro pooled single-cell-crispr-screen), and from Trapnell-lineage tools such as trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification.
Complements human developmental atlases like velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal by adding programmatic perturbation and individual-embryo resolution.
The zscape atlas is a reusable functional-single-cell-genomics resource for mapping genetic dependencies of neuronal-cell-types.

Open questions

Can arrayed embryo-scale crispant profiling scale to genome-wide perturbation sets or pooled multiplexing without losing individual resolution?
Are NLC/parachordal-cartilage findings evidence for an evolutionary co-option of the notochord sheath program into the vertebrate skull base?
How much F0 mosaicism confounds cell-type-abundance calls, and can guide-level genotyping per embryo be integrated?

wiki/sources/schwarz-2026-cortical-devel.title: schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism
wiki/sources/schwarz-2026-cortical-devel.content:
Cortical development dynamics across autism spectrum disorder mouse models
One-line: Single-nucleus multi-omics across 11 monogenic ASD mouse models reveals that genetically diverse mutations converge on a transient delay in the radial glial lineage and on stage-specific early-postnatal neuronal (synaptic/ion-channel) transcriptional changes, with convergence weakening by P14 and sex-specific effects often larger in females.
Key takeaways

Despite >100 functionally diverse causal genes, mutations linked to autism converge on perturbations of the radial glial cell lineage during neurogenesis — a transient developmental delay (mutant RG cells linger in the proliferating state) rather than lasting lineage misspecification, resolving by postnatal stages.
The largest transcriptional differences emerge in neuronal-cell-types at early postnatal stages (P4 excitatory neurons), dominated by downregulation of synaptic and ion-channel genes — consistent with homeostatic adaptation or delayed maturation during neurodevelopment.
Network analysis shows molecular convergence across models is strongest within each developmental stage but weakens by P14, with cross-genotype heterogeneity layered on top of shared stage-specific programs.
Electrophysiology corroborates the molecular pattern: a transcriptome–physiology coupling where greater ion-channel/synaptic gene divergence predicts larger excitability and synaptic deviations; relevant to the excitation–inhibition framing of autism and psychiatric-genetics.
Sex-specific gene-expression alterations are prominent, with female mutant mice often showing larger effect sizes than males.

Notable claims / data

Scale: 251 samples from 11 monogenic mouse models of ASD (Ash1l+/−, Bckdk−/−, Cul3+/−, HnrnpU+/−, Kdm6b+/−, Kmt5b+/−, Ptchd1−/y, Setd5+/−, Trip12+/−, Usp7+/−, Wac+/−), profiled by combined snRNA-seq + snATAC-seq using cholesterol-modified-oligo (CMO) multiplexing; 45 conditions, both sexes, two brain regions (forebrain, cerebellum), three developmental stages (E14.5, P4, P14).
Nuclei: 200,787 total — 66,969 (E14.5), 56,260 (P4 forebrain), 59,974 (P14 forebrain), 17,584 (P4 cerebellum). ~3,900 transcripts, ~2,000 genes, ~4,200 fragments per cell at ~25,000 reads/cell median depth.
Cell types: 12 (E14.5), 22 (P4 cortex), 26 (P14 cortex), 16 (P4 cerebellum).
RG lineage: cortical plate cells depleted at E14.5 with reciprocal increase in RG; OPCs/immature astrocytes depleted at P4; OPCs remained lower at P14 while other types normalized. Pseudotime split RG into RG1 (G1/S, proliferating, MKi67+) and RG2 (G2); mutants tended to remain in RG1. EdU labelling in Kdm6b+/− and HnrnpU+/− showed increased EdU+ cells (prolonged/expanded proliferation). Shared RG1 DEGs across all mutants: only Rsrp1 and Galnt17 downregulated; HnrnpU most affected (>150 DEGs).
DEGs: P4 layer II/IV (715 DEGs, adj P<0.05) and layer V/VI DLCPN (600 DEGs), mostly downregulated, in all-mutants analysis. ~10% of P4 excitatory-neuron DEGs shared by ≥4 mutants; ≥50% of DEGs in each cell type were genotype-restricted. Frequent cross-mutant DEGs were 34% ASD-associated genes, enriched for synaptic proteins (q=1.03×10⁻¹⁵). Histone-modifier mutants (Kmt5b, Setd5, Wac) showed few DEGs.
Convergence dynamics: network DEG localizations were primarily shaped by developmental stage; within a stage mutants were more similar than chance, but diverged by P14 (especially excitatory neurons).
Ion channels / synapses: Scn1a among most commonly affected (often with reduced Scn8a, increased Scn9a — putative compensation); Camk2a, Rims1/2, Stxbp5l downregulated; Cacng4 upregulated across genotypes.
Electrophysiology (P7 layer II/III pyramidal neurons): 3/4 tested mutants (Bckdk, HnrnpU, Usp7) showed altered intrinsic excitability/input–output; Trip12 was the exception (subtle changes). Reduced mEPSC/mIPSC frequency in Bckdk, Usp7, Trip12; Bckdk also reduced mEPSC amplitude (Gria1 downregulation). HnrnpU broadly altered transcriptomically but mild synaptic phenotype, attributed to buffering upregulation (e.g. Ap3b2, Akap12). A minimal conductance-based model reproduced observed firing changes from transcriptomically inferred conductance shifts.
Chromatin (snATAC): most DARs at E14.5, greatest in RG cells; HnrnpU+/− and Kdm6b+/− RG cells had 801 and 335 DARs respectively (more DARs than DEGs in RG). Aggregate mutant analysis showed net decreased accessibility. RG DARs enriched for WNT/BMP processes and TF motifs (e.g. Sp2, regulator of progenitor cell-cycle length).
Sex effects: few sex DEGs between controls (aside from sex-chromosome genes). At P4, Ash1l, Bckdk, HnrnpU, Trip12, Usp7, Wac showed more DEGs in females. Female-biased DEGs (fbDEGs) had higher Cohen’s d in females; female-specific DEGs (fsDEGs) enriched for synaptic (q=6.5×10⁻⁷), ion-channel (q=4.8×10⁻⁶), cell-adhesion (q=7.9×10⁻³) functions. Wac (a reported female-specific candidate gene) had the highest fsDEG proportion. ApoE downregulated specifically in female interneurons of Ash1l, Bckdk, Trip12, Usp7.
Cerebellum: no shared cross-mutant transcriptional changes; Bckdk−/− was the outlier (widespread changes mirroring its cortical amino-acid/metabolic profile; ~1,023 genes shared cortex–cerebellum). Bckdk−/− juveniles (P13–15) showed motor deficits (reduced stride/step length, speed, abnormal hindpaw positioning); Kmt5b mutants showed no gait abnormalities.

Connections

Mouse-model counterpart to human single-cell ASD work and bulk transcriptomic convergence: gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across.
Developmental/cellular framing connects to prenatal human cortical single-cell atlases: velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal and to early brain overgrowth findings: courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development.
The radial-glia/progenitor-delay theme links to in-vitro neurodevelopmental models: kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular (brain-organoids).
Stage-specific convergence and gene-by-cell-type architecture relate to dear-2024-cortical-gene-expression-architecture-links.
ASD as part of the broader psychiatric-genetics landscape: grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across.

Open questions

Can interventions targeting shared early-developmental changes (e.g. RG progression delay) redirect later disorder trajectories, given that convergence weakens with maturation?
What mechanisms underlie the stronger female effect sizes, and how do they relate to the female-protective effect in human ASD?
Two RG1 DEGs (Rsrp1, Galnt17) are insufficient to explain the RG progression phenotype — what are the upstream causes, which appear to act earlier than E14.5 in some mutants?
How generalizable is the transcriptome–electrophysiology coupling across cell types and stages beyond the four tested mutants, given post-transcriptional buffering complicates one-to-one attribution?
Why is the cerebellum largely spared (except Bckdk−/−) despite cortical convergence, and does this reflect regional or metabolic specificity?

wiki/sources/schwessinger-2020-deepc-pre.title: schwessinger-2020-deepc-predicting-3d-genome-folding
wiki/sources/schwessinger-2020-deepc-pre.content:
DeepC: predicting 3D genome folding using megabase-scale transfer learning
One-line: A transfer-learning deep neural network (deepc) that predicts 3D chromatin folding from megabase DNA sequence, resolving domain boundaries and the base-pair impact of variants.
Key takeaways

deepc predicts Hi-C-style chromatin interactions up to ~1 Mb directly from ~1-Mb DNA windows, bridging base-pair sequence to megabase genome-architecture.
A two-phase transfer-learning scheme is central: a convolutional module pretrained on 936 chromatin-feature datasets is transferred, then a dilated-convolution module learns to predict folding — enabling accurate prediction even from low-depth Hi-C.
It calls topologically-associating-domains boundaries at higher effective resolution than the input Hi-C, validated against high-sensitivity NG Capture-C and Tiled-C 3C data.
Saliency scoring and an in silico deletion screen reveal the sequence determinants of folding: CTCF sites plus active promoters and enhancers all shape genome architecture (cis-regulatory-grammar).
The sequence-based model predicts the impact of noncoding-variants from large structural deletions down to single SNPs.

Notable claims / data

Trained on 7 human + 1 mouse Hi-C datasets; main analysis on GM12878 (~3.6B reads) and K562 (~1.3B reads) at 5-kb resolution.
Distance-stratified Pearson correlation (prediction vs Hi-C skeleton) ~0.36 raw / ~0.57 smoothed in GM12878 (~0.28 / ~0.51 in K562); outperforms random-forest HiC-Reg on unseen chromosomes.
Robust to downsampling: 1B and 100M-contact models still learn structure (r 0.46 / 0.40 on hold-out chr16/17); the 10M model fails.
eQTLs in regulatory regions have significantly higher saliency than resampled SNPs (P < 1e-85, KS test) — saliency can fine-map eQTLs acting through chromatin architecture.
Deletion screen (n=96,805): CTCF-site deletions have the strongest predicted impact, followed by promoters (stronger than enhancers) and active-mark elements.
Recapitulates a ~30-kb 4-CTCF-site deletion domain fusion at the LMO2 locus (5C-validated, Hnisz et al.); no single CTCF deletion suffices, implying redundant boundaries.
On two 17q21 asthma SNPs (Schmiedel et al.), deepC prioritizes rs12936231 as causal (mean abs. interaction diff 0.176, top 1% of sampled CTCF SNPs) over rs4065275 (0.006); effect is lymphoid-specific (GM12878, not K562/IMR90).
Final models ~60M parameters; a variant prediction takes ~5 min on GPU / ~2 h on CPU.

Connections

Introduces the deepc model (new entity page).
A sequence-to-structure deep-learning model in the lineage of DNA sequence models (dna-language-models); shares a convolutional base with the parallel Akita model.
Complements chromatin-conformation-capture assays (Hi-C, NG Capture-C, Tiled-C) as the validation ground truth for predicted chromatin-loops and boundaries.
Saliency-based dissection is a model-interpretability approach for genomics; links sequence to genome-architecture and cis-regulatory-grammar.
Relevant to 3d-genome-and-disease: predicts how CTCF-disrupting SNPs and structural variants rewire enhancer-promoter insulation.
Thematically adjacent to che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome on 3D genome structure and boundaries.

Open questions

Can architecture/encoding refinements capture the punctate CTCF loop apex (loop-extrusion mechanics) and interdomain insulation that deepC currently under-models?
How to incorporate repressive lamina-associated domains, poorly predicted due to sparse annotation?
How do sequence-based predictors like deepC and Akita compare and consolidate, and can they scale to larger sequence contexts?

wiki/sources/scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicin.title: scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease
wiki/sources/scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicin.content:
RNA mis-splicing in disease
One-line: A Nature Reviews Genetics review surveying how errors in pre-mRNA splicing — from cis-acting sequence mutations to trans-acting splicing-factor and core-spliceosome defects — cause a growing list of human diseases, and the antisense/small-molecule therapies emerging to correct them.
Key takeaways

The human genome encodes a surprisingly small set of protein-coding genes (~20,000) relative to proteomic complexity; isoform diversity is generated mainly by alternative-splicing, with >90% of human protein-coding genes producing multiple mRNA isoforms. This makes splicing highly susceptible to hereditary and somatic mutation.
Splice-site selection is intrinsically error-prone: small exons (~80% <200 bp) embedded in large introns, extensive alternative splicing, and the co-transcriptional nature of splicing (modulated by RNA Pol II elongation rate) all complicate authentic 5’/3’ splice-sites identification.
Most (>90%) disease-associated SNPs lie outside protein-coding regions, and many disease SNPs in coding genes are proposed to act by altering splicing — underscoring splicing-and-disease as a major, under-recognized mechanism.
Three mutation classes are reviewed: (1) cis-acting mutations in core consensus sequences (5’ss, 3’ss, branch point) and regulatory elements (splicing-regulatory-elements: ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS); (2) mutations in core spliceosome components (e.g. tri-snRNP proteins in retinitis pigmentosa; sf3b1/u2af1/SRSF2 in myeloid cancers); (3) mutations in trans-acting splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins.
An emerging theme: mutation-induced aggregation of splicing factors. Low-complexity / prion-like domains of hnrnpa1, TDP-43 and FUS are mutation hotspots in ALS, linking proteostasis failure to mis-splicing.
Splicing-modulating therapy is advancing: antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) and small molecules are in clinical trials for Duchenne muscular dystrophy, spinal muscular atrophy and myotonic dystrophy (splicing-therapeutics).

Notable claims / data

One LMNA gene, via different splicing mutations, causes multiple distinct diseases (Fig. 1): 5’ss mutations cause intron-9 retention → LGMD1B (c.1608+5G>C) or intron-8 retention → FPLD2 (c.1488+5G>C), both yielding PTCs and NMD; a cryptic 5’ss in exon 11 (c.1824C>T) deletes 150 nt → progerin → HGPS; an alternative 3’ss (c.640-10A>G) adds 3 amino acids → dilated cardiomyopathy. Laminopathies span >14 diseases.
Retinitis pigmentosa: six adRP genes encode U4/U6.U5 tri-snRNP components (PRPF3, PRPF4, PRPF6, PRPF8, PRPF31, SNRNP200/BRR2). PRPF31 incomplete penetrance is explained by a haploinsufficiency model modulated by the CNOT3 modifier gene; the retina expresses the highest levels of some spliceosomal snRNAs, possibly explaining tissue specificity.
Cancer: PRPF6 is overexpressed in colorectal carcinoma and drives proliferation via an oncogenic ZAK isoform; recurrent somatic mutations hit SF3B1, U2AF1, U2AF2 and SRSF2 in myeloid neoplasms (MDS, CMML). U2AF1 mutations change 3’ss recognition and mis-splice hundreds of transcripts. Antitumour drugs targeting the spliceosome exist, notably for MYC-driven cancers.
Minor spliceosome: homozygous RNU4ATAC (U4atac snRNA) mutations disrupting the 5’ stem-loop cause MOPD I (Taybi-Linder syndrome). Minor introns act as stress-responsive switches — p38 MAPK stabilizes the normally unstable U6atac snRNA (t1/2 <2 h), enhancing splicing of minor-intron genes including PTEN.
ALS: TDP-43 and FUS mutations account for ~4% and ~5% of familial ALS, mostly in prion-like domains. Mouse TDP-43(Q331K)/(M337V) mutants cause motor neuron degeneration with target-specific mis-splicing without nuclear depletion or aggregation. A normal TDP-43 function is repressing non-conserved cryptic exons (often NMD-inducing) in distal introns. ~90% of ALS is sporadic; C9orf72 GGGGCC expansions cause C9ALS/FTD.
Alzheimer disease: U1 snRNP proteins (SNRNP70/U1-70K, SNRPA/U1-A) form tangle-like cytoplasmic inclusions associating with tau, correlating with accumulation of unspliced pre-mRNAs; SNRNP70 knockdown or U1 ASO blockade raises amyloid precursor protein.
Microexons (≤51 nt), highly conserved and regulated by SRRM4 (nSR100), RBFOX and PTBP1, are misregulated in autism; RBFOX1 mutations occur in several neurological disorders.
Therapies with specifics: DMD exon-51 skipping via drisapersen (2’OMePS) and eteplirsen (PMO) to restore reading frame (DMD affects ~1 in 3,500 male newborns; gene is 2.4 Mb, 79 exons). SMA (1 in ~10,000 births) treated with MOE ASO-10-27 blocking an SMN2 intron-7 ISS to boost exon-7 inclusion (Phase III); small molecules SMNC1-3 and NVS-SM1-4 raise SMN (NVS-SM1 extended SMNΔ7 mouse lifespan from ~15 to >35 days). Myotonic dystrophy (DM1 CTGexp in DMPK 3’UTR; DM2 CCTGexp in CNBP) sequesters MBNL proteins; ASO gapmers (RNase H) and small molecules (substituted naphthyridine, pentamidine, lomofungin) displace MBNL.

Connections

Sits squarely in splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics; complements the mechanism/regulation reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative by foregrounding pathology.
Provides disease context for splicing-factor entities already in the wiki: sf3b1, u2af1, srsf1 (SMN2 exon-7 ESE), hnrnpa1 (prion-like domain ALS mutations), ptbp1 (microexon regulation) and smn2 (SMA ASO target).
The G-quadruplex angle (splicing factors binding pre-formed RNA structures; small molecules targeting them) connects to g-quadruplex and peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Its closing emphasis on machine-learning “splicing code” models (citing the Xiong et al. 2015 splicing-code study) links to splicing-code and presages tool-driven work like spliceai.

Open questions

Why is the U4/U6.U5 tri-snRNP so selectively essential for retinal (RPE/photoreceptor) function despite its ubiquitous role?
For tri-snRNP and core-spliceosome diseases, when does pathology arise from haploinsufficiency versus dominant-negative/gain-of-function aggregation?
How many ALS/neurodegeneration splicing changes are direct (TDP-43/FUS targets) versus secondary to nuclear depletion or cytoplasmic aggregation? Do other hnRNPs also repress non-conserved cryptic exons?
Do lncRNAs (e.g. MIAT/Gomafu) and circRNAs have direct, causal roles in splicing regulation and disease, or are they correlative?

wiki/sources/searles-quick-2021-leveragi.title: searles-quick-2021-leveraging-large-genomic-datasets
wiki/sources/searles-quick-2021-leveragi.content:
Leveraging large genomic datasets to illuminate the pathobiology of autism spectrum disorders
One-line: A review of how “big data” human genomics (CNV, WES/WGS, GWAS) mapped ASD genetic architecture, and how foundational -omics databases are being used to translate risk genes into pathobiology.
Key takeaways

ASD genetic architecture is a continuum: common alleles of small effect carry the majority of population risk, while rare de novo variants of large effect dominate among diagnosed cases; the “syndromic vs non-syndromic” distinction blurs and can only be resolved by genetic testing, not phenotype.
Reliable gene discovery in autism rests on quantifying recurrence of de novo mutations at the same gene/locus rather than case-vs-control counting, giving high statistical power from few events (basis of the TADA method).
The core unsolved problem is translating >100 pleiotropic risk genes into mechanism; the field’s answer is convergence analysis — using large gene-coexpression-network and expression atlases to find where diverse risk genes intersect in cell types, brain regions, and developmental windows.
Human mid-fetal cortical excitatory neurons repeatedly emerge as a convergence point, identified via BrainSpan co-expression networks (psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource).
Gene-targeting therapies (ASOs, AAV) are becoming plausible for Mendelian ASD/ID, modeled on SMA where modulating smn2 splicing compensates for SMN1 loss.

Notable claims / data

ASD prevalence ~1.7% in the US; ~70 de novo variants per human genome vs ~3M common and ~300K rare inherited variants.
De novo germline CNVs present in 5–10% of affected individuals vs <1–2% of unaffected siblings; females show increased de novo CNV burden (female protective effect).
Largest WES to date analyzed 21,219 family-based + 14,365 case-control samples, identifying 102 ASD-risk genes (30 novel); high-confidence de novo variants raise risk ~20-fold; >30% of patients harbor a contributing de novo LoF/missense mutation.
ASD GWAS (18,391 cases / 27,969 controls) found 5 genome-wide significant loci; PRS odds ratio top-vs-bottom decile only ~3.6 (vs ~20 in schizophrenia). Common-variant deleteriousness scores discussed: pLI (>0.9 cutoff), cadd-score, MPC, LOEUF (Box 1).
CWAS on 519 SSC families found no significant noncoding category; authors estimate >8000 families needed — noncoding contribution to ASD risk appears modest vs coding.
Genetic testing yields a causative variant in only 15–40% of cases; findings have not yet meaningfully changed clinical care (mainstay remains behavioral intervention).

Connections

Chromatin-modifier convergence anchors on chd8 (increased head circumference phenotype), a paradigmatic ASD-risk gene; related ventral-neurogenesis work in nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8.
Contextualizes the missing-heritability and psychiatric-genetics framing via de novo vs common-variant partition and genome-wide-association-study-based polygenic risk.
Convergence/systems-biology framing overlaps with human-brain developmental atlas resources (psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource) and early-brain-development mapping (courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development).
Therapeutic rationale connects to splicing-therapeutics via the SMN2 antisense-oligonucleotide paradigm.

Open questions

Which noncoding elements/contexts carry ASD risk, and how to predict variant impact given WGS’s ~3 billion candidate sites?
How do common and rare variants functionally interact (e.g., outcome of a de novo CNV against a given polygenic background)?
What is the therapeutic window — does effective gene-targeting require in-utero intervention, or can postnatal rescue work?
What reliable, early biomarkers and outcome measures can capture change in social functioning for small-N gene-targeted trials?

wiki/sources/segers-2026-saser-juggling-.title: segers-2026-saser-juggling-offsets-unlocks
wiki/sources/segers-2026-saser-juggling-.content:
saseR: Juggling Offsets Unlocks RNA-seq Tools for Fast and Scalable Differential Usage, Aberrant Splicing and Expression Retrieval
One-line: saser — a Bioconductor R package (Clement lab, Ghent) showing that swapping the normalization offset in standard negative-binomial rna-seq tools (DEXSeq, edgeR, DESeq2) unlocks them for differential usage and aberrant splicing/expression outlier detection in one unified framework, far faster than and outperforming the state of the art (OUTRIDER, FRASER) for rare-disease diagnostics.
Key takeaways

The central trick — change the offset. Conventional bulk RNA-seq DGE models a feature count as NB(µ, θ) with log(µ) = xᵀβ + offset, where the offset normalizes for library size. Replacing it with the log of the total gene count (summed over the gene’s features) makes the mean-model parameters β estimate the log-ratio / usage of each feature within its gene — directly unlocking the same tools for differential usage without DEXSeq’s per-sample blocking covariates.
One framework, many applications (their Table 1): the same NB model serves DGE/aberrant expression (gene counts + library-size offset), differential usage / aberrant splicing (exon+intron bin counts, junction counts, or transcript counts, each + log-total-gene offset), and even allele usage (allele counts + log-total-allele offset). Future-proof to long reads and allele-specific expression.
Outlier detection instead of group comparison. In rare disease there are no replicates — each patient carries a different causal gene — so two-group testing is invalid. saseR plugs the estimated mean and dispersion into the NB distribution and computes a two-sided tail probability (“score”) per feature per sample; small scores prioritize outliers. Because it is not formal hypothesis testing, there is no multiple-testing correction.
A fast parameter estimator that scales. Assuming a quadratic variance structure reduces each Newton-Raphson iteration to a single matrix multiplication that updates all genes simultaneously, so estimation scales to the large design matrices (many latent confounders) that aberrant-expression cohorts need. Built on unbiased estimating equations → estimator stays consistent even if the variance structure is misspecified (but for that reason it cannot be used for differential inference).
Confounders & rank selection. Latent confounders are removed with RUVr (SVD on deviance residuals); the number of latent factors is set by the Gavish–Donoho optimal hard threshold, avoiding OUTRIDER/FRASER’s expensive grid-search hyperparameter optimization (which saseR can still do optionally, with near-identical accuracy).
Three splicing workflows: saseR-bins (exon+intron bin reads), saseR-junctions (junction reads, log-total-junction offset), saseR-ASpli (ASpli junction clusters — junctions sharing a splice site, needs no prior annotation, catches novel splice sites).

Notable claims / data

Aberrant splicing (Geuvadis, 39 LCLs, RSEM-simulated outliers, p=0.001): all three saseR workflows outperform FRASER 2.0 (its Intron Jaccard Index); saseR-bins and saseR-junctions have the best power, then saseR-ASpli. FRASER’s precision never reaches high levels even at low recall. saseR also beats LeafcutterMD and SPOT (which can’t control for confounders). Key conceptual edge: FRASER uses only junction reads, discarding exonic/intronic bin information.
Aberrant expression (GTEx suprapubic skin, 249 donors; + Kremer): saseR is on par with OutSingle and OUTRIDER-Autoencoder, slightly above OUTRIDER-PCA — but saseR and OutSingle are much faster than OUTRIDER, and saseR is ~2× faster than OutSingle (Gavish–Donoho avoids hyperparameter optimization).
Differential transcript usage: edgeR and DESeq2 with adapted offsets match DEXSeq and satuRn on bulk RNA-seq and beat edgeR-diffspliceDGE; on scRNA-seq satuRn still leads. DEXSeq is orders of magnitude slower and does not scale to real scRNA-seq volumes.
Case study (Kremer, 119 mitochondrial-disease fibroblasts): searching 12 validated disease genes across 12 patients in a single 119-sample analysis, saseR-junctions ranks disease genes better than FRASER 2.0 (notably recovers TAZ and TALDO1, which FRASER misses); saseR aberrant expression matches OUTRIDER/OutSingle.
Available on GitHub (statOmics/saseR) and Bioconductor.

Connections

The scalable, unified successor to the single-purpose rare-disease RNA-seq tools already in the wiki: it directly benchmarks against and beats OUTRIDER (aberrant expression — brechtmann-2018-outrider-a-statistical-method) on speed and FRASER (aberrant splicing, the engine behind absplice2) on both speed and accuracy, and reframes DEXSeq’s differential-usage model as a special case of “pick the right offset.”
Sits in the splicing-methods toolkit on the clinical / diagnostic axis (splicing-and-disease): RNA-seq as a complementary assay to WES/WGS that resolves non-coding and intronic variants by their molecular consequence.
Its design is explicitly future-proof to long-read-rna-seq (transcript counts) and allele-specific expression — any quantification recastable as feature counts + a proper offset. intron-retention bins are part of what its bin workflow exploits over junction-only methods.

Open questions

The total-gene-count offset is a plug-in estimator — its uncertainty (and that of multi-mapping reads) is not propagated through the model; what is the cost in calibration?
Benchmarks are purely transcriptomic; unlike Scheller et al., no matched large-scale genomic data was available for orthogonal real-world validation of called outliers.
Can the unified offset framework be extended to other usage problems (allele-specific expression, long-read transcript usage) in practice, and does it improve eQTL-style mapping?

wiki/sources/seplyarskiy-2023-a-mutation.title: seplyarskiy-2023-a-mutation-rate-model
wiki/sources/seplyarskiy-2023-a-mutation.content:
A mutation rate model at the basepair resolution identifies the mutagenic effect of polymerase III transcription
One-line: A genome-wide, basepair-resolution germline mutation rate model (roulette-model) that outperforms prior models and reveals a ~10-fold mutation-rate spike in Pol III-transcribed genes.
Key takeaways

Introduces roulette-model, a logistic-regression mutation rate model incorporating extended sequence context, strand asymmetry (transcription/replication), testis methylation and expression, and local rare-SNV density in 50 KB windows.
Trained on 524 M rare noncoding-variants (<0.1% frequency) from gnomAD v3 whole genomes; captures 80-90% of mutation rate heterogeneity along the genome.
Model outliers uncover a new mutagenic mechanism: polymerase III (Pol III) transcription (tRNA, RNU snRNA genes) elevates mutation rate ~tenfold (up to 32-fold in de novo trio data).
Also detects a ~1.6-fold elevated T>G rate at transcription factor binding sites active in testis and restricted to promoters.

Notable claims / data

Fits 91,176 separate logistic models (one per pentamer x genomic compartment), with 115-parameter (pairwise interactions) or 25-parameter variants selected by cross-validation.
Pseudo-R2 on synonymous SNVs: Roulette 0.86 vs Carlson 0.81 vs gnomAD 0.78 (gnomAD v2); 0.93/0.87/0.85 on 41,816 trios (2,759 de novo synonymous mutations).
Fewer per-gene outliers (|z|>2): 1,758 genes vs 2,468 (Carlson) / 2,295 (gnomAD).
Poisson recurrence correction recovers mutation-rate scale from monomorphic-site probabilities; refined European demographic inference (14% faster recent growth; Ne 8.1 M vs 2.5 M prior).
RNU genes: sevenfold SNV-density increase, but 32-fold (95% CI 19-50) elevated de novo rate; increase limited to actively transcribed genes, not pseudogenes.

Connections

Provides site-specific neutral mutation-rate baselines relevant to constraint/selection estimation and disease-gene mapping over noncoding-variants and coding sites.
Complements deep-learning mutation-rate-map efforts cited within (e.g. Fang et al.), here using interpretable logistic regression rather than neural approaches.

Open questions

What is the exact mechanism of Pol III-associated hypermutation (loss of transcription-coupled repair alone is insufficient; ribonucleotide incorporation, TFIIIB-driven DNA restructuring proposed)?
Can ML methods capturing higher-order interactions and sub-50 KB processes further reduce residual variance?
How to model recurrent mutations at high-rate sites as population datasets saturate?

wiki/sources/sestan-2018-lost-in-transla.title: sestan-2018-lost-in-translation-traversing
wiki/sources/sestan-2018-lost-in-transla.content:
Lost in Translation: Traversing the Complex Path from Genomics to Therapeutics in Autism Spectrum Disorder
One-line: A Perspective arguing that rare large-effect de novo mutations make non-syndromic autism uniquely tractable, and that studying convergence across risk genes in the developing human brain is the path to mechanisms and therapies.
Key takeaways

Gene discovery in non-syndromic autism (nsASD) has matured to yield dozens of well-established risk genes driven by rare, germline, heterozygous, de novo likely-gene-disrupting (LGD) coding mutations — contrasting sharply with the highly polygenic, small-effect common-variant architecture of later-onset psychiatric disorders.
The authors advocate a “convergence” strategy: rather than modeling one pleiotropic risk gene at a time in evolutionarily distant species, study many nsASD genes in parallel to triangulate the cell types, circuits, and developmental windows (spatiotemporal convergence) where functionally diverse genes intersect.
Deeply characterizing the molecular and cellular landscape of the developing human brain — including at single-cell resolution and in the context of human-specific neurodevelopment — is framed as essential to distinguish human pathophysiology from generic risk-gene biology.
Co-expression network integration of risk genes with human brain transcriptomes points to mid-fetal deep-layer (5/6) glutamatergic projection neurons as a recurring convergence point.
Emerging splice-modulating and gene-targeting approaches (e.g. nusinersen for SMA, leveraging smn2) motivate near-term therapeutic strategies that boost the intact normal allele in haploinsufficient nsASD genes.

Notable claims / data

De novo LGD mutations contribute to ~20%–30% of clinical nsASD cases; explained cases range ~10%–40% depending on ascertainment (female sex, seizure history, lower IQ increase detection).
A 2015 combined analysis identified 71 independent risk genes/loci (Sanders et al.); consensus projection is several hundred to ~1,000 genes conferring liability via rare de novo protein-damaging mutations.
A GWAS meta-analysis of ~8,000 (discovery) / >16,000 ASD cases found no genome-wide-significant locus, underscoring the extremely modest effect of common alleles despite major polygenic contribution.
One would need to be in the top decile of schizophrenia polygenic risk to approach the effect size of a single de novo LGD mutation in a highest-confidence nsASD gene.
Two enriched functional categories dominate risk genes: synaptic proteins and chromatin/transcriptional regulators (e.g. chd8, ARID1B, SCN2A as high-penetrance examples).
Perspective/review — no primary human data generated; caveats include heterogeneity, low per-gene mutation frequency (statistical power), and difficulty modeling heterozygous vs homozygous effects.

Connections

Provides the conceptual/genetic framing for developing-human-brain resources and single-cell atlases catalogued elsewhere in the wiki (e.g. velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal, patowary-2024-developmental-isoform-diversity-in-the).
The synaptic vs chromatin/transcription dichotomy and CHD8 focus connect to nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8 and tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related.
The allele-boosting / ASO therapeutic logic links to splicing-therapeutics and smn2; the pleiotropy/neurodegeneration cautions echo broader disease-gene modeling debates.
Autism transcriptomic dysregulation themes connect to gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna.

Open questions

Whether insights from the rare de novo LGD subset generalize to the polygenic majority of ASD cases, and how common and rare variation interact as disease modifiers.
How closely a model system must recapitulate the human spatiotemporal/cellular context for convergence experiments to be informative.
Whether a postnatal therapeutic window remains open despite mid-fetal vulnerability, and whether relevant cell types (e.g. deep-layer cortical neurons) can be targeted with ASO/gene approaches.
How to identify, enroll, and outcome-measure ultra-rare single-gene nsASD cohorts for trials.

wiki/sources/sharon-2018-functional-gene.title: sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel
wiki/sources/sharon-2018-functional-gene.content:
Functional Genetic Variants Revealed by Massively Parallel Precise Genome Editing
One-line: Introduces crispey, a retron-coupled CRISPR-Cas9 method that installs thousands of natural single-base variants into a common yeast background in one pooled experiment, then reads each variant’s relative-fitness effect to map functional genetic variation at single-base resolution.
Key takeaways

crispey (Cas9 Retron precISE Parallel Editing via homologY) fuses a bacterial Ec86 retron with an sgRNA into one chimeric retron-guide RNA; the retron reverse transcriptase generates an in-vivo single-stranded msDNA donor (100-nt, with 50-bp homology arms) covalently tethered to the guide, driving HDR editing efficiency up to 100% at ADE2.
The platform installed one RM allele at a time into the BY lab strain across 16,006 natural SNPs/short indels (32,000 guide/donor pairs), then competed the edited pool for 25 generations in glucose minimal media to estimate per-variant relative fitness.
572 variants showed significant fitness differences (IDR < 5%); these are strongly enriched in promoters and especially in transcription factor binding sites, identifying cis-regulatory-elements as the dominant locus of fitness variation.
Only 19.2% of significant variants alter amino acid sequence; the predominance of noncoding-variants contrasts with the coding-variant bias of prior single-locus yeast/plant/metazoan studies and is attributed to the absence of ascertainment bias.
Nearby significant variants (within 50 bp) almost always (98%) favor the same parent’s allele, evidence that lineage-specific natural selection produces clusters of reinforcing “composite QTL” variants.
Synonymous variants affect fitness about as often as missense variants, casting doubt on the assumption that synonymous mutations are neutral.

Notable claims / data

Editing: 100% efficiency at ADE2 after 48 hr with 0/95 colonies carrying undesired mutations; CRISPEY can also insert a 765-nt sequence at 92% efficiency with <0.01% intra-insert error; validation loci showed ~96% efficiency and 0% NHEJ.
Readout: abundance estimated from guide/donor sequencing across 6 replicate flasks and 11 time points; replicate abundances correlated r = 0.952-0.992; some strains shifted >30-fold in frequency over the competition.
Reproducibility: two independent editing experiments correlated only r = 0.5 overall (most variants neutral), but significant edits (IDR < 0.05) were reproducible across independent guides (r = 0.59); 14 individually re-tested edits all validated in direction and approximate effect size.
Effect sizes: 171 variants exceeded 1% fitness/generation and 17 exceeded 5%, magnitudes large enough to imply fitness tradeoffs / lineage-specific adaptation rather than unconditional advantage.
Enrichment: top 23 hits all in promoters (promoters = 29.8% of edits, hypergeometric p < 10^-11); divergent promoters 2.66-fold enriched; 33% of variants in known TFBS were significant vs 9.6% of all promoter variants; top motifs Reb1, Abf1, Rap1 (latter mostly in ribosomal protein promoters).
Selection: sign test found 25 cytoplasmic-translation (ribosomal) genes with RM-fitter promoter variants vs 2 BY-fitter (binomial p < 5x10^-9), indicating polygenic adaptation of the ribosome; these 25 genes were 2.3-fold enriched among trans-pQTLs whose RM alleles lower other proteins’ levels without changing mRNA.
Off-target characterization: Cas9 toxicity during editing tracked guide/off-target mismatch position (PAM/seed = lowest toxicity), agreed with in-vitro dCas9 binding equilibrium (r up to 0.85) but not binding rate, across >10,000 on-/off-target pairs.
Limitations stated: asymmetric power (easier to detect increasing strains), possible underestimation from imperfect editing (~4% unedited at 14 loci), and the requirement of a nearby NGG PAM (only ~70% of BY/RM SNPs targetable).

Connections

A precise single-base editing complement to reporter-based functional screens such as massively-parallel-reporter-assay work in deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory and the cis-regulatory mutation mapping of groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at; CRISPEY edits variants in their native genomic context rather than on episomal reporters.
Reinforces a recurring wiki theme that noncoding-variants in cis-regulatory-elements dominate functional/heritable variation.
The fitness competition is a relative-fitness pooled selection screen, a flavor of fitness-screen (no wiki page yet).

Open questions

Why do segregating variants with such large (>1%) fitness effects persist rather than fix or be lost — local adaptation, balancing selection, or fluctuating selection?
How environment- and strain-specific are the observed patterns (noncoding predominance, same-direction clustering) versus general features of genetic architecture?
Can CRISPEY be ported to low-HDR systems such as mammalian cells (via low-MOI transient retron msDNA delivery) to enable base-resolution screening of traits and disease variants?

wiki/sources/sharp-1994-split-genes-and-.title: sharp-1994-split-genes-and-rna-splicing
wiki/sources/sharp-1994-split-genes-and-.content:
Split Genes and RNA Splicing
One-line: Phillip Sharp’s Nobel Lecture reviewing the discovery of split genes and RNA splicing, the biochemistry and assembly cycle of the spliceosome, and the evolutionary kinship between spliceosomal and self-splicing introns.
Key takeaways

The discovery of splicing arose from comparing late adenovirus cytoplasmic mRNAs to the viral DNA, revealing that the mRNA body was assembled from non-contiguous DNA segments (Berget et al., 1977; Chow et al., 1977) — establishing the “split gene” structure and explaining the earlier hnRNA phenomenon.
Introns are pervasive in cellular genes (the average gene has ~8 introns; the primary transcript is typically 4-10x larger than the final mRNA) and bounded by a limited set of conserved consensus sequences common to vertebrate, plant, and yeast cells.
alternative-splicing lets one gene encode many proteins: ~1 in 20 genes is alternatively spliced, and the fibronectin gene alone yields ~20 distinct proteins through cell-type-specific exon inclusion/skipping.
The spliceosome is an ~60S complex assembling a substrate pre-mRNA with snRNPs (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6), splicing pre-mRNA in two transesterification steps through a lariat intermediate (2’-5’ branch at an intron adenosine).
Spliceosomal splicing chemically and evolutionarily resembles self-splicing group I and especially group II introns; the snRNAs may function as “a group II intron in pieces” forming the catalytic core.

Notable claims / data

~25% of human globin-gene mutations underlying thalassemia disrupt sequences specifying correct RNA splicing — splicing fidelity is a genetic constraint (splicing-and-disease).
~100 yeast genes (~2% of the genome) encode products important for splicing; temperature-sensitive PRP (pre-RNA-processing) mutants accumulate unspliced pre-mRNA and define specific transitions in the spliceosome cycle.
The spliceosome assembly cycle proceeds through biochemically distinguished complexes CC (commitment) → A → B1 → B2 → C1 → C2 → I, with U2 binding stabilized by the polypyrimidine-tract-binding protein U2AF (Arg-Ser-rich) and the U1-70 kDa protein.
Stereochemistry with chiral phosphorothioates (Moore & Sharp, 1993): both splicing steps are single transesterifications with inversion, and the Sp diastereomer is active in BOTH steps — implying two distinct catalytic centers (unlike group I, where the two steps are forward/reverse at a single center).
Pyruvate kinase has 9 coding-region introns in chicken but none in bacteria/yeast; intron positions are non-random relative to protein secondary structure and one is shared with alcohol dehydrogenase, supporting an exon-shuffling / ancient split-gene “progenote” hypothesis.
snRNPs concentrate in 20-50 nuclear “speckles” and in coiled bodies, are tethered to a nuclear matrix, and pre-RNA appears to be processed as it moves along curvilinear tracks toward nuclear pores (~4000 pores per mammalian nucleus).

Connections

Foundational ancestor to the entire splicing topic: it predates and underpins later mechanistic and regulatory reviews like lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
The Arg-Ser (SR) splicing factors it describes (SF2/ASF, SC35) connect to the splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins entities such as srsf1; U2AF anticipates u2af1.
The splice-sites consensus and U1/U2 base-pairing it lays out is the conceptual basis for modern splicing-code and spliceai work.
Its evolutionary argument relates to splicing-and-evolution and exon-shuffling themes echoed in wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.

Open questions

Why have introns persisted during evolution, given they drift rapidly and can be lost via cDNA-mediated gene conversion?
What is the mechanism transporting spliced mRNA from sites of transcription/splicing to nuclear pores?
How do the hypothetical PRP protein functions (RNA helicases, RNA-binding, protein-protein interaction) map onto specific spliceosome-cycle transitions?
Do U2AF and U1-70 kDa communicate directly across the intron during commitment-complex formation?

wiki/sources/shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-.title: shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection
wiki/sources/shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-.content:
rMATS: Robust and flexible detection of differential alternative splicing from replicate RNA-Seq data
One-line: The methods paper for rMATS — a hierarchical statistical model
that detects differential alternative-splicing from replicate RNA-seq
data by jointly modeling within-replicate estimation uncertainty and
between-replicate variability, and the canonical event-based ΔPSI
tool referenced throughout splicing-methods. See entity rmats.
Key takeaways

The gap it fills. First-generation tools (MISO, SpliceTrap, ALEXA-seq,
rSeqDiff) only compare two samples with no replicates; replicate-aware tools
(Cufflinks, FDM, DiffSplice, DEXSeq) fail to model the estimation
uncertainty of isoform proportion in individual replicates, and none
handle paired designs. rMATS targets both.
Lineage. rMATS = replicate MATS, the successor to MATS (Shen et al. 2012,
a Bayesian two-sample method); it replaces sampling-based p-values with a
faster likelihood-ratio test.
Hierarchical model. Two stacked layers per event:
a binomial for read-count–driven estimation uncertainty within each
replicate, and a logit-normal for variability among replicates —
estimated simultaneously. The metric is the exon inclusion level ψ
(percent-spliced-in).
Length normalization → all event types. A function f(ψ) corrects for the
effective lengths of the inclusion vs skipping isoforms, letting rMATS use both
splice-junction reads and exon-body reads and handle all five canonical AS
event types: skipped exon (SE), alternative 5′/3′ splice-sites (A5SS/A3SS),
mutually exclusive exons (MXE), and retained intron (RI).
Paired model. A bivariate-normal layer with an exon-specific correlation
ρᵢ models matched pairs (e.g. case–control / tumor–normal); it reduces to the
unpaired model when ρ=0 and boosts power for correlated pairs.
Flexible hypothesis test. A likelihood-ratio test for |Δψ| > c where the
threshold c is user-defined (e.g. 5%) — not merely the equality test
|Δψ| > 0 used by every other method. This tests for splicing changes of a
chosen magnitude.

Notable claims / data

Real-data validation (unpaired). Prostate cancer cell lines PC3E
(epithelial) vs GS689 (mesenchymal), 6 unpaired replicates, 746M paired reads →
721 differential AS events at |Δψ| > 5%, FDR ≤ 1%. RT-PCR on 34 events
validated 32/34 = 94%; RNA-seq vs RT-PCR Δψ correlation r = 0.96.
Paired model (TCGA ccRCC). 65 tumor–normal matched pairs → paired rMATS
found 315 differential exon-skipping events vs 304 for unpaired (a
superset); the 11 paired-only events are subtle-but-consistent (smaller |Δψ|
but also smaller cross-pair SD). Tumor splicing resembled the mesenchymal GS689
profile (consistent with ccRCC’s mesenchymal phenotype).
Benchmark vs JSD methods. rMATS AUC 86% > Cufflinks 83% >
DiffSplice 81%; the gap widens at low false-positive rate and with
outlier/low-coverage replicates (TPR improvement up to +19 pp). Mechanism:
rMATS down-weights low-count replicates, whereas Jensen–Shannon-divergence
methods weight all replicates equally regardless of read depth.
Experimental-design findings (a second contribution beyond the tool):

Replicates are essential; pooling/merging RNA before sequencing is
not recommended — at fixed total reads, replicate analysis always beats
pooled, and pooled data is highly sensitive to outliers.
The optimal sample-size vs sequencing-depth trade-off depends on
within-group variability: at low SD (0.01–0.02) ~3 replicates suffice; at
high SD more replicates are needed (up to +31% power). Over-splitting a
fixed budget into too many replicates reduces power by raising per-replicate
uncertainty. A low-coverage pilot to estimate variability is advised.


I/O. Inputs: raw reads + genome + transcript annotation (TopHat used for
mapping in the paper); outputs per-event P value + FDR. Source:
rnaseq-mats.sourceforge.net.

Connections

Primary source for splicing-methods — the “rMATS-style event callers”
already referenced there; the empirical, event-based counterpart to the
long-read / single-cell toolkit in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Defines the working metric percent-spliced-in (ψ/PSI, ΔPSI) used across
the splicing literature in this wiki.
Cancer splicing — its EMT (epithelial↔mesenchymal) and tumor–normal use
cases connect to splicing-and-disease.
Complements, does not overlap, the sequence-prediction line. rMATS
quantifies splicing from observed reads; splicing-code models
(spliceai, from capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the)
predict splicing from sequence — measurement vs prediction.

Open questions

Two-isoform limit. rMATS models two isoforms per region; the authors note
the binomial could be generalized to a multinomial for complex events
yielding >2 isoforms.
Bias correction. rMATS uses raw counts; prior bias-correction methods gave
no improvement in their hands, but the framework can ingest adjusted counts if
shown to help.
Beyond splicing. The isoform-ratio framework is claimed to extend to
alternative polyadenylation and RNA editing — untested here.

wiki/sources/shin-2023-rare-variation-in.title: shin-2023-rare-variation-in-noncoding-regions
wiki/sources/shin-2023-rare-variation-in.content:
Rare variation in noncoding regions with evolutionary signatures contributes to autism spectrum disorder risk
One-line: Rare, likely-recessive inherited variants in three classes of evolutionarily-flagged noncoding regions (HARs, Vista Enhancers, conserved neural enhancers) contribute to ASD risk, with contribution strongly modulated by family structure.
Key takeaways

Examines three classes of noncoding regions as candidate ASD-risk cis-regulatory-elements: Human Accelerated Regions (HARs; human positive-selection signal), neural Vista Enhancers (VEs; experimentally validated), and conserved neural enhancers (CNEs; conserved + human-constrained + predicted brain enhancers) — spanning human-brain-evolution and cross-species conservation.
Rare, recessive, inherited noncoding-variants in all three classes are enriched in autism probands, but the effect size tracks family structure: largest in consanguineous families, smaller in male cases of non-consanguineous multiplex cohorts, and only detectable in females in simplex families.
Used campra (capture-based MPRA) to show VEs (24.4%) and CNEs (21.5%) act as enhancers in N2A cells more often than HARs (14.2%); a mutagenesis variant of the assay showed HAR single-nucleotide variants gain activity nearly as often as they lose it.
Functionally validated patient variants near ASD gene IL1RAPL1 (HAR3091, HAR3094) and neurobehavioral gene SIM1 (a VE) that alter enhancer activity; used crispri-crispra (dCas9-KRAB) to show HAR3094 regulates IL1RAPL1 in iPSC-derived neurons.
Implicates both human-evolved and evolutionarily-conserved regulatory regions in ASD, nominating a set of candidate patient variants and new candidate genes for further study.

Notable claims / data

Cohorts: >16,600 samples / >4,900 probands across HMCA (consanguineous, 193 probands / 662 individuals), NIMH (660 multiplex + 1,911 simplex probands), and SSC (Simons Simplex Collection; 2,167 probands / 8,186 individuals). Analyzed 3,171 HARs, 2,057 VEs, 7,386 CNEs.
HMCA (WGS, AF < 0.005, conserved bases): HAR OR = 2.142 (adj p = 0.001), VE OR = 2.074 (adj p = 0.004), CNE OR = 1.546 (adj p = 0.041). Estimated ASD contributions of ~9.9% / 11.1% / 10.0% for HARs / VEs / CNEs.
NIMH males (AF < 0.001): HAR OR = 1.262 (adj p = 0.050), VE OR = 1.255 (adj p = 0.030); CNEs not enriched. Estimated contribution 2.6% (HAR) / 3.7% (VE) — similar to the 3–5% from rare recessive coding variants, smaller than the ~10% in the consanguineous cohort. Larger effect in multiplex than simplex families.
SSC (simplex): essentially no enrichment overall; a significant excess only in HAR rare recessive variants in female cases (OR = 2.657, adj p = 0.044; ~1.7% contribution), consistent with a female protective effect.
caMPRA mutagenesis: 1,281 variants across 485 HARs; 81.5% no significant change, 10.8% increased, 7.6% decreased activity — contrasting prior mutagenesis studies where most changes decreased expression.
HARs/VEs/CNEs enriched near ASD-associated (SFARI) genes (adj p < 10^-29), DECIPHER developmental-disease genes, and dosage-sensitive loss-of-function-intolerant genes (pLI > 0.9; adj p < 10^-17). Chimp vs human HAR3091/HAR3094 differ in regional (telencephalon vs midbrain) enhancer activity, showing evolutionary rewiring.
Caveat: preprint (not peer-reviewed); enhancer assays in N2A cells / episomal reporters lack native chromatin context; individual elements are underpowered for element-level statistical significance.

Connections

Extends noncoding-variants and cis-regulatory-elements framing into recessive/inherited ASD risk, complementing the common-variant MPRA of lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric and regulatory-variant MPRA of deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory.
Shares massively-parallel-reporter-assay methodology (via the campra capture-based variant) with those studies but targets rare inherited noncoding variation rather than common GWAS variants.
Uses crispri-crispra for enhancer-gene validation, as in CRISPR CRE-mapping work such as groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at.

Open questions

Do HAR/VE/CNE recessive contributions replicate and reach element-level significance in larger consanguineous WGS cohorts?
Which specific target genes and cell types mediate the regulatory effects, and how do human-specific HAR activity changes causally alter social behavior?
How much ASD risk resides in noncoding classes beyond these three evolutionarily-defined sets, and via non-enhancer regulatory modalities?

wiki/sources/shine-2024-co-transcription.title: shine-2024-co-transcriptional-gene-regulation-in
wiki/sources/shine-2024-co-transcription.content:
Co-transcriptional gene regulation in eukaryotes and prokaryotes
One-line: A comparative review of how RNA processing — splicing, 3′-end cleavage/polyadenylation, capping, RNA folding — is coordinated with transcription in both eukaryotes and prokaryotes, framed around recent sequencing and imaging methods that capture transient nascent-RNA intermediates.
Key takeaways

Many RNA processing steps occur during transcription. In eukaryotes cotranscriptional-splicing is the central theme; the review draws striking parallels with prokaryotic co-transcriptional translation (ribosomes trailing RNA polymerase) and shows neither coupling is universal.
Two modes of co-transcriptional splicing recur across species: rapid splicing (exon ligation soon after the 3′ splice site emerges from Pol II) and delayed splicing (exon ligation after Pol II transcribes ~2 kb of downstream intron); these mirror prokaryotic “tightly coupled” versus “runaway transcription” translation modes.
New nascent-RNA long-read sequencing methods (LRS of nascent RNA, nano-COP, POINT-nano, CoLa-seq, dNET-seq) and metabolic-labelling/imaging approaches map Pol II position relative to splicing/cleavage status, allowing inference of event timing and order.
Transcription elongation rate shapes outcomes: the “kinetic competition” model links fast Pol II to alternative-exon skipping and slow Pol II to weak-splice-site recognition/exon inclusion; elongation speed also biases alternative polyadenylation (APA) site choice.
U1 snRNP emerges as a general Pol II elongation factor (beyond its splicing role): it suppresses premature cleavage/polyadenylation (telescripting) and propels Pol II through AT-rich introns, reframing intron–exon Pol II density differences as a GC-content effect.
RNA structure regulates processing co-transcriptionally; the only known eukaryotic riboswitches act on splicing, and heat-melting intron structures (e.g. tomato HSFA2) drive stress-responsive intron retention. Relevant to rna-structure-and-splicing.
Under cellular stress, 3′-end cleavage is suppressed, causing transcriptional readthrough and downstream-of-gene (DoG) transcripts — now considered a hallmark of the mammalian stress response.

Notable claims / data

Budding yeast: spliced fraction plateaus by the time Pol II is ~130 nt downstream of an intron (single-molecule intron tracking); a fast Pol II mutant substantially delays splicing completion.
A typical mammalian gene has 8–10 introns with internal exons of ~150 nt; introns constitute roughly 90% of the transcript that must be removed.
Mouse erythroleukaemia (MEL) cells: 75% of splice-junction-to-nearest-Pol-II distances are ≤300 nt (rapid splicing). CoLa-seq in human K562: most introns spliced before Pol II transcribes 1.5 kb downstream of the 3′ splice site.
POINT-nano (human) and dNET-seq (Drosophila): both rapid splicing and delayed splicing (~2 kb downstream) detected; nano-COP detected abundant splicing at 2 kb (human) and 4 kb (fly) downstream.
Operationally, the review sets thresholds: rapid co-transcriptional splicing while Pol II is <1.5 kb into the gene, delayed while >1.5 kb, and post-transcriptional after Pol II completes the gene (Fig. 2a). Caveat: distance-based and time-based (intron half-life) estimates cannot yet be directly compared owing to variable elongation rates.
U1 snRNP binding propels Pol II elongation ~six times faster over AT-rich introns than GC-rich exons; the ~sixfold elongation-rate range in mammals is better explained by GC vs AU content than by intron–exon architecture per se (TT-seq + PRO-seq “elongation index”).
Cryo-EM captured a human Pol II–DNA–nascent-RNA–U1 snRNP complex; the U1-70K subunit contacts RPB2/RPB12, and binding does not require a 5′ splice site in the nascent RNA.
“All-or-none” pre-mRNA processing: nascent transcripts are either fully spliced+cleaved or fully unspliced+uncleaved despite high overall efficiency (e.g. β-globin ~50/50); a β-thalassaemia IVSI-110 minigene mutation that strengthens a 3′ splice site increased both splicing and cleavage efficiency, evidencing splicing–cleavage coupling.
Methodological caveats flagged: metabolic labelling with uracil analogues enriches intron-containing transcripts and can slow splicing; PCR/size bias favours detection of rapidly spliced (shorter) products; reverse transcription limits study to ~6 kb transcripts; DoGs can reach up to 200 kb.
Prokaryotic contrasts: in B. subtilis RNA polymerase moves ~2× faster than ribosomes (“runaway transcription”), decoupling transcription and translation, contradicting the E. coli textbook tight-coupling model; <20% of E. coli operons have single defined start/end sites.

Connections

Anchors and complements cotranscriptional-splicing and post-transcriptional-splicing with concrete kinetic measurements and the rapid-vs-delayed dichotomy.
Strongly relevant to splicing-and-chromatin: histone post-translational modifications and nucleosome-induced Pol II pausing near the first 5′ splice site, FISH-detected stiff chromatin loops at transcribed long genes, and nuclear-periphery localization of long introns.
Connects to rna-structure-and-splicing (riboswitches, heat-melting intron structures, DMS/SHAPE probing) and to splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins (U1/U2 snRNP, SR proteins, PTB, U2AF, SF3B1, ELAV).
The spliceosome and splice-sites framing connects to mechanistic exon-definition and “first come, first served” / out-of-order splicing debates.
Among sibling sources, pairs naturally with rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of (mechanistic/regulatory reviews) and with sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying (methods, including the nascent-RNA LRS this review builds on).
The sf3b1 entity is directly invoked: CDK11 phosphorylates SF3B1 (U2 snRNP branchpoint selection), tying transcript elongation to splice-site definition.

Open questions

What molecular mechanism enforces all-or-none pre-mRNA processing and coordinates splicing across multiple introns within one transcript?
Can distance-based (Pol II position) and time-based (metabolic labelling) splicing measurements be unified, and can local Pol II elongation rates be measured in absolute time units in vivo?
Does pre-mRNA processing feed back on Pol II in eukaryotes the way co-transcriptional translation feeds back on RNA polymerase in prokaryotes?
How are long introns spliced — do current LRS methods (length-biased toward short transcripts) underestimate delayed/long-intron splicing?
Does intramolecular base pairing in nascent RNA block cleavage-site recognition, and do DoG intergenic regions have intron–exon structure / undergo splicing?
How general is post-transcriptional 3′-end cleavage (observed upon DNA damage) across biological contexts?

wiki/sources/sibley-2016-lessons-from-no.title: sibley-2016-lessons-from-non-canonical-splicing
wiki/sources/sibley-2016-lessons-from-no.content:
Lessons from non-canonical splicing
One-line: A review cataloguing the many “non-canonical” splicing events revealed by improved transcriptome sequencing — cryptic exons, microexons, recursive splicing, retained introns, exitrons, circular RNAs, chimeric/trans-spliced transcripts and atypical splice sites — their mechanisms, evolutionary role as a source of new transcripts (especially from transposable elements), their quality control, and their relevance to disease and therapy.
Key takeaways

Non-canonical splicing covers events that use cryptic splice-sites located far from annotated exons, or that deviate from the standard rules: cryptic exons, microexons, recursive splice sites, retained introns, exitrons, circular RNAs (circRNAs), chimeric RNAs and atypical splice sites. New RNA-seq methods + tailored aligners revealed these on a transcriptome-wide scale.
Exon definition — combinatorial recognition by U1/U2 snRNPs of the spliceosome, the U2AF complex and SR proteins bound to exonic enhancers — maintains fidelity and explains why most cryptic splice sites do not fire; many non-canonical events require unconventional exon definition.
Cryptic exons frequently arise from transposable elements (TEs), especially antisense Alu elements whose poly(U) tracts recruit splicing factors (U2AF2, TIA). They are repressed by RBPs (HNRNPC, NOVA, RBFOX2, TDP43); loss of repression can convert them into regulated alternative-splicing exons. Many introduce premature termination codons (PTCs) and are degraded by nonsense-mediated decay (NMD).
Microexons (<30 nt) are preferentially included in neurons (~60%), conserved, length usually a multiple of three (preserving the ORF), and enriched in synapse/axonogenesis genes; promoted by SRRM4/nSR100, which binds the long polypyrimidine tract upstream. SRRM4 is reduced in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), lowering microexon inclusion.
Recursive splicing removes a long intron in steps via recursive splice (RS) sites — a 3′ then reconstituted 5′ splice site — and in vertebrates requires a cryptic “RS exon” for exon definition. Found in extremely long introns of brain-expressed axon-guidance/cell-adhesion genes.
